### Post by scorp123 on 2008-01-12
```
# Insert the following into that new file:
[open_ldap]
nss_passwd=passwd: compat ldap
nss_group=group: compat ldap
nss_shadow=shadow: compat ldap
pam_auth=auth       required     pam_env.so
        auth       sufficient   pam_unix.so likeauth nullok
        auth       sufficient   pam_ldap.so use_first_pass
        auth       required     pam_deny.so
pam_account=account    sufficient   pam_unix.so
        account    sufficient   pam_ldap.so
        account    required     pam_deny.so
pam_password=password   sufficient   pam_unix.so nullok md5 shadow use_authtok
        password   sufficient   pam_ldap.so use_first_pass
        password   required     pam_deny.so
pam_session=session    required     pam_limits.so
        session    required     pam_mkhomedir.so skel=/etc/skel/
        session    required     pam_unix.so
        session    optional     pam_ldap.so

# Backup the /etc/nsswitch.conf file        
cp /etc/nsswitch.conf /etc/nsswitch.conf.original

# Backup the /etc/pam.d/ files
cd /etc/pam.d/
mkdir bkup
cp * bkup/

# Enable the new LDAP Authentication Profile by executing the following command:
auth-client-config -a -p open_ldap
``` So far so good ...

```
# Reboot the server and test to ensure that you can still log in using SSH and LDAP.
reboot
``` Nope, doesn't work for me. After I reboot I can no longer login. The "slapd" process seems to hang forever (guessing from the messages all the various daemons produce during system boot): "Starting slapd ..... "

Other symptoms:
- The network interface doesn't come up, I can't ping, I can't SSH into the machine
- I can't login locally on the console: After entering username and password the last thing I see is something like "Last login: bla bla bla .... " but then I never ever get a shell prompt, it just hangs.

Also I'd like to add: I did this in VMware server (running on my Ubuntu 7.10 installation), so we're talking about a virtual host here.

The only workaround I have so far is to add "init=/bin/bash" to the boot options (e.g. by hitting ESC at system start then adding this line in GRUB's menu) and then once I'm in the root prompt again, remount the "/" partition read-write and then remove the boot scripts in /etc/rc*.d  ... If I do this the system will boot normally again.

If re-enable "slapd" via "update-rc.d" then it will hang again at the next system start.

Any idea what I am doing wrong? Everything else seemed to work up to that point (never got any error message).

I'd welcome your suggestions :)

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-01-12
This is the stepping stone for single sign on (SSO). For mail servers you just need the right LDAP mail server package and configure it to go against your LDAP.

-Richard

```

myuser@mother:~$ ldapsearch -x -b 'dc=example,dc=local'
# extended LDIF
#
# LDAPv3
# base <dc=example,dc=local> with scope subtree
# filter: (objectclass=*)
# requesting: ALL
#

# example.local
dn: dc=example,dc=local
objectClass: top
objectClass: dcObject
objectClass: organization
o: example.local
dc: example

# admin, example.local

objectClass: simpleSecurityObject
objectClass: organizationalRole
cn: admin
description: LDAP administrator

# Users, example.local

objectClass: top
objectClass: organizationalUnit
ou: Users

# Groups, example.local
dn: ou=Groups,dc=example,dc=local
objectClass: top
objectClass: organizationalUnit
ou: Groups

# Computers, example.local
dn: ou=Computers,dc=example,dc=local
objectClass: top
objectClass: organizationalUnit
ou: Computers

# Idmap, example.local

objectClass: organizationalUnit
ou: Idmap

# search result

result: 0 Success

# numResponses: 7
# numEntries: 6

```

##################################################
## Special Items of Interest
##################################################

* My hostname during the installation was set to: dc01-ubuntu **(I substitued "mother" everywhere )**
* My fully qualified domain name will be: dc01-ubuntu.example.local
* After the installation my /etc/hostname was changed to: dc01-ubuntu.example.local
* After the installation my /etc/hosts was changed so that the line 127.0.1.1 contained "dc01-ubuntu dc01-ubuntu.example.local" to ensure no issues with name resolution.
* My LDAP domain is: example.local
* This translates to a Base DN of: dc=example,dc=local
* All passwords used are "12345" to keep things simple. **(I reused the same password as my root account which contains an asterisk)**
* I am not using TLS or SSL for my LDAP directory. Too much work for this tutorial.
* The user I created during the installation is: sysadmin **(I used a different named user account when running all your steps above)**
* The password I assigned during the installation is: 12345 **(as before, mine was different but contained an asterisk which I presume should be fine?)**
* This local user will be used for all configuration purposes.**(I used sudo before the commands in the tutorial when ssh'ed to my server from the client linux workstation)**

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-01-12
> **rickyjones said:**
>  For mail servers you just need the right LDAP mail server package and configure it to go against your LDAP. Any particular mail server package you'd recommend?

BTW, seems I solved my "slapd" problem. I increased the memory of my virtual machine from 256 MB to 512 MB ... and now it seems to work. No more hanging and I can login to the machine, both via its (virtual) console and via SSH.

So I guess the root of the problem was VMware's virtual machine instance hanging because it all of a sudden didn't have enough RAM when "slapd" became active.

On my real server hardware here I probably won't have this problem again, as each server here has at least 4 GB RAM. This will be fun to install ... :)

### Post by tmugan on 2008-01-12
Sorted out my problem by using webmin and "Open LDAP server" under the servers tab.
There was no admin account identified so I set that to
"cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local"
and then set the password in the same screen.

The previously stated errors no longer occur.

:guitar:

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-01-12
> **tombutcher1990 said:**
>  just the windows 98 client is a biatch!!  As far as I remember Windows 98 it is not capable to authenticate against a server. Being marketed at home users the only networking mode it understands is working in a "peer-to-peer" mode, e.g. so called "Workgroups", where all participants technically have the same rights and every user only authenticates against their own machine. For a "client-server" environment where the client authenticates against a server, e.g.  Microsoft calls this "Windows Domains", you'd have to use on OS capable of this, e.g. Windows NT 4 Workstation, Windows 2000 Professional, Windows XP Professional, or even one of the newer Windows Vista versions.

Windows 98 being MS-DOS based won't be able to work this way, as far as I remember its inner workings and functionalities.

---

### Post by tombutcher1990 on 2008-01-12

but thanks for the info

basically i was thinking it would, it does seem to have some DNS capabilities, if you go into network in control panel etc, however these are EXTREMELY primative and don't seem to do anything at all!!

i'm currently trying out windows me, altho if i remember rightly, this is just 98 in disguise!!

i will see if i can get hold of a copy of 2000. i reckon thats my best bet tbh. i don't really wana have to use xp, as the pc's i've got to work with aren't great by any stretch of the imagination, plus i don't wna have to either pay for or use "stolen" copies of xp due to the fact its a church group that i'm setting up!!

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-01-13
> **tombutcher1990 said:**
>  basically i was thinking it would, it does seem to have some DNS capabilities This has ***nothing*** to do with this whatsoever. "DNS" is a name-resolution protocol that originates from **TCP/IP** networking. So if you want to surf the Internet, your OS has to be able to send and receive DNS queries or else you will be forced to type IP addresses all the time ... e.g. **66.102.9.104** instead of the more user-friendly "**[www.google.com](www.google.com)**" .... That's what DNS is for. It translates human-readable names ([www.google.com](www.google.com)) to machine-readable IP addresses.

This has nothing whatsoever to do with how Windows (any version) authenticates a user. Don't get yourself confused over Microsoft's non-standard usage of the term "Domain" ... that's just Microsoft-specific terminology but has nothing to do with "Domain Name System" aka "DNS".

I don't think that Windows ME will be of any help to you ... If the machines in question are low-spec, why not use Linux on them? If it really has to be Windows then you might try your luck with Windows NT4 .... that should still run on low-spec machines. Just be aware that Windows NT4 has already reached its "end of life" and "end of support", e.g. there are no more patches for it. And soon the same will happen with Windows 2000 (that's why all the companies I know where they have used Windows 2000 are now evaluating their options: Upgrade to Vista? Or rather switch to Linux ... )

It's no fun to work with Microsoft OS'es :)

> **scorp123 said:**
> This has ***nothing*** to do with this whatsoever. "DNS" is a name-resolution protocol that originates from **TCP/IP** networking. So if you want to surf the Internet, your OS has to be able to send and receive DNS queries or else you will be forced to type IP addresses all the time ... e.g. **66.102.9.104** instead of the more user-friendly "**[www.google.com](www.google.com)**" .... That's what DNS is for. It translates human-readable names ([www.google.com](www.google.com)) to machine-readable IP addresses.

This has nothing whatsoever to do with how Windows (any version) authenticates a user. Don't get yourself confused over Microsoft's non-standard usage of the term "Domain" ... that's just Microsoft-specific terminology but has nothing to do with "Domain Name System" aka "DNS".

I don't think that Windows ME will be of any help to you ... If the machines in question are low-spec, why not use Linux on them? If it really has to be Windows then you might try your luck with Windows NT4 .... that should still run on low-spec machines. Just be aware that Windows NT4 has already reached its "end of life" and "end of support", e.g. there are no more patches for it. And soon the same will happen with Windows 2000 (that's why all the companies I know where they have used Windows 2000 are now evaluating their options: Upgrade to Vista? Or rather switch to Linux ... )

It's no fun to work with Microsoft OS'es :)

ok thanks very much for the info

i am trying 2000 out at the moment, altho i don't know if it will work because i need to be able to run games (1 in particular) that the kids who use this network like. and it says not compatible with 2000. but we will see eh!! lol

:(

oh well, we will see how it goes with using 2000. if not, i may give linux a go, but that is a last resort really

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-01-22
> **rickyjones said:**
>  I'll be perfectly honest here. No problem :)

> **rickyjones said:**
>  I have no advanced knowledge of NFS or its options. You see, NFS doesn't do any sort of authentication. You just mount it, and *poooof!* there it is. So having access nor not having access to something on a NFS share is defined on the filesystem level itself. So if you on your local machine have a user ID uid=1000 and group ID gid=1000 then when you mount a remote NFS share you will have instant and unrestricted access to anything that has these same uid and gid bits (uid=1000, gid=1000). So for NFS it makes a lot of sense if a user always has the same uid and gid values, this avoids weird problems when for example one file share works from one system but it doesn't work from another system (e.g. because the uid= and gid= combo are different there).

The problems start when "root" accesses a NFS share. Root's powers are infinite, so by definition anyone having a uid=0 and gid=0 should be "root" and be allowed unrestricted access to anything. That might indeed be a system administrator somewhere somehow. But it might also be an overly eager user who just sudo'd into his own "root" account on his own client machine; or it might even be an intruder who somehow managed to sneak into your network. Unnecessary to say that this is dangerous! So NFS per default says: "OK, fine, everyone gets the same permissions as if they had all my files locally .. except "root"! Anyone claiming to be "root" gets no access at all!"

But with "no_root_squash" you're turning off this very option!

So NFS treats anyone claiming to have "root" rights on their own client machine as if they had "root" rights too on the entire file share!

With this option in place one client user could "sudo su -" into the "root" account, mount the /ldaphome share, and then manipulate all the other user's shares at will ... You probably don't want that :)

The only scenario where "no_root_squash" makes sense and where you have to use it is when you work with diskless clients, e.g. where all the filesystems such as /boot, /, /usr and so on have to be mounted from a remote server. This is often done in environments where they use clusters, e.g. only few of the machines really have disks, the rest boots off the network and all that is really needed are the powerful CPU's which do the number crunching, simulate nuke explosions, earth quakes, and so on.

But on a home folder share you should leave "root_squash" (the option which NFS would default to) intact and not enable it. :)

---

### Post by glauco.b on 2008-01-22
Hello

I posted this question on this forum, but got no answers, so I'll try to ask your help.

I set up a samba based PDC with LDAP on 7.10 system (kubuntu actually, but it's the same as ubuntu I guess)

I did exactly the same steps as you suggest in your detailed tutorial, but then I ran into a problem.

At first: there is no way to connect to shares from a not joined windows host using a domain name in the form "domain\name"

At second: the XP host refuses to join the domain, because it simply tries to open an LDAP over UDP connection and because no application is listening on UDP port 389, the join request fails.

My question is:
Can you check on your samba PDC and tell me if the port 389/UDP is up?

In case it is not, are you able to join an XP host to the domain?

thanks

### Post by Smigma on 2008-01-24
Ok first off Thank you for taking the time to write this excellent guide. I myself was looking for a basic setup to get things working,which you have provided in much detail. You have saved me 2 weeks of research with your well written guide!

Everything is working the way it should be, so I know the configuration is working, just a quick observation. The part of the guide where you join the win machine to the domain seems repetitive. 

You already have the add machine script in your smb.conf file for win machines.

So why do the task of making the machine account manually, then going to the client machine and joining the domain? To me it just seemed like a unnecessary step when joining windows clients to the domain. Now i can understand doing that for Linux machines because (as far as I know) do not have this kind of built in function.

Now i tested my configuration by not making the win machine account and letting samba do that for me. I went to the client machine and joined it to the domain without making the account manually. It correctly made the machine account for me and was allowing my test users to logon to the newly added machine.

For example having 30 win clients and having to manually make 30 machine accounts using there hostnames, then having to go to each machine to join it to the domain seems repetitive (not including time consuming). When in reality you could just go to each machine and join the domain and have samba take care of the ldap side of things. Which it seemed to do on my setup. Just a thought.

THANKS AGAIN!

[COLOR="Red"][B]Attention Everyone:

There are several items that I am working on fixing right now. The major thing is that OpenLDAP does not work as expected when installing with apt-get. Apt-get automatically pre-configures OpenLDAP even though I tell it not to. The end result is that the initial database is created in /var/lib/ldap and the reconfigure process does not let us change the location. I am working on this and I promise you that I will have results within the week. At that time I will be reposting the guide in the initial post with the updated steps. I will also be correcting some other items (options with NFS, adding more personal dialog with the steps, etc...) I thank EVERYONE for helping out with this guide. Without my beta testers and end users I would have no feedback, and as an author I need as much feedback as possible. THANK YOU!!![/B][/COLOR]

-Richard

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-01-24
> **rickyjones said:**
>  There are several items that I am working on fixing right now. Excellent :)

> **rickyjones said:**
>   The major thing is that OpenLDAP does not work as expected when installing with apt-get. Apt-get automatically pre-configures OpenLDAP even though I tell it not to. The end result is that the initial database is created in /var/lib/ldap and the reconfigure process does not let us change the location. Why do you even want to change the location? That's something that puzzled me, but I did not mention it so far :confused:

If you really want to have your LDAP location on RAID or some other form of storage, well .... there is nothing stopping you from partitioning your system in advance so that **/var/lib/ldap** would be on it's own mount point, disk, storage, RAID or whatever other device you as system admin appoint as mount point for it.

Instead of trying to tweak the behaviour of "slapd" and change its default location, I'd rather tweak /etc/fstab and put that default location where I want it, e.g. on a RAID array. That would be the easier path I guess.

Chances are that on a real server installation **/var** is going to be on its own disk anyway, because on a server you got so much stuff going on in **/var** (e.g. all the web server stuff ends up in **/var/www**, and so on), so you probably will have a partitioning scheme for **/var** anyway and you will probably do this during the installation of the server; you might just as well adjust it and include **/var/lib/ldap** in the planning and do all this during the Ubuntu Server OS installation.

My suggestion: Remove the RAID part from the instructions. Instead I'd mention further above that it would be wise to have a proper partitioning scheme in place already during the OS installation. As I said ... just a suggestion. Your guide should focus on OpenLDAP and Samba IMHO. Anyone trying to follow your guide probably already has the minimum skills required to properly partition their server the way they need to; also, configuring RAID inside of VMware is kinda an interesting exercise but not really needed IMHO; I'd rather mirror the real disks where my VMware instances are running on (where the virtual machines are being stored). Again: these are just suggestions.

> **rickyjones said:**
>   I thank EVERYONE for helping out with this guide. And I thank YOU for taking the time to write this excellent and easy-to-follow guide. Really. There are many guides about Samba -OR- OpenLDAP out there... but there is hardly one good guide that takes care of -BOTH- and that actually shows how these two can go "hand in hand" (which is what most people would want!). You have my applause and my gratitude for that! :)

Something else that just caught my attention ... another poster mentioned repetitive steps. I noticed that several of the steps require you to go back and edit the same config file ... again, and in a later step again, and then once again ....

What do you think ... couldn't those steps be "optimized" a bit?

Again .... just a suggestion. :)

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-01-24
> **scorp123 said:**
> 
Why do you even want to change the location? That's something that puzzled me, but I did not mention it so far :confused:

I want to change the location to make things easier on the end user when it comes to backing up the database, etc... Remember, I come from a background of Windows where when I install software like this I can usually choose the location of the database, or change it later on. True, that a professional who is installing a new server will probably already have the partition scheme customized so that /var is on its own hard drive. I personally wish to leave the main parts of the OS alone because a reinstallation will not hurt. What I want is for the **data** to exist independently of the OS. Again .... just a suggestion. :)
Ah yes, the age old repetition. I will be doing my best to consolidate a bit. The only thing is that I also wanted to make this as simple as possible, which means more steps. Otherwise some things can get lost if the step is too long. But I will be trying to optimize some of the files, like slapd.conf.

-Richard

---

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-01-24
> **rickyjones said:**
>  What I want is for the **data** to exist independently of the OS. That's not really a problem. 

There are various good books and guides about doing a backup of OpenLDAP. Some of those guides suggest to export your entire DB into a **LDIF** file, and if necessary import it again (and yes, this may even work across products and OS boundaries, e.g. OpenLDAP can read LDIF's created by other products and vice versa ... chances aren't too bad that this will work). The data inside  the LDIF dump (= the directory, its hierarchy, all the data stored in there ...) doesn't care about its own physical location, be that **/var/lib/ldap** or **/ldapdata**  ... or **/opt/ldap/data** or whatever. Sun's Directory Server installs its stuff underneath **/opt/dsee** for example. 

And yet when you export and re-import LDIF's it will all work (with LDAP being a standard chances aren't too bad for this!), and stuff originally stored underneath Sun's **/opt/dsee** will happily live in **/var/lib/ldap** if properly imported into OpenLDAP and vice versa. LDIF even has its own RFC:
[http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2849](http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2849)

And Wikipedia claims that even Microsoft Server OS products ship with LDIF tools:
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LDIF](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LDIF)

So ... export your data to LDIF (regardless where it originally was stored) and voila: the data format itself pretty much guarantees that it will be independent from the OS.

So in the end whether it's **/ldapdata** or **/var/lib/ldap** doesn't really matter for the data stored there.

Again ... this is just a suggestion: For the sake of keeping this guide simple I'd leave the LDAP data directory in its default location. That's at least two less steps (no extra mount point needed, no editing the /etc/fstab file, one less line to change in the ldap configuration ...) for your guide. :)  Again ... just suggestions. Hey, I mean your guide is great, so please forgive me my nitpicking here :)

The thing with **/ldaphome** however and keeping it separate from **/home** is a good move. **In fact it's very smart.** I too prefer to have these different account types (LDAP account vs. local system account) in different locations, just to avoid confusion. The cool thing is ... If anything happens to the LDAP service, or to the storage device **/ldaphome** lives on etc., local users with a UNIX shell account would still be able to login because whatever happened to **/ldaphome** most likely would not affect Linux's **/home** ... As I said: it's smart to keep those two separate. :)

I just did EXACTLY everithing that is written in the howto, but webmin won't let me log in

I just can't figure out what I did wrong. I repeated the whole setup three times starding from scratch (i mean: from a clean ubuntu install), and I get always the same error.

OK, solved

It seems that in my particular install the PAM auth module of Webmin does not work.

Don't know why, since it is a clean install configured step by step for LDAP using the tutorial.

Using internal users instead of having webmin ask PAM for authentication works.

It is sufficient to change the webmin root password with the changepass.pl utility

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-01-26
Wow, sounds like some people are running into issues. Let me address a few of the above posts.

> Something I am thinking about and it was also mentioned in another thread on this forum: How about using pam_mount?
I had considered using an automounter of some sort but when I thought about it I went, "well, I need to touch the client to configure an automounter. Or I could just have the system mount the filesystem normally. Same amount of work and I'm not reliant on another piece of software." That was my train of thought and I could be wrong, but this is the easiest solution that I saw.

> Talking of which: About the d***mned WIndows user profiles. I was playing around with this (e.g. I deviated from your guide for this). My intention was that Windows users would be able to have their profiles and settings stored remotely on the server ... except it doesn't really work, I get error messages and stuff telling me that the profile could not be stored and that a local profile has to be used (and this repeats everytime my experimental user logs in ... which of course sucks).

Did you have any success with that? That would be a nice feature. With this in place a Windows user could login from anywhere *and* always get their exact settings (because their profile is on the server). This would be cool to have here in this guide too
In my opinion roaming profiles are the devil. With that said, good luck! With that said, the problem is most likely due to permissions. Try chmodding the /home/samba/profiles folder to be 777 as a test. Make it recursive as well. Also, is the share enabled?

> I was wondering for version 3 of this guide, if you could make it a little more modular.
I feel that modular is in the eye of the beholder. Yes, I am trying to break the guide up in such a way so that users can discern for themselves whether or not they need to follow this step or that step. But, the guide is aimed to replace the basics of a Windows Small Business Server (err, just the file sharing/authentication part though), so we do need to have the SAMBA steps, etc... But, it is up to you to figure out if you /need/ to follow it.

> Also noting the minimum requirements for the system, such as people have realised systems hang when only running 256mb of RAM. Could anyone run any tests on how much memory you may need per extra client being attached to the system?
System requirements are being a bother for me. When I first started with version 1.0 my VM had 256MB of memory. I want to say that 15% of the time some process hung the machine during bootup. This caused logins to fail. A hard reboot fixed it. I upped the memory to 384. Same thing about the same percentage. I have a feeling that this will work fine with 256MB for several linux clients with almost no file usage. In regards to the hang, I think BIND has issues on startup and I have NO CLUE on how to troubleshoot this. I don't even know where to begin! Anyone have an idea on that? 

> 
One last note/question. Once you've installed Webmin and logged in, there is a LDAP Server Settings option, but it says the module isnt configured. Is there a way you could configure the module for Webmin so you could admin users and groups from Webmin rather than having to do it through a terminal?
AH! You noticed this too? You can thank Ubuntu for breaking this behavior if I remember correctly. Worked fine on Ubuntu 7.04... Anyways, even with the older version I was not able to add users/groups because the Webmin module does not have the same schema setup as we are using with the smbldap-tools package. Lame, I know. I'm hoping to get more advanced with users/groups in version 3.0, but that is just a hope.

> It seems that in my particular install the PAM auth module of Webmin does not work.
Following the guide exactly netted that response? I ran into that if the system was having issues (hung process at bootup) but not otherwise. Webmin should always let "sysadmin" log in. Odd. I'm glad you found a work around!

> And I get the error: [code]mount.nfs: can't get address for dc01-ubuntu.example.local[code]
This looks exactly like a DNS problem. Did you configure your domain name in BIND using Webmin? On the client try typing "nslookup dc01-ubuntu" and see what comes back. If it comes back with an error then DNS is not working correctly. To supplement add this line to your clients /etc/hosts file: 192.168.0.60 dc01-ubuntu.example.local

Replace the IP (192.168.0.60) above with the IP for your server.

Whew, I hope most people are having some luck with this guide!

-Richard

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-01-26
> **HornedBeast said:**
>  Once you've installed Webmin and logged in, there is a LDAP Server Settings option, but it says the module isnt configured. If you take a close look there is a link that would take you to the configuration section of the module. What I noticed is that **webmin** will default to a config file that doesn't exist on Ubuntu, e.g. it searches for **/etc/ldap-nss.conf**  (or something like that). Just remove the "nss" parts so that the configuration points to **/etc/ldap.conf** and voila, it works. 

**[COLOR="Red"]But I don't recommend using it for anything but browsing through the data.[/COLOR]** See below.

> **HornedBeast said:**
>   Is there a way you could configure the module for Webmin so you could admin users and groups from Webmin rather than having to do it through a terminal?  I don't recommend that. I hosed my system via Webmin. Webmin makes some false assumptions and when you modify the your LDAP data via Webmin things can go wrong.

Instead I'd recommend **phpldapadmin** (which gets installed too per ricky's guide). You just have to login with the proper credentials, e.g.
cn=admin,dc=yourdomain,dc=yournet

... and you're in. With **phpldapadmin** it's easy to e.g. clone users with minimum efforts (just remember to change their full name details, their user id's, etc.) and it will work and not hose the data.

So I'd clearly recommend that over webmin.

Another thing I looked at was "LAT" (LDAP admin tool). You will find it in the repos (e.g. via Synaptic): ```
sudo apt-get install lat
```

Haven't played around with it a lot so far, but if you need a GUI-based tool this is probably worth taking a look at.

**"a policy is in effect which prevents you from connecting to this print queue"**

Do you have any hint on what might be wrong here?

**_Some (IMHO) relevant config file snippets:_**

```

[global]
workgroup = MYCOMPANY
server string = %h (OpenLDAP, Samba, Ubuntu 7.10)
dns proxy = yes
log file = /var/log/samba/log.%m
syslog = 0
panic action = /usr/share/samba/panic-action %d

security = user
encrypt passwords = true
passdb backend = ldapsam:ldap://localhost/

obey pam restrictions = no
#
#	Begin: Custom LDAP Entries
#
ldap admin dn = cn=admin,dc=mycompany,dc=com
ldap suffix = dc=mycompany,dc=com
ldap group suffix = ou=Groups
ldap user suffix = ou=Users
ldap machine suffix = ou=Computers
ldap idmap suffix = ou=Users
; Do ldap passwd sync
ldap passwd sync = Yes
passwd program = /usr/sbin/smbldap-passwd %u
passwd chat = *New*password* %n\n *Retype*new*password* %n\n *all*authentication*tokens*updated*
add user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -m "%u"
ldap delete dn = Yes
delete user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-userdel "%u"
add machine script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -w "%u"
add group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupadd -p "%g"
delete group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupdel "%g"
add user to group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -m "%u" "%g"
delete user from group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -x "%u" "%g"
set primary group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-usermod -g "%g" "%u"
#
#	End: Custom LDAP Entries
#

;   invalid users = root
;   unix password sync = no

passwd program = /usr/bin/passwd %u
passwd chat = *Enter\snew\sUNIX\spassword:* %n\n *Retype\snew\sUNIX\spassword:* %n\n *passwd:*password\supdated\ssuccessfully* .

# This boolean controls whether PAM will be used for password changes
# when requested by an SMB client instead of the program listed in
# 'passwd program'. The default is 'no'.
;   pam password change = no

########## Domains ###########

domain logons = yes
logon path = \\%N\profiles\%U
logon drive = H:
logon home = \\%N\%U
logon script = logon.cmd

########## Printing ##########

############ Misc ############

domain master = auto
[homes]
comment = Home Directories
browseable = no
writable = yes
create mask = 0700
directory mask = 0700

[netlogon]
comment = Network Logon Service
path = /home/samba/netlogon
guest ok = no
writable = yes
share modes = no

[profiles]

path = /home/samba/profiles
read only = no
store dos attributes = yes
guest ok = no
browseable = no
printable = no
create mask = 0600
directory mask = 0700
#  hide files = /desktop.ini/outlook*.lnk/*Briefcase*/

[printers]
comment = All Printers
browseable = yes
guest ok = yes
path = /var/spool/samba
printable = yes
public = yes
writable = no
create mode = 0700

[print$]
comment = Printer Drivers
path = /var/lib/samba/printers
browseable = yes
read only = yes
guest ok = no
write list = root, @lpadmin
```

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-01-28
> **HornedBeast said:**
> Just a very quick question. If I wish to ignore all the Windows parts as I dont have Windows XP anywhere, do I just skip all the Samba sections of the guide I assume?

Yes, that should be safe to do. However you might run into issues adding users, etc, since that part of the guide is dependent on SAMBA and the related smbldap-tools scripts.

The best bet, in my opinion, would be to install the LDAP server and use PHPLDAPAdmin to add users and groups. That /might/ work. I have not tested that though.

-Richard

```

logon drive = H:

logon script = logon.cmd

########## Printing ##########

############ Misc ############

[homes]
comment = Home Directories

writable = yes
create mask = 0700
directory mask = 0700

[netlogon]

path = /home/samba/netlogon
guest ok = no
writable = yes
share modes = no

[profiles]

read only = no

[print$]

write list = root, @lpadmin
```

I like how you have your smb.conf file laid out, easy to read. :)

I'm not sure on how CUPS/SAMBA/Printing works but what I recommend doing is changing the "read only" parameter for all print share items to be "no". Also, for "[print$]" it looks like root and lpadmin have write privileges. Try adding in your user there and see if it works.

-Richard

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-01-29
Hmmm... I found something about my problem. There are Samba commands which will grant rights to users. It's all explained in details here:

[http://us3.samba.org/samba/docs/man/Samba-HOWTO-Collection/NetCommand.html#id364498](http://us3.samba.org/samba/docs/man/Samba-HOWTO-Collection/NetCommand.html#id364498)

So if I wanted to give printing rights to a certain user, I could do something like this: ```
sudo net rpc rights grant "MYCOMPANY\useraccount" SePrintOperatorPrivilege -U root"
``` If I do that here the system responds with:
```
Successfully granted rights.
``` I have no time now to test this (gotta rush to a meeting with a customer ... ) but the response from the system looks encouraging :)

---

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-01-31
> **jonnydobson said:**
>  Unfortunately I haven't solved this hang issue yet.  Sorry if I ask something stupid here: But you're using VMware, right? When I did this on VMware I too had some hangs. Increasing the RAM from 256 MB to 512 MB helped. A few days later it also hung with that setup, so I then went to the **/etc/rc2.d** and changed the order of some of the boot scripts, e.g. I renamed **S14slapd** to **S99slapd** so it would start as very very last program. This helped too.

RIght now I am trying out variations of this guide (e.g. with roaming Windows profiles) on a real PC which too only has 512 MB RAM (just perfect for a test machine!). And here I experience no hangs at all, it seems that the Pentium-4 1.6 GHz CPU does a better job than the simulated CPU inside of VMware.

So my suggestions would be these:

- double-check the passwords and the LDAP config files. Wrong passwords or wrong parameters somewhere somehow can cause hangs too.

- Change the order of the boot scripts by renaming them, e.g. I moved **S14slapd** to **S99slapd** ... this helped in my case.

- Try this on a real PC (if you haven't already), maybe it's something in VMware which is causing this. I have not yet experienced this hang on my real PC here whereas the identical setup inside of VMware hangs here and there.

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-02-01
> **rickyjones said:**
> Changing the order of the boot scripts is exactly what I wanted to test, but I had NO CLUE on how to do this!  OK, I will explain it here in details ... in the hope that this is useful too others who may stumble across this:

If you go to the **/etc** directory you will notice these directories:

[B]init.d/
rc0.d/
rc1.d/
rc2.d/
rc3.d/
rc4.d/
rc5.d/
rc6.d/
rcS.d/[/B]

These directories are ***very important*** for the boot process of a Linux machine. **[COLOR="Red"]Don't you ever ever mess around with those directories unless you are perfectly sure you know what you do.[/COLOR]** 

The numbers and the letter "S" in the **/etc/rc*.d** directories represent the **runlevel** these sets of scripts need to be started or killed in. 

**Now what is a "runlevel"?** Wikipedia explains it in a very nice way:
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Runlevel](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Runlevel)
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Init](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Init)

In short: A **runlevel** is a certain state a UNIX-like machine may be or not be in; it's also a way to start or to kill a whole lot of stuff in one go. For example: Traditionally (and please note that Debian-based distros ***break*** this tradition!) **runlevel 5** meant: **"GUI + network services + multi-user logins enabled" ** whereas for example **runlevel 4 is not defined or used** and **runlevel 3** means: **"No GUI, command-line only + network services running + multi-user logins enabled"** (e.g. on SUSE you'd use this for a server).

As I mentioned above: [COLOR="Red"]Debian-based distros such as Ubuntu ***break*** this tradition[/COLOR], e.g. **everything is in [COLOR="Red"]runlevel 2[/COLOR].**

To find out your current runlevel, try this command in a terminal: **runlevel**

The system will most likely respond with this:
```
N 2
``` ... Where "N" is the previous runlevel and "2" is the current runlevel. "N" usually means "powered off".

On a SUSE system you might get answers such as:
```
5 3
``` ... which means the system in question was in GUI mode before an admin turned the GUI off by issueing the **"init 3"** command.

Another way to see the current runlevel is this command: **who -r**```
> who -r
         run-level 2  2008-02-01 09:24                   last=
```


**_Back to those directories up there:_**

**/etc/init.d** contains all the **"init" and "kill" scripts** which need to be executed upon **changing runlevels**. **Booting, rebooting, powering off ... it's all the same**: You are changing runlevels when you do that. That's how the system sees this.

The directories named **/etc/rc?.d** (e.g. **/etc/rc2.d** and so on) contain **symbolic links** that link to the scripts inside of **/etc/init.d** ...

**_And there is a certain logic to the names of those links:_**

**S<number>**:
These are **start scripts**. Those scripts get executed when **starting into** this runlevel. E.g. for **runlevel 2** all the scripts inside the directory **/etc/rc2.d** would be executed in the order of their numbers: **S00something, S01somethingelse, S02something .... S99lastThing**

**K<number>**:
These are **kill scripts**. Those scripts will be executed **upon leaving the runlevel**, before the start scripts of the new runlevel (if any) are started. Same logic as above: the number behind the "K" letter determines in which order things get killed or not.


**_And now, to the main topic (finally!)_**

[COLOR="Red"]How do we change the boot order of certain things?[/COLOR]


**_The "quick + dirty" way:_**

Go to the offending symbolic link and just move it to a new number! Example from my system:  Let's suppose I am somehow convinced that the start script for VMware is causing me trouble, Let's go to **/etc/rc2.d** and take a look: ```
S20vmware-server -> ../init.d/vmware-server
``` ... So as we can see from the number, VMware is supposed to be the 20th thing that is supposed to start during boot. Now all I need to do is to move it to a new number:
```
cd /etc/rc2.d
sudo mv S20vmware-server S999vmware-server
``` ... now I can be quite sure that it will be pretty much the last thing that will start here on this system.

**This way is quick + dirty** ... but it's nontheless a perfectly valid way to e.g. troubleshoot things that hang during boot.


**_The proper way:_**

If we open the offending script we will see a few interesting things. Don't you wonder how come that VMware is the 20th script that will execute? And not the 19th ... or the 21st? How does the system determine that? Let's take a look at the script and its first few lines:
**sudo gedit /etc/init.d/vmware-server**

We see this:
```
...
...
### BEGIN INIT INFO
# Provides: VMware
# [COLOR="Red"]Required-Start: $network $syslog[/COLOR]
# Required-Stop:
# Default-Start: 2 3 5
# Default-Stop: 0 6
# Short-Description: Manages the services needed to run VMware software
# Description: Manages the services needed to run VMware software
### END INIT INFO
...
...

``` This is a so called **init-Info Header** ... Not all scripts have that. But the more sophisticated ones do have it.

See the parts I marked in red?  Those parts define what other scripts need to be executed before VMware can execute!

And the proper way to install a new init script on Ubuntu is this command:  **update-rc.d**

So when I install a new script e.g. like this:
**sudo update-rc.d foobar defaults**

Then this tool will look at the info headers (if any) and based on this determine which scripts need to be started or killed in which order.

So the proper way (as opposed to the quick + dirty way above!) to change the boot order would be this:

- take a look at the offending script
- check its init-info header
- edit the "**# Required-Start: **" and "**# Required-Stop:** " parts as needed by adding or removing the names of scripts that need to be there or that need to be gone before your script can execute

... and then execute **update-rc.d** again (as root, e.g. by using "sudo").


Hope this helped. :)

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-02-01
> **rickyjones said:**
>  I may look into including basic email services in the future that authenticate against the LDAP. Would anyone be interested in that feature? I am currently investigating this. I found some guides which have a certain similarity to your own guide.

Some of the good stuff I found so far:

**Migrating a Mail Server to Postfix/Cyrus/OpenLDAP:**
[http://linuxgazette.net/124/pfeiffer.html](http://linuxgazette.net/124/pfeiffer.html)

[B]Secure Virtual Mailserver HOWTO
Postfix + OpenLDAP + Dovecot + Jamm + SASL + SquirrelMail:[/B]
[http://wanderingbarque.com/howtos/mailserver/mailserver.html](http://wanderingbarque.com/howtos/mailserver/mailserver.html)

**Large-Scale Mail with Postfix, OpenLDAP and Courier:**
[http://www.linuxjournal.com/article/5917](http://www.linuxjournal.com/article/5917)


Another thing I am currently investigating:

On my test server a user can now use both a Windows or a Linux desktop and have a roaming profile, e.g. login and get their settings from any configured LDAP-/Samba-Client station. Wonderful.

But: The profiles are separate at the moment, e.g. a Samba user's profile and documents live in **/home/samba/<username>** at the moment, and a Linux user's stuff lives in **/home/ldap/<same username>** ....

On both Windows and Linux there are pre-configured folders such as "My Documents", "My Music", "My Pictures" and so on that are supposed to hold the user's files. Wouldn't it be nice if those files were accessible from both OS'es? Of course it would.

I am evaluating various options here .... One option is to have this on a third share e.g. **/home/data/<username>** and have the other two network mounts point to this location so the data (pictures, music, bookmarks, etc.) is accessible from both client OS'es

Another cheap, quick and dirty way would be to use symbolic links. Apparently Samba maps symbolic links transparently to Windows clients, so I could have Windows' "My Documents" folder point to the equivalent Linux home folder. A user wouldn't notice anything, they'd just be happy that their stuff is available from both OS'es.

Just my thoughts here. :)

On both Windows and Linux there are pre-configured folders such as "My Documents", "My Music", "My Pictures" and so on that are supposed to hold the user's files. Wouldn't it be nice if those files were accessible from both OS'es? Of course it would.

I am evaluating various options here .... One option is to have this on a third share e.g. **/home/data/<username>** and have the other two network mounts point to this location so the data (pictures, music, bookmarks, etc.) is accessible from both client OS'es

Thanks for the links!

Now, as for the My Docs/Pics/etc... on WIndows. If you enable roaming profiles then all that stuff is copied to that network location. What you want to do is look into Folder Redirection via the local Group Policy. Redirect My Documents to \\server\ldaphome\%username%\Documents or something like that. Then My Documents is located in the /ldaphome/%username% folder on the server so all documents between Windows and Linux will be available at all times.

The basic premise here is that in Windows you may not want roaming profiles because if you have large files in My Docs this is copied to and from the server at login and log off this can take a while. The solution is to use Folder Redirection so My Documents is not copied at login/log off.

-Richard

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-08
I just wanted to say thanks Richard!!  Your guide got me going, when the last two months of following other guides got me nowhere!  My server is now authenticating thanks to you!  I even used your guide, and re-wrote it with specific details for my company as a disaster recovery guide in case something happens to me and they loose the server.  They can now set up a domain controller just like the one we have now from scratch if necessary, and pretty much anyone can do it.

Now all I have to do is figure out a few more things, and I can put this thing in service permanently.

In case anyone knows the answers to these, or a link that'll get me started, here are my next projects with the PDC I have created with Ubuntu 7.10 Server.

1)  Set up all users so that their My Documents folder and e-mail store folder are located in their home directory on the server.  I may consider roaming profiles (see #2)
2)  How do I set up roaming profiles?  I have to consider the fact that I have 40 workstations though.  If everyone logs in first thing in the morning at the same time, what's going to happen to my network?  It's not gigabit.. it's 10/100 with all workstations connected at 100 Base-T full duplex, however five of the workstations are at a remote location and are linked through a wireless bridge which (with encryption and security overhead) gets only about 6-10mbit max throughput.
3)  Backup options.  Since I do not have a BDC yet (see #4) this is very important.  I need to back up this server!  I'm thinking of buying Acronis software for that, since they tell me I can restore their backups to a VMWare server easily.  I have my doubts, and will be evaluating that fairly soon.  I tried remastersys, but have yet to get it to work (driver issues I think).  Even if I had to do a manual install and update, then just restore folders, I need some software to do it with.  I have an RD1000 drive in the server with 300GB removable disks so storage of the backup files shouldn't be a problem.  Storage of full roaming profiles might be, but remember my max is 40 workstations, and I actually use 27 or so on a regular basis, the rest are extras.  Anyone have recommendations?
4)  VMWare server.  This server happens to have a quad-core processor, 4gb ram, 1TB Raid 5 array W/7200rpm drives.  It's got more power than really needed, but I purchased it thinking seriously about running VM's on it for disaster recovery.  There are guides on other threads.. so this one I will figure out independantly.

I know Richard's guide said we were on our own if we wanted to set up some of these advanced features, so can anyone else make some suggestions?  When I'm done I'll put it all in one big guide and post it for the community.

The one thing I have found the most frustrating about Linux (I've only been learning Ubuntu for about 4 months) thus far is that documentation is weak and scattered.  Not so much weak as complicated for the newbie like me.  These forums are the best I have run across so far, and these HOW-TO guides are great!  Step-By-Step is how Ubuntu instructions should be written.  Thanks again Richard, and thanks to everyone here in this forum!

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-02-08
> **spliner said:**
>   2)  How do I set up roaming profiles?  See the postings between myself and Richard a few pages earlier. It isn't that hard to setup.

> **spliner said:**
>   I have to consider the fact that I have 40 workstations though.  If everyone logs in first thing in the morning at the same time, what's going to happen to my network?  That's bad! You will have to educate your users not to keep big files and God-knows-what (e.g. MP3 and video collections) on their desktops because that stuff will get sync'ed everytime a user logs in or logs out. If you don't have a network in the 1 GBit range or beyond this will definitely bring it to its knees.

> **spliner said:**
>  3)  Backup options.   My buddies here at work (especially the guys in charge of our dedicated backup server) use **bacula** ... See here:
[http://www.bacula.org/](http://www.bacula.org/)

And I myself also use "tar" and good old fashioned manual work:
[http://linuxmint.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=3969](http://linuxmint.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=3969)

Both methods work tip top and are free.

> **spliner said:**
>  I need to back up this server!  I'm thinking of buying Acronis software for that, since they tell me I can restore their backups to a VMWare server easily.  First, you don't need their software. It does nothing existing free solutions wouldn't be able to do as well. Second, if you want to spend money, spend it on this book -- and don't let yourself be fooled by its age. The age of this book is totally irrelevant:

[UNIX Backup & Recovery]("http://www.amazon.com/UNIX-Backup-Recovery-Curtis-Preston/dp/1565926420/ref=pd_bbs_sr_1/103-3018831-2988633?ie=UTF8&s=books&qid=1184798526&sr=8-1")

This is the "Holy Bible" (tm) of doing backups on UNIX-like systems. Trust me: It's a must-have :)

> **spliner said:**
>   I tried remastersys I like "remastersys" and I truly admire the guy who wrote it ... but let's face it: It's the wrong tool for this job! What you want is a dedicated backup server somehow, preferably with a tape drive and lots of storage ... you don't want a server backup lying around on a DVD which isn't exactly the most reliable media and most likely won't be able to hold all the files (you got 40 workstations you said!!) of all your users anyway. Spanning across multiple disks? Good luck with that ... if one disk gets scratched, damaged or lost your backup is worth nothing because you won't be able to perform a restore. 

And that's the golden rule here:  Every backup is only worth as much as the (hopefully simple!) restore procedure that will get the data back. If your restore depends on obscure formats or proprietary software that most likely isn't installed or readily available when disaster strikes and you need to perform a restore real fast ... then you already lost.

That's why I keep my stuff on a dedicated backup server with a DLT tape library plus I have tar.gz archives of everything on external USB disks which I can connect anywhere to get my data back ... just in case. (yes, I am paranoid .... But in my job that helps :D )

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-08
> **spliner said:**
> I just wanted to say thanks Richard!!  Your guide got me going, when the last two months of following other guides got me nowhere!  My server is now authenticating thanks to you!  I even used your guide, and re-wrote it with specific details for my company as a disaster recovery guide in case something happens to me and they loose the server.  They can now set up a domain controller just like the one we have now from scratch if necessary, and pretty much anyone can do it.

You're very welcome! I'm glad that you appreciate the work I put into this. I'm also glad that it was able to work for you. I wish you well in your future endeavors. If you'd like any more help from me off-forum then send me an email @ [email]richard@rrcomputerconsulting.com[/email]. I'd be glad to help out where I can.


> **spliner said:**
> 
1)  Set up all users so that their My Documents folder and e-mail store folder are located in their home directory on the server.  I may consider roaming profiles (see #2)

I'm going to split this up into 3 parts: My Documents, Email, and Roaming Profiles.

**My Documents**
For this I recommend "Folder Redirection" via a Group Policy Object (GPO) setting. You will need to make this change on each workstation. Another way to go is to create a login script that remaps the My Documents folder at every login. The path you would wish to map it to would be similar to \\server\ldaphome\%username%

In this manner all the documents would be stored on the server. Offline files on the client will be used to keep local copies of the files in the event that the server is running into issues.

**Email**
How is email configured in your org? Do you use Outlook? I see three options here: 1) Remap the .PST file to go to a network location. 2) Use a GPO to redirect the "Application Data" folder to a network location similar to the My Documents redirection above. 3) Switch your email services to something like IMAP and point all clients to the IMAP server so email is not saved in the profile.

**Roaming Profiles**
My official stance on this: DO NOT USE! Unless you have a superfast network and very little stored in the profiles... DO NOT USE! I have had bad experiences with it. Say for instance the workstation has issues connecting to the file server. Then all the work you put into it is for naught as the user now no longer has their normal files or email because you put it in a remote location. Unhappy users = unhappy admins. Extended login times are another issue pending on the size of the profile.


> **spliner said:**
> 
2)  How do I set up roaming profiles?  I have to consider the fact that I have 40 workstations though.  If everyone logs in first thing in the morning at the same time, what's going to happen to my network?  It's not gigabit.. it's 10/100 with all workstations connected at 100 Base-T full duplex, however five of the workstations are at a remote location and are linked through a wireless bridge which (with encryption and security overhead) gets only about 6-10mbit max throughput.

Roaming profiles are actually very easy to configure (see my warnings above though!). In your /etc/samba/smb.conf file there is a line that we added: **logon path =**

Replace that line with: **logon path = \\server\profiles\%username%**

Keep in mind that the above is based on you having defined the shared folder and you have the correct permissions in place.

> **spliner said:**
> 
3)  Backup options.  Since I do not have a BDC yet (see #4) this is very important.  I need to back up this server!  I'm thinking of buying Acronis software for that, since they tell me I can restore their backups to a VMWare server easily.  I have my doubts, and will be evaluating that fairly soon.  I tried remastersys, but have yet to get it to work (driver issues I think).  Even if I had to do a manual install and update, then just restore folders, I need some software to do it with.  I have an RD1000 drive in the server with 300GB removable disks so storage of the backup files shouldn't be a problem.  Storage of full roaming profiles might be, but remember my max is 40 workstations, and I actually use 27 or so on a regular basis, the rest are extras.  Anyone have recommendations?

If I had the funding and available materials then this is how I would have a production environment configured:

* A SAN or similar would store all user and LDAP data.
* Two servers would be the minimum. Each is attached to SAN and uses this as shared storage. Each server can write and read at the same time.
* DNS will be utilized to load balance and perform failover if necessary.
* Backups can be made over the SAN for the only data that matters.

After the first server is built and configured and working I would create a disk image of the OS so that deployment can be simple. Change the hostname and IP and you should be in business for several secondary servers.

> **spliner said:**
> 
4)  VMWare server.  This server happens to have a quad-core processor, 4gb ram, 1TB Raid 5 array W/7200rpm drives.  It's got more power than really needed, but I purchased it thinking seriously about running VM's on it for disaster recovery.  There are guides on other threads.. so this one I will figure out independantly.

Create the above images and boot them in VMWARE - DR at your fingertips. Please note, this is all theoretical and I have no working proof of this.

> **spliner said:**
> 
The one thing I have found the most frustrating about Linux (I've only been learning Ubuntu for about 4 months) thus far is that documentation is weak and scattered.  Not so much weak as complicated for the newbie like me.  These forums are the best I have run across so far, and these HOW-TO guides are great!  Step-By-Step is how Ubuntu instructions should be written.  Thanks again Richard, and thanks to everyone here in this forum!
I completely agree with your statements. Documentation is weak. I wish to address programmers here: If you are writing software and know that your documentation may not be understood by "average" people then PLEASE look into hiring or asking for volunteer technical writers to help create useful documentation.

I definitely wrote this 2 hours ago but only previewed and forgot to post it... I feel dumb!

Thanks,

-Richard

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-08
> **rickyjones said:**
> **My Documents**
For this I recommend "Folder Redirection" via a Group Policy Object (GPO) setting. You will need to make this change on each workstation. Another way to go is to create a login script that remaps the My Documents folder at every login. The path you would wish to map it to would be similar to \\server\ldaphome\%username%

In this manner all the documents would be stored on the server. Offline files on the client will be used to keep local copies of the files in the event that the server is running into issues.


I think I would rather have it done with a logon script.  It makes things more server-centric.  Once I get the netlogon working again I will give this a shot.

> **rickyjones said:**
> 
**Email**
How is email configured in your org? Do you use Outlook? I see three options here: 1) Remap the .PST file to go to a network location. 2) Use a GPO to redirect the "Application Data" folder to a network location similar to the My Documents redirection above. 3) Switch your email services to something like IMAP and point all clients to the IMAP server so email is not saved in the profile.


For the moment we are using OE.  We may switch to Outlook 2007 next year, but until then we'll be using OE's simple pop3 service.  The store folder should be easy enough to map over, I'll try this once things are working again.

> **rickyjones said:**
> 
**Roaming Profiles**
My official stance on this: DO NOT USE! Unless you have a superfast network and very little stored in the profiles... DO NOT USE! I have had bad experiences with it. Say for instance the workstation has issues connecting to the file server. Then all the work you put into it is for naught as the user now no longer has their normal files or email because you put it in a remote location. Unhappy users = unhappy admins. Extended login times are another issue pending on the size of the profile.

Roaming profiles are actually very easy to configure (see my warnings above though!). In your /etc/samba/smb.conf file there is a line that we added: **logon path =**

Replace that line with: **logon path = \\server\profiles\%username%**

Keep in mind that the above is based on you having defined the shared folder and you have the correct permissions in place.


It's too bad, but you're right, I'll bring my network to its knees.  I'm going to pass on this, that would just make more data to back up as well.  I'll stick with the OE store folder and the documents folder for the moment.  Permissions are still confusing me at the moment.  I've looked around for a good explanation of the chmod command but havn't found anything that is super easy to follow.  Part of my problem is going to be that I need each user to be able to see their home directory, but I do not want to allow them to browse other users home directory for security reasons.  Right now, well when it was working, the users could browse anyone's home folder.

> **rickyjones said:**

If I had the funding and available materials then this is how I would have a production environment configured:

* DNS will be utilized to load balance and perform failover if necessary.
* Backups can be made over the SAN for the only data that matters.

After the first server is built and configured and working I would create a disk image of the OS so that deployment can be simple. Change the hostname and IP and you should be in business for several secondary servers.

I have a couple of slower servers sitting around that can be configured as a BDC once this is complete, so I should be able to get by that way for the rest of the year. ### Post by ryjal on 2008-02-08
For those who are wondering how to do folder redirection with a login script this is how I did mine on my samba PDC.

To do this I had to play with my registry setting. Words of warning playing with registry setting can have adverse affects. Only change them if you know what you are doing.

First login to your window box as administrator and change the 
registry key to
[HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon]
"RunLogonScriptSync"=dword:00000001
If the key is not found create it.

This key described from the Microsoft site states that the RunLogonScriptSync key “Directs the system to wait for the logon scripts to finish running before it starts the Windows Explorer interface program and creates the desktop” 
Read more at  [http://www.microsoft.com/technet/prodtechnol/windows2000serv/reskit/regentry/93823.mspx?mfr=true]("http://www.microsoft.com/technet/prodtechnol/windows2000serv/reskit/regentry/93823.mspx?mfr=true")

Next change this key to
[HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon]
"SyncForegroundPolicy"=dword:00000001
If the key is not found create it.

This key described from the Microsoft site states that the SyncForegroundPolicy key “Stores configuration data for the policy setting Always wait for the network at computer startup and logon.”
Read more at [http://technet2.microsoft.com/windowsserver/en/library/b39b4962-bbef-48b9-a5e5-d65f08fcd90a1033.mspx?mfr=true]("http://technet2.microsoft.com/windowsserver/en/library/b39b4962-bbef-48b9-a5e5-d65f08fcd90a1033.mspx?mfr=true")

These registry key will tell window to wait until the login script from the network runs before continuing on.

The registry key to redirect the desktop and the My Documents folder are
[HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\User Shell Folders]
"Desktop"	For the Desktop Folder
"Personal" 	For the My Documents Folder

I redirected my “Desktop” to “%LOGONSERVER%\%USERNAME%\Desktop”
And my “My Documents” to ”%LOGONSERVER%\%USERNAME%\My Documents”
Both of these folders are located in the user home directory.

Now if you use a login script to change the registry keys in the “User Shell Folders” then values for the keys must be in hex format this is what my redirect.reg file looks like

redirect.reg```

"Personal"=hex(2):25,00,4c,00,4f,00,47,00,4f,00,4e,00,53,00,45,00,52,00,56,00,45,\
  00,52,00,25,00,5c,00,25,00,55,00,53,00,45,00,52,00,4e,00,41,00,4d,00,45,00,\
  25,00,5c,00,4d,00,79,00,20,00,44,00,6f,00,63,00,75,00,6d,00,65,00,6e,00,74,\
  00,73,00,00,00
"Desktop"=hex(2):25,00,4c,00,4f,00,47,00,4f,00,4e,00,53,00,45,00,52,00,56,00,45,\
  00,52,00,25,00,5c,00,25,00,55,00,53,00,45,00,52,00,4e,00,41,00,4d,00,45,00,\
  25,00,5c,00,44,00,65,00,73,00,6b,00,74,00,6f,00,70,00,00,00
```

To get the hex number, you need to open up regedit( click on the”start” menu then “run” and then type regedit). Find the registry entry
[HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\User Shell Folders]
Make sure that “User Shell Folders” is selected and then select file -> export and save As “backup”. Now open each key that you want to change Desktop, Personal, etc. and make the changes you want. Now select file -> export and save as “New Reg”. Open the file “New Reg” in Notepad and you now have the hex number you need. I am sure there are a number of better ways of retrieving the hex number for the redirect value but this was the fastest way I could think of at the time.

Now run the backup.reg to undo your changes you have made so that they do not affect your administrator account.

This is my login.bat file
```
@echo off
REM changing registry keys
regedit /s redirect.reg
```

Place both files where you have set, samba to look for the login files. When this is run on start up is should redirect the folders that you have set.

Hope this helps anyone out.

Ryjal

### Post by rocsak on 2008-02-10
Great guide! No realy, great guide.
I have this problem that is driving me crazy. I' me setting this up in a mixed enviroment (both Windows and Linux clients - I haven't tried it yet with Mac, but I will) and I skipped the NFS part because I would preffer that everything is shared through Samba. 
Joining the domain from a Windows machine worked without problems and I was able to access my home folder and a public share I had setup.
On the other hand accessing the shares through Samba from an Ubuntu 7.10 client  is driving me crazy. The ldap authentication works, the user logs in without a problem, but when I try to access my shares through Places it asks for user name and password. Also my home dir in the server is not visible. When I provide the username and password I can access my shares. ( even though my home dir in the server is still not visible, but I can access it through smb://server/user)
I thought it was supposed to be single sign on, or I am wrong? Am I missing something here? Any ideas?

And another thing as a suggestion for setting this up in a production enviroment with a lot fo computers in the domain. You need something more than simple dns. Basically you need DHCP and you need your clients to dynamically register their addresses in DNS. For something like that you can look at this [article ]("http://sipx-wiki.calivia.com/index.php/HowTo_Configure_DHCP_and_DNS_Servers#Configuring_the_DHCP_Server") for a place to start.
Thanks again for the wonderfull guide.

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-02-12
> **cpthk said:**
>  Any one know why do we need a DNS server? Reverse lookup has to be correct. So having a DNS server under your own control is an advantage, especially if you are experimenting around with SSO, Kerberos and other goodies (e.g. on top of this tutorial).

> **cpthk said:**
>  is it possible to skip the DNS server part? I did exactly that (see my earlier postings), but then again I already have a DNS server somewhere in my network and configured that one (e.g. I entered my LDAP server into the DNS tables, to make sure my clients can find it).

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-12
> **ryjal said:**
> OK I got the ldap authentication working I had a couple of mistakes in the config files. Now I am on the step "Install and Configure Apache2 + PHPLDAPAdmin" Everything installed but when I tried to login this is the error I get.
```
Error
Could not bind to the LDAP server.

LDAP said: Undefined attribute type
Error number: 0x11 (LDAP_UNDEFINED_TYPE)
Description: The attribute type specified is invalid. 
```

Any Ideas?

Also what is the difference between PHPLDAPAdmin and Webmin?

Ryjal

In order to log in to PHPLDAPAdmin the username must be the full LDAP entry: cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local

WebMin controls a ton of services on your system. PHPLDAPAdmin gives you an LDAP gui.

-Richard

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-12
Ok, here's what I have done:

1)  Removed the WINS server IP of 192.168.0.125
2)  Removed the secondary and tertiary DNS numbers from the /etc/resolv.conf file.
3)  Removed all profiles except local Administrator on XP Pro workstation.
4)  Double checked that the DNS server is 192.168.0.125 for the workstation and nothing else.
5)  Rebooted both server and workstation.

Now I can log in as local Administrator and ping both dc01-ubuntu and dc01-ubuntu.example.local and they both resolve to 192.168.0.125.  Flushed the dns cache on the workstation and it still works.  Now if I try to log into my domain example, it tells the the domain controller is not available.

So.. just to test.. I log back into the local Administrator account on the workstation, double click on My Network Places, and find my server dc01-ubuntu.  Double click on it and I get prompted for username/pass.  I use the test user account we created in the guide:

username:  example/ricky
pass:  12345

I can then view the shares, both netlogon and ldaphome.  So does that mean that my problems are on the workstation and not the server?  I still cannot log into the domain with the test user account on the workstation, but it does work using the test user account on the server itself.

I have also tried removing the computer from the domain and re-adding it.  It asks me for username/pass as soon as I put in the domain of example and click ok.  I put in ricky as username, 12345 as pass, and it gives me the error "the user name could not be found".

then re-added the user according to the guide again.

smbldap-useradd -w testclient

I then tried to join the domain again using the test account ricky/12345 and got a different error.. "access denied".

Looking back in the log /var/log/samba/log.testclient it now says:

[2008/02/12 11:05:38, 0] lib/smbldap.c:smbldap_open(1014)
  smbldap_open:  cannot access LDAP when not root..

hmmm....
