### Post by spliner on 2008-02-12
/etc/samba/smb.conf

#
# Sample configuration file for the Samba suite for Debian GNU/Linux.
#
#
# This is the main Samba configuration file. You should read the
# smb.conf(5) manual page in order to understand the options listed
# here. Samba has a huge number of configurable options most of which 
# are not shown in this example
#
# Any line which starts with a ; (semi-colon) or a # (hash) 
# is a comment and is ignored. In this example we will use a #
# for commentary and a ; for parts of the config file that you
# may wish to enable
#
# NOTE: Whenever you modify this file you should run the command
# "testparm" to check that you have not made any basic syntactic 
# errors. 
#

#======================= Global Settings =======================

[global]

## Browsing/Identification ###

# Change this to the workgroup/NT-domain name your Samba server will part of
   workgroup = example

# server string is the equivalent of the NT Description field
   server string = %h server (Samba, Ubuntu)

# Windows Internet Name Serving Support Section:
# WINS Support - Tells the NMBD component of Samba to enable its WINS Server
;   wins support = no

# WINS Server - Tells the NMBD components of Samba to be a WINS Client
# Note: Samba can be either a WINS Server, or a WINS Client, but NOT both
;   wins server = w.x.y.z

# This will prevent nmbd to search for NetBIOS names through DNS.
   dns proxy = no

# What naming service and in what order should we use to resolve host names
# to IP addresses
;   name resolve order = lmhosts host wins bcast

#### Networking ####

# The specific set of interfaces / networks to bind to
# This can be either the interface name or an IP address/netmask;
# interface names are normally preferred
;   interfaces = 127.0.0.0/8 eth0

# Only bind to the named interfaces and/or networks; you must use the
# 'interfaces' option above to use this.
# It is recommended that you enable this feature if your Samba machine is
# not protected by a firewall or is a firewall itself.  However, this
# option cannot handle dynamic or non-broadcast interfaces correctly.
;   bind interfaces only = true



#### Debugging/Accounting ####

# This tells Samba to use a separate log file for each machine
# that connects
   log file = /var/log/samba/log.%m

# Put a capping on the size of the log files (in Kb).
   max log size = 1000

# If you want Samba to only log through syslog then set the following
# parameter to 'yes'.
;   syslog only = no

# We want Samba to log a minimum amount of information to syslog. Everything
# should go to /var/log/samba/log.{smbd,nmbd} instead. If you want to log
# through syslog you should set the following parameter to something higher.
   syslog = 0

# Do something sensible when Samba crashes: mail the admin a backtrace
   panic action = /usr/share/samba/panic-action %d


####### Authentication #######

# "security = user" is always a good idea. This will require a Unix account
# in this server for every user accessing the server. See
# /usr/share/doc/samba-doc/htmldocs/Samba3-HOWTO/ServerType.html
# in the samba-doc package for details.
   security = user

# You may wish to use password encryption.  See the section on
# 'encrypt passwords' in the smb.conf(5) manpage before enabling.
   encrypt passwords = true

# If you are using encrypted passwords, Samba will need to know what
# password database type you are using.  
   passdb backend = ldapsam:ldap://localhost/

   obey pam restrictions = no

#######################################################################
#COPY AND PASTE THE FOLLOWING UNDERNEATH "OBEY PAM RESTRICTIONS = NO"
#######################################################################
#
#	Begin: Custom LDAP Entries
#
ldap admin dn = cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local
ldap suffix = dc=example, dc=local
ldap group suffix = ou=Groups
ldap user suffix = ou=Users
ldap machine suffix = ou=Computers
ldap idmap suffix = ou=Users
; Do ldap passwd sync
ldap passwd sync = Yes
passwd program = /usr/sbin/smbldap-passwd %u
passwd chat = *New*password* %n\n *Retype*new*password* %n\n *all*authentication*tokens*updated*
add user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -m "%u"
ldap delete dn = Yes
delete user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-userdel "%u"
add machine script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -w "%u"
add group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupadd -p "%g"
delete group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupdel "%g"
add user to group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -m "%u" "%g"
delete user from group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -x "%u" "%g"
set primary group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-usermod -g "%g" "%u"
domain logons = yes
#
#	End: Custom LDAP Entries
#
#####################################################
#STOP COPYING HERE! 
#####################################################


;   guest account = nobody
;   invalid users = root

# This boolean parameter controls whether Samba attempts to sync the Unix
# password with the SMB password when the encrypted SMB password in the
# passdb is changed.
;   unix password sync = no

# For Unix password sync to work on a Debian GNU/Linux system, the following
# parameters must be set (thanks to Ian Kahan <<kahan@informatik.tu-muenchen.de> for
# sending the correct chat script for the passwd program in Debian Sarge).
   passwd program = /usr/bin/passwd %u
   passwd chat = *Enter\snew\sUNIX\spassword:* %n\n *Retype\snew\sUNIX\spassword:* %n\n *passwd:*password\supdated\ssuccessfully* .

# This boolean controls whether PAM will be used for password changes
# when requested by an SMB client instead of the program listed in
# 'passwd program'. The default is 'no'.
;   pam password change = no

########## Domains ###########

# Is this machine able to authenticate users. Both PDC and BDC
# must have this setting enabled. If you are the BDC you must
# change the 'domain master' setting to no
#
;   domain logons = yes
#
# The following setting only takes effect if 'domain logons' is set
# It specifies the location of the user's profile directory
# from the client point of view)
# The following required a [profiles] share to be setup on the
# samba server (see below)
;   logon path = \\%N\profiles\%U
# Another common choice is storing the profile in the user's home directory
;   logon path = \\%N\%U\profile
logon path =

# The following setting only takes effect if 'domain logons' is set
# It specifies the location of a user's home directory (from the client
# point of view)
;   logon drive = H:
;   logon home = \\%N\%U

# The following setting only takes effect if 'domain logons' is set
# It specifies the script to run during logon. The script must be stored
# in the [netlogon] share
# NOTE: Must be store in 'DOS' file format convention
   logon script = allusers.bat

# This allows Unix users to be created on the domain controller via the SAMR
# RPC pipe.  The example command creates a user account with a disabled Unix
# password; please adapt to your needs
; add user script = /usr/sbin/adduser --quiet --disabled-password --gecos "" %u

########## Printing ##########

# If you want to automatically load your printer list rather
# than setting them up individually then you'll need this
;   load printers = yes

# lpr(ng) printing. You may wish to override the location of the
# printcap file
;   printing = bsd
;   printcap name = /etc/printcap

# CUPS printing.  See also the cupsaddsmb(8) manpage in the
# cupsys-client package.
;   printing = cups
;   printcap name = cups

# When using [print$], root is implicitly a 'printer admin', but you can
# also give this right to other users to add drivers and set printer
# properties
;   printer admin = @lpadmin


############ Misc ############

# Using the following line enables you to customise your configuration
# on a per machine basis. The %m gets replaced with the netbios name
# of the machine that is connecting
;   include = /home/samba/etc/smb.conf.%m

# Most people will find that this option gives better performance.
# See smb.conf(5) and /usr/share/doc/samba-doc/htmldocs/Samba3-HOWTO/speed.html
# for details
# You may want to add the following on a Linux system:
#         SO_RCVBUF=8192 SO_SNDBUF=8192
   socket options = TCP_NODELAY

# The following parameter is useful only if you have the linpopup package
# installed. The samba maintainer and the linpopup maintainer are
# working to ease installation and configuration of linpopup and samba.
;   message command = /bin/sh -c '/usr/bin/linpopup "%f" "%m" %s; rm %s' &

# Domain Master specifies Samba to be the Domain Master Browser. If this
# machine will be configured as a BDC (a secondary logon server), you
# must set this to 'no'; otherwise, the default behavior is recommended.
;   domain master = auto

# Some defaults for winbind (make sure you're not using the ranges
# for something else.)
;   idmap uid = 10000-20000
;   idmap gid = 10000-20000
;   template shell = /bin/bash
;
; The following was the default behaviour in sarge
; but samba upstream reverted the default because it might induce
; performance issues in large organizations
; See #368251 for some of the consequences of *not* having
; this setting and smb.conf(5) for all details
;
;   winbind enum groups = yes
;   winbind enum users = yes

#======================= Share Definitions =======================

# Un-comment the following (and tweak the other settings below to suit)
# to enable the default home directory shares.  This will share each
# user's home directory as \\server\username
;[homes]
;   comment = Home Directories
;   browseable = no

# By default, \\server\username shares can be connected to by anyone
# with access to the samba server.  Un-comment the following parameter
# to make sure that only "username" can connect to \\server\username
# This might need tweaking when using external authentication schemes
;   valid users = %S

# By default, the home directories are exported read-only. Change next
# parameter to 'yes' if you want to be able to write to them.
;   writable = no

# File creation mask is set to 0700 for security reasons. If you want to
# create files with group=rw permissions, set next parameter to 0775.
;   create mask = 0700

# Directory creation mask is set to 0700 for security reasons. If you want to
# create dirs. with group=rw permissions, set next parameter to 0775.
;   directory mask = 0700

# Un-comment the following and create the netlogon directory for Domain Logons
# (you need to configure Samba to act as a domain controller too.)
[netlogon]
   comment = Network Logon Service
   path = /home/samba/netlogon
   guest ok = yes
   writable = no
   share modes = no

# Un-comment the following and create the profiles directory to store
# users profiles (see the "logon path" option above)
# (you need to configure Samba to act as a domain controller too.)
# The path below should be writable by all users so that their
# profile directory may be created the first time they log on
;[profiles]
;   comment = Users profiles
;   path = /home/samba/profiles
;   guest ok = no
;   browseable = no
;   create mask = 0600
;   directory mask = 0700

[printers]
   comment = All Printers
   browseable = no
   path = /var/spool/samba
   printable = yes
   public = no
   writable = no
   create mode = 0700

# Windows clients look for this share name as a source of downloadable
# printer drivers
[print$]
   comment = Printer Drivers
   path = /var/lib/samba/printers
   browseable = yes
   read only = yes
   guest ok = no
# Uncomment to allow remote administration of Windows print drivers.
# Replace 'ntadmin' with the name of the group your admin users are
# members of.
;   write list = root, @ntadmin

# A sample share for sharing your CD-ROM with others.
;[cdrom]
;   comment = Samba server's CD-ROM
;   writable = no
;   locking = no
;   path = /cdrom
;   public = yes

# The next two parameters show how to auto-mount a CD-ROM when the
#	cdrom share is accesed. For this to work /etc/fstab must contain
#	an entry like this:
#
#       /dev/scd0   /cdrom  iso9660 defaults,noauto,ro,user   0 0
#
# The CD-ROM gets unmounted automatically after the connection to the
#
# If you don't want to use auto-mounting/unmounting make sure the CD
#	is mounted on /cdrom
#
;   preexec = /bin/mount /cdrom
;   postexec = /bin/umount /cdrom
#
# LDAPHOME share definition
[ldaphome]
path = /ldaphome
writeable = yes
browseable = yes
security mask = 0777
force security mode = 0
directory security mask = 0777
force directory security mode = 0

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-12
User "ricky" is now fixed.

1)  deleted the user from smbldap again...
smbldap-userdel -r ricky
2)  Removed the profile from the workstation again...
-Right Click My Computer
-Left Click Properties
-Click on the Advanced Tab
-Click Settings under Profiles & delete the ricky profile from the PC
3)  Ran Crap Cleaner and cleaned out the registry on the workstation (dunno if this actually did any good but can't hurt)
4)  Manually searched the registry for all entries pertaining to "ricky" and deleted them (there were 4 entries still).
5)  Re-added the user to smbldap...
smbldap-useradd -a -m -M ricky -c "Ricky" ricky
6)  Re-Set up Ricky's password...
smbldap-passwd ricky
12345
12345

Logged back in as ricky with the pass of 12345 and I was able to browse the h: folder just fine.  Windblows must have been causing the problem all along, but I could have done something wrong when re-adding the user ricky before (couldn't remember if I used "-r" when I removed him, which meant his home folder wouldn't have necessarily been owned by the account when I re-created it).

So .. what solved it.. I am not certain, but one of those steps above did the trick.

Now if I can get DNS solved.. (adding a secondary ISP DNS works just fine, just wish that bind would do all that for me).

-Spliner

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-12
Wish list for future versions of your guide...

-TLS/SSL Enabled - Secure everything.
-Managing users - How do we make someone an administrator on the local workstations (for instance "ricky" to be an administrator)?  Right now they are all limited accounts.  Only way to log in as an Administrator is by logging in locally as Administrator.  This is not a bad thing...  maybe it's how things should be.  I have however worked on domains where the Administrator account was stored in the PDC rather than allowing it locally.
-Example re-locating documents folders on the server.  This is easy enough, but a good example in the guide would be helpful for those who might be setting up their first PDC.
-How do we store desktop settings/etc on the PDC?  This kind of stuff might have to wait until Samba 4.0 is out and stable.  In other words, I had an NT domain a few years ago where I could store the desktop icons on the server, if I added a shortcut to their desktop folder on the server, it changed on their pc when they logged in.  Although profiles were not necessarily roaming, it worked quite well.  
-Can we make different netlogon scripts for each user or group?

And my final item...

-A distribution with customizable scripts for re-naming the server and domain on installation.  I really think this needs to be done, and I think Richard and everyone here in this thread could easily make one that would be a generic PDC on first install.  I'm not sure why Ubuntu doesn't release a distro similar to that.. maybe they are waiting for Active Directory support in Samba 4.x?

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-13
For those wishing to secure the server set up in this guide quickly, firestarter can help.  Here's a quick how-to:

Note:  I use the Ubuntu-desktop (apt-get install ubuntu-desktop), because it is what I am used to.  I can confirm that this does not affect Richard's guide in the slightest (his guide worked for me the same as it does for a command-line server).  I used the Ubuntu Gutsy 7.10 alternate server distribution, and then updated and upgraded the server (apt-get update, and apt-get upgrade), then I installed the desktop (apt-get install ubuntu-desktop), rebooted, and then installed what few updates were left when prompted.  I then started with richard's guide, using gedit instead of vim for editing files, and using a terminal from the gnome desktop for all steps (sudo bash used).  This how-to is assuming you used the gnome desktop.

1) Install Firestarter
apt-get install firestarter
Note:  This requires the universe repository to be enabled, but if you followed Richard's guide you already have it enabled.
2) Run Firestarter
Go to Applications, Internet, Firestarter
3) Open needed ports for the PDC.
In the lower pane, under policy tab, right click and click "Add Rule".
-Under name, click SAMBA from the drop down box.
-Where it says "When the source is", click the "IP,Host or Network" button and enter your local network range (I use 192.168.0.x, so I put in 192.168.0.1/24).
-Enter a comment if you wish, and click Add.
-Repeat the above steps for the following port as well (DNS):
-Port 53 (DNS) (You can just select DNS from the drop-down list) and again set it up for your local network only.
-I also add VNC (Port 5900) because I use TightVNC to connect to the server (Since I installed ubuntu-desktop), and set it up for the single IP of the machine from which I manage the server.

Hope this helps.  I will be working toward securing the server with Firestarter Inactive (there are several holes in the security of the server as it is now).  I am using the free version of Nessus to scan the server for vulnerabilities.  I'll post my results as I get them so Richard can include them in his guide if he wishes.

Edit:  If you would lke firestarter to keep running when you close the configuration window, just click Edit, then Preferences, and click "Minimize to tray when window is closed".  That way Firestarter will continue to run when you close out the window (graphical version).

-Spliner

---

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-02-13
> **spliner said:**
> For those wishing to secure the server set up in this guide quickly, firestarter can help.   Sorry if I sound harsh here, but this doesn't really help. You definitely would not keep a firewall on the same system where your (application-) servers are also running. That's what dedicated firewalls are for. And "securing a server" here in the context of LDAP means: Enable SSL and/or TLS, maybe even Kerberos to do all the authentication (nice if you want to use NFS4 too...).

With firestarter your LDAP server is not really secured. All the clients are still communicating with the LDAP service unencrypted, and someone on the same subnet can still sniff all the traffic and gain information they were not supposed to get. And clients could still mount NFS shares, pose as other user (if they know how) and access things they were not meant to access .... 

So installing Firestarter here is kinda pointless. It secures nothing really. All the traffic that you'd have to allow through is still insecure.

Just my opinion and my apologies if I sounded too harsh here.

---

> Thanks for such a good guide, I have a semi working Domain Controller :D

However, I have some issues :(

When a user logs on, the netlogin script gives an error, and won't map the network drives. I've removed the time line from it, but it's exactly the same as you have it. I can browse the network shares fine, yet can't ping the server.

```
@echo off
REM # DELETE ALL MAPPED DRIVES
net use h: /delete
REM # MAP ALL NECESSARY DRIVES
net use h: "\\xteamserv01.xteam.local\ldaphome\%username%\"
```

The error it's giving me is:
```
...>allusers.bat
The network connection could not be found.

More help is available by typing NET HELPMSG 2250.

System error 67 has occurred.

The network name cannot be found.
```

```
net use
New connections will be remembered.


Status       Local     Remote                    Network

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
OK                     \\XTEAMSERV01\LDAPHOME    Microsoft Windows Network
The command completed successfully.
```

My second issue is how I'd enable Roaming Profiles - I've seen earlier in this thread that I need to enable logonpath to use a nfs share, but how do I create a nfs share?

My third, is a question. How would I get a shared resources share? So that all the user's can see this share.

1) You have a DNS issue. Make sure BIND is working fine, is configured, and that all clients are using that server for DNS resolution. The reason for not being able to ping the server is most likely due to the name resolution.

2) For roaming profiles: In the /etc/samba/smb.conf file find the line called "logon path =" - it should be BLANK after the "=" sign. Add your UNC path there. E.g.: \\server\share\%username%. Please note that you need a directory that is shared for this to happen, and the permissions must be correct. You can share a folder the same way we shared the ldaphome directory.

3) A shared drive for all users? Create a new folder on the server. Share it using SAMBA just like LDAPHOME, but change the permissions so that everyone can read/write to it. I'm not positive on the absolute permissions necessary here, however.

I hope this helps!

-Richard

Why yes, the client and server being on separate subnets could indeed be causing the issue of there is an issue with routing the traffic. I highly recommend that you put these systems on the same subnet unless you know how to configure routers to route this traffic correctly.

-Richard

Securing those were my next step after I finish making a backup I am happy with.

-Spliner

One thing to remember is that Firestarter is just a GUI for iptables.  Even without installing Firestarter you still have iptables enabled by default in Ubuntu.  I believe it also blocks all incoming connections by default (except for services that are listening) but I'm not sure.  Theres a fairly long thread somewhere around here discussing whether or not Firestarter and ClamAV are necessary if you're interested, but I don't remember which forum it's in.

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-02-13
> **spliner said:**
>  I've heard many different opinions on Firestarter.  Many say its not needed, that Ubuntu is secure "out of the box" so to speak.  Firestarter is OK for the home user who does not have a dedicated + integrated firewall + router at home.

But we're talking about running a server here, e.g. inside a corporate network ... So Firestarter is kinda "out of place" here. The point about a server is that you *want* (the right) people have access to it ... the others are blocked by the dedicated firewalls and proxies that link your company's network to the Internet.

> **spliner said:**
>   Unfortunately I will be using this server where all possible holes must be closed That's kind of a contradiction here. A server offers services to users on the network ... and to do that it has to be *open*. You can't really secure an internal server by putting a firewall on it which will let all the insecure protocols through anyway.

What this guide here really needs (@ ricky: Include this in the next version please!) is to show us how we can enable NFS4 and SSL/TLS for secure traffic. :)

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-02-13
> **kextyn said:**
>  I believe it also blocks all incoming connections by default (except for services that are listening)  if a service isn't listening then it means it is not active and then there is no point in blocking it :)

iptables does nothing "out of the box" on Ubuntu. And incoming connections --as you describe it-- are "blocked" because the service isn't even active or else the service would be in "listen" mode, and only then would the connection succeed.

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-02-13
> **rickyjones said:**
> As time allows I will look into having at the very least SSL for LDAP enabled and in use by default. I will also look into NFS4. NFS4 is something that I have no knowledge about (I simply installed the NFS package and let it do its thing!).  NFS4 support is already there in the default package, it's just that the "exports" file will default to NFS3-style syntax (e.g. what you showed in the guide). NFS4-style syntax is a slight bit different and NFS4 allows interesting things: On NFS3 and before user ID's between client system and server always had to be the same, e.g. there was no further authentication. NFS4 allows for user ID mapping, e.g. you can map specific user ID's on a client system to specific user ID's on your LDAP + NFS server. And it plays nicely with Kerberos I heard ... But to properly setup Kerberos is a royal PITA.

It would really be nice to have a free variant of a Linux distro that did all this ... Commercial distros such as e.g. Novell OpenEnterprise Server ("OES") will do all this (LDAP, Samba, NFS, NIS, etc.) from start and out of the box ... but it costs lots of money.

I can see the background but the top and bottom panel are just gray.  After a little bit it will come up with a message saying "Failed to initialize HAL!"

### Post by rocsak on 2008-02-14
> **spliner said:**
> The final problem I am having.. is DNS resolution on the workstation.  Since 192.168.0.125 is their only DNS server, they cannot resolve Internet addresses.  I am sure I can fix it by using a secondary from my ISP on the workstations, but is there any way to make bind do that for me.. in other words act as some sort of DNS proxy for my network?
Yes, there are two wayw to do that. If you have webmin installed go to Servers-> BIND DNS Server->Global Server Options-> Forwarding and Transfers and add the IP's of the Dns servers of your ISP. 
Or you can edit the /etc/bind/named.conf.options file and the following to the options section to enable forwarding: 

forwarders {
		xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx;
                xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx;
		};

Make sure you have port 53 open in your firewall (only outgoing, both tcp & udp) in order for internet resolution to work.
Hope this helps

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-14
> **kextyn said:**
> I'm having a little trouble with an Ubuntu client.  I followed your guide exactly except for hostnames and ips.  I have an Ubuntu client that was authenticating fine, but I want to be able to use it while the server is offline or unreachable.  So I shutdown the server and started the client.  It takes forever to get through some of the startup services and after I log in (with a local user because I don't have pamccreds for cached login) the desktop never comes up quite right.  I can see the background but the top and bottom panel are just gray.  After a little bit it will come up with a message saying "Failed to initialize HAL!"

What can I do to make the client boot quicker and properly?  If I was to install the necessary software for caching successful logins would that have any effect on this?

Edit:  After rebooting and trying again I finally got the normal desktop to show up after the HAL error message.  The first time I waited a while and never got the full desktop.

1) Even with the server offline you should always be able to log in as a local user and have the desktop (as the local user). 

2) Because my guide calls for the mounting of an NFS share at bootup you will have a slow boot, as this process needs to time out.

3) At this point I do not know if there is a way to cache login credentials like there is in Windows.

4) Since the LDAP client has all the home folder information mounted via NFS even if a cached login could work they still would not be able to save their files because their home folder will not exist. I do not know of a resolution for this.

I hope this helps!

-Richard

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-14
> **rocsak said:**
> Yes, there are two wayw to do that. If you have webmin installed go to Servers-> BIND DNS Server->Global Server Options-> Forwarding and Transfers and add the IP's of the Dns servers of your ISP. 


Thanks Rocsak!  That solved all of my issues with DNS, even the nasty 2 second pause is gone when resolving names other than the server.  This should be added to the guide in the future.

Note:  I added the two ISP DNS numbers in the forwarding and Transfers section, then edited my /etc/resolv.conf and removed the ISP DNS numbers from there.  All is now fine.

-Spliner

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-02-14
> **spliner said:**
>  4)  Backup - Sigh.  I managed to use tar to back up the server, and at least I can use that backup to fix my own mistakes.  How well it works from scratch as a restore I havn't tested.   I'd recommend to tryout this guide on VMware Workstation first. The advantage being that you can do multiple snapshots anytime you need to (e.g. before messing with a config file + afterwards) and if something goes wrong or doesn't work right you can always go back to a known-to-work snapshot and have your virtual system back as if nothing had happened.

If you are trying this stuff on real hardware: Apply some "best practices" here and always make a backup of something before you change it! e.g. let's suppose you have working "ldap.conf" so you'd make a backup right there:
```
sudo cp ldap.conf ldap.conf_WORKING-OK_14-Feb-2008_2028_yourname.backup

```

And now let's suppose you are experimenting around and not sure if your changes work, you'd again make a copy:
```
sudo cp ldap.conf ldap.conf_EXPERIMENTAL_14-Feb-2008_2130_yourname.backup

```

Now if something goes wrong, you'd still have copies of both versions and could return to the version that did work OK by simply copying the original working file over the non-working one.

Another advantage here is you could do a **"diff"** (shows differences between two files) on those two files and then e.g. return here and discuss why this and that change didn't do what you expected. For ease-of-use I'd recommend the package **vim-full** which includes all the nifty extras, bells and whistles for the **"vim"** editor. The default version that comes with Ubuntu totally sucks and is a reduced version: **"vim-tiny"** :mad: . Biiiig difference! So to get rid of that spartanic "vim" variant I'd recommend to install this: ```
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install vim-full 
```

This will also give you vi's GUI variant:  **gvim**

The nice thing about **gvim** is that you don't need to know any of the cryptic vi-commands ... you can do all you want with your mouse.

So to have **gvim** show you the differences e.g. between two config files you'd type this into a terminal: ```
gvimdiff file1 file2
```

Trust me, this is extremely useful, especially if you're experimenting with such things and want to see what has changed between two files and why one config file is working and why the other isn't.

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-14
FYI:  the "net time \\dc01-ubuntu /set /y" command in the logon.bat script does not work.  Just noticed it.  It gives an error because by default XP Pro does not allow non-administrators to set the time using the net time command.  At least that's what I'm finding.  It gives an error like:

Current time at \\dc01-ubuntu.example.local is 2/14/2008 at 3:11 PM

System error 1314 has occurred.

A required priviledge is not held by the client.

I'd assume that may be fixed by default in a later version of the guide since the control of user accounts might be added.  Current users that are authenticated by the domain are limited.  It's not a fault of the ntpd service though, nor the PDC we have created in this guide from what I can tell.

-Spliner

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-14
> **spliner said:**
> 
2)  *SNIP* Also, how would we make different logon.bat files for different users?  


Undo the login script setting that we added to smb.conf. Then, use PHPLDAPAdmin to do the rest... Log in as "cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local" (customize if need be). In the tree on the left you will look for the users container and the user account. Click the uid=USER item (USER = the username). At the top of the RIGHT pane there is a link - "Add new attribute" - click it. In the new right pane window click the first drop down and select "sambaLogonScript". In the box to the right you will type in the name of your script as it appears in the NETLOGON folder. Example: If the script exists in /home/samba/netlogon/user.script.1.bat then enter "user.script.1.bat" in the field. Then click the "Add" button. Save the changes.

I just tested this and I know it works. You should have the same results.

Thanks,

-Richard

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-15
SSL Issues solved!  (Partially) (But Easily)

I didn't quite understand the webmin package.  Either it is its own web server, or it uses apache.  Webmin was what Nessus was picking up on a scan.  It, by default, uses SSL V2.  An easy fix is to go into Webmin > Webmin Configuration > SSL Encryption.  Change "SSL Protocol Version" at the bottom to a custom value of "3".  Save and restart Webmin.  Rich, you might want to add that to the guide.

Now, there's another problem.  By default Webmin supports using a 56bit key (from what I can tell), and should be configured to use no less than a 128bit key.. i think.  More on this later as I tinker and break things lol.  I believe it may be in the settings somewhere but I havn't found it yet.  I also am not sure at this point the problem isn't related to OpenSSL default conifig.

I won't quit til it's solved, whether I break it a hundred times or not, so expect an answer sometime soon lol.

---

I didn't quite understand the webmin package.  Either it is its own web server, or it uses apache.  Webmin was what Nessus was picking up on a scan.  It, by default, uses SSL V2.  An easy fix is to go into Webmin > Webmin Configuration > SSL Encryption.  Change "SSL Protocol Version" at the bottom to a custom value of "3".  Save and restart Webmin.  Rich, you might want to add that to the guide.

Now, there's another problem.  By default Webmin supports using a 56bit key (from what I can tell), and should be configured to use no less than a 128bit key.. i think.  More on this later as I tinker and break things lol.  I believe it may be in the settings somewhere but I havn't found it yet.  I also am not sure at this point the problem isn't related to OpenSSL default conifig.

I won't quit til it's solved, whether I break it a hundred times or not, so expect an answer sometime soon lol.

Thank you for working on this.

Securing Webmin anymore than it is by default really is not what this guide is about so I will not be spending much time on this regard. Others may think differently and I urge and welcome others to push the security limit and post the results so that the knowledge can be shared and transferred.

I am planning on focusing on two major items in the next iteration of this guide:

2) Securing LDAP - unfortunately I have not been able to find a lot of information on this... as with most opensource documentation that I encounter it is lacking in explanations.

Thank you to everyone for the help in this endeavor!

-Richard

Fortunately I don't have to talk to IT guys.. the IT guy is me, and I'm it.  I might have very little experience with Linux but I'm learning fast thanks to you guys!  I've been tempted for months to switch my entire network at home to Linux, and that day is getting closer by the minute.  I looked the other day and could only find one app that I use at home that wasn't supported by Wine.  And it could always be run in a VM (Office 2k7) of XP Pro.  Anyway, back on topic...

With the combination of VMWare server and tar, I have been doing ok fixing my mistakes (and using best practices).  I'm very happy with the server as-is, however I must fix a few more holes/issues before putting it into production.  SSL is one thing (both for LDAP and Apache2 (BTW:  It can be enabled in WebMin also.. havn't tried this yet)), the open share is another (havn't even looked at it yet), and disabling debugging functions on Apache is another.  I will be starting with Webmin, because I'm finding most of this is available for config in there.  Upgrade to 1.4x was easy as well right in the secure page.  I believe it now has some LDAP function as well.

-Spliner

---

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-15
Maybe we can put together an addendum or secondary guide for securing the server.  Just a thought.  You're right, it's not necessarily part of the guide, but maybe just a note would be warranted that it needs to be secured before being put into production use.  I'll help in any way I can, this guide has been the greatest help to me.  I've learned more about Linux using it than I have in the last few months.

I'll be giving the login scripts for users a try soon, and using a few of Scorp's suggestions as well.

BTW:  It's likely that after using your default guide SSL for Apache can be enabled by a couple of clicks in Webmin.  It won't for me because I changed the path on the default key in a conf file a while back (before I started listening to Scorp123 and backing them up), but should be an easy fix.  It should however work for anyone else following the guide closely.  Even if you don't include this in the guide, if I find out how to fix it, I'll post it in the thread anyway for future reference.

### Post by cpthk on 2008-02-15
> **scorp123 said:**
> Reverse lookup has to be correct. So having a DNS server under your own control is an advantage, especially if you are experimenting around with SSO, Kerberos and other goodies (e.g. on top of this tutorial).

 I did exactly that (see my earlier postings), but then again I already have a DNS server somewhere in my network and configured that one (e.g. I entered my LDAP server into the DNS tables, to make sure my clients can find it).

My if saying pure samba, it doesn't need a DNS for windows clients to lookup and log in. If my LDAP is just for samba to connect for now, and they are at the same server. Is it possible to skip DNS part that I just do connect localhost at samba?

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-15
> **cpthk said:**
> My if saying pure samba, it doesn't need a DNS for windows clients to lookup and log in. If my LDAP is just for samba to connect for now, and they are at the same server. Is it possible to skip DNS part that I just do connect localhost at samba?

Your Windows clients will need working DNS to function on the domain. This is the way it is. Your Linux clients will also need functioning DNS to work.

For the single server, without any clients, technically you can skip DNS because we use 127.0.0.1 for everything. But I do not recommend this!

---

### Post by cpthk on 2008-02-15
> **rickyjones said:**
> Your Windows clients will need working DNS to function on the domain. This is the way it is. Your Linux clients will also need functioning DNS to work.

For the single server, without any clients, technically you can skip DNS because we use 127.0.0.1 for everything. But I do not recommend this!

Sorry if I misunderstand you. Isn't windows client able to communicate through netbios name? My company doesn't have any DNS server thing. If I set a pure samba server with its account database(smbpasswd) without using LDAP. The client could log in to server with no problem. My server netbios name is simply SERVER.
I was wonder if I add LDAP service into the server. Can I just set samba configuration file to connect LDAP as localhost. The client can still log in samba using netbios name. In this way, can I have openLDAP with samba without DNS?

[IMG]http://img2.freeimagehosting.net/uploads/991e7e5f52.jpg[/IMG]

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-15
That might work but I am telling you that it will be unreliable. Netbios only resolution will cause delays on login. DNS will speed it up and make things a lot more reliable.

---

### Post by Lhach on 2008-02-15
First off, thank you for this guide, it has taught me much about Ubuntu and sped along my LDAP server setup.

I have LDAP and Samba working enough for Linux clients to authenticate successfully. However, I am having trouble getting the Windows clients to join the domain.

The error I receive is:
---
The following error occurred attempting to join the domain "wiselab.lan";
The user name could not be found.
---

I have deviated a little from the guide. The minor deviations are changes to the domain/workgroup used, the test user, IP/hostname, enabled user Home shares, and I'm binding to lo and eth0 only. The major deviation was to fix a boot problem after setting the server to look up LDAP users for logins. The issue was that klogd attempted to look up a username before slapd started. This prevented subsequent processes to start, though I was presented with a login prompt. I was unable to log in using either system or LDAP users. The kludge was to adjust the boot order so that slapd would start directly before klogd. I doubt this devation has any affect on my Windows issue, but is here for completeness.

I tried a couple different tests to get the Windows client to join the domain.
Initially the Windows client's name was testbox1. I added this name to the domain using smbldap-useradd -w testbox1. I was able to see the entry in Webmin's LDAP browser as uid=testbox1$,ou=Computers,dc=wiselab,dc=lan so I know at least that part of the Samba-LDAP connection works. I tried using my test user, testguy instead of ricky, and root to join the domain, however I still received the "user name" error above. I then changed the client's name to testbox2 and tried to join the domain without manually adding the machine to the LDAP. When prompted for a username/password I gave it the root username and no joy. The odd thing is that testbox2 was successfully added to the LDAP: uid=testbox2$,ou=Computers,dc=wiselab,dc=lan. So now I'm at a loss. Nothing is in the logs except for the following...

This gets printed out intermittantly after many attempt to join the domain:
[2008/02/15 14:38:47, 0] lib/util_sock.c:get_peer_addr(1232) getpeername failed. Error was Transport endpoint is not connected

This is printed whenever I try to make the client join the domain after the client's name is already in the LDAP (which seems to be normal operation...)
[2008/02/15 14:41:48, 0] passdb/pdb_interface.c:pdb_default_create_user(329)
  _samr_create_user: Running the command `/usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -w "testbox2$"' gave 9

I am also able to browse the shares using both root and the test user.

I got a little hopeful when I read spliner's EDIT2, but that didn't fix my issue.

DNS seems to be working... I can resolve both server and client forward and reverse.
I also have DHCP loaded on the server, but that's working fine.
IPTables is configured as a masquerading NAT. eth1 is the public side and eth0 is for the client computers only. There aren't any restrictions on eth0.

One thing I am curious about is the idmap setting in both smb.conf and smbldap.conf.
smbldap.conf has: idmapdn="ou=Idmap,${suffix}"
and smb.conf has: ldap idmap suffix = ou=Users

I haven't investigated as to what this setting actually does, but I have tried making them match both ways, still with no joy.

[http://anicon.sytes.net/ubundap/smb.conf]("http://anicon.sytes.net/ubundap/smb.conf")
EDIT: [concise version from testparm]("http://anicon.sytes.net/ubundap/concise.conf")

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-16
> **cpthk said:**
> when I do:
```
net getlocalsid
```

It will give me an error saying:
[2008/02/15 16:51:14, 0] lib/smbldap.c:smbldap_connect_system(982)
  failed to bind to server ldap://localhost/ with dn="cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local" Error: Invalid credentials
  
SID for domain UBUNTU is: S-1-5-21-151222819-1430390412-3110260789

It will still show the SID, but is that error okey?

Either you didn't tell SAMBA the correct credentials, or you have the wrong credentials in /etc/smbldap-tools/smbldap_bind.conf. I would check those two items. If it errors with credentials then you will NOT be OK.

-Richard

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-17

-Richard

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-17
> **cpthk said:**
> Is step 13 skip-able? I realized some other tutorials by different people does not have this step.

If you don't want LDAP logins on the server then yes, I guess step 13 can be skipped. But the whole point of this guide is to have a server that authenticates via LDAP. If you skip step 13 then the rest of the guide is quite meaningless as your LDAP users will not be able to login to access their shares.

-Richard

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-17
> **maxxownes said:**
> Is there a guide for accomplishing this? Not sure I feel so hot about manually editing the service boot seq with out any guidance.

**Update: **Ok changing the service boot order is not that hard.... Ive tried moving slapd before klogd and moving slapd to the first and last boot item... still no go.

I'm not sure if I am having the same problem as everyone else as I boot fine up until I enter my password. then the system hangs.
[B]
Update 2[/B] Ya... I think I finally got it... I went over moving slapd around and doing a hard boot after each change... S19slapd works for me after hard boot, this puts slapd at the start of the services chain.

I tested this earlier today as well. Moving the start script to a # before klogd worked for me (ten reboots in a row worked without issue). I'll do more testing as well.

-Richard

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-17
> **cpthk said:**
> So do you mean windows clients will be logging in openLDAP instead of samba? I thought openLDAP is just a database of user accounts and passwords and samba is taking care windows clients log in and all the shares things. And samba will check usernames and passwords throguh openLDAP. Isn't it like this? Or do you mean it's actually openLDAP doing all of it?

I am trying to clarify. Is this tutorial teaching you how to set up a PDC by using samba+openLDAP that letting windows users to ctrl+alt+del then log in using username and password to the server? Or is it just a password protection of shares in My Netowork Place?

When you login to the SAMBA domain then SAMBA is performing lookups via OpenLDAP. If you skip step 13 then, for example, you will not be able to SSH into the server using your LDAP account. If you don't care about this behavior then I guess it can be skipped.

### Post by giggsey on 2008-02-17
> **cpthk said:**
> when I do:
```
net getlocalsid
```

It will give me an error saying:
[2008/02/15 16:51:14, 0] lib/smbldap.c:smbldap_connect_system(982)
  failed to bind to server ldap://localhost/ with dn="cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local" Error: Invalid credentials
  
SID for domain UBUNTU is: S-1-5-21-151222819-1430390412-3110260789

It will still show the SID, but is that error okey?

Looking at the actual error, you can see that you haven't changed that line when doing the conf.

It's trying to setup it up for the domain EXAMPLE, yet, you've run it for domain UBUNTU. So somewhere in your edits, you've left an example as example instead of changing it to ubuntu.

What I did when doing it was copy and paste the guide into Word/whatever, and then find & replaced example with xteam, etc.

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-17
> **Arbiter said:**
> I've got this setup running and it seems to be working well, but I've got one thing I want to do and I'm not sure how to go about it...

I've got a few windows machines all running XP Pro, so NFS filesharing is unnecessary (but available should I want it).  I want to create samba shares for several users on the server so they can copy files to the server for backup purposes.  I tried creating a share on the server side, but when I do I can't see shares by any other computers on the network.  When I shut the server down, I can see the network shares again on the client machines.

Is there a port conflict here or something?

Let me try to understand this a little better.

When you add more shares to the SAMBA server, you can no longer see the shares on your other computers. Correct? 

If so, how do you "view the shares" on the other computers? Do you look through Network Neighborhood? Are the shares visible if you connect directly to a computer (Start - Run - "\\computer" - OK)?

It is possible that the SAMBA server is becoming the master browser and is thus not showing all the shares. But this does not mean the other computers are no longer accessible, it just means you have to directly connect to them via a UNC path.

I hope this helps!

-Richard

---

### Post by thetell on 2008-02-18
Hi all,

I have a very big problem. I installed a LDAP/SAMBA Environment on Ubuntu 7.10. My Problem is, that everything seems to work until I reboot the server. That means:
I configured the Samab and the LDAP Server 
I configured the smbldaptools

I configured the nsswitch.conf file with the auth-client-config and everything looks good. With my LDAP Frontend I can add use, then I can connect to the shares. If I delete a user the local directory is gone as I want. Means everything seems to work......

No I reboot the server. The server is coming up. Now I try to login with SSH => Doesn't work. No connection available. So I try to login directly on the console. I type in root as user press enter and the password prompt is following. So I type in the password and now nothing happens anymore. With the rescue disk I can reconfigure nsswitch to not using LDAP. After reboot I can login.
Here is my configuration in pam:
-----------------------------------------
# cat common-auth
auth       required     pam_env.so
auth       sufficient   pam_unix.so likeauth nullok
auth       sufficient   pam_ldap.so use_first_pass
auth       required     pam_deny.so

# cat common-account
account    sufficient   pam_unix.so
account    sufficient   pam_ldap.so
account    required     pam_deny.so

# cat common-password
password   sufficient   pam_unix.so nullok md5 shadow use_authtok
password   sufficient   pam_ldap.so use_first_pass
password   required     pam_deny.so

# cat common-session
session    required     pam_limits.so
session    required     pam_mkhomedir.so skel=/etc/skel/ umask=0077
session    required     pam_unix.so
session    optional     pam_ldap.so

When I check /var/log/auth.log i get this error:
Feb 18 14:44:50 srvdclux sshd[4531]: Server listening on :: port 22.

### Post by giggsey on 2008-02-19
> **rickyjones said:**
> 1) You have a DNS issue. Make sure BIND is working fine, is configured, and that all clients are using that server for DNS resolution. The reason for not being able to ping the server is most likely due to the name resolution.

2) For roaming profiles: In the /etc/samba/smb.conf file find the line called "logon path =" - it should be BLANK after the "=" sign. Add your UNC path there. E.g.: \\server\share\%username%. Please note that you need a directory that is shared for this to happen, and the permissions must be correct. You can share a folder the same way we shared the ldaphome directory.

3) A shared drive for all users? Create a new folder on the server. Share it using SAMBA just like LDAPHOME, but change the permissions so that everyone can read/write to it. I'm not positive on the absolute permissions necessary here, however.

I hope this helps!

-Richard


Right, I now have it in the working environment, with all the clients on the same subnet etc, and I have the same issue

I can't map any network shares :(

I've got a copy of the file from the network, and try running it locally. It prompts me for a username and password, which I've entered as XTEAM\user and user.

It gives me an error "System Error 53". 

Any ideas?

I'd love to get this working, cheers :)


Also, do you have a step by step guide for creating a new share for the roaming profiles?

Thanks :D

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-19

Also, do you have a step by step guide for creating a new share for the roaming profiles?

Thanks :D

System error 53 means that the network path was not found. Which means either 1) DNS is not properly configured or 2) The login script is trying to map the drive to a non-existent server. 

Did you change your server name and forget to update the script file to point to the correct server?

Hope this helps!

-Richard

### Post by Sternfan on 2008-02-19
Hi all,

In my opinion, this is one of the most important threads on using Ubuntu - the idea of setting up a Small Business Server.  This is something I have been thinking about for the last few years, and am hoping that someone at Ubuntu grabs onto this whole concept.

- Ubuntu needs a "Business" or "Enterprise" - (whatever you want to call it) - Forum unto itself.  Right now all of these types of posts get lumped into other parts of the forums and loses focus.

- **We need a "small business" (whatever you want to call it) edition of Ubuntu.**  Much in the way Edubuntu is for thin-clients in schools, there needs to be a "Small Business" Ubuntu for people wanting to setup a SmallOffice/HomeOffice.  An edition where the client/server relationship is designed & implemented from the outset.  Set up a Domain, create Users, setup clients for this Domain etc.  

- Speed - I agree with the author on this:

 *"this process in general, using open source software, is too time consuming. It took me approximately two weeks to accomplish something that can be done in less than an hour on all version of Windows server"*

I tried this years ago with Fedora.  To setup a domain client took something close to two hours (and more if you screwed something up).  I think this results from all the settings being in a hodge-podge of locations...  Make a single "domain control panel" (or whatever you want to call it) and my guess is that the time spent setting this up will be significantly shortened.

I think there is a huge, untapped market out there for Small Businesses that can be broken into two groups - 1. Ubuntu DC with Ubuntu clients and 2. Ubuntu DC with Windows clients.

Main issues:
- Ease of setup for the DC
- Centralized logon & account maintenance
- Centralized storage (for backups - mapped folders)
- Roaming Profiles - Users can go from PC to PC with all of their settings not having to be reset.

Anyways - I am praying that these ideas make their way up to the Ubuntu people and we see a "Ubuntu Small Business" edition...

Thanks,
Rob

---

### Post by Sternfan on 2008-02-19
Hi thetell,

I can't stop thinking about this thread this morning, so I thought I would post a scenario I just ran into where something like this could work.

I'm a network admin and a friend of mine recommended me to look over his Dentist's office.  So I set up a meeting to do a site survey.

Classic small office - 6 PCs running either XP or 2000 pro.  No real password security - same basic user accounts on all the PCs - (logons have to match so that network drives continue to work in a workgroup).  No meaningful backups- except to thumb drives.  The Dentist isn't sure what he wants - but sums up the following:

- Backups concern him - each PC has files that need backed up.
- Users all share the same logon/pwd - this has to change.
- Wants disaster recovery "if the receptionist PC blue screens, I want her to be able to work on the PC next to her."  Think Roaming Profiles.
- APPS - not much more than Office.  He has a Dentist dabase that is hosted on the net and run thru a browser, and the same thing with a billing program.

I haven't returned with recommendations yet - but if there was a way to manage this with Ubuntu - it's a no-brainer.  I just can't spend all of the time necessary to make this work - and it needs to work from day one.  I don't want to have the support headaches with this - and if he needs to create an account - change passord etc - it has to be easy for him to figure out.

Setting up a Windows domain would be roughly $2000 for server hardware, $1,000 for 2003 Server with 10 CALS.  And he is talking of eventually setting up his own email....

Thanks,
Rob

You're welcome!

Now, on to your issues.

Can you try to explain the overall goal a little more clearly? From my understanding you have a Windows client connecting to the server. The client used to have local accounts, one being for your wife. You now have an LDAP account for your wife but all of her Documents were in her old "My Documents" folder, correct? If so then you should be able to log into the workstation as the local Administrator and copy the old files to the new My Documents folder in C:\Documents and Settings\%username% where %username% is the LDAP username for your wife.

The permissions should default to the new LDAP account. 

I hope this helps!

-Richard

The Dentist isn't sure what he wants - but sums up the following:

- Backups concern him - each PC has files that need backed up.

- Wants disaster recovery "if the receptionist PC blue screens, I want her to be able to work on the PC next to her."  Think Roaming Profiles.

Rob,

Here is what I would do if I were you. First and foremost, if you were to recommend this domain controller as a solution then you will want to ensure that everything is working before you deploy it. Make sure that if it dies that users can still log in locally to complete basic work.

Now, onto the actual solution. You can definitely complete this project using this domain controller as is.

Do they use Microsoft Office on the client computers? Where are all the documents currently stored?

Assuming that they do use Microsoft Office and that documents are stored wherever, and that all the important mission critical applications are web based and hosted online, then here is what I would do.

1) Deploy the Ubuntu Domain Controller.
2) Statically assign IP addresses to the clients to ensure that DNS is pointing to the server.
3) Create user accounts on the server.
4) Join one of the computers to the domain - test functionality.
5) Configure the local group policy of this computer to redirect the My Documents folder to the \\server\ldaphome\%username% network location. This will put the My Documents on the server without any additional configuration (for all users).
6) Configure a "public" share on the server where all shared documents will be stored. 
7) Map a network drive to this location on the client. 
8) Test all users on this PC and make sure that all shares work and that documents can be opened and saved to the network.
9) Replicate the steps on other client PCs.

The only issue here is that if the server dies then you no longer have access to the shared documents. But, for a small office that is usually how it works.

Having the critical apps being web-based and offsite will allow us to use restricted user accounts in the Windows environment. Web browsing will work and Office will work without issue, but users will not be able to install software. This will prevent malware/viruses from infecting the systems saving IT costs.

Each user will have their own user/password. They will be able to use each computer. If they move to a new PC they will have access to their My Documents and the shared drive. 

How is email handled? If each user has an Outlook profile then you WILL need to enable roaming profiles... this is pretty easy to do, just takes an extra step for configuring.

I hope this points you in the correct direction!

-Richard

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-19
> **Sternfan said:**
> Thanks for in-depth reply.

Right now I'm holding off on this issue.  I simply do not have the time for support...  Also the office is a considerable distance from me.  I just don't see me getting this up and running in a few hours one afternoon - after all, this thread is 30+ pages long full of issues and tweaks.   And I have two jobs so the idea of setting it up and coming back to it every once in a while for an issue or tweak is just not going to work.

My point is that Ubuntu as an organization needs to seriously consider this - it is a HUGE market.  

One last question...

When I enable roaming profiles on a AD DC, it is done thru AD users & computers - with a path to the profile location.  How is this done with an Ubuntu DC?  I would love to try this just to see it work...

Thanks,
Rob

Roaming profiles can be done globally. In the smb.conf file find the line "logon path =" and add the UNC path for the profiles after the = sign. For example: logon path = \\server\profiles\%username%

When users log in the profile will be created and copied over at log in.

-Richard

---

### Post by Sternfan on 2008-02-19
> **rickyjones said:**
> Roaming profiles can be done globally. In the smb.conf file find the line "logon path =" and add the UNC path for the profiles after the = sign. For example: logon path = \\server\profiles\%username%

When users log in the profile will be created and copied over at log in.

-Richard

Nice - did not know this...  Ok.  So when I create the profiles folder above - what are the permissions?

Rob

Very good question! I would recommend chmodding the parent folder as 777 just to test and make sure it works. Then keep restricting permissions until you get the right combination. I'm going to guess 755 would work, giving the user all rights and the group/others the ability to read. 

-Richard

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-19
Wow.. a lot of activity in this thread since last week!  Unfortunately I havn't had time to work on the server much this week, but will eventually.   I noticed in some of the more recent posts, people were having trouble with the guide.  The three main things I did wrong when first following the guide were:

1)  Not having root access while completing the steps.  Save yourself the trouble and use sudo bash before starting (and after each reboot) or "su" every command in Richard's tutorial.
2)  Don't forget to change the SID in step 10 to YOURS.  There is already one in the file, you need to replace it with the one you get.  I did that twice and wondered why nothing worked.
3)  DNS...  I had many issues with DNS because I was trying to enable Internet access while completing the guide.  I had originally put my ISP's DNS nubmers in /etc/resolv.conf.  It was causing things to resolve slow, and time out, which caused many issues that were intermittant.  Leave your DNS numbers out (IE:  Do exactly as the guide suggests and change /etc/resolv.conf to the IP of your server only), and configure them in forwarding after you are configuring BIND iteslf.  Things work much smoother for me now.
4)  I tried customizing the guide to suit my own needs and failed twice.  I was always missing something.  Finally I loaded the whole thing up in a word processor and used find/replace to look for things I needed to change.  It worked great to make my own versions of the .conf files to use on my server.  Of course now that I've set this thing up successfully three times, and managed to break it twice, it's getting easier.

Thanks again Richard!

-Spliner

---

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-19
> **rickyjones said:**
> 127.0.1.1 is just another localhost IP entry. It is there by default, so I just end up using that line.


I would change it to something like:

127.0.0.1 localhost
127.0.1.1 pdc pdc.herguan.local

-Spliner

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-19
> **spliner said:**
> I would change it to something like:

127.0.0.1 localhost
127.0.1.1 pdc pdc.herguan.local

-Spliner

Exactly - which is how I used it in the guide :)

  Can't fetch domain SID for name: PDC
root@pdc:/etc/smbldap-tools#
