Here is my smb.conf:
```
#
# Sample configuration file for the Samba suite for Debian GNU/Linux.
#
#
# This is the main Samba configuration file. You should read the
# smb.conf(5) manual page in order to understand the options listed
# here. Samba has a huge number of configurable options most of which 
# are not shown in this example
#
# Any line which starts with a ; (semi-colon) or a # (hash) 
# is a comment and is ignored. In this example we will use a #
# for commentary and a ; for parts of the config file that you
# may wish to enable
#
# NOTE: Whenever you modify this file you should run the command
# "testparm" to check that you have not made any basic syntactic 
# errors. 
#

#======================= Global Settings =======================

[global]

## Browsing/Identification ###

# Change this to the workgroup/NT-domain name your Samba server will part of
   workgroup = herguan

# server string is the equivalent of the NT Description field
   server string = %h server (Samba, Ubuntu)

# Windows Internet Name Serving Support Section:
# WINS Support - Tells the NMBD component of Samba to enable its WINS Server
;   wins support = no

# WINS Server - Tells the NMBD components of Samba to be a WINS Client
# Note: Samba can be either a WINS Server, or a WINS Client, but NOT both
;   wins server = w.x.y.z

# This will prevent nmbd to search for NetBIOS names through DNS.
   dns proxy = no

# What naming service and in what order should we use to resolve host names
# to IP addresses
;   name resolve order = lmhosts host wins bcast

#### Networking ####

# The specific set of interfaces / networks to bind to
# This can be either the interface name or an IP address/netmask;
# interface names are normally preferred
;   interfaces = 127.0.0.0/8 eth0

# Only bind to the named interfaces and/or networks; you must use the
# 'interfaces' option above to use this.
# It is recommended that you enable this feature if your Samba machine is
# not protected by a firewall or is a firewall itself.  However, this
# option cannot handle dynamic or non-broadcast interfaces correctly.
;   bind interfaces only = true



#### Debugging/Accounting ####

# This tells Samba to use a separate log file for each machine
# that connects
   log file = /var/log/samba/log.%m

# Put a capping on the size of the log files (in Kb).
   max log size = 1000

# If you want Samba to only log through syslog then set the following
# parameter to 'yes'.
;   syslog only = no

# We want Samba to log a minimum amount of information to syslog. Everything
# should go to /var/log/samba/log.{smbd,nmbd} instead. If you want to log
# through syslog you should set the following parameter to something higher.
   syslog = 0

# Do something sensible when Samba crashes: mail the admin a backtrace
   panic action = /usr/share/samba/panic-action %d


####### Authentication #######

# "security = user" is always a good idea. This will require a Unix account
# in this server for every user accessing the server. See
# /usr/share/doc/samba-doc/htmldocs/Samba3-HOWTO/ServerType.html
# in the samba-doc package for details.
   security = user

# You may wish to use password encryption.  See the section on
# 'encrypt passwords' in the smb.conf(5) manpage before enabling.
   encrypt passwords = true

# If you are using encrypted passwords, Samba will need to know what
# password database type you are using.  
   passdb backend = ldapsam:ldap://localhost/

   obey pam restrictions = no
##########
ldap admin dn = cn=admin,dc=herguan,dc=local
ldap suffix = dc=herguan, dc=local
ldap group suffix = ou=Groups
ldap user suffix = ou=Users
ldap machine suffix = ou=Computers
ldap idmap suffix = ou=Users
; Do ldap passwd sync
ldap passwd sync = Yes
passwd program = /usr/sbin/smbldap-passwd %u
passwd chat = *New*password* %n\n *Retype*new*password* %n\n *all*authentication*tokens*updated*
add user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -m "%u"
ldap delete dn = Yes
delete user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-userdel "%u"
add machine script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -w "%u"
add group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupadd -p "%g"
delete group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupdel "%g"
add user to group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -m "%u" "%g"
delete user from group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -x "%u" "%g"
set primary group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-usermod -g "%g" "%u"
domain logons = yes
##########

;   guest account = nobody
;   invalid users = root

# This boolean parameter controls whether Samba attempts to sync the Unix
# password with the SMB password when the encrypted SMB password in the
# passdb is changed.
;   unix password sync = no

# For Unix password sync to work on a Debian GNU/Linux system, the following
# parameters must be set (thanks to Ian Kahan <<kahan@informatik.tu-muenchen.de> for
# sending the correct chat script for the passwd program in Debian Sarge).
   passwd program = /usr/bin/passwd %u
   passwd chat = *Enter\snew\sUNIX\spassword:* %n\n *Retype\snew\sUNIX\spassword:* %n\n *passwd:*password\supdated\ssuccessfully* .

# This boolean controls whether PAM will be used for password changes
# when requested by an SMB client instead of the program listed in
# 'passwd program'. The default is 'no'.
;   pam password change = no

########## Domains ###########

# Is this machine able to authenticate users. Both PDC and BDC
# must have this setting enabled. If you are the BDC you must
# change the 'domain master' setting to no
#
;   domain logons = yes
#
# The following setting only takes effect if 'domain logons' is set
# It specifies the location of the user's profile directory
# from the client point of view)
# The following required a [profiles] share to be setup on the
# samba server (see below)
;   logon path = \\%N\profiles\%U
# Another common choice is storing the profile in the user's home directory
;   logon path = \\%N\%U\profile

# The following setting only takes effect if 'domain logons' is set
# It specifies the location of a user's home directory (from the client
# point of view)
;   logon drive = H:
;   logon home = \\%N\%U

# The following setting only takes effect if 'domain logons' is set
# It specifies the script to run during logon. The script must be stored
# in the [netlogon] share
# NOTE: Must be store in 'DOS' file format convention
;   logon script = logon.cmd

# This allows Unix users to be created on the domain controller via the SAMR
# RPC pipe.  The example command creates a user account with a disabled Unix
# password; please adapt to your needs
; add user script = /usr/sbin/adduser --quiet --disabled-password --gecos "" %u

########## Printing ##########

# If you want to automatically load your printer list rather
# than setting them up individually then you'll need this
;   load printers = yes

# lpr(ng) printing. You may wish to override the location of the
# printcap file
;   printing = bsd
;   printcap name = /etc/printcap

# CUPS printing.  See also the cupsaddsmb(8) manpage in the
# cupsys-client package.
;   printing = cups
;   printcap name = cups

# When using [print$], root is implicitly a 'printer admin', but you can
# also give this right to other users to add drivers and set printer
# properties
;   printer admin = @lpadmin


############ Misc ############

# Using the following line enables you to customise your configuration
# on a per machine basis. The %m gets replaced with the netbios name
# of the machine that is connecting
;   include = /home/samba/etc/smb.conf.%m

# Most people will find that this option gives better performance.
# See smb.conf(5) and /usr/share/doc/samba-doc/htmldocs/Samba3-HOWTO/speed.html
# for details
# You may want to add the following on a Linux system:
#         SO_RCVBUF=8192 SO_SNDBUF=8192
   socket options = TCP_NODELAY

# The following parameter is useful only if you have the linpopup package
# installed. The samba maintainer and the linpopup maintainer are
# working to ease installation and configuration of linpopup and samba.
;   message command = /bin/sh -c '/usr/bin/linpopup "%f" "%m" %s; rm %s' &

# Domain Master specifies Samba to be the Domain Master Browser. If this
# machine will be configured as a BDC (a secondary logon server), you
# must set this to 'no'; otherwise, the default behavior is recommended.
;   domain master = auto

# Some defaults for winbind (make sure you're not using the ranges
# for something else.)
;   idmap uid = 10000-20000
;   idmap gid = 10000-20000
;   template shell = /bin/bash
;
; The following was the default behaviour in sarge
; but samba upstream reverted the default because it might induce
; performance issues in large organizations
; See #368251 for some of the consequences of *not* having
; this setting and smb.conf(5) for all details
;
;   winbind enum groups = yes
;   winbind enum users = yes

#======================= Share Definitions =======================

# Un-comment the following (and tweak the other settings below to suit)
# to enable the default home directory shares.  This will share each
# user's home directory as \\server\username
;[homes]
;   comment = Home Directories
;   browseable = no

# By default, \\server\username shares can be connected to by anyone
# with access to the samba server.  Un-comment the following parameter
# to make sure that only "username" can connect to \\server\username
# This might need tweaking when using external authentication schemes
;   valid users = %S

# By default, the home directories are exported read-only. Change next
# parameter to 'yes' if you want to be able to write to them.
;   writable = no

# File creation mask is set to 0700 for security reasons. If you want to
# create files with group=rw permissions, set next parameter to 0775.
;   create mask = 0700

# Directory creation mask is set to 0700 for security reasons. If you want to
# create dirs. with group=rw permissions, set next parameter to 0775.
;   directory mask = 0700

# Un-comment the following and create the netlogon directory for Domain Logons
# (you need to configure Samba to act as a domain controller too.)
;[netlogon]
;   comment = Network Logon Service
;   path = /home/samba/netlogon
;   guest ok = yes
;   writable = no
;   share modes = no

# Un-comment the following and create the profiles directory to store
# users profiles (see the "logon path" option above)
# (you need to configure Samba to act as a domain controller too.)
# The path below should be writable by all users so that their
# profile directory may be created the first time they log on
;[profiles]
;   comment = Users profiles
;   path = /home/samba/profiles
;   guest ok = no
;   browseable = no
;   create mask = 0600
;   directory mask = 0700

[printers]
   comment = All Printers
   browseable = no
   path = /var/spool/samba
   printable = yes
   public = no
   writable = no
   create mode = 0700

# Windows clients look for this share name as a source of downloadable
# printer drivers
[print$]
   comment = Printer Drivers
   path = /var/lib/samba/printers
   browseable = yes
   read only = yes
   guest ok = no
# Uncomment to allow remote administration of Windows print drivers.
# Replace 'ntadmin' with the name of the group your admin users are
# members of.
;   write list = root, @ntadmin

# A sample share for sharing your CD-ROM with others.
;[cdrom]
;   comment = Samba server's CD-ROM
;   writable = no
;   locking = no
;   path = /cdrom
;   public = yes

# The next two parameters show how to auto-mount a CD-ROM when the
#	cdrom share is accesed. For this to work /etc/fstab must contain
#	an entry like this:
#
#       /dev/scd0   /cdrom  iso9660 defaults,noauto,ro,user   0 0
#
# The CD-ROM gets unmounted automatically after the connection to the
#
# If you don't want to use auto-mounting/unmounting make sure the CD
#	is mounted on /cdrom
#
;   preexec = /bin/mount /cdrom
;   postexec = /bin/umount /cdrom

```

Here is my slapd.conf:
```
# This is the main slapd configuration file. See slapd.conf(5) for more
# info on the configuration options.

#######################################################################
# Global Directives:

# Features to permit
#allow bind_v2

# Schema and objectClass definitions
include         /etc/ldap/schema/core.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/cosine.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/nis.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/inetorgperson.schema
include		/etc/ldap/schema/samba.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/misc.schema

# Where the pid file is put. The init.d script
# will not stop the server if you change this.
pidfile         /var/run/slapd/slapd.pid

# List of arguments that were passed to the server
argsfile        /var/run/slapd/slapd.args

# Read slapd.conf(5) for possible values
loglevel        0

# Where the dynamically loaded modules are stored
modulepath	/usr/lib/ldap
moduleload	back_bdb

# The maximum number of entries that is returned for a search operation
sizelimit 500

# The tool-threads parameter sets the actual amount of cpu's that is used
# for indexing.
tool-threads 1

#######################################################################
# Specific Backend Directives for bdb:
# Backend specific directives apply to this backend until another
# 'backend' directive occurs
backend		bdb
checkpoint 512 30

#######################################################################
# Specific Backend Directives for 'other':
# Backend specific directives apply to this backend until another
# 'backend' directive occurs
#backend		<other>

#######################################################################
# Specific Directives for database #1, of type bdb:
# Database specific directives apply to this databasse until another
# 'database' directive occurs
database        bdb

# The base of your directory in database #1
suffix          "dc=nodomain"

# rootdn directive for specifying a superuser on the database. This is needed
# for syncrepl.
# rootdn          "cn=admin,dc=nodomain"

# Where the database file are physically stored for database #1
directory       "/var/lib/ldap"

# For the Debian package we use 2MB as default but be sure to update this
# value if you have plenty of RAM
dbconfig set_cachesize 0 2097152 0

# Sven Hartge reported that he had to set this value incredibly high
# to get slapd running at all. See http://bugs.debian.org/303057
# for more information.

# Number of objects that can be locked at the same time.
dbconfig set_lk_max_objects 1500
# Number of locks (both requested and granted)
dbconfig set_lk_max_locks 1500
# Number of lockers
dbconfig set_lk_max_lockers 1500

# Indexing options for database #1
index           objectClass eq

# Save the time that the entry gets modified, for database #1
lastmod         on

# Where to store the replica logs for database #1
# replogfile	/var/lib/ldap/replog

# The userPassword by default can be changed
# by the entry owning it if they are authenticated.
# Others should not be able to see it, except the
# admin entry below
# These access lines apply to database #1 only
access to attrs=userPassword,shadowLastChange,sambaNTPassword,sambaLMPassword
        by dn="cn=admin,dc=nodomain" write
        by anonymous auth
        by self write
        by * none

# Ensure read access to the base for things like
# supportedSASLMechanisms.  Without this you may
# have problems with SASL not knowing what
# mechanisms are available and the like.
# Note that this is covered by the 'access to *'
# ACL below too but if you change that as people
# are wont to do you'll still need this if you
# want SASL (and possible other things) to work 
# happily.
access to dn.base="" by * read

# The admin dn has full write access, everyone else
# can read everything.
access to *
        by dn="cn=admin,dc=nodomain" write
        by * read

# For Netscape Roaming support, each user gets a roaming
# profile for which they have write access to
#access to dn=".*,ou=Roaming,o=morsnet"
#        by dn="cn=admin,dc=nodomain" write
#        by dnattr=owner write

#######################################################################
# Specific Directives for database #2, of type 'other' (can be bdb too):
# Database specific directives apply to this databasse until another
# 'database' directive occurs
#database        <other>

# The base of your directory for database #2
#suffix		"dc=debian,dc=org"
```

I also did the smbpasswd -w qwer, which qwer is my password.

# also give this right to other users to add drivers and set printer
# properties
;   printer admin = @lpadmin


############ Misc ############

# Using the following line enables you to customise your configuration
# on a per machine basis. The %m gets replaced with the netbios name
# of the machine that is connecting
;   include = /home/samba/etc/smb.conf.%m

# Most people will find that this option gives better performance.
# See smb.conf(5) and /usr/share/doc/samba-doc/htmldocs/Samba3-HOWTO/speed.html
# for details
# You may want to add the following on a Linux system:
#         SO_RCVBUF=8192 SO_SNDBUF=8192
   socket options = TCP_NODELAY

# The following parameter is useful only if you have the linpopup package
# installed. The samba maintainer and the linpopup maintainer are
# working to ease installation and configuration of linpopup and samba.
;   message command = /bin/sh -c '/usr/bin/linpopup "%f" "%m" %s; rm %s' &

# Domain Master specifies Samba to be the Domain Master Browser. If this
# machine will be configured as a BDC (a secondary logon server), you
# must set this to 'no'; otherwise, the default behavior is recommended.
;   domain master = auto

# Some defaults for winbind (make sure you're not using the ranges
# for something else.)
;   idmap uid = 10000-20000
;   idmap gid = 10000-20000
;   template shell = /bin/bash
;
; The following was the default behaviour in sarge
; but samba upstream reverted the default because it might induce
; performance issues in large organizations
; See #368251 for some of the consequences of *not* having
; this setting and smb.conf(5) for all details
;
;   winbind enum groups = yes
;   winbind enum users = yes

#======================= Share Definitions =======================

# Un-comment the following (and tweak the other settings below to suit)
# to enable the default home directory shares.  This will share each
# user's home directory as \\server\username
;[homes]
;   comment = Home Directories
;   browseable = no

# By default, \\server\username shares can be connected to by anyone
# with access to the samba server.  Un-comment the following parameter
# to make sure that only "username" can connect to \\server\username
# This might need tweaking when using external authentication schemes
;   valid users = %S

# By default, the home directories are exported read-only. Change next
# parameter to 'yes' if you want to be able to write to them.
;   writable = no

# File creation mask is set to 0700 for security reasons. If you want to
# create files with group=rw permissions, set next parameter to 0775.
;   create mask = 0700

# Directory creation mask is set to 0700 for security reasons. If you want to
# create dirs. with group=rw permissions, set next parameter to 0775.
;   directory mask = 0700

# Un-comment the following and create the netlogon directory for Domain Logons
# (you need to configure Samba to act as a domain controller too.)
;[netlogon]
;   comment = Network Logon Service
;   path = /home/samba/netlogon
;   guest ok = yes
;   writable = no
;   share modes = no

# Un-comment the following and create the profiles directory to store
# users profiles (see the "logon path" option above)
# (you need to configure Samba to act as a domain controller too.)
# The path below should be writable by all users so that their
# profile directory may be created the first time they log on
;[profiles]
;   comment = Users profiles
;   path = /home/samba/profiles
;   guest ok = no
;   browseable = no
;   create mask = 0600
;   directory mask = 0700

[printers]
   comment = All Printers
   browseable = no
   path = /var/spool/samba
   printable = yes
   public = no
   writable = no
   create mode = 0700

# Windows clients look for this share name as a source of downloadable
# printer drivers
[print$]
   comment = Printer Drivers
   path = /var/lib/samba/printers
   browseable = yes
   read only = yes
   guest ok = no
# Uncomment to allow remote administration of Windows print drivers.
# Replace 'ntadmin' with the name of the group your admin users are
# members of.
;   write list = root, @ntadmin

# A sample share for sharing your CD-ROM with others.
;[cdrom]
;   comment = Samba server's CD-ROM
;   writable = no
;   locking = no
;   path = /cdrom
;   public = yes

# The next two parameters show how to auto-mount a CD-ROM when the
#	cdrom share is accesed. For this to work /etc/fstab must contain
#	an entry like this:
#
#       /dev/scd0   /cdrom  iso9660 defaults,noauto,ro,user   0 0
#
# The CD-ROM gets unmounted automatically after the connection to the
#
# If you don't want to use auto-mounting/unmounting make sure the CD
#	is mounted on /cdrom
#
;   preexec = /bin/mount /cdrom
;   postexec = /bin/umount /cdrom

```

Here is my slapd.conf:
```
# This is the main slapd configuration file. See slapd.conf(5) for more
# info on the configuration options.

#######################################################################
# Global Directives:

# Features to permit
#allow bind_v2

# Schema and objectClass definitions
include         /etc/ldap/schema/core.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/cosine.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/nis.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/inetorgperson.schema
include		/etc/ldap/schema/samba.schema

# Where the pid file is put. The init.d script
# will not stop the server if you change this.
pidfile         /var/run/slapd/slapd.pid

# List of arguments that were passed to the server
argsfile        /var/run/slapd/slapd.args

# Read slapd.conf(5) for possible values
loglevel        0

# Where the dynamically loaded modules are stored
modulepath	/usr/lib/ldap
moduleload	back_bdb

# The maximum number of entries that is returned for a search operation
sizelimit 500

# The tool-threads parameter sets the actual amount of cpu's that is used
# for indexing.
tool-threads 1

#######################################################################
# Specific Backend Directives for bdb:
# Backend specific directives apply to this backend until another
# 'backend' directive occurs
backend		bdb
checkpoint 512 30

#######################################################################
# Specific Backend Directives for 'other':
# Backend specific directives apply to this backend until another
# 'backend' directive occurs
#backend		<other>

#######################################################################
# Specific Directives for database #1, of type bdb:
# Database specific directives apply to this databasse until another
# 'database' directive occurs
database        bdb

# The base of your directory in database #1
suffix          "dc=nodomain"

# rootdn directive for specifying a superuser on the database. This is needed
# for syncrepl.
# rootdn          "cn=admin,dc=nodomain"

# Where the database file are physically stored for database #1
directory       "/var/lib/ldap"

# For the Debian package we use 2MB as default but be sure to update this
# value if you have plenty of RAM
dbconfig set_cachesize 0 2097152 0

# Sven Hartge reported that he had to set this value incredibly high
# to get slapd running at all. See http://bugs.debian.org/303057
# for more information.

# Number of objects that can be locked at the same time.
dbconfig set_lk_max_objects 1500
# Number of locks (both requested and granted)
dbconfig set_lk_max_locks 1500
# Number of lockers
dbconfig set_lk_max_lockers 1500

# Indexing options for database #1
index           objectClass eq

# Save the time that the entry gets modified, for database #1
lastmod         on

# Where to store the replica logs for database #1
# replogfile	/var/lib/ldap/replog

# The userPassword by default can be changed
# by the entry owning it if they are authenticated.
# Others should not be able to see it, except the
# admin entry below
# These access lines apply to database #1 only
access to attrs=userPassword,shadowLastChange,sambaNTPassword,sambaLMPassword
        by dn="cn=admin,dc=nodomain" write
        by anonymous auth
        by self write
        by * none

# Ensure read access to the base for things like
# supportedSASLMechanisms.  Without this you may
# have problems with SASL not knowing what
# mechanisms are available and the like.
# Note that this is covered by the 'access to *'
# ACL below too but if you change that as people
# are wont to do you'll still need this if you
# want SASL (and possible other things) to work 
# happily.
access to dn.base="" by * read

# The admin dn has full write access, everyone else
# can read everything.
access to *
        by dn="cn=admin,dc=nodomain" write
        by * read

# For Netscape Roaming support, each user gets a roaming
# profile for which they have write access to
#access to dn=".*,ou=Roaming,o=morsnet"
#        by dn="cn=admin,dc=nodomain" write
#        by dnattr=owner write

#######################################################################
# Specific Directives for database #2, of type 'other' (can be bdb too):
# Database specific directives apply to this databasse until another
# 'database' directive occurs
#database        <other>

# The base of your directory for database #2
#suffix		"dc=debian,dc=org"
```

I also did the smbpasswd -w qwer, which qwer is my password.

I do not believe that you have OpenLDAP configured properly.
```

# The base of your directory in database #1
suffix          "dc=nodomain"

```

That part, specifically, tells me that you did not enter in your domain name correctly for LDAP when you did a **dpkg-reconfigure slapd** command.

Try reconfiguring OpenLDAP and see if that helps.

-Richard

### Post by cpthk on 2008-02-19
> **rickyjones said:**
> I do not believe that you have OpenLDAP configured properly.
```

# The base of your directory in database #1
suffix          "dc=nodomain"

```

That part, specifically, tells me that you did not enter in your domain name correctly for LDAP when you did a **dpkg-reconfigure slapd** command.

Try reconfiguring OpenLDAP and see if that helps.

-Richard

```
#
# Sample configuration file for the Samba suite for Debian GNU/Linux.
#
#
# This is the main Samba configuration file. You should read the
# smb.conf(5) manual page in order to understand the options listed
# here. Samba has a huge number of configurable options most of which 
# are not shown in this example
#
# Any line which starts with a ; (semi-colon) or a # (hash) 
# is a comment and is ignored. In this example we will use a #
# for commentary and a ; for parts of the config file that you
# may wish to enable
#
# NOTE: Whenever you modify this file you should run the command

# errors. 
#

#======================= Global Settings =======================

[global]

## Browsing/Identification ###

# Change this to the workgroup/NT-domain name your Samba server will part of
   workgroup = herguan

# server string is the equivalent of the NT Description field
   server string = %h server (Samba, Ubuntu)

# Windows Internet Name Serving Support Section:

# WINS Server - Tells the NMBD components of Samba to be a WINS Client

# This will prevent nmbd to search for NetBIOS names through DNS.

# Only bind to the named interfaces and/or networks; you must use the

# not protected by a firewall or is a firewall itself.  However, this
# option cannot handle dynamic or non-broadcast interfaces correctly.

#### Debugging/Accounting ####

# This tells Samba to use a separate log file for each machine
# that connects
   log file = /var/log/samba/log.%m

# Put a capping on the size of the log files (in Kb).
   max log size = 1000

# If you want Samba to only log through syslog then set the following
# parameter to 'yes'.

# We want Samba to log a minimum amount of information to syslog. Everything
# should go to /var/log/samba/log.{smbd,nmbd} instead. If you want to log
# through syslog you should set the following parameter to something higher.
   syslog = 0

# Do something sensible when Samba crashes: mail the admin a backtrace
   panic action = /usr/share/samba/panic-action %d


####### Authentication #######

# "security = user" is always a good idea. This will require a Unix account
# in this server for every user accessing the server. See
# /usr/share/doc/samba-doc/htmldocs/Samba3-HOWTO/ServerType.html
# in the samba-doc package for details.

# You may wish to use password encryption.  See the section on

   encrypt passwords = true

# If you are using encrypted passwords, Samba will need to know what
# password database type you are using.  
   passdb backend = ldapsam:ldap://localhost/

   obey pam restrictions = no
##########
ldap admin dn = cn=admin,dc=herguan,dc=local
ldap suffix = dc=herguan, dc=local
ldap group suffix = ou=Groups
ldap user suffix = ou=Users
ldap machine suffix = ou=Computers
ldap idmap suffix = ou=Users
; Do ldap passwd sync
ldap passwd sync = Yes
passwd program = /usr/sbin/smbldap-passwd %u
passwd chat = *New*password* %n\n *Retype*new*password* %n\n *all*authentication*tokens*updated*
add user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -m "%u"
ldap delete dn = Yes
delete user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-userdel "%u"
add machine script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -w "%u"
add group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupadd -p "%g"
delete group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupdel "%g"
add user to group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -m "%u" "%g"
delete user from group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -x "%u" "%g"
set primary group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-usermod -g "%g" "%u"
domain logons = yes
##########

slapd.conf:
```
# This is the main slapd configuration file. See slapd.conf(5) for more
# info on the configuration options.

#######################################################################
# Global Directives:

# Features to permit
#allow bind_v2

# Schema and objectClass definitions
include         /etc/ldap/schema/core.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/cosine.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/nis.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/inetorgperson.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/samba.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/misc.schema

# Where the pid file is put. The init.d script
# will not stop the server if you change this.
pidfile         /var/run/slapd/slapd.pid

# List of arguments that were passed to the server
argsfile        /var/run/slapd/slapd.args

# Read slapd.conf(5) for possible values
loglevel        0

# Where the dynamically loaded modules are stored
modulepath	/usr/lib/ldap
moduleload	back_bdb

# The maximum number of entries that is returned for a search operation
sizelimit 500

# The tool-threads parameter sets the actual amount of cpu's that is used
# for indexing.
tool-threads 1

#######################################################################
# Specific Backend Directives for bdb:
# Backend specific directives apply to this backend until another
# 'backend' directive occurs
backend		bdb
checkpoint 512 30

#######################################################################
# Specific Backend Directives for 'other':
# Backend specific directives apply to this backend until another
# 'backend' directive occurs
#backend		<other>

#######################################################################
# Specific Directives for database #1, of type bdb:
# Database specific directives apply to this databasse until another
# 'database' directive occurs
database        bdb

# The base of your directory in database #1
suffix          "dc=herguan,dc=local"

# rootdn directive for specifying a superuser on the database. This is needed
# for syncrepl.
# rootdn          "cn=admin,dc=herguan,dc=local"

# Where the database file are physically stored for database #1
directory       "/var/lib/ldap"

# For the Debian package we use 2MB as default but be sure to update this
# value if you have plenty of RAM
dbconfig set_cachesize 0 2097152 0

# Sven Hartge reported that he had to set this value incredibly high
# to get slapd running at all. See http://bugs.debian.org/303057
# for more information.

# Number of objects that can be locked at the same time.
dbconfig set_lk_max_objects 1500
# Number of locks (both requested and granted)
dbconfig set_lk_max_locks 1500
# Number of lockers
dbconfig set_lk_max_lockers 1500

# Indexing options for database #1
index           objectClass eq

# Save the time that the entry gets modified, for database #1
lastmod         on

# Where to store the replica logs for database #1
# replogfile	/var/lib/ldap/replog

# The userPassword by default can be changed
# by the entry owning it if they are authenticated.
# Others should not be able to see it, except the
# admin entry below
# These access lines apply to database #1 only
access to attrs=userPassword,shadowLastChange,sambaNTPassword,sambaLMPassword
        by dn="cn=admin,dc=herguan,dc=local" write
        by anonymous auth
        by self write
        by * none

# Ensure read access to the base for things like
# supportedSASLMechanisms.  Without this you may
# have problems with SASL not knowing what
# mechanisms are available and the like.
# Note that this is covered by the 'access to *'
# ACL below too but if you change that as people
# are wont to do you'll still need this if you
# want SASL (and possible other things) to work 
# happily.
access to dn.base="" by * read

# The admin dn has full write access, everyone else
# can read everything.
access to *
        by dn="cn=admin,dc=herguan,dc=local" write
        by * read

# For Netscape Roaming support, each user gets a roaming
# profile for which they have write access to
#access to dn=".*,ou=Roaming,o=morsnet"
#        by dn="cn=admin,dc=herguan,dc=local" write
#        by dnattr=owner write

#######################################################################
# Specific Directives for database #2, of type 'other' (can be bdb too):
# Database specific directives apply to this databasse until another
# 'database' directive occurs
#database        <other>

# The base of your directory for database #2
#suffix		"dc=debian,dc=org"
```

hosts:
```
127.0.0.1	localhost
127.0.1.1	pdc pdc.herguan.local

# The following lines are desirable for IPv6 capable hosts
::1     ip6-localhost ip6-loopback
fe00::0 ip6-localnet
ff00::0 ip6-mcastprefix
ff02::1 ip6-allnodes
ff02::2 ip6-allrouters
ff02::3 ip6-allhosts
```

hostname:
```
pdc.herguan.local
```

Weird thing is that if I do:
```
net getlocalsid herguan
```
then it works fine!?

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-20

   workgroup = herguan

```

Change the workgroup name so that it is all capital letters and try again. See if that changes anything.

### Post by ddave01 on 2008-02-20
Hi,
SOLUTION!

I have been struggling to get an XP machine to join the domain as per 
[http://www.howtoforge.com/openldap-samba-domain-controller-ubuntu7.10-p2](http://www.howtoforge.com/openldap-samba-domain-controller-ubuntu7.10-p2)
( I used debian instead of ubuntu).

The error from the xp client was user name not found.

The solution was to add a line to the smb.conf file and all was well.

[http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/linux-networking-3/username-could-not-be-found-samba-163486/add](http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/linux-networking-3/username-could-not-be-found-samba-163486/add) machine script = /usr/sbin/useradd -s /bin/false -d /home/nobody %u

Hope this helps others.
ddave01

---

### Post by redcharlie on 2008-02-20
RESOLVED (well, mostly ;-)
Wow, I gotta check this thread more often...lotta posts since mine...

Before I get into things, I did find the "search this thread" drop down...somehow I missed that over the past couple of years.  But is there a way to officially "thank" someone, you know, so that it increments their "thanked in posts" counter, as Ricky needs his incremented a few more times!

Okay, moving on...

Ricky wrote:
> From my understanding you have a Windows client connecting to the server. The client used to have local accounts, one being for your wife. You now have an LDAP account for your wife but all of her Documents were in her old "My Documents" folder, correct? If so then you should be able to log into the workstation as the local Administrator and copy the old files to the new My Documents folder in C:\Documents and Settings\%username% where %username% is the LDAP username for your wife.

Yep.  I copied the Desktop, My Documents, and Favorites
from C:\Documents and Settings\carla
to
 C:\Documents and Settings\carla.FIREPLACE

(yeah my better half and I have the same name, charlie and carla)
...where "carla" is her local acct name and "carla.FIREPLACE" is the ldap account.
I suppose XP is tagging the Domain name on there to distinguish the two.

> The permissions should default to the new LDAP account.
Nope.  After doing the copy as the local administrator, I logged in as carla.FIREPACE and no dice.  Permission denied all over the place.  I logged back in as the local admin, and tried to change ownership, but I could change ownership only to local objects/users.  I was able to give permissions to carla.FIREPLACE for individual folders, however I had to literally logon to FIREPLACE every time I did so (I kept getting a pop-up box asking for an account/passwd that had authority to deal with FIREPLACE objects/users).

I also tried logging in as FIREPLACE\root but discovered (using whoami.exe I got from the MS "resource kit") that root.FIREPLACE was not a domain admin and had no special privs on the box.

I eventually got things to work by setting permissions on each folder so that carla.FIREPLACE had access to them.  If she can read/write stuff, it really doesn't matter who the actual owner is.  But I had to do this for many individual folders, even though I checked the inherit boxes when I set permissions for parent folders.

My first stabs at googling what to do suggested that I should use "net groupmap modify" to point the "Domain Admins" group to a unix group, but I could never get that to work.  --"uable to update group database" was its constant refrain, even at debug level 8 --  So, I gave up and just added root to the "Domain Admins".
I got that general idea from reading the[ samba docs]("http://us4.samba.org/samba/docs/man/Samba-Guide/happy.html#id348843") at [http://us4.samba.org/samba/docs/man/Samba-Guide/happy.html#id348843](http://us4.samba.org/samba/docs/man/Samba-Guide/happy.html#id348843)

First, I confirmed root wasn't in the "Domain Admins" group to begin with:
```
charlie@myPDC:~$ sudo getent group | grep Domain
Domain Users:*:513:
Domain Guests:*:514:
Domain Computers:*:515:
Domain Admins:*:512:
```

Then I added root to the Domain Admins group
```
charlie@myPDC:~$ man smbldap-usermod
charlie@myPDC:~$ sudo smbldap-usermod -G "Domain Admins" root
charlie@myPDC:~$ sudo getent group | grep Domain
Domain Users:*:513:
Domain Guests:*:514:
Domain Computers:*:515:
Domain Admins:*:512:root
```

And I think that's what made FIREPLACE\root a domain admin on my wife's XP desktop.
But I also did the below:

from [http://us4.samba.org/samba/docs/man/Samba-Guide/happy.html#id348843](http://us4.samba.org/samba/docs/man/Samba-Guide/happy.html#id348843)
> Procedure 5.8. LDAP Directory Initialization Steps
...
Step 13.
The root account must have UID=0; if not, this means that operations conducted from a Windows client using tools such as the Domain User Manager fails under UNIX because the management of user and group accounts requires that the UID=0. Additionally, it is a good idea to make certain that no matter how root account credentials are resolved, the home directory and shell are valid. You decide to effect this immediately as demonstrated here:

```
root#  cd /opt/IDEALX/sbin
root#  ./smbldap-usermod -u 0 -d /root -s /bin/bash root
```

That last bit is key.  The ldap root user had a home of /home/root and /bin/false for a shell
```
charlie@myPDC:~$ sudo getent passwd | grep root
root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
root:x:0:0:Netbios Domain Administrator:/home/root:/bin/false
```

so I did
```
charlie@myPDC:~$ sudo smbldap-usermod -u 0 -d /root -s /bin/bash root
charlie@myPDC:~$ sudo getent passwd | grep root
root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
root:x:0:0:Netbios Domain Administrator:/root:/bin/bash
```

I'm not sure if the root shell and homedir are necessary, but the samba docs said they were a "good idea" so, security be darned, I gave root a valid shell.

Voila, I can now logon to my wife's XP desktop as FIREPLACE\root, and I have domain admin privs, so I can swizzle permissions (like on shared docs in the All Users folder) to my heart's delight w/o endless prompting for credentials.  (Changing ownership still fails, but I guess I can't have everything).

1)  Everything works great.  I can even create new users, log them into my XP machine using my domain.  I'll restart the PDC to be sure things still work, even create yet another test user and log in with the workstation to make srue things are working.  I then back up the system with tar (entire root dir).  I shut down the PDC, workstation and go home for the weekend.  First time I try it the next week I can no longer log into the PDC with accounts that I had not previously logged in with the week before. /etc/init.d/slapd restart works, it restarts with no issues

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-21
Found the problem.. and it wasn't the PDC at all.  My testing environment is:

1)  Ubuntu server.. not a VMWare server, it's actual hardware.
2)  Windows XP Pro client.. Installed on VMWare server on my desktop XP Pro machine.

"TCP/IP has reached the security limit imposed on the number of concurrent TCP connect attempts."

The limitation of no more than 10 connections is being reached by an antivirus update server I have installed.  So in a sense.. it was sort of breaking over time lol.  All I had to do was wait until the AV server finished pushing out updates and it was fine.

-Spliner

Classic small office - 6 PCs running either XP or 2000 pro.  No real password security - same basic user accounts on all the PCs - (logons have to match so that network drives continue to work in a workgroup).  No meaningful backups- except to thumb drives.  The Dentist isn't sure what he wants - but sums up the following:

- Backups concern him - each PC has files that need backed up.
- Users all share the same logon/pwd - this has to change.
- Wants disaster recovery "if the receptionist PC blue screens, I want her to be able to work on the PC next to her."  Think Roaming Profiles.
- APPS - not much more than Office.  He has a Dentist dabase that is hosted on the net and run thru a browser, and the same thing with a billing program.

I haven't returned with recommendations yet - but if there was a way to manage this with Ubuntu - it's a no-brainer.  I just can't spend all of the time necessary to make this work - and it needs to work from day one.  I don't want to have the support headaches with this - and if he needs to create an account - change passord etc - it has to be easy for him to figure out.

Setting up a Windows domain would be roughly $2000 for server hardware, $1,000 for 2003 Server with 10 CALS.  And he is talking of eventually setting up his own email....

Rob

Hi Rob,
I am also neebie on Linux. I have some preferences in learning from other posts. Means I know now a little bit about LDAP and SAMBA. Also MCC is working fine now. I have one open thread about DHCP in LDAP. But the work with MMC is very easy. Alternatively you can use Webmin. For me Webmin is no alternative, because to much possibilities. MMC is small and nice. I can manager Users/Shares and DNS and DHCP issues.

If you want, I can share my documentation about installing Ubuntu 7.10 with MMC/SLAPD and SAMBA without DNS and DHCP. Backup on Linux is another story, but LDAp I think makes it easy to Backup users and the SAMBA stuff, because you make a backup of ldap directory. If the Server brokes, you only have to reinsert the backup file in ldap and everything is back working.

Greets Stefan

### Post by andb on 2008-02-22
SOLVED: solution below
Im having a problem like HornedBeast's in post #146. 

Client machines can "ping dc01-ubuntu" but cannot "ping dc01-ubuntu.example.local".
Client machines can ping other machines on the network which are in the BIND zone list only by their short names, never by the FQDN
```
ping dc01-ubuntu.example.local
ping: unknown host dc01-ubuntu.example.local
```
```
nslookup dc01-ubuntu.example.local
Server:         192.168.0.60
Address:        192.168.0.60#53

Name:   dc01-ubuntu.example.local
Address: 192.168.0.60
```


The dc01-ubuntu machine can ping via both short names and FQDN.

HornedBeast "solved" this by putting an entry into his /etc/hosts file, which defeats the whole purpose of DNS :)

Has anyone else seen this issue and solved it?

SOLVED:
The problem does not exist on a 6.10edgy machine, but does on my 7.10gutsy machines
edit /etc/nsswitch.conf
change
"hosts: files mdns4_minimal [NOTFOUND=return] dns mdns4"
to be
"hosts: files dns mdns4"
It seems that this may only be the case of you chose ".local" as your top level domain. Do others have this problem? If yes, then it may be wise to change the HOWTO to use .localnet or some other alternative top level domain. Im currently thinking whether it is wiser to do this "hack" on every one of my machines, or remake the dc with another domain name...
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=661772](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=661772)

EDIT: It would be useful to include known problems in the howto, such as the error you get if you forget to change smbldap_bind.conf to admin from Manager... Also, not a single reboot is needed through the entire procedure. saves tons of time. Just restart the service in question. Rebooting is a sign of using MS too much ;)

### Post by redcharlie on 2008-02-22
I think I told'ja wrong, but you figured it out.  It's the Domain Admins GID, (not SID) that should end in "512".

The tricks below I got from reading the [samba docs]("http://us4.samba.org/samba/docs/man/Samba-Guide/happy.html#id348843") so all I can really do is refer you to there.

I used the getent command to check groups:
```
charlie@myPDC:~$ sudo getent group | grep Domain
Domain Users:*:513:
Domain Guests:*:514:
Domain Computers:*:515:
Domain Admins:*:512:
```

Then I used "smbldap-usermod" to add users to groups:
```
charlie@myPDC:~$ man smbldap-usermod
charlie@myPDC:~$ sudo smbldap-usermod -G "Domain Admins" root
charlie@myPDC:~$ sudo getent group | grep Domain
Domain Users:*:513:
Domain Guests:*:514:
Domain Computers:*:515:
Domain Admins:*:512:root
```

The smbldap user tools seem to more or less work like the regular 'nix CLI tools, adduser, usermod, userdel, etc. just with the prefix "smbldap-"

---

### Post by redcharlie on 2008-02-22
Alrighty, I've got a Jekyll and Hyde problem for anyone interested.

My wife's desktop dual boots: Ubuntu 7.10 (Dr. Jekyll) and WinXPSP2 (Mr. Hyde).  To her credit she uses Ubuntu about 90% of the time, only using winders if she has to do something for grad-school that doesn't quite work in 'nix (She loves Abiword and OpenOffice, but sometimes docs edited in Abiword show up funny in MSWord, and her instructors complain...)

I followed the instructions of the illustrious Mr. Jones, joined Mr. Hyde to the domain, and things were copasetic (well, except for the Domain Admin group issue, but I eventually figured that one out too).

Then I rebooted Mr. Hyde as Dr. Jekyll, and got LDAP authentication working (had a typo to fix in /etc/ldap.conf). That's workin' great too.

Then just to play with it, I had Dr. Jekyll cycle back into Mr. Hyde, and then things didn't work.  I could not log on as DOMAIN\carla nor as DOMAIN\root.  No cached creds, nothing.  Got the "can't authenticate..PDC might be down...contact yer admin" msg each time.

Didn't even try a local user, just went back to Dr. Jekyll to see if I could figure this one out.

Here's the samba log (on myPDC) for Mr. Hyde:
```
[2008/02/19 21:49:16, 1] smbd/service.c:make_connection_snum(1033)
  km400mlv (192.168.0.4) connect to service carla initially as user carla (uid=1001, gid=100) (pid 9307)
[2008/02/19 22:44:37, 1] smbd/service.c:close_cnum(1230)
  km400mlv (192.168.0.4) closed connection to service carla
[2008/02/21 21:34:02, 0] rpc_server/srv_netlog_nt.c:_net_auth_2(478)
  _net_auth2: creds_server_check failed. Rejecting auth request from client KM400MLV machine account KM400MLV$
[2008/02/21 21:35:02, 0] rpc_server/srv_netlog_nt.c:_net_auth_2(478)
  _net_auth2: creds_server_check failed. Rejecting auth request from client KM400MLV machine account KM400MLV$
```

As one can see, things worked fine on the 19th, then it rejected the MACHINE ACCOUNT on the 21st.

What happened between the 19th and the 21st?  I rebooted Hyde into Jekyll and got LDAP authentication working from KM400MLV (yeah, I name my boxes after their boards...yes, I'm a nerd).  

Important point: BOTH Jekyll and Hyde use the hostname of "KM400MLV".  And I think that's the issue.  I don't know how LDAP manages machine accounts but there is certainly a machine account for "KM400MLV" on myPDC.  My idea is that rebooting Hyde into Jekyll, but with the same hostname, I've somehow torqued the machine account in samba as well.

I haven't tried this yet, but I'm going to reboot Jekyll (which I'm using to write this post) back into Hyde, rejoin Hyde into the domain (again as host "KM400MLV") see if that fixes things, then cycle back as Jekyll, try logging in as an LDAP user, got back into Hyde, and see if I can repeat the problem.

If that works as expected I'll give Hyde his own distinct hostname, but just incase anyone has had this issue before, is there a way that Jekyll and Hyde can share the same hostname & machine account credentials?

---

### Post by Lhach on 2008-02-22
> **ddave01 said:**
> Hi,
SOLUTION!

I have been struggling to get an XP machine to join the domain as per 
[http://www.howtoforge.com/openldap-samba-domain-controller-ubuntu7.10-p2](http://www.howtoforge.com/openldap-samba-domain-controller-ubuntu7.10-p2)
( I used debian instead of ubuntu).

The error from the xp client was user name not found.

The solution was to add a line to the smb.conf file and all was well.

[http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/linux-networking-3/username-could-not-be-found-samba-163486/add](http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/linux-networking-3/username-could-not-be-found-samba-163486/add) machine script = /usr/sbin/useradd -s /bin/false -d /home/nobody %u

Hope this helps others.
ddave01

Surprisingly enough this fixed my issue as well.
This to me is a very bad sign. It seems that Samba at some point is not looking to the LDAP, or something is wrong with the smbldap-passwd -w %c entry. It's also possible that ddave01 and I have made similar mistakes while following the guide, especially since I haven't seen any other posts mentioning a similar problem.
I haven't extensively tested this so I could just be jumping the gun.

A refresher from my earlier posts:

My system is a real Ubuntu server install, no virtualization. The install is quite fresh (early Feb). Before running the command quoted above, any attempt to join a Windows client to the domain would result in "username not found" however the machine's name would be added to the LDAP directory if it did not already exist. Subsequent attempts to join the domain (after the machine was listed in the LDAP) would still fail with the same message.
This is why I suspect something is amiss with the more recent versions of our software pieces. It seems that Samba can talk to the LDAP through the smbldap- tools however it might query another DB/userlist when joining machines to the domain.
Like I said, I might be a bit premature in posting this, but I'll be doing some more investigation throughout the day.

---

### Post by Lhach on 2008-02-22
> **Lhach said:**
> Surprisingly enough this fixed my issue as well.
This to me is a very bad sign. It seems that Samba at some point is not looking to the LDAP, or something is wrong with the smbldap-passwd -w %c entry. It's also possible that ddave01 and I have made similar mistakes while following the guide, especially since I haven't seen any other posts mentioning a similar problem.
I haven't extensively tested this so I could just be jumping the gun.

A refresher from my earlier posts:

My system is a real Ubuntu server install, no virtualization. The install is quite fresh (early Feb). Before running the command quoted above, any attempt to join a Windows client to the domain would result in "username not found" however the machine's name would be added to the LDAP directory if it did not already exist. Subsequent attempts to join the domain (after the machine was listed in the LDAP) would still fail with the same message.
This is why I suspect something is amiss with the more recent versions of our software pieces. It seems that Samba can talk to the LDAP through the smbldap- tools however it might query another DB/userlist when joining machines to the domain.
Like I said, I might be a bit premature in posting this, but I'll be doing some more investigation throughout the day.

Quick update....
Samba is still checking /etc/passwd during at least one phase of joining a Windows machine to the domain.  After the machine is joined to the domain, its entry in the LDAP is updated. It seems like there's one last setting left in Samba to tweak for the LDAP. It also might not directly be Samba related. As evidenced by the slapd/klogd bootup problem, the system is not completely following the paramters in /etc/nsswitch.conf
These are all just guesses though.
More investigation to follow...

### Post by redcharlie on 2008-02-22
BTW, I misspoke (mistyped)...

What probably fouled things up for Mr. Hyde was not getting Jekyll's LDAP working, but actually joining Doc Jekyll to the domain, using the command
```
net join MYDOMAINNAME
```
so that Jekyll could browse and access the samba drives on the 20th.

I think that was what fouled up Hyde's machine acct, which seemed to work fine on the 19th, and then not on the 21st.

Again, this has a VERY simple solution in just giving Jekyll and Hyde distinct hostnames (and yeah, after all this I probably will name them "JEKYLL" and "HYDE"). I am just curious if Jekyll can somehow peek onto Hyde's NTFS partition and use his credentials, wherever they have been squirreled away by XP.

BTW. the wifey was VERY pleased that she can now access her directories on any of our machines from any other machine--I'd been pretty lax about setting up networked shared drives, NFS mounts etc, as I was pretty content with scp to move stuff around, but she wanted something a bit more user friendly.  Between NFS shares and samba, she can get her stuff from any machine whatever OS it's running.

The SWEETEST success was that as soon as I joined Hyde to the domain, I heard Hyde's printer whir, and I gave an astonished look at my wife, working on her Gutsy laptop (Toshiba satellite A135-S2386), and she said, "Yeah, I'm printing, so what?"

I replied, "But it's booted into XP, and I NEVER Have been able to get it to share the blessed printer when it's in XP!!" (okay, "blessed" wasn't my exact word choice...) I had struggled to get Hyde to share it's drives and printer since, gosh, Oct of 05 when I set the box up for her.  Too lazy until now to set up a domain, I had previously only tried simple workgroup based sharing, and it had NEVER worked.  (Of course, the solution there was just to keep it booted up as Doc Jekyll and use ipp and CUPS, but again, occasionally she does need Winders, and gol-dangit, after trying off and on for so long I was gonna get XP to share that printer even if that was the last thing I was ever going to do in winders...)

But as soon as I joined the domain, voila, it happened.  I did NOTHING to her laptop...I wasn't even thinking about it.  I suppose she used an older printer entry that had not worked before, but whatever...she can print, so THANX AGAIN!!

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-22
> **spliner said:**
> The groups themselves just don't seem like they are doing what they should at the moment .. at least for me.

-Spliner

I stand corrected.  It did not work the first time I tried, but I removed them from the groups all together except "root" and when I came back from lunch, adding them to the "Domain Admins" group worked to give all three of my accounts (root + 2 test accounts) Administrator access.  Excellent!  Now.. why it did not work the first time.. it's likely because I tried it in phpldapadmin or because I tinkered with the SIDs as well.

-Spliner

I am assuming a command like:

sudo smbldap-usermod -G "Domain Admins","Domain Users" testuser

...would add that user to both of those groups.

Edit:  Yes, it does work.  You can add a user to multiple groups using the syntax above.

### Post by andb on 2008-02-22
Ive resolved a big problem with ubuntu ldap clients, but first-
This thread has reached a pretty large scale, and I think it has the potential to really grow. Id like to see Kerberos, SSL conenctions and such here too. There is, in fact, so much that the forum maybe isn't the best place for this. It really seems that a group wiki with a modular / page hierarchy format may be better. The work done by ricky to update the first post is amazing, a wiki would help everyone help him out.  

So, fixing linux ldap client-
The tread is mainly aimed at windows clients from what I've read, but I had an interesting experience with an Ubuntu client. I found that it would freeze before reaching the GDM (login screen).The spining "working icon" would just sit there. In the /etc/nsswitch.conf, removing ldap from "group" allowed the machine to boot. I could then put it back in, end session and log in as any user, and all works fine. Broken again on reboot. 

In [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/libnss-ldap/+bug/155947](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/libnss-ldap/+bug/155947) I found a solution, adding an ignore groups directive with all of your local groups to /etc/ldap.conf (I also added to /etc/ldap/ldap.conf just to be safe)
```
nss_initgroups_ignoreusers root,root.slocate,daemon,bin,sys,sync,games,man,lp,mail,news,uucp,proxy,www-data,backup,list,irc,gnats,nobody,dhcp,syslog,klog,avah
i-autoipd,messagebus,avahi,cupsys,haldaemon,hplip,statd,ntp,sshd,beagleindex,clamav
```
edit the above list as per your own local groups. This has the major disadvantage that machines need to be uniform, I expect that it will break again if a new local group is created by installing software and not put into the ignore directive.

As the thread founder wrote, it takes a huge amount of time to work everything out. I personally don't use MS, nor do I want to, but wow, AD is a lot easier to configure than this.

---

### Post by andb on 2008-02-23
Id like to ask, what is the motivation behind using the rootbinddn option in the ldap.conf file? When you consider that you then need the root password stored on the clients, this is a massive security hole. Using a Gutsy client, you can log on just fine without it. 

Just to change passwords?  Wouldnt a PHP page on the server which uses an id other then adimn, one which has read all access (as normal) and write passwd, which would confirm an entered passwd then write the new? Wouldn't this be a lot more secure? 

In windows client Appendix C, point 8, there are instructions to use root, 12345. What happens with windows clients if you use something else, an anonymous connection? There must be a better way than dropping LDAP root passwords around.

---

### Post by redcharlie on 2008-02-23
I've got to resume my life, but as for my Jekyll and Hyde issue, this is what I've done so far:

I would like it so that other users on the network don’t have to know what OS the dual boot box is currently running in order to access that machine’s printer or shared drives. (e.g. having to guess between \\dualbox_winxp\printer and \\dualbox_ubun2\printer )

I’ve tried getting both to use the same machine account name, SID, and password, (using things like NETDOM.exe and “net -f CHANGESECRETPW”) but so far not much luck.  I can change the nt and lanman hashes in lDAP (using perl to generate the hashes) for a new passwd for the machine acct, and change that password on the ubuntu client (with net -f CHANGESECRETPW) but after I do that, "net rpc testjoin MYDOMAIN" always fails from the Ubuntu client.  NT_ACCESS_DENIED

I need someway of directly manipulating machine acct passwords if it's going to work, as I can only join the domain once from one OS then I would have to make sure that both sides use the same creds (at least SID and machine acct passwd, I figure).

I know it’s possible for two different OS installs to share the same machine account, as M$ actually makes provisions for this, due to the necessity of disabling automatic machine acct password changes if it’s going to work over time.

see [http://support.microsoft.com/kb/154501](http://support.microsoft.com/kb/154501)

But getting Linux and Windows to share, I don’t know…

There may be an easier way, however, using netbios aliases instead of actually using the same machine account. After all, I’m mostly concerned with how things appear on the network than the actual accts.

see
[http://www.hostingforum.ca/293959-re-samba-linux-active-directory-integration-problem.html]("http://www.hostingforum.ca/293959-re-samba-linux-active-directory-integration-problem.html")

If aliases work, that would be MUCH easier than trying to figure out how to get Linux and XP to share one machine acct.

SERVER HOSTNAME: server
workgroup in smb.conf: LAMBRECHTSFAMILY
LDAP: DC=lambrechtsfamily,dc=com

Client DNS: 192.168.1.100

I can't figure out how to connect my windows XP machine. When I type in my samba domain (LAMBRECHTSFAMILY) it returns this:

```
The query was for the SRV record for _ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs.lambrechtsfamily 
  
Common causes of this error include the following: 
  
- The DNS SRV record is not registered in DNS. 
  
- One or more of the following zones do not include delegation to its child zone: 
  
lambrechtsfamily
. (the root zone)
```

How do I add the right SRV record?

here is output of **nslookup server.lambrechtsfamily.com**
```

*** Can't find server name for the address 192.168.1.100: non-existent domain
*** Default servers are not available

Server:  UnKnown

Address:  192.168.1.100


Name:    server.lambrechtsfamily.com

Address:  192.168.1.100


```

Also, wins support = no as per tutorial's smb.conf.

$ttl 38400
lambrechtsfamily.com.	IN	SOA	server.lambrechtsfamily.com. john-admin.lambrechtsfamily.com. (
			1203794824
			10800

			38400 )

server.lambrechtsfamily.com.	IN	A	192.168.1.100

                                                                         [ OK ]

cpthk, It looks like smbd is not running at first, but it does look like you ARE able to start it with "/etc/init.d/samba restart"

If this happens, do something like the below:

```
charlie@km400mlv:~$ sudo smbcontrol smbd ping
[sudo] password for charlie:
PONG from pid 5317
PONG from pid 5181
(wait a tic)
charlie@km400mlv:~$ sudo smbcontrol nmbd ping
PONG from pid 5179
charlie@km400mlv:~$ ps -ef | grep mbd
root      5179     1  0 08:11 ?        00:00:00 /usr/sbin/nmbd -D
root      5181     1  0 08:11 ?        00:00:00 /usr/sbin/smbd -D
root      5317  5181  0 08:11 ?        00:00:00 /usr/sbin/smbd -D
charlie   8204  8168  0 08:56 pts/0    00:00:00 grep mbd

charlie@km400mlv:~$ sudo /etc/init.d/samba restart
 * Stopping Samba daemons...                                             [ OK ] 
 * Starting Samba daemons                                                [ OK ] 

charlie@km400mlv:~$ sudo smbcontrol smbd ping
PONG from pid 8253
PONG from pid 8249
charlie@km400mlv:~$ sudo smbcontrol nmbd ping
PONG from pid 8247
charlie@km400mlv:~$ ps -ef | grep mbd
root      8247     1  0 08:59 ?        00:00:00 /usr/sbin/nmbd -D
root      8249     1  0 08:59 ?        00:00:00 /usr/sbin/smbd -D
root      8253  8249  0 08:59 ?        00:00:00 /usr/sbin/smbd -D
charlie   8257  8168  0 08:59 pts/0    00:00:00 grep mbd
```

The above should let you know what's running before and after you attempt a restart. (You don't have to do BOTH "smbcontrol smbd ping" and "ps -ef | grep mbd" as either should work).

If samba is not running after you boot but you are able to start samba using "/etc/init.d/samba restart", then it may simply a config issue with your services (try reenabling via System->adminstration->services, and make sure samba is checked)

### Post by redcharlie on 2008-02-25
andb wrote:> 
d like to ask, what is the motivation behind using the rootbinddn option in the ldap.conf file? When you consider that you then need the root password stored on the clients, this is a massive security hole. Using a Gutsy client, you can log on just fine without it.

Just to change passwords? Wouldnt a PHP page on the server which uses an id other then adimn, one which has read all access (as normal) and write passwd, which would confirm an entered passwd then write the new? Wouldn't this be a lot more secure?

Yeah, really.  Well, as an experiment, I removed /etc/ldap.secret and /etc/ldap/ldap.secret and rebooted (just in case the passwd was being held somewhere in memory or in a temp file) and things still worked fine.

To test I did a "sudo userdel charlie" on my wife's desktop and then tried to login and change my password. Note that unless you specify the "-r" switch, userdel does not touch files owned by that user.  As I was only converting my account from local to LDAP, I did NOT want to delete my files.

WARNING1: do NOT do this when logged on as the user you are deleting.

WARNING2: If you are converting a local user to an LDAP user (as I was), you have to be careful about the actual Unix User ID ("UUID" in phpldapadmin) in LDAP and on the system where your local user exists.  They should be the same.  My initial problem was that my old local user had ID 1000 but LDAP had me as 30003.  This doesn't seem to be an issue when a local user exists in /etc/passwd, etc. as the login process seems to defer to local system files.  But after deleting my local user acct with userdel, when I logged on via LDAP I was now user 30003, not user 1000, and could no longer access my home directory, etc.  ssh login still worked (it complained about .profile, but that was it) but graphical login failed miserably.  So, I changed my UUID in LDAP (via phpldapadmin) to "1000" from "30003".  This seemed to solve (most of) the issue, and did not affect my domain logon on the winders side of things.

But, continuing on, to test whether or not I needed the root LDAP passwd hanging out on all the local machines, I removed the secrets file, converted my local acct to LDAP only, and then tried loging in and changing my passwd.

It all worked fine.  So, yeah, it really doesn't look like you need it, and I'm not sure why it's there.  My success, of course, could be due to some security hole on the server which allows anonymous binds to wreak havoc, but phpldapadmin seems to restrict my access when I do bind anonymously, and objects when I fatfinger the root password when I don't, so that seems to be as it should be.

I don't know how else to test it, except by just continuing to run w/o the root password stashed on the client.  If something goes bang at some point, I'll post it here.

One more note:
To get passwd to work, I had to tweak my /etc/pam.d/common-password file, but I had to fix that before I even thought about the secrets file.  

Contents of common-password:
```
password  sufficient   pam_unix.so nullok md5 shadow use_authtok
#password   sufficient   pam_ldap.so use_first_pass
password   sufficient   pam_ldap.so
password   required     pam_deny.so
```

I had to remove the "use_first_pass" argument to pam_ldap.so, otherwise when I tried to change my password, I got this:
```
charlie@km400mlv:/etc$ passwd
Enter login(LDAP) password: 
passwd: Authentication information cannot be recovered
passwd: password unchanged
```

But after removing "use_first_pass" it worked fine.  And no, I have no clue what the "use_first_pass" option does, I removed it just as a guess.

### Post by redcharlie on 2008-02-26
esandgren:
> I'd like to set up samba/ldap so that some of my windows xp users are restricted to be logged on betwen say 8:00 am and 10:00 pm?
I've searched trough the phpadmin tool but didn't find any apparent parameters to be used.

mmm, you can add the "LOGONHOURS" Active Directory attribute to a user via phplpadmin but good luck knowing how to populate the value.   It's a 21 byte hex string, 3 bytes/day, I think, from the looks of it.  One description I've found is [here]("http://activeroles.inside.quest.com/entry.jspa?externalID=1048")
phpldapadmin does not seem to have any way of editing it meaningfully, although I haven't searched for plugins.

I haven't had any luck so far trying to set it manually, and if you do get it to work, it's only valid for windows users.

Other tools, like LDAP account manager ([http://lam.sourceforge.net]("http://lam.sourceforge.net"))
and web2ldap([www.web2ldap.de]("www.web2ldap.de")) DO have plugins to properly edit the LOGONHOURS attribute.  
The web2ldap demo server does not seem to be up but LAM has two demo servers.
Go to [http://lam.sourceforge.net/live-demo/index.htm]("http://lam.sourceforge.net/live-demo/index.htm")
pick a server, logon, edit a user, and click on the Samba 3 tab.  Near the bottom of the samba attribs you'll see "Logon hours" and an "edit" button next to it.  Click on edit and it will show a big grid of checkboxes, hours x days.

I have no clue what if anything needs to happen on the windows client side to get this to work!

This begs the question, of course, as to how one could enable or disable accounts for all clients (M$ & 'nix) and I don't have a clue there either, although I found a hint here:
[http://www.mail-archive.com/ldap@listserver.itd.umich.edu/msg00289.html](http://www.mail-archive.com/ldap@listserver.itd.umich.edu/msg00289.html)

1)  Secure Apache2.  This means, disable SSL 2.0 and have only SSL3 and TLS1.0 as available options.
2)  Generate your own certificate for Apache2.  This is a local server (not accessable to the outside world in my case) however, security scans reveal SSL 2.0 to be enabled, along with other non-secure encrytion (<128bit) methods.

Webmin also seems to be using SSL 2.0, and might even be the cause of the warning with my security scans. Most insist that to disable SSL2 you must re-compile mod_ssl to not enclude the encryption methods you do not want.  I have no clue how to do that, and after reading guides, I'm still uncertain. ### Post by spliner on 2008-02-27
A solution for Apache2's SSLv2 use (I hope):

Steps taken from (and modified somewhat heavily):  [http://linuxhappy.wordpress.com/2008/01/21/success-subversion-apache2-ssl-ubuntu-710-with-users/](http://linuxhappy.wordpress.com/2008/01/21/success-subversion-apache2-ssl-ubuntu-710-with-users/)

Note:  That guide above also installs Subversion.. I have no idea if it is needed, so I only took the steps I needed to secure Apache and then added a few steps I had found from around the net (I did not install subversion.. I don't even know what it is!).

Here are the steps I took to secure Apache2's pages:

1)  Set yourself up as root (or use sudo in front of all commands)

sudo bash

2)  Enable SSL

a2enmod ssl

3)  Download apache2-ssl.tar.gz  and create your own certificate.  I can't vouch for the source of the file below.  Is there a better place to get this?  Do we already have it installed somewhere?  I don't know.. this step worked for me though.  Also.. this certificate was only valid for less than 60 days.. why.. I don't know.  Hopefully these steps can be refined.

cd \
mkdir temp
cd temp
wget [http://launchpadlibrarian.net/7477840/apache2-ssl.tar.gz](http://launchpadlibrarian.net/7477840/apache2-ssl.tar.gz)
tar -xzvf apache2-ssl.tar.gz
cp ssleay.cnf /usr/share/apache2/
mkdir /etc/apache2/ssl
apache-ssl-certificate

<At this point, fill in all information you can when asked>

<This next step may not even be necessary.. I did however perform these steps>

cd /etc/apache2/sites-available
cp default myown-ssl
gedit myown-ssl

change NameVirtualHost *
to
NameVirtualHost *:443

change <VirtualHost *>
to
<VirtualHost *:443>

At the end of the file, before </VirtualHost> add the following lines:
...You will need to add these to every SSL site listed in /etc/apache2/sites-available...

SSLEngine on
SSLCertificateFile /etc/apache2/ssl/apache.pem
SSLProtocol -all +SSLv3 +TLSv1
SSLCipherSuite HIGH:MEDIUM
TraceEnable off

<Save and Quit the file(s)>

<Now add the 'TraceEnable off' to /etc/apache2/sitesavailable/default, and any other non-ssl web sites you have created.>

Restart apache...

/etc/init.d/apache2 restart

Note:  I am fairly certain this will work with the apache2 install given in this guide.  I have modified my server several times
trying to get this to work, but I happened across this guide today and to my surprise these steps secured the server for me.  Someone will need to test this method and confirm it works for them.  Also, if anyone can spot steps that are wrong or not needed above, please correct them!  I am no expert.  I am simply trying to secure Apache2 and Webmin.

I perform my security scans with Tenable Nessus from an XP workstation on my network.  I make no guarantees.  This simply fixes the issues listed by a Nessus Scan of the PDC.

Note2:  I have not found a way to fix Webmin.  I had to uninstall it to get Nessus to quit warning me about the use of SSLv2.  It's likely, even by using the steps listed above
Nessus will still give you that warning unless you uninstall Webmin.  There is a way to force webmin to use Apache2's server instead of it's unsecure built-in server, but from what I have read it limits your use of webmin.  My suggestion is use webmin to set things up the way you need them, then simply uninstall it if you are creating a production server.  I will try later to secure Webmin, but like I said, you use functionality by converting it to a virtualhost on Apache2.

I have not solved all of my issues with Nessus scans.  I have however closed all holes in the server without a firewall so far.  I still get 5 warnings that I will be working on in the
next couple of weeks.  If anyone gets a hole listed on a Nessus scan, post it here and I'll be happy to find out how I closed it if I can remember (I always try to take notes).

One last thing.. I'm a newb.. I don't know half of what these commands do.  Don't flame me, help me.  ;)

Hope this is useful information Richard.. I'd love to see something similar put in the final tutorial although I realize it has nothing to do with OpenLDAP or SAMBA.  

-Spliner

---

### Post by redcharlie on 2008-02-27
I help manage a CentOS (think free RHES) sandbox at work, and last Oct we started getting zinged with internal security scans and I had to  do a bit of work on the sandbox to come into compliance.  Cleaning up SSL2 and expired certificates were some of the things I (vaguely) remember doing.   I certainly remember NOT having to recompile anything.  Generating new certs was the most tedious part.

We have name-based virtual hosting (port 80) with all the virtual hosts defined in the main httpd.conf file.

I have only skimmed your most recent post, so I don't know if what I've done contributes anything, but I thought I'd at least show what I  did.  I can say that the SSLv2 security issue no longer appeared on our "non-compliance" list for this box.

And I have no idea about how to secure webmin.

To restrict SSL to v3, all I did was add the below to httpd.conf
```
# 06Nov2007 charlie Turn off SSLv2, force SSLv3
SSLProtocol -all +SSLv3
```
and then I restarted apache.
Version:
```
[charlie@sandbox conf]$ /usr/sbin/httpd -v
Server version: Apache/2.0.59
Server built:   Oct 26 2007 13:55:48
```

I created a new SSL key and self-signed cert per [http://www.howtoforge.com/secure_websites_using_openssl_and_apache](http://www.howtoforge.com/secure_websites_using_openssl_and_apache) 

```
$ ls -l /etc/httpd/conf/ssl*/*charlie*
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 3583 Nov 14 17:01 /etc/httpd/conf/ssl.crt/sandbox_14Nov2007_charlie.crt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root  891 Nov 14 17:54 /etc/httpd/conf/ssl.key/sandbox_14Nov2007_charlie.key
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 1659 Nov 14 16:57 /etc/httpd/conf/ssl.key/sandbox_14Nov2007_charlie.key.encrypted
```

I added the new key and cert and turned off support for weak SSL certs by editing /etc/httpd/conf.d/ssl.conf as below:
```
# SSLCipherSuite ALL:!ADH:!EXPORT56:RC4+RSA:+HIGH:+MEDIUM:+LOW:+SSLv2:+EXP 
# 14Nov2007 charlie turned off weak SSL Cert support for security audit
SSLCipherSuite ALL:!aNULL:!ADH:!eNULL:!LOW:!EXP:RC4+RSA:+HIGH:+MEDIUM
...
# 14Nov2007 charlie added new key with new cert
SSLCertificateFile /etc/httpd/conf/ssl.crt/sandbox_14Nov2007_charlie.crt

#   Server Private Key:
#   If the key is not combined with the certificate, use this
#   directive to point at the key file.  Keep in mind that if
#   you've both a RSA and a DSA private key you can configure
#   both in parallel (to also allow the use of DSA ciphers, etc.)

# 14Nov2007 charlie added new key with new cert
SSLCertificateKeyFile /etc/httpd/conf/ssl.key/sandbox_14Nov2007_charlie.key


```
