### Post by spliner on 2008-02-28
Another fix on the road to securing the PDC...

OpenLDAP allows anonymous binds by default.  I managed to secure this by adding these lines to slapd.conf today.  I added them right underneath the section that says "# Features to Permit".  I then restarted the PDC and all was well:

disallow bind_anon
require authc

To my knowledge, the disallow bind_anon simply dissallows anonymous binds.  The require authc simply tells slapd to require authentication for access to the LDAP directory.  Adding these two lines has not caused any adverse effects on my server so far.  I have just rebooted both the server and the client and tried just about everything I can think of, and all is working.  Nessus no longer gives me a warning about OpenLDAP allowing anonymous binds.  Hope this is helpful!

Disclaimer:  I'm no expert, use my suggestions at your own risk.  I am simply on a personal quest to turn the server created with this guide into a production server which is as secure as possible.  I am a long way from achieving that goal, but I am getting there.  I am likely going to need some serious help to get SSL/TLS working for all communication, but hopefully Rich will include that in a future version of the guide. 

-Spliner

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-28
My next task...

The netlogon share is open to everyone (read only).  I'd like it to remain read only, but I want only authenticated users of the PDC to have read access to it, all other attempts should be denied.  Anyone know how to accomplish this?

-Spliner

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-28
> **spliner said:**
> My next task...

The netlogon share is open to everyone (read only).  I'd like it to remain read only, but I want only authenticated users of the PDC to have read access to it, all other attempts should be denied.  Anyone know how to accomplish this?

-Spliner

Here are the permissions assigned to the NETLOGON folder on an actual Active Directory domain controller:
```

Type	Name				Permission
-----	-------------------		--------------
Allow	Authenticated Users	        Read & Execute
Allow	Server Operators	        Read & Execute
Allow	Administrators		        Full Control
Allow	SYSTEM			        Full Control
Allow	CREATOR OWNER	                Full Control

```

I'm not sure on exactly how to translate this into the SAMBA file but it should give you an idea.

-Richard

---

### Post by spliner on 2008-02-28
Those permissions sound about right, I just havn't figured out how to do it on SAMBA either.  The home directory seems to be correct.  Only the authenticated user can access the home folders, and they cannot access anyone else's home folder.  I just need to duplicate that without breaking the netlogon scrips all together.

I can say one thing.. if you go into System > Administration > Shared Folders (Ubuntu Desktop is installed on my server) and do anything in there at all it trashes your /etc/samba/smb.conf file.  There's no way to use that utility to do what I want, I just thought it strange that my netlogon share would stop working after I accessed that.  Sure enough.. go in there click a few tabs, close out of it and it'll garble the end of your smb.conf file (deletes some lines, puts others in the wrong place).  Good thing I have finally learned to back stuff up frequently lol.

-Spliner

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-02-29
Attention Thread:

I just went through and compiled a list of items that I will try to work on for the next version of the guide.

There are about 28 "Issues" for me to look at on my list. The ones that I will be focusing the most amount of time on will be:
[LIST]
[*] Address "hung" servers at boot up - reordering startup services
[*] Installation, Configuration, and Use of the LDAP Account Manager (LAM)
[*] Configure and use NFS4
[*] Add additional DNS configuration for smooth integration with a Windows environment
[*] Secure the NETLOGON share
[*] Best practices for user permissions/group assignment when operating in a Windows-client environment
[*] Begin looking at DHCP services and Dynamic DNS Updates for a truly dynamic and productive network
[*] No anonymous LDAP connections
[/LIST]

Please, continue posting your findings and your wishes/wants/ideas/etc... I accept every idea!

Thank you for your support.

Sincerely,

-Richard

### Post by redcharlie on 2008-03-01
Many thanks Ricky for an incredibly useful thread!

I would like to add one thing to your list of "issues", if it's not there already.
Laptops.

Brilliant guy that I am, I decided to set up LDAP authentication on my wife's laptop (Toshiba A135-8236 running Gutsy).  I very quickly had to backout most of my changes, trying to figure out why gdm login was hanging (console login was still okay fortunately).

Best I can figure, I had a bunch of auto NFS mounts set up in /etc/fstab, but her laptop for some reason doesn't connect to our wireless network automatically (she has to use wifi-radar to get an ip).  But the boot-time NFS mounts didn't exactly fail...when I tried to access the mount points I got an I/O error.  And I noticed something called "gdl_fs_crawler" tying up one whole cpu (and filling my logs).  Turns out she has Google desktop installed, and I assume it was crawling the filesystem looking for modified files, but probably hanging on the bad NFS mounts.

I made those mount points noauto in fstab, and then gdm login worked again. So, I think it was not really authentication related, but I was chastened enough to know I had to think this whole laptop + LDAP thing thru before I try it again.

### Post by spliner on 2008-03-03
> **thechitowncubs said:**

I would have to agree with Scorp123, it's likely a typo or something left out somewhere along the line.  I've tested this tutorial on two VMware installations, and an actual server installation and it works.  My first two attempts were a failure though, both had to do with a step I missed or a typo I had in a config somewhere, and even once had to do with a step I completed without having root shell access at the time (it mucked up permissions on some files).  Can you post  your config files (both slapd.conf and smb.conf) so we can take a peek?  If those look good then we can narrow the problem down to one of the steps.  Now, for my use, I skipped the NFS stuff, installing it once cause my server to stop working.. i thought.. actually the issue ended up being with DNS in the end, but I left NFS off because I don't have any linux clients in the network.

-Spliner

---

### Post by spliner on 2008-03-03
> **rickyjones said:**
> There are about 28 "Issues" for me to look at on my list. The ones that I will be focusing the most amount of time on will be:
[LIST]
[*] Address "hung" servers at boot up - reordering startup services
[*] Installation, Configuration, and Use of the LDAP Account Manager (LAM)
[*] Configure and use NFS4
[*] Add additional DNS configuration for smooth integration with a Windows environment
[*] Secure the NETLOGON share
[*] Best practices for user permissions/group assignment when operating in a Windows-client environment
[*] Begin looking at DHCP services and Dynamic DNS Updates for a truly dynamic and productive network
[*] No anonymous LDAP connections
[/LIST]

Please, continue posting your findings and your wishes/wants/ideas/etc... I accept every idea!


Any chance of adding secure communication (encryption) for the LDAP/SAMBA database?  It's currently disabled in the setup, and isn't a big deal since I'm using this server on a secure network, but it sure would be nice to have that added layer of security.  For now though, after disabling the anon LDAP connections, even Nessus scans don't whine about encryption for LDAP/SAMBA (but it may not be looking for it).

The last few security issues I have left are (and I had posted these earlier I believe):

-NETLOGON share is not secured (only hole left on a Nessus Scan) (already in your list)

These next items are somewhat un-related to this guide, but related to the software chosen to make this PDC work, so they should be addressed at some time in the future (imho), even if they are simply addendums to the guide you have written:

-DNS (Bind9) accepts recursive lookups (2 warnings in Nessus Scan about this) (No solution yet) (I have found a solution and managed not to implement it correctly, its on the back burner for me right now)
-Securing Apache2 (Solved in an earlier post, although a refined setup of Apache2 could serve this purpose within the guide... disable SSLv2 and enable SSLv3 and TLSv1).
-Webmin is a terrible security hazard.  It uses SSLv2 and there doesn't seem to be a way of fixing that at this time.  (Only solution I found was to remove it after using it for initial configuration)  It can be converted to use Apache2 instead of its internal web server, but it cripples some options.
-Anonymous binds to phpldapadmin (solved in an earlier post, at least it looks like it is to me) (already in your list)
-SAMBA+OpenLDAP are not using encryption for all communication.  Password encryption is enabled, but all other transmission may be unencrypted at this point.  (No solution yet, but it looks as if the options are there, I'm just too chicken at this point to try it) (not in your list, but may be too much for the next revision)

Anyway, I hope these topics help for future versions, even if they don't make it into the next release!  I've gotten to the point that I enjoy reading this thread so much it's now one of my home pages!  Thanks Richard, and everyone in this thread, for all the hard work!

-Spliner

---

### Post by spliner on 2008-03-03
> **thechitowncubs said:**
> now that I think about it, what effect would having a domain such as lambrechtsfamily.com, which I have also registered with [http://freedns.afraid.org](http://freedns.afraid.org) have on my local setup?

I don't think it will have any effect, at least not on the local network.  All the free dns sites do is point a name to an IP address.  But if you tried to set that up during the initial use of this guide, you may have inadvertantly caused yourself a problem somewhere along the line.

-Spliner

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-03-04
> **thechitowncubs said:**
> now that I think about it, what effect would having a domain such as lambrechtsfamily.com, which I have also registered with [http://freedns.afraid.org](http://freedns.afraid.org) have on my local setup?

You can use that domain name without issues with this guide. But you must remember to make it universal when following the guide.

That domain name translates, in LDAP, to "dc=lambrechtsfamily,dc=com" - so you will need to make sure that you use that suffix EVERYWHERE that you see "dc=example,dc=local" in my guide.

Changing the domain name in one step and not following through in other steps is probably the #1 problem that people are running in to.

-Richard

---

### Post by zathrusuk on 2008-03-04
Thanks for a great guide, after weeks of bashing my head against a brick wall with Fedora, I installed ubuntu and had samba and ldap up in about an hour.

### Post by zathrusuk on 2008-03-04
Found the solution, i changed the 

add user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -m "%u"

to 

add user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -a -m "%u"

Hope that helps someone else

---

### Post by spliner on 2008-03-05
I had mentioned before that I had removed webmin to make the server more secure.  Although this solves my problem, I'm left with having to re-install it any time I need to use it, then uninstall it again because it uses weak ciphers and shows up on security scans.  I've been doing some research and have found the following information (no gaurantees this information is without flaw):

1)  There is no way known to set the ciper strength in webmin (that I have found) for its internal web server.  There may however be a way to set up your certificates for SSL to only accept certain ciphers, I simply don't have enough experience with that yet to know what I am talking about.
2)  Disabling the internal web server and using Apache2 for webmin is possible, and mentioned in the documentation, however it disables some core functions of webmin (from what I understand, I have not tried it).
3)  The only way I can come up with to fix the problem would be to re-compile SSL support for Ubuntu without the weak cipers included (this is the only thing I can come up with so far)

Since I don't have a clue how to re-compile OpenSSL for Ubuntu without weak cipher support included, I won't be attempting it unless someone can write me a step-by-step guide.  Since by default it seems OpenSSL includes support for the weak ciphers, the only way to fix Apache2 is to make changes to the configuration to only use SSLv3 and TLSv1 and use MEDIUM:HIGH cipher strength only.  Unfortunately I can find no way of setting those options in the free version of webmin.

Just some food for thought, Richard has mentioned a new application for managing SAMBA/LDAP users, so I'll just wait to see what he comes up with.  Webmin 1.4x now is available and evidently has more support for LDAP functions, but I have not tried it.

Here's where I read about the re-compile theory:

[http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/linux-security-4/weak-ssl-ciphers-in-webmin-252160/](http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/linux-security-4/weak-ssl-ciphers-in-webmin-252160/)

-Spliner

### Post by spliner on 2008-03-07
Here's an interesting tidbit.. might not be a bug.. but it might be...

If I load up my test client (VMWare server - Windows XP Pro), and right click My Computer > Properties.. then click Computer Name, then click Change at the bottom and try to change the name of the computer, I get a password prompt asking me to enter the username and pass of an account that has the rights to change the name of the computer on the domain.  Nothing here works, not root, not an administrator account, nor a user account.

I got it to work by:

1)  Log in as local Administrator
2)  Change the PC back to a workgroup rather than a domain and change the computer name at the same time.
3)  Reboot, and log back in as local Administrator
4)  Add it back into the domain (change it back to domain in the properties page of My Computer > Computer Name) and enter the username root and my root password.
5)  Reboot again.

Just thought that interesting ;P

All I did was copy my VM (duplicate it for testing) and renamed it.  I did not want the two to conflict each other so I had to change the PC name and IP address before booting them both up. 

-Spliner

---

### Post by spliner on 2008-03-07
Very nice!  It does work.

I now have two XP Pro clients both on the domain.  I logged into both of them and created a shared folder, clicked permissions, removed Everyone from the list, then clicked Add, then clicked Advanced and then Find Now to get a list of users and selected EXAMPLE\testuser and changed its permissions to full control.

I then logged in as a different user (testuser2) on the other XP Pro VM and browsed to the share and double clicked it.  I was denied access.  ;)

I logged in as testuser and was granted access!

Furthermore:

If I log into the PC that hosts the share as a different user, that folder shows up but doesn't show its shared.  You can access it because you're on the local machine in which the share resides, but from another machine you can't unless you're logged in as testuser.

Something I havn't tested yet:

Does the workstation only list the usernames of users that have logged into the local machine (because all of mine have) or does it actually get a list from the OpenLDAP server?  I'll create a user and try that shortly.

Edit:  Yes, it gets its user list from the domain.  I have confirmed this by adding a new user to the PDC, then modifying my shares to add another user.  The new users shows up in the list of available users when getting a list of users, even though that user has never logged into any workstation in the past.  So at least that is working just like a Win2k Active Directory server.  ;)

-Spliner

---

### Post by spliner on 2008-03-07
I have confirmed that you cannot change the name of the computer while it is on the domain.  I tried it on both VM's, even the one I just added to the domain five minutes prior with the root username and pass.  I get access denied when using any username/pass combo.  So, someone with a physical machine will need to test that to be sure that it isn't just some quirk with VMWare server and SIDs.

Here's what the log file said when I tried to change the name:

[2008/03/07 13:22:33, 0] rpc_server/srv_samr_nt.c:set_user_info_21(3246)
  set_user_info_21: failed to rename account: NT_STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED

So, for now that workaround I posted above will work. You're just going to have to remove it from the domain first using the root username and pass when it prompts you, then change the name, then re-add it to the domain (add in a few reboots there).

-Spliner

How are you shareing FC and AC  are they samba shares?  Here is how I trouble shoot.

Check users: make sure all users belong to the same group
**Use smbldap-usershow *username***

Check Samba Share: make sure FC and AC have the same samba config in /etc/samba/smb.conf
**Use vim /etc/smaba/smb.conf**

Check folder permissions, group ownership: do folders have group R/W access are they part of your Domain Users Group?
**Use: ls -l** to check perm

### Post by Deb.Early on 2008-03-12
I would like to add my thin little voice to all the rest of the Ubuntu heavyweights in thanking Richard for his *fantabulous* work! I had more fun working through his guide, and ended up with a lovely working server with a happily logged-in XP client. Rock on!

So far I've stumbled across just about all of the bugs you folks have, and managed to bail myself out using your solutions. Thanks to one and all for sharing!

Being something of an Ubuntu neophyte, however, I'm still working with my Gutsy desktop. I spent the last 20 or so hours working an issue where I was unable to ping the server using anything other than its IP. This turned out to be a [_known Network Manager bug_]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/network-manager/+bug/159114").

In Richard's section "Configure Ubuntu Server 7.10 (client) for LDAP Authentication", he writes "You have your /etc/resolv.conf file configured so that it is looking at your server for DNS and that it is searching your domain. For my setup I used the same /etc/resolv.conf as I did for the server." This did not work for me, the client's resolv.conf was always wiped out at the next boot. I use DHCP and it may just be a DHCP thing, but the workaround in the bug report worked very well.

Just in case anyone else runs into it, and I can save them 20 hours of head-scratching!  ;-)

Cheers to all!

---

You may want to either start from scratch with this guide or compare the two closely.  I noticed this guide re-posted on howtoforge but I'm not sure it is the most recent version.  Unfortunately I did not use the roaming profiles, so I may not be much help.

> **mr. Twitch said:**
> regarding roaming profiles however, it doesnt seem to be copying the files to the server when i log off, or grab them off the server when i log on

i hvae my logon path set as \\gollum\profiles\%U

Just curious, but once you log on, can you manually browse to the \\gollum\profiles\username folder and read from that share?  I had a similar problem with the netlogon share early on, it simply didn't work.  I found I could not actually browse that share manually.  Further research and I found some corruption in my smb.conf file caused by the ubuntu-desktop's samba utility (that is the only incompatibility I have found with using the ubuntu-desktop and this guide).  Not sure if you are using the desktop (gnome) on the server or not, so that's not really my point, but if you can manually read and write to the \\gollum\profiles\username folder when you are logged in, then it should be working.  If you can't, then there's something not set up right with the share itself (permissions or whatever).  Can you post your smb.conf file as well?  I may be terrible at permissions (and might not be able to help if that is the problem) but I have had considerable experience comparing smb.conf files over the last few weeks thanks to Richard's guide.  Maybe one of us can spot something.

-Spliner

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-03-17
> **rocsak said:**
> I still haven't found a solution to the problem of authenticating Linux clients machines to Samba shares automatically (windows machines work fine) that I mentioned in an earlier post. I know I must be doing something wrong so if you have any suggestions I'd be happy to hear them. 

Windows, by default, sends the currently logged in username when connecting to network shares. I don't believe Linux does this, nor do I know if it can be configured to do this.

For a Linux client, instead of using SAMBA, have you considered using NFS for other shares? Because of the central LDAP system NFS will be a secure alternative to SAMBA, and it integrates so much more nicely on Linux.

The login script line in smb.conf is a global setting. You can use PHPLDAPAdmin or LDAP Account Manager to edit individual user LDAP accounts. There is a setting in each user account that allows you to set the login script.

-Richard

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-03-19
A simple search of this thread found my post.

**Different Login Scripts for Each User - PHPLDAPAdmin**

Undo the login script setting that we added to smb.conf. Then, use PHPLDAPAdmin to do the rest... Log in as "cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local" (customize if need be). In the tree on the left you will look for the users container and the user account. Click the uid=USER item (USER = the username). At the top of the RIGHT pane there is a link - "Add new attribute" - click it. In the new right pane window click the first drop down and select "sambaLogonScript". In the box to the right you will type in the name of your script as it appears in the NETLOGON folder. Example: If the script exists in /home/samba/netlogon/user.script.1.bat then enter "user.script.1.bat" in the field. Then click the "Add" button. Save the changes.

-Richard

### Post by spliner on 2008-03-20
Update:  I have had a production version of my server up and running for about a two weeks now.  It's been flawless with 100% uptime so far.  I incorporated all of the fixes I needed here in these threads, including the security fixes and it's been working great with 5 of my users testing it full time.  Security scans only report the one directory (netlogon) as a hole, and we all know it's read-only, so until the next version of this guide I will definitely live with that.  

I ended up using tar to back the server up each night at midnight (just the needed directories) with a secondary script backing up the /ldaphome directory for the users files.  All five users had their My Documents folder moved to their home directory (manually, I have never tested doing this automatically) and all of their files are backed up each night to my external hard drive(s).

I can confirm the following things work:

-Users can change their own passwords through WinXP Pro, simply hit CTL-ALT-DEL and click "Change Password".  The server allows the change as normal.
-Moving the My Documents folder to the H: drive so that it is backed up on the server works great.
-DNS for all functions, including Internet Access works.
-Making people an administrator on the local workstations is as simple as adding their logon to the "Domain Admins" group using 'smbldap-usermod -G "Domain Admins" username'
-Windows sees users from the domain just fine when adding permissions to shares/etc.
-Sharing folders between workstations seems to still work if it is needed.  You can choose who gets access again with the user list from the domain and local PC.  Sweet.

A few minor glitches I've noticed (all have been correctable)(some have to do more with WinXP/Windows Networking than Samba):

-Non-Administrator (Domain Admins) users don't seem to have much control.  They can use the PC, but can't delete things they did not create (including previously made folders on the pc), and cannot control much in the CP.  This is likely by design though (limited account).  It would be nice however to have another group that is somewhere in the middle.. delete access but no settings.. no biggie.
-If you leave yourself logged into the server and walk away for an extended period of time, sometimes Windows will report that you are no longer connected to the server and that your files are available offline.. this has to do with moving the My Documents folder over.. some sort of time-out.  Simply click on the little icon and click OK and it'll re-connect you, or log out and back in again.  Seems not to be an issue.  I have instructed my users to get used to logging OFF their pc's at the end of the day, that way their files are synched to the server for backup that night.  I've tripple-checked for power-saving crap.. the server doesn't go to sleep, neither do the pcs affected.  Again, not an issue if you just log off the pc when done.

----

Just wanted to say "Thanks Again!!" to Richard and everyone in this thread who contributed.  Not only is the server stable, but I have run into no Linux/Samba bugs so far, other than a few of the known ones listed in this thread (NTP server not compatible with XP, etc.).  Any problems that have arisen have been easy to fix and have been with Windows rather than the Samba server.

I've decided to test the server with just the 5 users for now, but in 30 days I will add 20 more if all goes well.

;)

-Spliner

---

Yes, I' ve configured succesfully the NFS shares. But, in an enviroment with both windows and linux clients I would prefer that only one service is used for sharing folders and files for two reasons. First for ease of configuration, maintenance and troubleshooting and second because I have no idea what (if any) problems may occur by the simultaneously sharing of the same files by two different services.
So I will keep looking for a solution and I will post it  here if I ever manage to find one!

### Post by andguent on 2008-03-26
Richard,

Wonderful guide. Thanks for writing it. It is extremely easy to read and well laid out. I wish more people would include Appendix A & B: final conf files.

I have a few comments. If they have already been mentioned in previous responses, please ignore.

* slapd occasionally hangs at 6:25 in the morning due to logrotate pulling syslog out from under it via cron.daily. Logrotate pre/post scripts for syslog should be manually adjusted to stop/start slapd to avoid this issue. You may not see this issue until running slapd for a few months.
* db4.2-util is a highly recommended package (and/or similar version). If you restart slapd from command line after a fresh install, it usually mentions that recovery tools aren't available. Installing the db utils will allow slapd to repair its own ldap database.
* dnsmasq is a wonderful lightweight alternative to bind and dhcpd. Usually two or three lines of modification and you have a fully functional dns forwarding, dns cache, and dhcp server up and running.
* If you havin't found it already, do be aware of smbldap-usermod, It will allow you to set groups on ldap users.

I will have to read the entire thread in order to see if anything else jogs my memory. Again, wonderful guide.

---

* db4.2-util is a highly recommended package (and/or similar version). If you restart slapd from command line after a fresh install, it usually mentions that recovery tools aren't available. Installing the db utils will allow slapd to repair its own ldap database.

### Post by spliner on 2008-03-26
Anyone new to Linux/Ubuntu may want to set up regular backups with tar for saftey.  I have managed to do this with help from some folks over at this thread (and some help from users in this thread):

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=572194](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=572194)

Thought it might be of interest to anyone trying to accomplish this who's new at scripting.  It was a big help to me.  I was originally going to purchase a third party software package for my production SAMBA+OpenLDAP server, but found that tar was included with Ubuntu and is all that is needed.  Enjoy.  

(this was mentioned as well somewhere in this thread, however I posted my final solution to backups at the link above)

-Spliner

Not precisely. I'm trying to configure a Samba server running on Ubuntu Gutsy 64-bit, using the standard tools provided by apt-get. I'm not following your guide, but my Samba config looks almost exactly like yours. 

The OpenLDAP server is running on an Apple Xserve, but it's already configured and working properly. It's properly providing authentication to this Ubunutu server in question, as well as other machines. 

For whatever it's worth, the Darwin OpenLDAP configuration includes default schemas for Samba. It's designed to mimic a PDC for Mac, Windows and Linux SMB clients.

> It might have issues with this: dc=my,dc=server_dn,dc=com

It might see that additional "dn" in there as an error. Have you tried reinstalling and using just "dc=my,dc=server,dc=com"?

dc=my,dc=server_dn I just made up for purposes of posting on this board. I don't like to publicly post identifying information. The actual server DN is correct, and it's the same one powering logins on the Ubuntu (and other) clients. 

I believe that this:
```

sambaDomainName=XSERVE7

```
is the issue, and that smbldap-populate isn't creating that entry correctly. It is also failing to create anything under cn=Groups, even though it's clear that it exists:
```

entry cn=Groups,dc=my,dc=made_up_server_dn_for_posting_to_ubuntu_forum_only,dc=com already exist.

```

Again, from the LDAP server's slapd.log 
```

do_add: invalid dn (sambaDomainName=XSERVE7,dc=my,dc=made_up_server_dn_for_posting_to_ubuntu_forum_only,dc=com)

```
is extremely fishy. I've never seen a DN start with "sambaDomainName=" before, usually they have a "cn=" or "o=" or some gobbledygook like that.

### Post by rocsak on 2008-03-28
> **rickyjones said:**
> As a small business server a simple backup mechanism should also be employed. I will consider possibilities as I work on this project.

For a small business server backup solution I think you should consider using rsync, which is included in Ubuntu by default. I use it for backing up my office machines because it's great and easy to configure. You can read this excellent guide for instructions on how to set it up: [http://www.mikerubel.org/computers/rsync_snapshots/]("http://ubuntuforums.org/*%20http://www.mikerubel.org/computers/rsync_snapshots/")

For a GUI solution, [Grsync]("http://www.opbyte.it/grsync/") is in the Ubuntu repositories so you can install it with apt-get.

Another solution is [Backuppc]("http://backuppc.sourceforge.net/info.html") which is based on rsync and has more options for mutliple hosts, but I haven't tried it yet.

And finally for scheduling the backup procedure you can use Cron which is included in Ubuntu by default. [Here are instructions]("http://www.linuxhelp.net/guides/cron/") of how to set it up. GUI lovers can install  gnome-schedule from the repositories.

---

### Post by rocsak on 2008-03-28
> **rocsak said:**
> And also it would be nice if anyone has a solution about the nm-applet disappearing act after the configuration as an ldap client both in Ubuntu and Xubuntu 7.10. 

Well I know I 'm quoting myself here but finally I found a solution!!! As it is explained in [this ]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/NetworkManager") the problem occurs from modifying the PAM authentication module. So you have to edit the /etc/auth-client-config/profile.d/open_ldap that you created 
and add this line to the end:

```
session required        pam_foreground.so
```

run again the command

```
sudo auth-client-config -a -p open_ldap

```

Now reboot and the network manager appears again!!!

---

### Post by rocsak on 2008-03-28

Thank you. This line must be important because adding it solved and some other minor problems I had in Xubuntu, like the fact that both the shutdown and restart option were unavailable in the Quit menu after configuring it as an LDAP client. So, double victory for me today!

> **spliner said:**

/usr/sbin/smbldap-passwd: user root doesn't exist

```

I checked my smbldap_bind.conf file, and it looks ok:

```
############################
# Credential Configuration #
############################
# Notes: you can specify two differents configuration if you use a
# master ldap for writing access and a slave ldap server for reading access
# By default, we will use the same DN (so it will work for standard Samba
# release)
slaveDN="cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local"
slavePw="12345"
masterDN="cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local"
masterPw="12345"

```

I also reconfigured sldap to make sure the password and domain were entered correctly.

I should note that during step 6, after the apt-get install, I was not prompted to enter and confirm the admin password. I assumed this was ok because I would later reconfigure anyway.

Where else might my password be entered incorrectly? 

I highly recommend running through the guide exactly as is without changing the usernames, passwords, or anything. Once you have it working I then recommend printing the guide and anywhere you see a password/username that you wish to change, change it to what you want.

> **sricketts said:**
> 
Why does it not see the user root when I populate?

Thanks,
S.
It does not see the root user because the smbldap-tools script is not able to authenticate to your directory. Somewhere, somehow, LDAP is using a different username and password than what you have in the smbldap_bind.conf file. This is why the root user does not exist!

**People with "Invalid Credential" Problems and Other Password Issues:**
It is extremely important to keep track of exactly what username and password you use when going through this guide. If you change "12345" in the beginning then you must also change it everywhere! LDAP does not arbitrarily change the password. At some point it was mistyped or one password was used in one step and then a different one used at a later step. It is virtually impossible to figure out which step, however.

I hope this helps!

Sincerely,
Richard

```
host myname.example.local

```Is the output of the command the correct ip address of your server;

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-04-11
> **sricketts said:**
> Unfortunately I am still new to LAN administration. I am using this project as a learning experience. Thanks for all of your help.

You're very welcome. Don't worry about being new, everyone has to start somewhere. I think you'll learn a lot, fairly quickly, by asking questions on these forums.

> **sricketts said:**
> 

This is from another machine on the network:
```

sricketts@XXX:~$ host YYY.example.local
YYY.example.local has address 192.168.1.70
YYY.example.local has address 192.168.1.70
Host YYY.example.local not found: 3(NXDOMAIN)

```
where 192.168.1.70 is the correct ip address of the server. I can ping YYY, but not YYY.example.local.

If you can't ping using the fully qualified domain name (FQDN) [yyy.example.local] then this is because there is not a DNS server configured for this network. A bypass would be to configure the **hosts** file on each computer to contain the FQDN of your server.

> **sricketts said:**
> 
The network consists of the server, a couple of other ubuntu desktops, as well as some windows and mac machines, all connected to a switch->router->DSL. I have never explicitly set up any kind of DNS server.

You'll want to configure a DNS server at some point. I go through this configuration in the guide and I highly recommend you do that.

> **sricketts said:**
> 
I tried the static configuration from the howto, and I could not access the internet. When I changed back to dhcp, I had access again. I am not sure why -- how can I investigate this?

If you applied the static configuration from the howto exactly then the problem is that you did not replace my values with the values for your own network.

My network uses an IP range of 192.168.0.0-254. Your network is probably different. You need to modify the network setting to match your network. For example, if you are on a basic 10.0.0.0 network then your server IP could be 10.0.0.2. Your gateway might be 10.0.0.1 (whatever your router is). Your subnet mask would be 255.0.0.0 unless you use something special.

> **sricketts said:**
> 

Thanks,
S.
You're welcome!

I hope this helps you out.

Sincerely,
Richard

---

### Post by sricketts on 2008-04-11
> **rickyjones said:**
> 
You'll want to configure a DNS server at some point. I go through this configuration in the guide and I highly recommend you do that.


I don't see instructions in your guide about a DNS server. I am using this howto: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=236093](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=236093)

Also, I have a quick question: I did cat /etc/resolv.conf to find out that my router (192.168.1.254) is my DNS forwarder. That concept makes sense to me. What is confusing is that I can use the single word host names to ping between machines (like ping hostA, ping hostB) instead of their local IP addresses. So who is responsible for managing these names? Does the router take care of this, or do I already have a local DNS server somewhere?

Thanks,
S.

### Post by sricketts on 2008-04-11

Alright, I upgraded to 7.10, but without doing a fresh reinstall from scratch.

Indeed, when I originally tried a static configuration, I had the wrong gateway. This time, I used the correct gateway, and static config worked fine.

>

S.

### Post by tombutcher1990 on 2008-04-13
hey ricky

right, i've been following guide version 2 on your website, but i have come unstuck a bit! 

i got to the end of step 13 and when i reboot it hangs on the "starting kernel log daemon" part.

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-04-13
> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> hey ricky

right, i've been following guide version 2 on your website, but i have come unstuck a bit! 

i got to the end of step 13 and when i reboot it hangs on the "starting kernel log daemon" part.

i tried hard rebooting it but still nowt.

i also tried adding more RAM to the machine as i read somewhere that if you don't have enough memory then the system can hang.


any ideas? do i have to start over? cos that wudn't be good!

SLAPD needs to start first. In the directory /etc/rc2.d you'll see several files, one of them is "XXslapd" where "XX" is a number. You'll need to move this file so that the "XX" number is "09" (that's "zero nine").

End result should be: 09slapd

Then reboot. This will resolve the hanging issue.

Sincerely,
Richard

**How To Fix Ubuntu Domain Controller Hang on Bootup:**
```

cd /etc/rc2.d
mv S19slapd S09slapd

```

Sincerely,
Richard

That is extremely odd! You seem to be missing:
```

lrwxrwxrwx  1 root root   18 2007-10-19 10:49 S10sysklogd -> ../init.d/sysklogd
lrwxrwxrwx  1 root root   15 2007-10-19 10:49 S11klogd -> ../init.d/klogd

### Post by tombutcher1990 on 2008-04-15
they are both in /etc/rc1.d/ and it is:

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-04-15
> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> they are both in /etc/rc1.d/ and it is:

k89klogd
k90sysklogd

i duno y they are k's!


also, in /rc6.d/ i get a k80slapd file :confused:

Then we'll manually tell slapd to start in a different way. This should work:
```

cd /etc/rc1.d/
ln -s /etc/init.d/slapd K88slapd

```

Try that and reboot...

Thanks,
Richard

---

### Post by tombutcher1990 on 2008-04-15
why k88? does that mean something cos i don't understand what these numbers do lol

anyway, i wrote that and am now restarting. fingers crossed!

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-04-15
> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> why k88? does that mean something cos i don't understand what these numbers do lol

anyway, i wrote that and am now restarting. fingers crossed!

The "K88" just gives it a place in the start up process. Think of it as the priority. Each run level has a specific set of services that it starts. It starts those services in the order that you specify them.

-Richard

-Richard

### Post by timtodd on 2008-04-15
Thanks for the nice guide!  Very helpful.

Have you considered editing the first post to add the S09slapd fix?  
Would have saved me ~5 hours (ran through the guide three times (including reinstalling Ubuntu), searched the web, read this entire thread, tried numerous variations, etc. ).  :)

In the explanation it might be nice to reference (for more information/documentation about runtime levels and how Ubuntu handles startup scripts):  [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4247684&postcount=187](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4247684&postcount=187).

I was able to boot into recovery mode and make the change.  Two important things to note:  1) It is case sensitive.  i.e. s09slapd is different than S09slapd.  2) S9slapd is different than S09slapd.  Make sure to move it to S09slapd.  I booted into recovery mode, made the change and it is working great.  Hasn't hung since the fix.

**On a separate note**, Is there a way to expire the password for new users, so that they are forced to change their password (winxp) at first login?

Thanks again,
Tim

> Thanks for the nice guide!  Very helpful.

Have you considered editing the first post to add the S09slapd fix?  
Would have saved me ~5 hours (ran through the guide three times (including reinstalling Ubuntu), searched the web, read this entire thread, tried numerous variations, etc. ).  :)

In the explanation it might be nice to reference (for more information/documentation about runtime levels and how Ubuntu handles startup scripts):  [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4247684&postcount=187](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4247684&postcount=187).

I was able to boot into recovery mode and make the change.  Two important things to note:  1) It is case sensitive.  i.e. s09slapd is different than S09slapd.  2) S9slapd is different than S09slapd.  Make sure to move it to S09slapd.  I booted into recovery mode, made the change and it is working great.  Hasn't hung since the fix.

**On a separate note**, Is there a way to expire the password for new users, so that they are forced to change their password (winxp) at first login?

Thanks again,
Tim

Since I'm working on creating the next version of the guide I have not had the time to update the first post. It will be updated soon though!

On the other note, I'm not sure on how to configure that. I don't even know if its possible at this point. I wish I could help further!

Thanks,
Richard

---

1) Please post your two smbldap tools files (in /etc/smbldap_tools) so that I can verify they are correct.
2) What is your domain name?
3) Did you be sure to use your individual domain name at every step of the guide that it is needed?
4) Did you remember to give SAMBA the password to OpenLDAP?

I hope this helps,
Richard

### Post by kwisher on 2008-04-15
> **rickyjones said:**
> 1) Please post your two smbldap tools files (in /etc/smbldap_tools) so that I can verify they are correct.
2) What is your domain name?
3) Did you be sure to use your individual domain name at every step of the guide that it is needed?
4) Did you remember to give SAMBA the password to OpenLDAP?

I hope this helps,
Richard

my domain is mint4.wisher.com

smbldap_bind.conf:

```
############################
# Credential Configuration #
############################
# Notes: you can specify two differents configuration if you use a
# master ldap for writing access and a slave ldap server for reading access
# By default, we will use the same DN (so it will work for standard Samba
# release)
slaveDN="cn=admin,dc=wisher,dc=com"
slavePw="1971flh"
masterDN="cn=admin,dc=wisher,dc=com"
masterPw="1971flh"


```

smbldap.conf:

```
# $Source: /opt/cvs/samba/smbldap-tools/smbldap.conf,v $
# $Id: smbldap.conf,v 1.18 2005/05/27 14:28:47 jtournier Exp $
#
# smbldap-tools.conf : Q & D configuration file for smbldap-tools

#  This code was developped by IDEALX (http://IDEALX.org/) and
#  contributors (their names can be found in the CONTRIBUTORS file).
#
#                 Copyright (C) 2001-2002 IDEALX
#
#  This program is free software; you can redistribute it and/or
#  modify it under the terms of the GNU General Public License
#  as published by the Free Software Foundation; either version 2
#  of the License, or (at your option) any later version.
#
#  This program is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
#  but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
#  MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
#  GNU General Public License for more details.
#
#  You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
#  along with this program; if not, write to the Free Software
#  Foundation, Inc., 59 Temple Place - Suite 330, Boston, MA 02111-1307,
#  USA.

#  Purpose :
#       . be the configuration file for all smbldap-tools scripts

##############################################################################
#
# General Configuration
#
##############################################################################

# Put your own SID. To obtain this number do: "net getlocalsid".
# If not defined, parameter is taking from "net getlocalsid" return
SID="S-1-5-21-4158514501-2046276458-3817976005"

# Domain name the Samba server is in charged.
# If not defined, parameter is taking from smb.conf configuration file
# Ex: sambaDomain="IDEALX-NT"
sambaDomain="wisher"

##############################################################################
#
# LDAP Configuration
#
##############################################################################

# Notes: to use to dual ldap servers backend for Samba, you must patch
# Samba with the dual-head patch from IDEALX. If not using this patch
# just use the same server for slaveLDAP and masterLDAP.
# Those two servers declarations can also be used when you have 
# . one master LDAP server where all writing operations must be done
# . one slave LDAP server where all reading operations must be done
#   (typically a replication directory)

# Slave LDAP server
# Ex: slaveLDAP=127.0.0.1
# If not defined, parameter is set to "127.0.0.1"
slaveLDAP="127.0.0.1"

# Slave LDAP port
# If not defined, parameter is set to "389"
slavePort="389"

# Master LDAP server: needed for write operations
# Ex: masterLDAP=127.0.0.1
# If not defined, parameter is set to "127.0.0.1"
masterLDAP="127.0.0.1"

# Master LDAP port
# If not defined, parameter is set to "389"
masterPort="389"

# Use TLS for LDAP
# If set to 1, this option will use start_tls for connection
# (you should also used the port 389)
# If not defined, parameter is set to "1"
ldapTLS="0"

# How to verify the server's certificate (none, optional or require)
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
verify="require"

# CA certificate
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
cafile="/etc/opt/IDEALX/smbldap-tools/ca.pem"

# certificate to use to connect to the ldap server
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
clientcert="/etc/opt/IDEALX/smbldap-tools/smbldap-tools.pem"

# key certificate to use to connect to the ldap server
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
clientkey="/etc/opt/IDEALX/smbldap-tools/smbldap-tools.key"

# LDAP Suffix
# Ex: suffix=dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG
suffix="dc=wisher,dc=com"

# Where are stored Users
# Ex: usersdn="ou=Users,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for usersdn
usersdn="ou=Users,${suffix}"

# Where are stored Computers
# Ex: computersdn="ou=Computers,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for computersdn
computersdn="ou=Computers,${suffix}"

# Where are stored Groups
# Ex: groupsdn="ou=Groups,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for groupsdn
groupsdn="ou=Groups,${suffix}"

# Where are stored Idmap entries (used if samba is a domain member server)
# Ex: groupsdn="ou=Idmap,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for idmapdn
idmapdn="ou=Idmap,${suffix}"

# Where to store next uidNumber and gidNumber available for new users and groups
# If not defined, entries are stored in sambaDomainName object.
# Ex: sambaUnixIdPooldn="sambaDomainName=${sambaDomain},${suffix}"
# Ex: sambaUnixIdPooldn="cn=NextFreeUnixId,${suffix}"
sambaUnixIdPooldn="sambaDomainName=wisher,${suffix}"

# Default scope Used
scope="sub"

# Unix password encryption (CRYPT, MD5, SMD5, SSHA, SHA, CLEARTEXT)
hash_encrypt="SSHA"

# if hash_encrypt is set to CRYPT, you may set a salt format.
# default is "%s", but many systems will generate MD5 hashed
# passwords if you use "$1$%.8s". This parameter is optional!
crypt_salt_format="%s"

##############################################################################
# 
# Unix Accounts Configuration
# 
##############################################################################

# Login defs
# Default Login Shell
# Ex: userLoginShell="/bin/bash"
userLoginShell="/bin/bash"

# Home directory
# Ex: userHome="/home/%U"
userHome="/ldaphome/%U"

# Default mode used for user homeDirectory
userHomeDirectoryMode="700"

# Gecos
userGecos="System User"

# Default User (POSIX and Samba) GID
defaultUserGid="513"

# Default Computer (Samba) GID
defaultComputerGid="515"idealx

# Skel dir
skeletonDir="/etc/skel"

# Default password validation time (time in days) Comment the next line if
# you don't want password to be enable for defaultMaxPasswordAge days (be
# careful to the sambaPwdMustChange attribute's value)
defaultMaxPasswordAge="45"

##############################################################################
#
# SAMBA Configuration
#
##############################################################################

# The UNC path to home drives location (%U username substitution)
# Just set it to a null string if you want to use the smb.conf 'logon home'
# directive and/or disable roaming profiles
# Ex: userSmbHome="\\PDC-SMB3\%U"
userSmbHome=

# The UNC path to profiles locations (%U username substitution)
# Just set it to a null string if you want to use the smb.conf 'logon path'
# directive and/or disable roaming profiles
# Ex: userProfile="\\PDC-SMB3\profiles\%U"
userProfile=

# The default Home Drive Letter mapping
# (will be automatically mapped at logon time if home directory exist)
# Ex: userHomeDrive="H:"
userHomeDrive=

# The default user netlogon script name (%U username substitution)
# if not used, will be automatically username.cmd
# make sure script file is edited under dos
# Ex: userScript="startup.cmd" # make sure script file is edited under dos
userScript=

# Domain appended to the users "mail"-attribute
# when smbldap-useradd -M is used
# Ex: mailDomain="idealx.com"
mailDomain="wisher.com"

##############################################################################
#
# SMBLDAP-TOOLS Configuration (default are ok for a RedHat)
#
##############################################################################

# Allows not to use smbpasswd (if with_smbpasswd == 0 in smbldap_conf.pm) but
# prefer Crypt::SmbHash library
with_smbpasswd="0"
smbpasswd="/usr/bin/smbpasswd"

# Allows not to use slappasswd (if with_slappasswd == 0 in smbldap_conf.pm)
# but prefer Crypt:: libraries
with_slappasswd="0"
slappasswd="/usr/sbin/slappasswd"

# comment out the following line to get rid of the default banner
# no_banner="1"
```

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-04-15
> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> right, how are people copy and pasting so much from their CLI's!?!

I connect to my server using PUTTY. When you select text from the PUTTY program it is automatically copied to the Windows clipboard. Then I just paste it in where I need it to go.

To paste into the PUTTY session you copy text from a different program and then right-click in the PUTTY window.

> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
also, i have a problem again :(

i got to step 17 this time!!

but when you say now use a web browser to go to....

do i use a web browser on the server or on a pc on the same network? i presumed it didn't matter, altho unfortunately, my pc won't connect. i get the message "took too long to respond" (firefox browser)

any ideas?

When I say use a web browser I do mean from another computer. You say it takes too long. From your workstation can you PING the server? Is Apache installed and working?

> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
i went back and checked my ip address in that file we set it in at the start, i noticed i had a typo in the gateway so i changed it, will this have mucked up any of the later steps? strange thing is, it still managed to connect to the internet to download webmin :confused:

What kind of a typo? If it was an additional zero before a number then it probably didn't matter. Otherwise I'm not sure how that worked.

> **tombutcher1990 said:**

one comment as well, i'm using your guide from your rrcomputing website cos its easier to read etc, found with the webmin bit that you tell me to download webmin_1.400.conf but then install webmin_1.390.conf. i presume this is when you updated it at some point and just missed a bit.

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-04-15
> **kwisher said:**
> smbldap_bind.conf:

```
############################
# Credential Configuration #
############################
# Notes: you can specify two differents configuration if you use a
# master ldap for writing access and a slave ldap server for reading access
# By default, we will use the same DN (so it will work for standard Samba
# release)
slaveDN="cn=admin,dc=wisher,dc=com"
slavePw="1971flh"

```

smbldap.conf:

```
# $Source: /opt/cvs/samba/smbldap-tools/smbldap.conf,v $
# $Id: smbldap.conf,v 1.18 2005/05/27 14:28:47 jtournier Exp $
#
# smbldap-tools.conf : Q & D configuration file for smbldap-tools

#  This code was developped by IDEALX (http://IDEALX.org/) and
#  contributors (their names can be found in the CONTRIBUTORS file).
#
#                 Copyright (C) 2001-2002 IDEALX
#
#  This program is free software; you can redistribute it and/or
#  modify it under the terms of the GNU General Public License
#  as published by the Free Software Foundation; either version 2
#  of the License, or (at your option) any later version.
#
#  This program is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
#  but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
#  MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
#  GNU General Public License for more details.
#
#  You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
#  along with this program; if not, write to the Free Software
#  Foundation, Inc., 59 Temple Place - Suite 330, Boston, MA 02111-1307,
#  USA.

#  Purpose :
#       . be the configuration file for all smbldap-tools scripts

##############################################################################
#
# General Configuration
#
##############################################################################

# Put your own SID. To obtain this number do: "net getlocalsid".
# If not defined, parameter is taking from "net getlocalsid" return
SID="S-1-5-21-4158514501-2046276458-3817976005"

# Domain name the Samba server is in charged.
# If not defined, parameter is taking from smb.conf configuration file
# Ex: sambaDomain="IDEALX-NT"
sambaDomain="wisher"

##############################################################################
#
# LDAP Configuration
#
##############################################################################

# Notes: to use to dual ldap servers backend for Samba, you must patch
# Samba with the dual-head patch from IDEALX. If not using this patch
# just use the same server for slaveLDAP and masterLDAP.
# Those two servers declarations can also be used when you have 
# . one master LDAP server where all writing operations must be done
# . one slave LDAP server where all reading operations must be done
#   (typically a replication directory)

# Slave LDAP server
# Ex: slaveLDAP=127.0.0.1
# If not defined, parameter is set to "127.0.0.1"
slaveLDAP="127.0.0.1"

# Slave LDAP port
# If not defined, parameter is set to "389"
slavePort="389"

# Master LDAP server: needed for write operations
# Ex: masterLDAP=127.0.0.1
# If not defined, parameter is set to "127.0.0.1"
masterLDAP="127.0.0.1"

# Master LDAP port
# If not defined, parameter is set to "389"
masterPort="389"

# Use TLS for LDAP
# If set to 1, this option will use start_tls for connection
# (you should also used the port 389)
# If not defined, parameter is set to "1"
ldapTLS="0"

# How to verify the server's certificate (none, optional or require)
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
verify="require"

# CA certificate
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
cafile="/etc/opt/IDEALX/smbldap-tools/ca.pem"

# certificate to use to connect to the ldap server
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
clientcert="/etc/opt/IDEALX/smbldap-tools/smbldap-tools.pem"

# key certificate to use to connect to the ldap server
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
clientkey="/etc/opt/IDEALX/smbldap-tools/smbldap-tools.key"

# LDAP Suffix
# Ex: suffix=dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG
suffix="dc=wisher,dc=com"

# Where are stored Users
# Ex: usersdn="ou=Users,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for usersdn
usersdn="ou=Users,${suffix}"

# Where are stored Computers
# Ex: computersdn="ou=Computers,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for computersdn
computersdn="ou=Computers,${suffix}"

# Where are stored Groups
# Ex: groupsdn="ou=Groups,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for groupsdn
groupsdn="ou=Groups,${suffix}"

# Where are stored Idmap entries (used if samba is a domain member server)
# Ex: groupsdn="ou=Idmap,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for idmapdn
idmapdn="ou=Idmap,${suffix}"

# Where to store next uidNumber and gidNumber available for new users and groups
# If not defined, entries are stored in sambaDomainName object.
# Ex: sambaUnixIdPooldn="sambaDomainName=${sambaDomain},${suffix}"
# Ex: sambaUnixIdPooldn="cn=NextFreeUnixId,${suffix}"
sambaUnixIdPooldn="sambaDomainName=wisher,${suffix}"

# Default scope Used
scope="sub"

# Unix password encryption (CRYPT, MD5, SMD5, SSHA, SHA, CLEARTEXT)
hash_encrypt="SSHA"

# if hash_encrypt is set to CRYPT, you may set a salt format.
# default is "%s", but many systems will generate MD5 hashed
# passwords if you use "$1$%.8s". This parameter is optional!
crypt_salt_format="%s"

##############################################################################
# 
# Unix Accounts Configuration
# 
##############################################################################

# Login defs
# Default Login Shell
# Ex: userLoginShell="/bin/bash"
userLoginShell="/bin/bash"

# Home directory
# Ex: userHome="/home/%U"
userHome="/ldaphome/%U"

# Default mode used for user homeDirectory
userHomeDirectoryMode="700"

# Gecos
userGecos="System User"

# Default User (POSIX and Samba) GID
defaultUserGid="513"

# Default Computer (Samba) GID
defaultComputerGid="515"idealx

# Skel dir
skeletonDir="/etc/skel"

# Default password validation time (time in days) Comment the next line if
# you don't want password to be enable for defaultMaxPasswordAge days (be
# careful to the sambaPwdMustChange attribute's value)
defaultMaxPasswordAge="45"

##############################################################################
#
# SAMBA Configuration
#
##############################################################################

# The UNC path to home drives location (%U username substitution)
# Just set it to a null string if you want to use the smb.conf 'logon home'
# directive and/or disable roaming profiles
# Ex: userSmbHome="\\PDC-SMB3\%U"
userSmbHome=

# The UNC path to profiles locations (%U username substitution)
# Just set it to a null string if you want to use the smb.conf 'logon path'
# directive and/or disable roaming profiles
# Ex: userProfile="\\PDC-SMB3\profiles\%U"
userProfile=

# The default Home Drive Letter mapping
# (will be automatically mapped at logon time if home directory exist)
# Ex: userHomeDrive="H:"
userHomeDrive=

# The default user netlogon script name (%U username substitution)
# if not used, will be automatically username.cmd
# make sure script file is edited under dos
# Ex: userScript="startup.cmd" # make sure script file is edited under dos
userScript=

# Domain appended to the users "mail"-attribute
# when smbldap-useradd -M is used
# Ex: mailDomain="idealx.com"
mailDomain="wisher.com"

##############################################################################
#
# SMBLDAP-TOOLS Configuration (default are ok for a RedHat)
#
##############################################################################

# Allows not to use smbpasswd (if with_smbpasswd == 0 in smbldap_conf.pm) but
# prefer Crypt::SmbHash library
with_smbpasswd="0"
smbpasswd="/usr/bin/smbpasswd"

# Allows not to use slappasswd (if with_slappasswd == 0 in smbldap_conf.pm)
# but prefer Crypt:: libraries
with_slappasswd="0"
slappasswd="/usr/sbin/slappasswd"

# comment out the following line to get rid of the default banner
# no_banner="1"
```

I would capitalize the SAMBA domain in the first file. See if that makes a difference.

Also, did you supply the LDAP admin password to SAMBA when we configured SAMBA?

Thanks,
Richard

### Post by timtodd on 2008-04-16
Ricky,

Have you considered adding ACL and extended attribute support in your next guide?

A reference on setting up ACL on Ubuntu:
[http://tlug.dnho.net/?q=node/171](http://tlug.dnho.net/?q=node/171)

To add the extended attribute support add "user_xattr" via fstab on the desired partition.

Some Samba ACL parameters:

dos filemode = yes.
Users with write access are able to assume ownership and modify the object's ACL.

map acl inherit = yes. 
Samba will remember an ACL's inherited properties and whether the ACL should be propagated to subfolders and files.

Tim

### Post by timtodd on 2008-04-16
Ah, cool.  A temporary fix!  Serv Tools will let you force password change on first login for the selected user(s).  Still need to figure out a scriptable way.  :)

[Edit]
A while later...
One command line method I found to add a domain group: [net rpc group add rickygroup -S dc01-ubuntu -Uroot%12345]

rickygroup = group to be added.
-S = servername
-U = user with priviledges to add domain group.  If the password is left off, you are prompted for a password which is masked out.

[Edit]
And even later...

### Post by timtodd on 2008-04-16
Hi Ringonian,

I think Ricky is referring to "Step 9: Configure SAMBA" of the original post (toward the very end of that step):
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=3952782&postcount=1](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=3952782&postcount=1)

The particular command is: smbpasswd -w 12345.  "12345" is the password you have been using throughout.  If you changed from "12345", make sure you use the same password you have been using.  If you have been using "12345", enter as given. 

Here is a quote from step 9:

> "Very important! We need to tell SAMBA what the "admin" password for the OpenLDAP server is. Hint: If you changed your "admin" password to be different from mine then you MUST replicate that change here! I guarantee you that someone will do this step and will have issues with SAMBA... this might be why!"

The populate command is in "Step 11: Populate LDAP using smbldap-tools."

Tim

### Post by timtodd on 2008-04-17
Meanwhile... Much Later... Back at the Farm... *Discovery* takes place.
 
**To force a password change (Could be scripted)**:
 
[INDENT]At location of choice create a file called modify_sambaPwdLastSet.

vim modify_sambaPwdLastSet.
 
Add these lines:
 
[INDENT]dn: uid=rman,ou=Users,dc=example,dc=local
changetype: modify
replace: sambaPwdLastSet
sambaPwdLastSet:0[/INDENT]
Now save and exit.

Execute this command: 

[INDENT]ldapmodify -D "cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local" -f modify_sambaPwdLastSet -x -w 12345[/INDENT]
The next time rman logs into winXP, he will be asked to change his password.  
For more information on the ldapmodify command: [http://docs.sun.com/source/816-6400-10/lmodify.html](http://docs.sun.com/source/816-6400-10/lmodify.html)[/INDENT]

**Next task**: Change user password, given user input (in a scriptable manner).  Any suggestions out there?

### Post by ringonian on 2008-04-17
> **timtodd said:**
> Questions are good.  Keep asking.  I thought you might have been working off a different guide, so I thought it would be good to precise. :)

Were you able to fix your problem?

hehe.  i like ricky's guide better.  but i can see how the password may not have been set and may have been overlooked (especially if you are copying and pasting from the guide to the shell.  it turns out i had to escape characters in my password:

$ smbpasswd -w 12;45

Setting stored password for ...
-bash: 45: command not found

should be:

$ smbpasswd -w 12\;45
Setting stored password for ...

### Post by timtodd on 2008-04-17
Ah, cool.  Nice that you figured it out.

And back at the proverbial farm...

A modified smbldap-passwd looks like it might do the trick:
[http://alex.ricobon.ro/wiki/index.php?title=How_to_make_a_script_that_adds_users_from_a_list](http://alex.ricobon.ro/wiki/index.php?title=How_to_make_a_script_that_adds_users_from_a_list)

Will have to test that tomorrow.  :)

[Edit]
Works very nicely...  :)

1) nano smbldap-passwd-mod.pl.
2) copy and paste (in nano, right-click to paste) code from the linked page above.
3) change permissions (chmod 700 smbldap-passwd-mod.pl).
4) ./smbldap-passwd-mod.pl ricky 56789.

changes ricky's password to 56789.

**Next task**: Work on automating share setup (in a scriptable manner).

```

```

> **timtodd said:**
> 
2) What permissions do you have on the /ldaphome and /joe directories?


On the server:

```

drwx------  2 joe Domain Users 4096 2008-04-11 18:00 joe

```
-```

drwxr-xr-x   3 root root  4096 2008-04-11 17:36 ldaphome

```

And on the client:
```
drwx------  2     30000       513 4096 2008-04-11 18:00 joe
```
```
drwxr-xr-x   3 root      root       4096 2008-04-11 17:36 ldaphome
```

> **timtodd said:**
> 
3) Also what does your smb.conf share for [ldaphome] look like?
nano /etc/samba/smb.conf.


I added this snippet from the guide:

```

# LDAPHOME share definition
[ldaphome]
path = /ldaphome
writeable = yes
browseable = yes
security mask = 0777
force security mode = 0
directory security mask = 0777
force directory security mode = 0

```

-Scott

### Post by kwisher on 2008-04-18
> **andguent said:**
> Richard,

Wonderful guide. Thanks for writing it. It is extremely easy to read and well laid out. I wish more people would include Appendix A & B: final conf files.

I have a few comments. If they have already been mentioned in previous responses, please ignore.

* slapd occasionally hangs at 6:25 in the morning due to logrotate pulling syslog out from under it via cron.daily. Logrotate pre/post scripts for syslog should be manually adjusted to stop/start slapd to avoid this issue. You may not see this issue until running slapd for a few months.
* db4.2-util is a highly recommended package (and/or similar version). If you restart slapd from command line after a fresh install, it usually mentions that recovery tools aren't available. Installing the db utils will allow slapd to repair its own ldap database.
* dnsmasq is a wonderful lightweight alternative to bind and dhcpd. Usually two or three lines of modification and you have a fully functional dns forwarding, dns cache, and dhcp server up and running.
* If you havin't found it already, do be aware of smbldap-usermod, It will allow you to set groups on ldap users.

I will have to read the entire thread in order to see if anything else jogs my memory. Again, wonderful guide.

I Seem to be having DNS problems with my sever. If I install dnsmasq, will I need to remove bind?

TIA

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-04-21
> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> wahey!

thank you so much :D it works perfectly setup in the place it shall be used.

i have 2 client machines setup with windows 2000 running on them :) which is perfect for what i want to use it for and is setup in exactly the same way as xp is.


i now, however, have a few questions!

You're welcome! Questions are good and I will do my best to answer them.

> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
no access to control panel or C: drive of the client machines

Combination of group policy and registry entries. Mainly group policy. Unfortunately, with a Linux domain controller, you will not be able to manage group policy for the entire domain from the server. You'll have to enact group policies on each workstation locally.

> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
have access to a shared network drive (possibly new partition? but most likely a seperate HDD in the server)

You can script this just like we did in the allusers.bat file. The "net use" command to map the H: drive can be replicated to map another shared drive. Just follow the syntax and change the drive letter and the share.

> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
limited access to start menu buttons (e.g. cannot use the "run" button)

Group policy again.

> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
make all users have the same background image, not allowed to change it

Group policy and/or mandatory profiles.

> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
not allowed to install programs etc

Don't add domain users to the local workstation. Keep them as roaming domain users and, by default, they should not be able to install software or make system wide changes. This is standard behavior for Microsoft operating systems.

> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
not allowed to fiddle with any settings on the computers that they shouldn't basically!

As a simple user this should be covered. They can always break their profile on the computer but this should not affect others.

> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
have a "log off" button on the desktop or on the start menu (may well just put a shortcut to the log off on the desktop)

Group policy. Or right click Start Menu > Properties > Look for the option in one of the menus.

> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
possibly block access to certain websites (although this could be acheived through the router)

You'll want to enact a separate proxy server and/or filter in your router. Much easier.


> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
i'm presuming that it needs to be done in the "allusers.bat" file that we made, although i may well be wrong!!

Only some stuff can be modified using a start up script. The rest you'll be looking for in Group Policy. I highly recommend learning more about it.

> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> 
ohhh and also, i need it so that i can have my own user which is admin. or, can i login to the windows client as "sysadmin"? and will i then be affected by these changes?
The user "root" exists in LDAP. This user is a member of the "Domain Admins" group and, as such, is an administrator on all the domain workstations.

I hope that this points you in the correct direction!

Sincerely,
Richard

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-04-21
> **sricketts said:**
> I have my server and client (ubuntu) set up, with one ldap user (joe). On the client, the /ldaphome/joe directory gets mounted, but I can't access it, even with root. The owner of the directory is joe, but joe is not a user on the client OS. 

This example exposes a major confusion I have. There seem to be two user spaces, one is the local user space, and the other is the ldap user space. So for each user that I add to the ldap space, how should I add this user locally to the client so that authentication and permissions work properly?

Thanks,
Scott

You are correct, there are two storage locations for user home directories.

/home is for LOCAL users on the Linux workstation or server.
/ldaphome is for LDAP users.

As long as the Linux workstation or server, or the Windows workstation or server, is a member of the Linux domain, LDAP users will be able to access their /ldaphome directory. Assuming, of course, that you configured the workstation or server correctly.

You do not need to add an LDAP user to the local passwd file. The whole point of using LDAP is to NOT need to use that file for authentication control.

I hope this helps!

Sincerely,
Richard

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-04-21
> **kwisher said:**
> Is anyone using a Linksys or other router with this PDC project? 

Yes, I use Linksys routers. I've configured this guide in various environments.

> **kwisher said:**
> 
I have tried reconfiguring it in different ways trying to resolve DNS issues without any luck. I have disabled Dynamic DNS, could this be the problem? 

Dynamic DNS on your router is generally for syncing your WAN IP with a hostname hosted with, say, DynDNS.org. 

> **kwisher said:**
> 
This was disabled before I started the project. I have DHCP toggled on but my server's IP is outside this range. I can ping the sever from another machine with it's IP but not it's host name. 

Any computer in your LAN that is getting a DHCP address from your router is getting a DNS entry from your router. It either points to the router or to your ISPs DNS servers. This is perfectly normal.

> **kwisher said:**
> 
This is my third time through Richard's how-to. This last time I have followed the instructions exactly verbose. Everything seems to be working except for the DNS issue. I have double checked everything including reading all 500+ posts to this thread.

Any advise would be greatly appreciated.
For the configuration of this project I highly recommend using STATIC IPs on your server and clients. Follow my configuration for BIND and you'll have a working DNS server on your network that your clients will be talking to. Eventually you'd be able to disable DHCP on the router and enable DHCP on the server and ALL computers in your house will be able to resolve these names using DHCP. For the time being, however, you will need to use STATIC IPs or else manually input the DNS servers on your client.

The problem, from what I can see, is that hostnames are not resolving. This is normal when you are not talking to the correct DNS server.

I hope that this points you in the right direction!

Sincerely,
Richard

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-04-22
> **Wickednix said:**
> Sighs, I guess that what i get for reading only 50 pages of this thread. :P


Question, I have to reboot my server 3 -4 times before I can get a login for the sysadmin account, Do you know what is causing this hangup?


Other then that question I can get my user to log in, now to see if I can get a workstation to join the domain...


Cheers

You are most likely suffering from the hung system logger that is waiting for slapd to start. The remedy is to change service start up files so that slapd starts first. This can be done in the /etc/rc2.d/ directory.

Change the slapd filename to a lower number so that it is first in the lineup.

-Richard

> **Wickednix said:**

Did you want to enable roaming profiles? It sounds like you got them half working. My SAMBA configuration explicitly disables roaming profiles because they cause way too many problems. 

The login script not working could also be related to a problem with your SAMBA configuration. We'll need to see /etc/samba/smb.conf and also /etc/smbldap-tools/ conf files.

-Richard

It looks like it is resolving just fine. The "Can't find server name for xxxx" means that reverse DNS is not working, because in this version of the guide I did not configure it.

Can you ping "dc01-ubuntu"? Can you ping "dc01-ubuntu.example.local"? If you can ping the second, but not the first, then you just need to configure your client with the correct DNS suffix.

-Richard

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-04-24
> **kwisher said:**
> I can ping the server as you described. I seen where to set this but I didn't understand it. What exactly is the suffix? Please excuse my ignorance.

DNS suffix refers to the suffix of the DNS name.

For example - you have the fully qualified domain name of server.**example.local** - the suffix would be **example.local**.

So, in Windows, where you find the "DNS Suffix" option you would enter "example.local".

I hope this helps!

-Richard
