```

> **rickyjones said:**
> 
Can you post /etc/ldap.conf?


On server:

```
##
## Configuration of this file will be managed by debconf as long as the
## first line of the file says '###DEBCONF###'
##
## You should use dpkg-reconfigure to configure this file via debconf
##

#
# @(#)$Id: ldap.conf,v 1.38 2006/05/15 08:13:31 lukeh Exp $
#
# This is the configuration file for the LDAP nameservice
# switch library and the LDAP PAM module.
#
# PADL Software
# http://www.padl.com
#

# Your LDAP server. Must be resolvable without using LDAP.
# Multiple hosts may be specified, each separated by a 
# space. How long nss_ldap takes to failover depends on
# whether your LDAP client library supports configurable
# network or connect timeouts (see bind_timelimit).
host 127.0.0.1

# The distinguished name of the search base.
base dc=gateway,dc=2wire,dc=net

# Another way to specify your LDAP server is to provide an
# uri with the server name. This allows to use
# Unix Domain Sockets to connect to a local LDAP Server.
uri ldap://127.0.0.1/
#uri ldaps://127.0.0.1/   
#uri ldapi://%2fvar%2frun%2fldapi_sock/
# Note: %2f encodes the '/' used as directory separator

# The LDAP version to use (defaults to 3
# if supported by client library)
#ldap_version 3

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with.
# Optional: default is to bind anonymously.
#binddn cn=proxyuser,dc=padl,dc=com

# The credentials to bind with. 
# Optional: default is no credential.
#bindpw secret

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with
# if the effective user ID is root. Password is
# stored in /etc/ldap.secret (mode 600)
rootbinddn cn=admin,dc=gateway,dc=2wire,dc=net

# The port.
# Optional: default is 389.
#port 389

# The search scope.
#scope sub
#scope one
#scope base

# Search timelimit
#timelimit 30

# Bind/connect timelimit
#bind_timelimit 30

# Reconnect policy: hard (default) will retry connecting to
# the software with exponential backoff, soft will fail
# immediately.
#bind_policy hard
bind_policy soft

# Idle timelimit; client will close connections
# (nss_ldap only) if the server has not been contacted
# for the number of seconds specified below.
#idle_timelimit 3600

# Filter to AND with uid=%s
#pam_filter objectclass=account

# The user ID attribute (defaults to uid)
#pam_login_attribute uid

# Search the root DSE for the password policy (works
# with Netscape Directory Server)
#pam_lookup_policy yes

# Check the 'host' attribute for access control
# Default is no; if set to yes, and user has no
# value for the host attribute, and pam_ldap is
# configured for account management (authorization)
# then the user will not be allowed to login.
#pam_check_host_attr yes

# Check the 'authorizedService' attribute for access
# control
# Default is no; if set to yes, and the user has no
# value for the authorizedService attribute, and
# pam_ldap is configured for account management
# (authorization) then the user will not be allowed
# to login.
#pam_check_service_attr yes

# Group to enforce membership of
#pam_groupdn cn=PAM,ou=Groups,dc=padl,dc=com

# Group member attribute
#pam_member_attribute uniquemember

# Specify a minium or maximum UID number allowed
#pam_min_uid 0
#pam_max_uid 0

# Template login attribute, default template user
# (can be overriden by value of former attribute
# in user's entry)
#pam_login_attribute userPrincipalName
#pam_template_login_attribute uid
#pam_template_login nobody

# HEADS UP: the pam_crypt, pam_nds_passwd,
# and pam_ad_passwd options are no
# longer supported.
#
# Do not hash the password at all; presume
# the directory server will do it, if
# necessary. This is the default.
#pam_password clear

# Hash password locally; required for University of
# Michigan LDAP server, and works with Netscape
# Directory Server if you're using the UNIX-Crypt
# hash mechanism and not using the NT Synchronization
# service. 
#pam_password crypt

# Remove old password first, then update in
# cleartext. Necessary for use with Novell
# Directory Services (NDS)
#pam_password clear_remove_old
#pam_password nds

# RACF is an alias for the above. For use with
# IBM RACF
#pam_password racf

# Update Active Directory password, by
# creating Unicode password and updating
# unicodePwd attribute.
#pam_password ad

# Use the OpenLDAP password change
# extended operation to update the password.
#pam_password exop

# Redirect users to a URL or somesuch on password
# changes.
#pam_password_prohibit_message Please visit http://internal to change your password.

# RFC2307bis naming contexts
# Syntax:
# nss_base_XXX          base?scope?filter
# where scope is {base,one,sub}
# and filter is a filter to be &'d with the
# default filter.
# You can omit the suffix eg:
# nss_base_passwd       ou=People,
# to append the default base DN but this
# may incur a small performance impact.
#nss_base_passwd        ou=People,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_shadow        ou=People,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_group         ou=Group,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_hosts         ou=Hosts,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_services      ou=Services,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_networks      ou=Networks,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_protocols     ou=Protocols,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_rpc           ou=Rpc,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_ethers        ou=Ethers,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_netmasks      ou=Networks,dc=padl,dc=com?ne
#nss_base_bootparams    ou=Ethers,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_aliases       ou=Aliases,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_netgroup      ou=Netgroup,dc=padl,dc=com?one

# attribute/objectclass mapping
# Syntax:
#nss_map_attribute      rfc2307attribute        mapped_attribute
#nss_map_objectclass    rfc2307objectclass      mapped_objectclass

# configure --enable-nds is no longer supported.
# NDS mappings
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member

# Services for UNIX 3.5 mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount User
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount User
#nss_map_attribute uid msSFU30Name
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember msSFU30PosixMember
#nss_map_attribute userPassword msSFU30Password
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFU30HomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFUHomeDirectory
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup Group
#pam_login_attribute msSFU30Name
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# configure --enable-mssfu-schema is no longer supported.
# Services for UNIX 2.0 mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount User
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount user
#nss_map_attribute uid msSFUName
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember posixMember
#nss_map_attribute userPassword msSFUPassword
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFUHomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute shadowLastChange pwdLastSet
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup Group
#nss_map_attribute cn msSFUName
#pam_login_attribute msSFUName
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# RFC 2307 (AD) mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount user
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount user
#nss_map_attribute uid sAMAccountName
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory unixHomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute shadowLastChange pwdLastSet
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup group
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member
#pam_login_attribute sAMAccountName
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# configure --enable-authpassword is no longer supported
# AuthPassword mappings
#nss_map_attribute userPassword authPassword

# AIX SecureWay mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount aixAccount
#nss_base_passwd ou=aixaccount,?one
#nss_map_attribute uid userName
#nss_map_attribute gidNumber gid
#nss_map_attribute uidNumber uid
#nss_map_attribute userPassword passwordChar
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup aixAccessGroup
#nss_base_group ou=aixgroup,?one
#nss_map_attribute cn groupName
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member
#pam_login_attribute userName
#pam_filter objectclass=aixAccount
#pam_password clear

# Netscape SDK LDAPS
#ssl on

# Netscape SDK SSL options
#sslpath /etc/ssl/certs

# OpenLDAP SSL mechanism
# start_tls mechanism uses the normal LDAP port, LDAPS typically 636
#ssl start_tls
#ssl on

# OpenLDAP SSL options
# Require and verify server certificate (yes/no)
# Default is to use libldap's default behavior, which can be configured in
# /etc/openldap/ldap.conf using the TLS_REQCERT setting.  The default for
# OpenLDAP 2.0 and earlier is "no", for 2.1 and later is "yes".
#tls_checkpeer yes

# CA certificates for server certificate verification
# At least one of these are required if tls_checkpeer is "yes"
#tls_cacertfile /etc/ssl/ca.cert
#tls_cacertdir /etc/ssl/certs

# Seed the PRNG if /dev/urandom is not provided
#tls_randfile /var/run/egd-pool

# SSL cipher suite
# See man ciphers for syntax
#tls_ciphers TLSv1

# Client certificate and key
# Use these, if your server requires client authentication.
#tls_cert
#tls_key

# Disable SASL security layers. This is needed for AD.
#sasl_secprops maxssf=0

# Override the default Kerberos ticket cache location.
#krb5_ccname FILE:/etc/.ldapcache

# SASL mechanism for PAM authentication - use is experimental
# at present and does not support password policy control
#pam_sasl_mech DIGEST-MD5

```

On client:

```

sricketts@blink2:~$ cat /etc/ldap.conf
###DEBCONF###
##
## Configuration of this file will be managed by debconf as long as the
## first line of the file says '###DEBCONF###'
##
## You should use dpkg-reconfigure to configure this file via debconf
##

#
# @(#)$Id: ldap.conf,v 1.38 2006/05/15 08:13:31 lukeh Exp $
#
# This is the configuration file for the LDAP nameservice
# switch library and the LDAP PAM module.
#
# PADL Software
# http://www.padl.com
#

# Your LDAP server. Must be resolvable without using LDAP.
# Multiple hosts may be specified, each separated by a 
# space. How long nss_ldap takes to failover depends on
# whether your LDAP client library supports configurable
# network or connect timeouts (see bind_timelimit).
#host 127.0.0.1
host dimE521.gateway.2wire.net

# The distinguished name of the search base.
#base dc=padl,dc=com
base dc=gateway,dc=2wire,dc=net

# Another way to specify your LDAP server is to provide an
# uri with the server name. This allows to use
# Unix Domain Sockets to connect to a local LDAP Server.
#uri ldap://127.0.0.1/
#uri ldaps://127.0.0.1/   
#uri ldapi://%2fvar%2frun%2fldapi_sock/
# Note: %2f encodes the '/' used as directory separator
uri ldap://gateway.2wire.net/

# The LDAP version to use (defaults to 3
# if supported by client library)
ldap_version 3

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with.
# Optional: default is to bind anonymously.
#binddn cn=proxyuser,dc=padl,dc=com

# The credentials to bind with. 
# Optional: default is no credential.
#bindpw secret

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with
# if the effective user ID is root. Password is
# stored in /etc/ldap.secret (mode 600)
rootbinddn cn=admin,dc=gateway,dc=2wire,dc=net

# The port.
# Optional: default is 389.
#port 389

# The search scope.
#scope sub
#scope one
#scope base

# Search timelimit
#timelimit 30

# Bind/connect timelimit
#bind_timelimit 30

# Reconnect policy: hard (default) will retry connecting to
# the software with exponential backoff, soft will fail
# immediately.
#bind_policy hard
bind_policy soft

# Idle timelimit; client will close connections
# (nss_ldap only) if the server has not been contacted
# for the number of seconds specified below.
#idle_timelimit 3600

# Filter to AND with uid=%s
#pam_filter objectclass=account

# The user ID attribute (defaults to uid)
#pam_login_attribute uid

# Search the root DSE for the password policy (works
# with Netscape Directory Server)
#pam_lookup_policy yes

# Check the 'host' attribute for access control
# Default is no; if set to yes, and user has no
# value for the host attribute, and pam_ldap is
# configured for account management (authorization)
# then the user will not be allowed to login.
#pam_check_host_attr yes

# Check the 'authorizedService' attribute for access
# control
# Default is no; if set to yes, and the user has no
# value for the authorizedService attribute, and
# pam_ldap is configured for account management
# (authorization) then the user will not be allowed
# to login.
#pam_check_service_attr yes

# Group to enforce membership of
#pam_groupdn cn=PAM,ou=Groups,dc=padl,dc=com

# Group member attribute
#pam_member_attribute uniquemember

# Specify a minium or maximum UID number allowed
#pam_min_uid 0
#pam_max_uid 0

# Template login attribute, default template user
# (can be overriden by value of former attribute
# in user's entry)
#pam_login_attribute userPrincipalName
#pam_template_login_attribute uid
#pam_template_login nobody

# HEADS UP: the pam_crypt, pam_nds_passwd,
# and pam_ad_passwd options are no
# longer supported.
#
# Do not hash the password at all; presume
# the directory server will do it, if
# necessary. This is the default.
pam_password md5

# Hash password locally; required for University of
# Michigan LDAP server, and works with Netscape
# Directory Server if you're using the UNIX-Crypt
# hash mechanism and not using the NT Synchronization
# service. 
#pam_password crypt

# Remove old password first, then update in
# cleartext. Necessary for use with Novell
# Directory Services (NDS)
#pam_password clear_remove_old
#pam_password nds

# RACF is an alias for the above. For use with
# IBM RACF
#pam_password racf

# Update Active Directory password, by
# creating Unicode password and updating
# unicodePwd attribute.
#pam_password ad

# Use the OpenLDAP password change
# extended operation to update the password.
#pam_password exop

# Redirect users to a URL or somesuch on password
# changes.
#pam_password_prohibit_message Please visit http://internal to change your password.

# RFC2307bis naming contexts
# Syntax:
# nss_base_XXX          base?scope?filter
# where scope is {base,one,sub}
# and filter is a filter to be &'d with the
# default filter.
# You can omit the suffix eg:
# nss_base_passwd       ou=People,
# to append the default base DN but this
# may incur a small performance impact.
#nss_base_passwd        ou=People,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_shadow        ou=People,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_group         ou=Group,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_hosts         ou=Hosts,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_services      ou=Services,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_networks      ou=Networks,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_protocols     ou=Protocols,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_rpc           ou=Rpc,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_ethers        ou=Ethers,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_netmasks      ou=Networks,dc=padl,dc=com?ne
#nss_base_bootparams    ou=Ethers,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_aliases       ou=Aliases,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_netgroup      ou=Netgroup,dc=padl,dc=com?one

# attribute/objectclass mapping
# Syntax:
#nss_map_attribute      rfc2307attribute        mapped_attribute
#nss_map_objectclass    rfc2307objectclass      mapped_objectclass

# configure --enable-nds is no longer supported.
# NDS mappings
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member

# Services for UNIX 3.5 mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount User
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount User
#nss_map_attribute uid msSFU30Name
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember msSFU30PosixMember
#nss_map_attribute userPassword msSFU30Password
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFU30HomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFUHomeDirectory
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup Group
#pam_login_attribute msSFU30Name
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# configure --enable-mssfu-schema is no longer supported.
# Services for UNIX 2.0 mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount User
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount user
#nss_map_attribute uid msSFUName
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember posixMember
#nss_map_attribute userPassword msSFUPassword
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFUHomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute shadowLastChange pwdLastSet
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup Group
#nss_map_attribute cn msSFUName
#pam_login_attribute msSFUName
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# RFC 2307 (AD) mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount user
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount user
#nss_map_attribute uid sAMAccountName
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory unixHomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute shadowLastChange pwdLastSet
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup group
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member
#pam_login_attribute sAMAccountName
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# configure --enable-authpassword is no longer supported
# AuthPassword mappings
#nss_map_attribute userPassword authPassword

# AIX SecureWay mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount aixAccount
#nss_base_passwd ou=aixaccount,?one
#nss_map_attribute uid userName
#nss_map_attribute gidNumber gid
#nss_map_attribute uidNumber uid
#nss_map_attribute userPassword passwordChar
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup aixAccessGroup
#nss_base_group ou=aixgroup,?one
#nss_map_attribute cn groupName
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member
#pam_login_attribute userName
#pam_filter objectclass=aixAccount
#pam_password clear

# Netscape SDK LDAPS
#ssl on

# Netscape SDK SSL options
#sslpath /etc/ssl/certs

# OpenLDAP SSL mechanism
# start_tls mechanism uses the normal LDAP port, LDAPS typically 636
#ssl start_tls
#ssl on

# OpenLDAP SSL options
# Require and verify server certificate (yes/no)
# Default is to use libldap's default behavior, which can be configured in
# /etc/openldap/ldap.conf using the TLS_REQCERT setting.  The default for
# OpenLDAP 2.0 and earlier is "no", for 2.1 and later is "yes".
#tls_checkpeer yes

# CA certificates for server certificate verification
# At least one of these are required if tls_checkpeer is "yes"
#tls_cacertfile /etc/ssl/ca.cert
#tls_cacertdir /etc/ssl/certs

# Seed the PRNG if /dev/urandom is not provided
#tls_randfile /var/run/egd-pool

# SSL cipher suite
# See man ciphers for syntax
#tls_ciphers TLSv1

# Client certificate and key
# Use these, if your server requires client authentication.
#tls_cert
#tls_key

# Disable SASL security layers. This is needed for AD.
#sasl_secprops maxssf=0

# Override the default Kerberos ticket cache location.
#krb5_ccname FILE:/etc/.ldapcache

# SASL mechanism for PAM authentication - use is experimental
# at present and does not support password policy control
#pam_sasl_mech DIGEST-MD5

##
## Configuration of this file will be managed by debconf as long as the
## first line of the file says '###DEBCONF###'
##
## You should use dpkg-reconfigure to configure this file via debconf
##

#
# @(#)$Id: ldap.conf,v 1.38 2006/05/15 08:13:31 lukeh Exp $
#
# This is the configuration file for the LDAP nameservice
# switch library and the LDAP PAM module.
#
# PADL Software
# http://www.padl.com
#

# Your LDAP server. Must be resolvable without using LDAP.
# Multiple hosts may be specified, each separated by a 
# space. How long nss_ldap takes to failover depends on
# whether your LDAP client library supports configurable
# network or connect timeouts (see bind_timelimit).
host 127.0.0.1

# The distinguished name of the search base.
base dc=gateway,dc=2wire,dc=net

# Another way to specify your LDAP server is to provide an
# uri with the server name. This allows to use
# Unix Domain Sockets to connect to a local LDAP Server.
uri ldap://127.0.0.1/
#uri ldaps://127.0.0.1/   
#uri ldapi://%2fvar%2frun%2fldapi_sock/
# Note: %2f encodes the '/' used as directory separator

# The LDAP version to use (defaults to 3

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with.
# Optional: default is to bind anonymously.
#binddn cn=proxyuser,dc=padl,dc=com

# The credentials to bind with. 
# Optional: default is no credential.
#bindpw secret

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with
# if the effective user ID is root. Password is
# stored in /etc/ldap.secret (mode 600)
rootbinddn cn=admin,dc=gateway,dc=2wire,dc=net

# The port.
# Optional: default is 389.
#port 389

# The search scope.
#scope sub
#scope one
#scope base

# Search timelimit
#timelimit 30

# Bind/connect timelimit
#bind_timelimit 30

# Reconnect policy: hard (default) will retry connecting to
# the software with exponential backoff, soft will fail
# immediately.
#bind_policy hard
bind_policy soft

# Idle timelimit; client will close connections
# (nss_ldap only) if the server has not been contacted
# for the number of seconds specified below.
#idle_timelimit 3600

# Filter to AND with uid=%s
#pam_filter objectclass=account

# The user ID attribute (defaults to uid)
#pam_login_attribute uid

# Search the root DSE for the password policy (works
# with Netscape Directory Server)
#pam_lookup_policy yes

# Check the 'host' attribute for access control
# Default is no; if set to yes, and user has no
# value for the host attribute, and pam_ldap is
# configured for account management (authorization)
# then the user will not be allowed to login.
#pam_check_host_attr yes

# Check the 'authorizedService' attribute for access
# control
# Default is no; if set to yes, and the user has no
# value for the authorizedService attribute, and
# pam_ldap is configured for account management
# (authorization) then the user will not be allowed
# to login.
#pam_check_service_attr yes

# Group to enforce membership of

# Group member attribute
#pam_member_attribute uniquemember

# Specify a minium or maximum UID number allowed
#pam_min_uid 0
#pam_max_uid 0

# Template login attribute, default template user
# (can be overriden by value of former attribute
# in user's entry)
#pam_login_attribute userPrincipalName
#pam_template_login_attribute uid
#pam_template_login nobody

# HEADS UP: the pam_crypt, pam_nds_passwd,
# and pam_ad_passwd options are no
# longer supported.
#
# Do not hash the password at all; presume
# the directory server will do it, if
# necessary. This is the default.
#pam_password clear

# Hash password locally; required for University of
# Michigan LDAP server, and works with Netscape
# Directory Server if you're using the UNIX-Crypt
# hash mechanism and not using the NT Synchronization
# service. 
#pam_password crypt

# Remove old password first, then update in
# cleartext. Necessary for use with Novell
# Directory Services (NDS)
#pam_password clear_remove_old
#pam_password nds

# RACF is an alias for the above. For use with
# IBM RACF
#pam_password racf

# Update Active Directory password, by
# creating Unicode password and updating
# unicodePwd attribute.
#pam_password ad

# Use the OpenLDAP password change

#pam_password exop

# Redirect users to a URL or somesuch on password

#pam_password_prohibit_message Please visit http://internal to change your password.

# RFC2307bis naming contexts

# attribute/objectclass mapping

#nss_map_attribute uid msSFUName
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember posixMember
#nss_map_attribute userPassword msSFUPassword

#nss_map_attribute cn msSFUName

# RFC 2307 (AD) mappings

#pam_groupdn cn=PAM,ou=Groups,dc=padl,dc=com

# Group member attribute
#pam_member_attribute uniquemember

# Specify a minium or maximum UID number allowed
#pam_min_uid 0
#pam_max_uid 0

# Template login attribute, default template user
# (can be overriden by value of former attribute
# in user's entry)
#pam_login_attribute userPrincipalName
#pam_template_login_attribute uid
#pam_template_login nobody

# HEADS UP: the pam_crypt, pam_nds_passwd,
# and pam_ad_passwd options are no
# longer supported.
#
# Do not hash the password at all; presume
# the directory server will do it, if
# necessary. This is the default.
pam_password md5

# Hash password locally; required for University of
# Michigan LDAP server, and works with Netscape
# Directory Server if you're using the UNIX-Crypt
# hash mechanism and not using the NT Synchronization
# service. 
#pam_password crypt

# Remove old password first, then update in
# cleartext. Necessary for use with Novell
# Directory Services (NDS)
#pam_password clear_remove_old
#pam_password nds

# RACF is an alias for the above. For use with
# IBM RACF
#pam_password racf

# Update Active Directory password, by
# creating Unicode password and updating
# unicodePwd attribute.
#pam_password ad

# Use the OpenLDAP password change
# extended operation to update the password.
#pam_password exop

# Redirect users to a URL or somesuch on password
# changes.
#pam_password_prohibit_message Please visit http://internal to change your password.

# RFC2307bis naming contexts
# Syntax:
# nss_base_XXX          base?scope?filter
# where scope is {base,one,sub}
# and filter is a filter to be &'d with the
# default filter.
# You can omit the suffix eg:
# nss_base_passwd       ou=People,
# to append the default base DN but this
# may incur a small performance impact.
#nss_base_passwd        ou=People,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_shadow        ou=People,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_group         ou=Group,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_hosts         ou=Hosts,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_services      ou=Services,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_networks      ou=Networks,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_protocols     ou=Protocols,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_rpc           ou=Rpc,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_ethers        ou=Ethers,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_netmasks      ou=Networks,dc=padl,dc=com?ne
#nss_base_bootparams    ou=Ethers,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_aliases       ou=Aliases,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_netgroup      ou=Netgroup,dc=padl,dc=com?one

# attribute/objectclass mapping
# Syntax:
#nss_map_attribute      rfc2307attribute        mapped_attribute
#nss_map_objectclass    rfc2307objectclass      mapped_objectclass

# configure --enable-nds is no longer supported.
# NDS mappings
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member

# Services for UNIX 3.5 mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount User
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount User
#nss_map_attribute uid msSFU30Name
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember msSFU30PosixMember
#nss_map_attribute userPassword msSFU30Password
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFU30HomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFUHomeDirectory
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup Group
#pam_login_attribute msSFU30Name
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# configure --enable-mssfu-schema is no longer supported.
# Services for UNIX 2.0 mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount User
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount user
#nss_map_attribute uid msSFUName
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember posixMember
#nss_map_attribute userPassword msSFUPassword
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFUHomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute shadowLastChange pwdLastSet
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup Group
#nss_map_attribute cn msSFUName
#pam_login_attribute msSFUName
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# RFC 2307 (AD) mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount user
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount user
#nss_map_attribute uid sAMAccountName
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory unixHomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute shadowLastChange pwdLastSet
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup group
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member
#pam_login_attribute sAMAccountName
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# configure --enable-authpassword is no longer supported
# AuthPassword mappings
#nss_map_attribute userPassword authPassword

# AIX SecureWay mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount aixAccount
#nss_base_passwd ou=aixaccount,?one
#nss_map_attribute uid userName
#nss_map_attribute gidNumber gid
#nss_map_attribute uidNumber uid
#nss_map_attribute userPassword passwordChar
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup aixAccessGroup
#nss_base_group ou=aixgroup,?one
#nss_map_attribute cn groupName
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member
#pam_login_attribute userName
#pam_filter objectclass=aixAccount
#pam_password clear

# Netscape SDK LDAPS
#ssl on

# Netscape SDK SSL options
#sslpath /etc/ssl/certs

# OpenLDAP SSL mechanism
# start_tls mechanism uses the normal LDAP port, LDAPS typically 636
#ssl start_tls
#ssl on

# OpenLDAP SSL options
# Require and verify server certificate (yes/no)
# Default is to use libldap's default behavior, which can be configured in
# /etc/openldap/ldap.conf using the TLS_REQCERT setting.  The default for
# OpenLDAP 2.0 and earlier is "no", for 2.1 and later is "yes".
#tls_checkpeer yes

# CA certificates for server certificate verification
# At least one of these are required if tls_checkpeer is "yes"
#tls_cacertfile /etc/ssl/ca.cert
#tls_cacertdir /etc/ssl/certs

# Seed the PRNG if /dev/urandom is not provided
#tls_randfile /var/run/egd-pool

# SSL cipher suite
# See man ciphers for syntax
#tls_ciphers TLSv1

# Client certificate and key
# Use these, if your server requires client authentication.
#tls_cert
#tls_key

# Disable SASL security layers. This is needed for AD.
#sasl_secprops maxssf=0

# Override the default Kerberos ticket cache location.
#krb5_ccname FILE:/etc/.ldapcache

# SASL mechanism for PAM authentication - use is experimental
# at present and does not support password policy control
#pam_sasl_mech DIGEST-MD5

```

On the client I see:
```

# Another way to specify your LDAP server is to provide an
# uri with the server name. This allows to use
# Unix Domain Sockets to connect to a local LDAP Server.
#uri ldap://127.0.0.1/
#uri ldaps://127.0.0.1/   
#uri ldapi://%2fvar%2frun%2fldapi_sock/
# Note: %2f encodes the '/' used as directory separator
uri ldap://gateway.2wire.net/

```

Try making it:
```

# Another way to specify your LDAP server is to provide an
# uri with the server name. This allows to use
# Unix Domain Sockets to connect to a local LDAP Server.
#uri ldap://127.0.0.1/
#uri ldaps://127.0.0.1/   
#uri ldapi://%2fvar%2frun%2fldapi_sock/
# Note: %2f encodes the '/' used as directory separator
uri ldap://dimE521.gateway.2wire.net/

```

See if that does anything.

### Post by kwisher on 2008-04-27
Hello All,

My comments below are not intended to be taken in a negative way against Richard or anyone else who has contributed helpful info in this post. Even though I was not successful getting this to work following Richard's how-to, I learned [COLOR="Red"]***_A LOT_***[/COLOR] of useful info about Linux and servers.

I believe I have found a simpler solution to creating a Linux Primary Domain Controller. Please visit the link below.

[HTML]http://wiki.contribs.org/Main_Page[/HTML]

After trying to use this how-to unsuccessfully on five different occasions, I did some searching for another solution and found the link above for SME Server. I successfully had this server set-up today in about 2 hours.

In one of Richard's previous posts he was discussing how Ubuntu should step up with a solution to a PDC server and how easy getting a MicroSoft server running in about 2 hours was. I believe SME Server covers this solution quite well. Ubuntu should check this out as well.

Installing and configuring SME Server could not have been easier. There is no editing of any config files, just simple selections done via a screen menu. After the initial setup you then access the server from any client with a web browser and change your required settings there.

I will post back later to let everyone know how SME Server is working.

> **fredEbay said:**

You replicated the 12345 pw all thru, but during initial OS install you used sysadmin but not in the rest of the tutorial - could this have any bearing on the few of us who are experiencing the error returned when running 'net gelocalsid'?

I am getting the SID, but only after the following:

":~# net getlocalsid 
[2008/04/27 17:45:57, 0] lib/smbldap.c:smbldap_connect_system(982)
  failed to bind to server ldap://localhost/ with dn="cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local" Error: Invalid credentials"

I too, used something other than 'sysadmin' (or even admin) at OS install, but maintained 'admin' for the entire process but I did use the exact same password for the OS install and the tutorial.

Great howto by the way, have gone thru it 3 times now just to get a good feel for it, about ready to put the server online in my local network - just a few things to fine tune.


As Richard states on one of the thread pages, this error is produced when you have one of the passwords out of sync somewhere through your steps. I got this too the first time I followed this howto. I wiped and started again and followed the howto line for line and now have a server in production use.

The sysadmin is the local user account on the box and the admin is the ldap user account to maintain your ldap users via phpldapadmin. This is the only spot that I have used the admin account that is listed in the howto. Ldap install uses manager as the user by default, I assume you can use any name there as long as you remember the username.

### Post by spliner on 2008-04-30
> **batibot said:**
> Hi there, 

I already have a working server(openldap+samba) and need to install another for backup, how do i implement a redundant server?

Any help please. 

Harvey


> **rickyjones said:**
> That I'm not sure of. I have not gotten that far in the tutorial yet.

-Richard

Actually that's on my list of things to get done as well, however I havn't gotten very far with it (time).  There are settings in the config files we edit in this tutorial that are for backup servers.  For instance, on an old NT 4 server you had to promote or demote servers to be BDC/PDC.  This is done (as far as I can tell) in the smb.conf file on a SAMBA server.  The default configuration file even has a little explanation if you take a closer look.  Now.. what else needs to be done, I am not certain.  Right now I'm relying on good backup policies and procedures, but will be creating a BDC myself later.  My suggestion is to use VMware and create a separate PDC, then attempt to create a BDC for testing purposes.  That is my plan rather than risk screwing up a working production PDC in the process.

If I had to guess, after learning everything I have from Rich in this thread, I would say a PDC can be configured to be a BDC by modifying the following line to read:

domain master = no

Beyond that, replication of your password database, among other things, will likely need to be done.  The majority of the other configs can be identical (I think) to what we've created from this tutorial.

Again, I have NOT tried this yet.  If you do however, please post your results here so that we can replicate it.  I will do the same once I find the time to get one created on my test domain.

-Spliner

### Post by tombutcher1990 on 2008-05-04
> **rickyjones said:**
> You're welcome! Questions are good and I will do my best to answer them.


Combination of group policy and registry entries. Mainly group policy. Unfortunately, with a Linux domain controller, you will not be able to manage group policy for the entire domain from the server. You'll have to enact group policies on each workstation locally.


You can script this just like we did in the allusers.bat file. The "net use" command to map the H: drive can be replicated to map another shared drive. Just follow the syntax and change the drive letter and the share.


Group policy again.


Group policy and/or mandatory profiles.


Don't add domain users to the local workstation. Keep them as roaming domain users and, by default, they should not be able to install software or make system wide changes. This is standard behavior for Microsoft operating systems.


As a simple user this should be covered. They can always break their profile on the computer but this should not affect others.


Group policy. Or right click Start Menu > Properties > Look for the option in one of the menus.


You'll want to enact a separate proxy server and/or filter in your router. Much easier.



Only some stuff can be modified using a start up script. The rest you'll be looking for in Group Policy. I highly recommend learning more about it.


The user "root" exists in LDAP. This user is a member of the "Domain Admins" group and, as such, is an administrator on all the domain workstations.

I hope that this points you in the correct direction!

Sincerely,
Richard

hey

much help, thanks very much

i found somewhere that said to edit a group policy on windows 2000, i have to use "gpedit.msc" in a run command.

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-05-06
> **tombutcher1990 said:**
> hey

much help, thanks very much

i found somewhere that said to edit a group policy on windows 2000, i have to use "gpedit.msc" in a run command.

i did this, edited what i wanted to, then went to "export". it exported to a txt document. only problem is, the txt document doesn't work (i was expecting to be able to put this on the server and apply it to all clients) and now i can't do anything to the computer because my Administrator user has the policy applied to it too! :( what do i do!

also, is there a way i can copy the policy onto all the machines? i presume i can just go to the directory of the file, copy it to my pen drive and paste it onto every machine yes?

You will not be able to distribute group policy from the server. At least not until the SAMBA team comes out with a way to do it via SAMBA. This is an inherent limitation with using a Linux domain controller.

From what I understand you'll need to make each change on each workstation. I don't believe this can be automated fully without a Microsoft domain controller.

Sorry about the grim news!

-Richard

---

### Post by rickyjones on 2008-05-06
> **sebastian18 said:**
> :popcorn: I guys!

First of all I want congradulate the creators of this super tutorial.
I've tested the server, and it is working ok.
But I have 1 questions:

I want to create a samba share, but a want to be access and modify , create files in that directory only from a specific computer who isn't in a samba domain or is local account(this computer)

Thank you!
Have a nice week!

Let me make sure I understand you.

You wish to create a shared folder on the server that:
a) Can only be accessed by a single computer.
b) This computer is not a member of the domain.
c) This user does not have an account on the domain.

Correct?

Solution is to create an account for the user. Create the share and only allow this account access to that share. On the client when they attempt to access the share they will be prompted for a username and password. Enter in the assigned username and password.

That should sufficiently work for you.

I hope this helps!

Sincerely,
Richard

I finally got a 8.04 desktop client to authenticate yesterday.  It turns out the settings for network manager weren't getting saved unless you stay in the login session.  This may have been been a bug in network manager's roaming mode, but I'm not sure. It wasn't until I ran the updates yesterday did everything get fixed.  Well, sort of.  As long as I log in locally initially, change hostname to dc-ubuntu.example.local add example.local to the search domain, save the settings - then I can authenticate against the directory  as long as I don't reboot the client.

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-05-26
> **Mrwasab1 said:**
> I didnt really understand the whole dc, cn, ou stuff...kinda got lost there... I'd suggest to "apt-get install lat" ... "lat" = LDAP Administration Tool. It's a nice LDAP browser, ie. you use "lat" to connect to your LDAP server (using your 'administrator' login, e.g. "cn=Administrator,dc=example,dc=com" ... or whatever you set the "dc=" parts to) and then you can browse the LDAP tree, e.g. see what all those values are, how they are organised hierarchically, and so on. You can also use it to remove values from the LDAP DB again (e.g. user or computer accounts) without having to mess with the command line.

As for LDAP lingo ....

dc = Domain Component, e.g. "example.org" is "dc=example,dc=org" in LDAP
cn = canonical name; can be used for mapping names to accounts, e.g. "cn=Administrator" usually is mapped to the LDAP super-user and whatever it is set to (could be "root" ... or "Manager", or "admin", or whatever the LDAP server in question has been configured with)

o = Organisation; can be used instead of "dc=" and vice versa.
ou = Organisational Unit; can be used together with "o=" instead of "dc=" and vice versa ... depending on what you want your LDAP tree to look like.

Maybe you wish that your LDAP server mirrors the DNS setup of your company? So if your official assigned domain name is "mycompany.com" then something such as "dc=mycompany,dc=com" is perfectly OK for you? 

But maybe it isn't and maybe your company is more complex and you need a lot finer distinction between its sub-units? Then you'd use something such as "o=mycompany,ou=Engineering" and/or "o=mycompany,ou=HumanResources" etc.

The "lat" tool will show you many things that go on inside your LDAP server, it will also show what all those abbreviations mean and what further attributes (e.g. phone numbers, addresses, departments, etc.) could be possibly assigned to entries. I highly recommend it.

One small warning though: "lat" has a menu option that says "Populate for SAMBA" .... just stay away from that one. The tutorial already covers this part and "populating" the server again with SAMBA entries will most likely hose your LDAP server ... so I'd suggest you don't use that button if you don't really have to. But other than that "lat" is pretty cool :)

### Post by errr on 2008-06-06
I was able to get my system setup using this guide on 8.04. I have also setup roaming profiles with my setup. One thing I did run into with regards to the roaming profiles was desktop.ini kept opening in notepad when my windows users would log in. I solved this by going to my profiles share and doing
```
find . -iname desktop.ini -exec rm '{}' \;
```
Im not sure if there was a better more correct way to get rid of this problem but it did work.

One other thing I am trying to do now is that this setup is great for a home, but in a production environment this needs to work with SSL. Any tips or anything to help get this going with SSL?

I have tried making a cert in /etc/ldap/ssl
```
 openssl req -newkey rsa:1024 -x509 -nodes -out server.pem -keyout server.pem -days 3650
```
Next I edited /etc/defaults/slapd and I added
```
SLAPD_SERVICES="ldaps://127.0.0.1/"
```
(I also tried using SLAPD_SERVICES="ldaps://127.0.0.1:636/")
Next in /etc/ldap/ldap.conf I changed the uri line
```
uri ldaps://127.0.0.1/
```
(I also tried using the uri ldaps://127.0.0.1:636/ line here too)
Next in /etc/ldap/slapd.conf I added
```
TLSCipherSuite HIGH:MEDIUM:-SSLv2
TLSCACertificateFile /etc/ldap/ssl/server.pem
TLSCertificateFile /etc/ldap/ssl/server.pem
TLSCertificateKeyFile /etc/ldap/ssl/server.pem
```
then I restarted slapd
```
=> /etc/init.d/slapd restart
Stopping OpenLDAP: slapd.
Starting OpenLDAP: slapd - failed.
The operation failed but no output was produced. For hints on what went
wrong please refer to the system's logfiles (e.g. /var/log/syslog) or
try running the daemon in Debug mode like via "slapd -d 16383" (warning:
this will create copious output).

Below, you can find the command line options used by this script to 
run slapd. Do not forget to specify those options if you
want to look to debugging output:
  slapd -h 'ldaps://127.0.0.1/' -g openldap -u openldap -f /etc/ldap/slapd.conf 

```
But as you can see it doesnt work. My syslog output shows this:
```

Jun  6 09:40:33 pdc01 slapd[8304]: @(#) $OpenLDAP: slapd 2.4.7 (Apr 29 2008 08:44:27) $ ^Ibuildd@rothera:/build/buildd/openldap2.3-2.4.7/debian/build/servers/slapd 
Jun  6 09:40:33 pdc01 slapd[8304]: main: TLS init def ctx failed: -1 
Jun  6 09:40:33 pdc01 slapd[8304]: slapd stopped. 
Jun  6 09:40:33 pdc01 slapd[8304]: connections_destroy: nothing to destroy.

### Post by errr on 2008-06-06
I have now got ldap running on both ssl and non ssl ports. I followed this: [http://www.openldap.org/pub/ksoper/OpenLDAP_TLS.html#4.0](http://www.openldap.org/pub/ksoper/OpenLDAP_TLS.html#4.0)
I did have to make some changes. Where in its example it used
```
TLSCipherSuite HIGH:MEDIUM:+SSLv2
TLSCACertificateFile /usr/var/openldap-data/cacert.pem
TLSCertificateFile /usr/var/openldap-data/servercrt.pem
TLSCertificateKeyFile /usr/var/openldap-data/serverkey.pem
```
I had to comment out the first line ```
#TLSCipherSuite HIGH:MEDIUM:+SSLv2
``` or I would get the error about the -1
I also changed the ownership of my certs to be owned by the openldap user
I still need to setup another couple of servers in vmware and make them clients and make client certs and make sure it all works. I will report back my findings.

NOTE: If you use that link I posted above please note that the location of the scripts it uses are not in the same place, and it is not a cut & paste guide like this guide was. You will need to just read it and follow the steps adjusting paths, and file locations as needed.
```
/usr/lib/ssl/misc/CA.sh
``` Is the location of the CA script on ubuntu 8.04

### Post by kmfdmz3 on 2008-06-09
> **chrispottrell said:**
> Hi guys,

I have the above configuration running w/ 30-40 clients, it's brilliant! Once again thanks a lot Ricky.

I have a quick question regarding shares and mount points.

I'm trying to discover the best method for administrating share points on the client machines, can this be handled at login via OpenLDAP at all w/ autofs?

I need to give the clients some mount points on their desktop so they all have the ability to reach some files, but also have the ability to change them at a later date without having to go around 40 Ubuntu boxes and changing them manually.

Is this possible?

I would say make some samba shares. It's essentially the same as when you created your ldaphomes share in samba. Read the samba documentation on how you want to restrict/grant access on said share. If you don't set up the mount manually on the client computer, they can access it by browsing the servers network shares.

### Post by kmfdmz3 on 2008-06-17
Hello iskatyel,

I resolved the issue. Make sure you set your krb5.keytab to root : openldap and (this is the one I forgot) chmod 0640 the file as well. That is what did it for me. Good luck. I successfully managed to get windows, linux and macs to all authenticate through my test server. Doing a howto on it as I go and will post it up. If you need anymore help post up here or PM me.

Mike

### Post by kcnnc on 2008-07-10
> **rickyjones said:**
> 
[QUOTE=scorp123;4184680]No problem :)

 You see, NFS doesn't do any sort of authentication. You just mount it, and *poooof!* there it is. So having access nor not having access to something on a NFS share is defined on the filesystem level itself. So if you on your local machine have a user ID uid=1000 and group ID gid=1000 then when you mount a remote NFS share you will have instant and unrestricted access to anything that has these same uid and gid bits (uid=1000, gid=1000). So for NFS it makes a lot of sense if a user always has the same uid and gid values, this avoids weird problems when for example one file share works from one system but it doesn't work from another system (e.g. because the uid= and gid= combo are different there).

The problems start when "root" accesses a NFS share. Root's powers are infinite, so by definition anyone having a uid=0 and gid=0 should be "root" and be allowed unrestricted access to anything. That might indeed be a system administrator somewhere somehow. But it might also be an overly eager user who just sudo'd into his own "root" account on his own client machine; or it might even be an intruder who somehow managed to sneak into your network. Unnecessary to say that this is dangerous! So NFS per default says: "OK, fine, everyone gets the same permissions as if they had all my files locally .. except "root"! Anyone claiming to be "root" gets no access at all!"

But with "no_root_squash" you're turning off this very option!

So NFS treats anyone claiming to have "root" rights on their own client machine as if they had "root" rights too on the entire file share!

With this option in place one client user could "sudo su -" into the "root" account, mount the /ldaphome share, and then manipulate all the other user's shares at will ... You probably don't want that :)

The only scenario where "no_root_squash" makes sense and where you have to use it is when you work with diskless clients, e.g. where all the filesystems such as /boot, /, /usr and so on have to be mounted from a remote server. This is often done in environments where they use clusters, e.g. only few of the machines really have disks, the rest boots off the network and all that is really needed are the powerful CPU's which do the number crunching, simulate nuke explosions, earth quakes, and so on.

But on a home folder share you should leave "root_squash" (the option which NFS would default to) intact and not enable it. :)
That right there makes perfect sense to me. Thank you for taking the time to explain that! Now if only all documentation read like that.

-Richard[/QUOTE]

After some investigation, I found that there might be a problem with turning off root_squash (no_root_squash) and pam_mkhomedir.so

When the user try to sign in first time without a home, the system tries to create the home folder. I presume this is running as root privilege since it has to create any user on /ldaphome.

This cause the cannot create directory error as commented in
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5342721#post5342721](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5342721#post5342721)

With * no_root_squash* added to the export option, the problem is fixed.

Since I'm no nfs expert, the only question is this fix an expected use of the option and is it secure in this use case?

# The userPassword by default can be changed
# by the entry owning it if they are authenticated.

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-07-23
A few minor additions ... "corrections" sort of.

for the "passwd" command to work **on client Linux computers** for both LDAP and their local users a few files need to be changed **on such a client** and ought to look like this:

/etc/nsswitch.conf: ```
passwd: ldap files
group: ldap files
shadow: ldap files

hosts:          files mdns4_minimal [NOTFOUND=return] dns mdns4
networks:       files

protocols:      db files
services:       db files
ethers:         db files
rpc:            db files

netgroup:       nis
```

/etc/pam.d/common-password:
```
password   sufficient   pam_unix.so nullok obscure md5 shadow
password   sufficient   pam_ldap.so
password   required     pam_deny.so
```

/etc/pam.d/common-account:
```
account    sufficient   pam_unix.so
account    sufficient   pam_ldap.so
account    required     pam_deny.so
```

/etc/pam.d/common-auth:
```
auth       required     pam_env.so
auth       sufficient   pam_unix.so likeauth nullok
auth       sufficient   pam_ldap.so use_first_pass
auth       required     pam_deny.so
```

/etc/pam.d/common-session:
```
session    required     pam_limits.so
session    required     pam_mkhomedir.so skel=/etc/skel/
session    required     pam_unix.so
session    optional     pam_ldap.so
```

After applying these little changes and rebooting the "passwd" command will work OK again for all local users and for LDAP users ... however: it won't change the SAMBA password. I am not sure why ...

For all password to be reliably changed: ssh to the server and use the "smbpasswd" program, e.g.:
```
ssh yourname@ldapserver "smbpasswd"
```

I guess if you make your Linux client computer a member of the SAMBA domain as well (... but why would I? I use LDAP + NFS on Linux ... ) you could use "smbldap-passwd" and such similar tools.

.
.
.
.

---

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-07-23
In my opinion there are a few errors in the guide, especially regarding the "open_ldap" auth-profile ...

According to the guide the file "/etc/auth-client-config/profile.d/open_ldap" should look like this:
```
[open_ldap]
nss_passwd=passwd: compat ldap
nss_group=group: compat ldap
nss_shadow=shadow: compat ldap
pam_auth=auth       required     pam_env.so
 auth       sufficient   pam_unix.so likeauth nullok
 auth       sufficient   pam_ldap.so use_first_pass
 auth       required     pam_deny.so
pam_account=account    sufficient   pam_unix.so
 account    sufficient   pam_ldap.so
 account    required     pam_deny.so
pam_password=password   sufficient   pam_unix.so nullok md5 shadow use_authtok
 password   sufficient   pam_ldap.so use_first_pass
 password   required     pam_deny.so
pam_session=session    required     pam_limits.so
 session    required     pam_mkhomedir.so skel=/etc/skel/ umask=0077
 session    required     pam_unix.so
 session    optional     pam_ldap.so
```

... but this introduces a few odd problems. Namely that the "passwd" program cannot be used any longer to change one's password.

In my opinion this would be correct (based on my findings in the previous posting above -- changes are marked in red): ```
[open_ldap]
nss_passwd=passwd: [COLOR="Red"]ldap files[/COLOR]
nss_group=group: [COLOR="Red"]ldap files[/COLOR]
nss_shadow=shadow: [COLOR="Red"]ldap files[/COLOR]
pam_auth=auth       required     pam_env.so
 auth       sufficient   pam_unix.so likeauth nullok
 auth       sufficient   pam_ldap.so use_first_pass
 auth       required     pam_deny.so
pam_account=account    sufficient   pam_unix.so
 account    sufficient   pam_ldap.so
 account    required     pam_deny.so
pam_password=[COLOR="Red"]password   sufficient   pam_unix.so nullok obscure md5 shadow[/COLOR]
 [COLOR="Red"]password   sufficient   pam_ldap.so[/COLOR]
 password   required     pam_deny.so
pam_session=session    required     pam_limits.so
 session    required     pam_mkhomedir.so skel=/etc/skel/ umask=0077
 session    required     pam_unix.so
 session    optional     pam_ldap.so
```

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-07-23
> **kcnnc said:**
> ... and is it secure in this use case? No, it's not.

Anyone having "root" on a client machine (e.g. a user with their own laptop) now has full "root" rights over the share as well, e.g. he can walk into any directory regardless of the permissions and manipulate anything as they please.

This was already explained here in postings #118 and #122:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4184300&postcount=118](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4184300&postcount=118)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4184680&postcount=122](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4184680&postcount=122)

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-07-24
> **rz6bm7 said:**
>  'failed to bind to server ldap://localhost/ with dn="cn=admin,dc=tredp,dc=local" Error: Invalid credentials (unknown) This happened to me too. In my case a typo in a config file was the cause. It's probably the same in your case.

BTW, as server OS we use Novell's "SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 10" now. Setting up LDAP + SAMBA is just a matter of a few mouse clicks there. The config files you end up with look very similar to those of this guide here. So from that point of view there is not a big difference. The really big difference is the time it takes to get there: here it could take hours whereas on SLES it just takes a few mouse clicks on the right spots and voila, you're done and ready to go.

It's a pity that Ubuntu and/or Canonical don't offer an easier way to achieve all this (as SLES does), e.g. a nice and friendly tool (can be text-based too ... for as long as it's usable!) that would help us avoid having to type so much :)

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-07-25
> **shizakapayou said:**
>  Case in point: in addition to users being unable to change passwords, Ubuntu clients won't even join the domain :(  Ironic, isn't it? Ubuntu (and any other Linux) client systems are not supposed join the domain ... you authenticate via LDAP (as per guide) and mount things via NFS, but you don't join the domain ... you don't need to. Or why would you then authenticate via LDAP and mount the /home directory via NFS? Having a Linux client in both mechanisms (LDAP/NFS + SAMBA) is quite redundant. If you want to do things exclusively via SAMBA then you could leave away the LDAP authentication part. There are guides on this forum and more can be found via Google which explain this, e.g. how to have a Linux box as a pure SAMBA client. But this is not what this guide was about. The cool thing about this guide is that once you're done and everything works you get a nice server that can handle "both worlds" (UNIX + Windows) and authenticate both with a mechanism native to them ...

The server is a different story ... as it has to work as source for authentication for both LDAP and SAMBA it needs to be in the domain as well (step #10 in the guide).

---

### Post by shizakapayou on 2008-07-25

If anyone has any suggestions for Windows users passwords, I'd love to hear it.  That should be about the only thing slowing me down now!

> **L-UniX! said:**
> thanks for the guide! but after step 13 i got trouble with the login.

"Enable the new OpenLDAP profile by running the following command. If you did all the previous steps correctly then this will run without issue."

Code: auth-client-config -a -p open_ldap

"The final step is to simply reboot the server. When the server is running again then test to see if you can log in with your new LDAP user. No matter what you should be able to log in with a local user (unless the system is hung). If the system hangs then reboot HARD and try again."
Code: reboot

dc-01.pro-direct-finance.com: steven (local user)
pw = test1234

after that the server hang...

the same problem...

i followed every step in this guide without any errors,
and i don't know why it doesn't work...

please help me out guys :(

Check this post: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5041812&postcount=637](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5041812&postcount=637)

I had the same problem (LDAP users could not login on clients or server), though I never had a problem with a local user on the server.  Make sure you've got

```
uri ldap://127.0.0.1/
```

instead of

```
uri ldapi://127.0.0.1/
```

Note the 'i' at the end of ldap in the second example.  That one letter was causing me a lot of pain.

### Post by L-UniX! on 2008-07-29
host 127.0.0.1
base dc=mydomain,dc=com
uri ldap://127.0.0.1/
ldap_version 3
rootbinddn cn=admin,dc=mydomain,dc=com
bind_policy soft


/etc/ldap/ldap.conf

###DEBCONF###
##
## Configuration of this file will be managed by debconf as long as the
## first line of the file says '###DEBCONF###'
##
## You should use dpkg-reconfigure to configure this file via debconf
##

#
# @(#)$Id: ldap.conf,v 1.38 2006/05/15 08:13:31 lukeh Exp $
#
# This is the configuration file for the LDAP nameservice
# switch library and the LDAP PAM module.
#
# PADL Software
# [http://www.padl.com](http://www.padl.com)
#

# Your LDAP server. Must be resolvable without using LDAP.
# Multiple hosts may be specified, each separated by a
# space. How long nss_ldap takes to failover depends on
# whether your LDAP client library supports configurable
# network or connect timeouts (see bind_timelimit).
host 127.0.0.1

# The distinguished name of the search base.
#base dc=padl,dc=com
base dc=mydomain,dc=com

# Another way to specify your LDAP server is to provide an
# uri with the server name. This allows to use
# Unix Domain Sockets to connect to a local LDAP Server.
uri ldap://127.0.0.1/
#uri ldaps://127.0.0.1/
#uri ldapi://%2fvar%2frun%2fldapi_sock/
# Note: %2f encodes the '/' used as directory separator

# The LDAP version to use (defaults to 3
# if supported by client library)
ldap_version 3

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with.
# Optional: default is to bind anonymously.
#binddn cn=admin,dc=mydomain,dc=com

# The credentials to bind with.
# Optional: default is no credential.
#bindpw secret

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with
# if the effective user ID is root. Password is
# stored in /etc/ldap.secret (mode 600)
rootbinddn cn=admin,dc=mydomain,dc=com

# The port.
# Optional: default is 389.
#port 389

# The search scope.
#scope sub
#scope one
#scope base

# Search timelimit
#timelimit 30

# Bind/connect timelimit
#bind_timelimit 30

# Reconnect policy: hard (default) will retry connecting to
# the software with exponential backoff, soft will fail
# immediately.
#bind_policy hard
bind_policy soft

# Idle timelimit; client will close connections
# (nss_ldap only) if the server has not been contacted
# for the number of seconds specified below.
#idle_timelimit 3600

# Filter to AND with uid=%s
#pam_filter objectclass=account

# The user ID attribute (defaults to uid)
#pam_login_attribute uid

# Search the root DSE for the password policy (works
# with Netscape Directory Server)
#pam_lookup_policy yes

# Check the 'host' attribute for access control
# Default is no; if set to yes, and user has no
# value for the host attribute, and pam_ldap is
# configured for account management (authorization)
# then the user will not be allowed to login.
#pam_check_host_attr yes

# Check the 'authorizedService' attribute for access
# control
# Default is no; if set to yes, and the user has no
# value for the authorizedService attribute, and
# pam_ldap is configured for account management
# (authorization) then the user will not be allowed
# to login.
#pam_check_service_attr yes

# Group to enforce membership of
#pam_groupdn cn=PAM,ou=Groups,dc=padl,dc=com

# Group member attribute
#pam_member_attribute uniquemember

# Specify a minium or maximum UID number allowed
#pam_min_uid 0
#pam_max_uid 0

# Template login attribute, default template user
# (can be overriden by value of former attribute
# in user's entry)
#pam_login_attribute userPrincipalName
#pam_template_login_attribute uid
#pam_template_login nobody

# HEADS UP: the pam_crypt, pam_nds_passwd,
# and pam_ad_passwd options are no
# longer supported.
#
# Do not hash the password at all; presume
# the directory server will do it, if
# necessary. This is the default.
pam_password md5

# Hash password locally; required for University of
# Michigan LDAP server, and works with Netscape
# Directory Server if you're using the UNIX-Crypt
# hash mechanism and not using the NT Synchronization
# service.
#pam_password crypt

# Remove old password first, then update in
# cleartext. Necessary for use with Novell
# Directory Services (NDS)
#pam_password clear_remove_old
#pam_password nds

# RACF is an alias for the above. For use with
# IBM RACF
#pam_password racf

# Update Active Directory password, by
# creating Unicode password and updating
# unicodePwd attribute.
#pam_password ad

# Use the OpenLDAP password change
# extended operation to update the password.
#pam_password exop

# Redirect users to a URL or somesuch on password
# changes.
#pam_password_prohibit_message Please visit [http://internal](http://internal) to change your password.

# RFC2307bis naming contexts
# Syntax:
# nss_base_XXX          base?scope?filter
# where scope is {base,one,sub}
# and filter is a filter to be &'d with the
# default filter.
# You can omit the suffix eg:
# nss_base_passwd       ou=People,
# to append the default base DN but this
# may incur a small performance impact.
#nss_base_passwd        ou=People,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_shadow        ou=People,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_group         ou=Group,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_hosts         ou=Hosts,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_services      ou=Services,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_networks      ou=Networks,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_protocols     ou=Protocols,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_rpc           ou=Rpc,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_ethers        ou=Ethers,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_netmasks      ou=Networks,dc=padl,dc=com?ne
#nss_base_bootparams    ou=Ethers,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_aliases       ou=Aliases,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_netgroup      ou=Netgroup,dc=padl,dc=com?one

# attribute/objectclass mapping
# Syntax:
#nss_map_attribute      rfc2307attribute        mapped_attribute
#nss_map_objectclass    rfc2307objectclass      mapped_objectclass

# configure --enable-nds is no longer supported.
# NDS mappings
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member

# Services for UNIX 3.5 mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount User
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount User
#nss_map_attribute uid msSFU30Name
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember msSFU30PosixMember
#nss_map_attribute userPassword msSFU30Password
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFU30HomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFUHomeDirectory
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup Group
#pam_login_attribute msSFU30Name
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# configure --enable-mssfu-schema is no longer supported.
# Services for UNIX 2.0 mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount User
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount user
#nss_map_attribute uid msSFUName
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember posixMember
#nss_map_attribute userPassword msSFUPassword
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFUHomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute shadowLastChange pwdLastSet
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup Group
#nss_map_attribute cn msSFUName
#pam_login_attribute msSFUName
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# RFC 2307 (AD) mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount user
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount user
#nss_map_attribute uid sAMAccountName
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory unixHomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute shadowLastChange pwdLastSet
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup group
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member
#pam_login_attribute sAMAccountName
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# configure --enable-authpassword is no longer supported
# AuthPassword mappings
#nss_map_attribute userPassword authPassword

# AIX SecureWay mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount aixAccount
#nss_base_passwd ou=aixaccount,?one
#nss_map_attribute uid userName
#nss_map_attribute gidNumber gid
#nss_map_attribute uidNumber uid
#nss_map_attribute userPassword passwordChar
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup aixAccessGroup
#nss_base_group ou=aixgroup,?one
#nss_map_attribute cn groupName
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member
#pam_login_attribute userName
#pam_filter objectclass=aixAccount
#pam_password clear

# Netscape SDK LDAPS
#ssl on

# Netscape SDK SSL options
#sslpath /etc/ssl/certs

# OpenLDAP SSL mechanism
# start_tls mechanism uses the normal LDAP port, LDAPS typically 636
#ssl start_tls
#ssl on

# OpenLDAP SSL options
# Require and verify server certificate (yes/no)
# Default is to use libldap's default behavior, which can be configured in
# /etc/openldap/ldap.conf using the TLS_REQCERT setting.  The default for
# OpenLDAP 2.0 and earlier is "no", for 2.1 and later is "yes".
#tls_checkpeer yes

# CA certificates for server certificate verification
# At least one of these are required if tls_checkpeer is "yes"
#tls_cacertfile /etc/ssl/ca.cert
#tls_cacertdir /etc/ssl/certs

# Seed the PRNG if /dev/urandom is not provided
#tls_randfile /var/run/egd-pool

# SSL cipher suite
# See man ciphers for syntax
#tls_ciphers TLSv1

# Client certificate and key
# Use these, if your server requires client authentication.
#tls_cert
#tls_key

# Disable SASL security layers. This is needed for AD.
#sasl_secprops maxssf=0

# Override the default Kerberos ticket cache location.
#krb5_ccname FILE:/etc/.ldapcache

# SASL mechanism for PAM authentication - use is experimental
# at present and does not support password policy control
#pam_sasl_mech DIGEST-MD5

I am using Ubuntu Server 8.04 and having an issue with the tutorial, during the openldap installation and configuration I have gotten to the second reconfigure stage and attempt to start openldap using:

```
/etc/init.d/slapd start
```

I then receive the following error stating that openldap has failed to start:

```
root@vervet:~# /etc/init.d/slapd start
Starting OpenLDAP: slapd - failed.

The operation failed but no output was produced. For hints on what went wrong please refer to the system's logfiles (e.g. /var/log/syslog) or try running the daemon in Debug mode like via "slapd -d 16383" (warning this will create copious output).

Below, you can find the command line options used by this script to run slapd. Do not forget to specify those options if you want to look to debugging output:
slapd -g openldap -u openldap -f /etc/ldap/slapd.conf

```

In the syslog the following error was produced:

```

Jul 30 16:53:43 vervet slapd[4255]: @(#) $OpenLDAP: slapd 2.4.9 (Jun 16 2008 08:09:52) $ ^Ibuildd@palmer:/build/buildd/openldap2.3-2.4.9/debian/build/servers/slapd

Jul 30 16:41:58 vervet slapd[4255]: /etc/ldap/slapd.conf: line 63: invalid path: Permission denied

Jul 30 16:41:58 vervet slapd[4255]: connections_destroy: nothing to destroy.

```

Line 63 refers to the directory parameter that above in the instructions told me to set to /ldap_data, which I did and does exist in my directory structure. The ldap_data directory has the following permissions which were also set earlier in the instructions:

```

drwxr-xr-x  2 openldap openldap ... ldap_data

```

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-07-31
> **chris2100 said:**
>  Is this normal that I cant login locally with an ldap user?   Nope, this is not normal. Did you follow the guide to the letter ... or did you deviate from it here and there? What I noticed is that you have to watch out with the user ID's + group ID's ... You have to make sure there are no duplicate ID numbers between the local config and the LDAP server config, otherwise there could be strange side-effects.

On my system I chose ID's of 5000+ and upwards for anything LDAP; user and group ID's of 4999 and below are always local. LDAP users are never ever members of a local group. The "adm" (gid=4; as per Ubuntu installer) and "admin" (gid=115; as per Ubuntu installer) groups are local, so IMHO a LDAP user should not be a member of such a group. I might be wrong about this ... But it seems to work better this way.

On each Linux client which I installed I created a "sysadmin" user as first (and only local) user; the user is therefore always local (= there is no such user in LDAP), always has uid=1000 and gid=1000 and is always in the "admin" group ... and that on each system. I also moved the home directory from "/home/sysadmin" to "/sysadmin" to make sure that this user's login would work even when the NFS server should be down.

As I see it this has the advantage that even if the LDAP + NFS should all of a sudden not be available I would still be able to login and perform admin tasks because "sysadmin" authenticates locally without LDAP and all settings are kept away from "/home" (which I mount via NFS from our storage system).

Other things I did: I changed the ownership of the "su" and "sudo" binaries so that now only the local "sysadmin" user can execute them. Maybe this is a little "overkill" but we saw this as being desirable as otherwise people might keep trying "funny things" with these two programs. Now if they try they get a "Access denied" error right away and don't fill my system logs anymore with useless "sudo su -" and "su - root" attempts.

---

### Post by shizakapayou on 2008-07-31
> **chris2100 said:**
> I have a quick question that I hope has a quick answer.  I followed this follow through and have an ldap server running seemingly ok.  I have a few test windows clients and a few ubuntu clients that can join the domain and login with their ldap user accounts and everything seems peachy.  

    However, when I try to login locally on the ldap server with one of those account, for instance mine "chris", I get prompted that my password is old and needs to be changed.  It asks me to enter my "Login(LDAP)" password.  So i do and I get a "The change of the Authentication Token failed" error and so I can only login with the local account I had set up. 

  Is this normal that I cant login locally with an ldap user?  My account "chris" is in the administrators group.  I've been searching online to make sure I have my files in my /etc/pam.d/ directory setup right but they all look ok and are setup exactly the same as the walkthrough here.  I can post any info needed but I thought somebody might have come across the same problem and have a quick tip for me.

Thanks in advance

Chris

You should be able to login locally on the server.  When you login to the server using the local account, try using this command:

```
ldapsearch -x -b dc=example,dc=local
```

and see what happens.  If everything is working, it should give you a list of all your LDAP objects.  If not, it'll give you an error.

### Post by kcnnc on 2008-08-04
> **scorp123 said:**
> No, it's not.

Anyone having "root" on a client machine (e.g. a user with their own laptop) now has full "root" rights over the share as well, e.g. he can walk into any directory regardless of the permissions and manipulate anything as they please.

This was already explained here in postings #118 and #122:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4184300&postcount=118](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4184300&postcount=118)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4184680&postcount=122](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4184680&postcount=122)

I saw those post which is why I referred to them and in the light of it, how would the problem with pam_mkhomedir.so not able to create the home folder when a user first logs in be solved?

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5355422#post5355422](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5355422#post5355422)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5342721#post5342721](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5342721#post5342721)

---

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-08-04
> **kcnnc said:**
>  I saw those post which is why I referred to them and in the light of it, how would the problem with pam_mkhomedir.so not able to create the home folder when a user first logs in be solved?

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5355422#post5355422](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5355422#post5355422)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5342721#post5342721](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5342721#post5342721) You'd need to have super-tight network security and what-not in place to make sure nobody can get to the "root" account on their local machine and pose as "root" on the network share. But then all it takes is a live CD and voila: your share is wide open for manipulations ...

And besides ... do you really want home directories to be auto-generated?

The reason I ask: I had an interesting problem here today where one user's name was written wrong, e.g. "Jaeger" instead of "Jaegger" <== one "g" was missing. Also the information for the home directory was wrong (the entry was pointing to "/home/jaegerh" instead of "/home/jaeggerh").

With the automatic generation of home directories being impossible it only took me a few seconds to figure out what the problem was. I just tried to login as this user via SSH and I was promptly greeted with messages that said something about trying to generate a new home directory (why??? I already created all the directories when I configured the accounts ... or didn't I???? Something is wrong here .... ) and I was kicked out of the server again.

Taking a really really close look at the error messages and the LDAP entries I noticed the typo: "jaeger" vs. "jaegger".

Had the automatic generation of home directories worked I would never have noticed this problem and I probably would have spent the entire afternoon trying to find out why this user can't access any of the applications they'd need to have access to ... But with this nice error message it was all fixed within a couple of minutes.

Food for thought, IMHO.

---

### Post by kcnnc on 2008-08-04
> **scorp123 said:**
> You'd need to have super-tight network security and what-not in place to make sure nobody can get to the "root" account on their local machine and pose as "root" on the network share. But then all it takes is a live CD and voila: your share is wide open for manipulations ...

And besides ... do you really want home directories to be auto-generated?


I know what you mean, I was contemplating about the auto-generated home as well.

The reason I brought this up was that in the 70+ pages of discussion, so many people had followed the steps to the 'T', yet no one had the problem of auto generating home directory. I though there must be something missing with my configuration... and this was what I found.

If in fact auto generating home folder is not possible without opening the root privilege then there should be a serious note in the howto about it. IMO

---

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-08-05
> **kcnnc said:**
>  If in fact auto generating home folder is not possible without opening the root privilege then there should be a serious note in the howto about it. IMO Well, IMHO the only way you can get this to work AND be on the (somewhat) "safe side" (absolute safety is impossible anyway in IT I guess?) is to use thin-client computers, e.g. "Sun Ray" stations from SUN Microsystems. No local drives, no local disks, no local OS (everything is running off the server), no way whatsoever that a local user could manipulate this thing by booting a live CD or something like that.

If that isn't an option and you have to use PC's ... well, you'd have to seriously lock those PC's down, e.g. prevent booting from CD-ROM or USB drives, and so on. Especially you should take care of the "su" and "sudo" binaries on each system: e.g. you should change their ownership permissions so that 'normal' users (who should not be in any "admin" group!) cannot use these two programs or change into a user account that could.

But all that involves a lot of hassle IMHO, so the best thing probably would be not to risk anything and leave "root_squash" untouched.

### Post by shizakapayou on 2008-08-13
> **mad-hatta said:**
> I've noticed that Tombutcher was looking for a way to standardize background images, I have found a nice solution, called BGinfo [http://technet.microsoft.com/en-us/sysinternals/bb897557.aspx]("http://technet.microsoft.com/en-us/sysinternals/bb897557.aspx")
Although it doesn't prevent users from changing their background, everytime users logon it changes to the standard again, plus the added benefit of having IP Addy,username... all on tap. I have implemented this App succesfully

If anybody would like to know the switches i used let me know, will be glad to give back even a tiny bit to those who helped me!

Next up... Figuring out how to assign different netlogon scripts for different LDAP groups

Nice idea, though you'd need to make sure to put some security on the executable so users don't delete it.

Note that on individual machines, you can use use local group policy to setup all sorts of rules, similar to what Windows Server would let you do, just not on a network-wide basis.  If you use local group policy, make your rules and use Windows Explorer to navigate to C:\Windows\system32\GroupPolicy (it's a hidden folder).  Set security permissions on this - read only for everyone but your admins, no rights for them.  Otherwise, everyone will get hit by it.  Among other things, you can use group policy to enforce a background that cannot be changed.

I need to look at my netlogon script some more.  I know with Windows Server you can make it map drives according to the groups the user is in.  Surely that can be done here as well.
