Nevermind it, because I have done some LDAP authentications against Win Server 2008 AD at work, so I'm probably able to find the problem by myself. 

But the biggest problem is this:

smbldap-userlist
uid   |username
  0   |root
65534 |nobody
30000 |testi
30001 |testi2

Then I try to add my Windows (Vista) box into domain, but after the username and password prompt, it returns with the error message:

But:
smbldap-userlist
uid   |username
  0   |root
65534 |nobody
30000 |testi
30001 |testi2
30008 |testmachina$ 

There it is, the name is right and so on. Whats the problem here, can someone explain it to me?

Best Regards:
Sampo Saarela

Verify your DNS server settings - make sure the Vista box is using the server for DNS.  Check that you can ping the server by name.  I don't know for sure that it's an official requirement, but in my experience the domain controller needs to be the DNS server for Windows to play well on a domain.

### Post by sopsaare on 2008-08-14
Hey all!

I got rid of all those problems. It seems to me that somehow the smbldap-populate -g 30000 didnt work. It just used GID's from 500+. So it ****** things up, but I modified the values by hand and got rid of that problem. Then I saw that somehow dpkg hadn't make the "ldap.secret" file. But I made it by hand and got rid of those problems too. 

Then I faced some problems with "Network path not found" while I tried to join the domain. I fooled around with the windows and linux network configurations and got rid of it. Then I was able to join the domain. So first step closed.

root@lin:~#

### Post by sopsaare on 2008-08-15
¨Hi everyone once again!

I got rid of the problems. The GID things had nothing to do with the initial problems, the only thing was the missing "ldap.secret".

The last error, unworking -a switch was caused by invalid argument in some configuration file (I'm at work, so I cant remember it now what the config file was). But the argument was default GID for new users. It needs to be same as "Domain\ Users" GID to make the -a work. 

Now I have my windows work stationts contacted to my Domain controller. There is some more tweaking to be done (write permissions to some networks shares and allusers.bat is not executed). But everything else is working fine.

Next thing is to make my debian workstation log into the Domain. 

And the last thing to research is to search if LDAP authentications can be "offline" like Windows authentications. So I could use my laptop as part of the Domain, and still be able to fully use it somewhere else.

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-08-16
> **shizakapayou said:**
> That's a good point - let me know how that goes.  I haven't bothered looking into that yet as almost all of my users have Windows.  I figure I'll deal with the Linux machines at the end of the migration. That's easy. ```
> apt-cache show nss-updatedb
Package: nss-updatedb
Priority: optional
Section: universe/net
Installed-Size: 80
Maintainer: Ubuntu MOTU Developers <ubuntu-motu@lists.ubuntu.com>
Original-Maintainer: Guido Guenther <agx@sigxcpu.org>
Architecture: i386
Version: 7-1
Depends: libc6 (>= 2.5-0ubuntu1), libdb4.3 (>= 4.3.28-1)
Recommends: libnss-db
Filename: pool/universe/n/nss-updatedb/nss-updatedb_7-1_i386.deb
Size: 11636
MD5sum: 27dcbabee69ed9f16dcd900b5fc6bf18
SHA1: c3cdb82cde5992848d2020ba550d6713b1311443
SHA256: c1f19090aa678bbed6293e9acefe3e89a37fda1bb0d8956765ca159fa6e9e31b
Description: Cache name service directories in DB format
 This package contains a script, nss_updatedb, which can be
 used to maintain local caches of user and group directories.
 These can then be used by the DB Name Service Switch module
 (libnss-db) to provide name service when the system is offline.
 .
 These tools are designed to work with libpam-ldap and libnss-ldap.
``` apt-get install that package ... And don't forget to edit /etc/nsswitch.conf to include the "db" statement (check with Google if in doubt) and make sure that the update script is running in regular intervals.

With everything working correctly you could still login to a system with your (now cached) LDAP account even though the system can't reach the LDAP server.

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-08-16
> **shizakapayou said:**
>  is it possible to make an LDAP user a local sudoer?   You'd need to change your sudoers file so that certain LDAP users and groups are able to use it. There are many examples that can be found via Google on how to do this. But honestly: I don't like the idea of remote LDAP users being members of a purely local group, I see potential security problems. 

What I did on my systems:

Each system has a purely 100% local "sysadm" account, and that's the only one who can use "sudo" (I changed the permissions accordingly). But: If I or any other of the IT guys need to "sudo" something, we'd first "su - sysadm" (which gets logged into /var/log/messages => nice!) --of course: the permissions of the "su" binary are changed too!-- and then "sudo" whatever it is that needs "root" superpowers. This way if someone managed to steal my LDAP password they'd still not be able to take control of any "root" account on any of the client systems, they'd still need to know the password of each local "sysadm" user (each one has a different password!). And "sysadm" is blocked from logging in via SSH (thanks to the "DenyUser" directive; see here: [http://www.cyberciti.biz/tips/openssh-deny-or-restrict-access-to-users-and-groups.html](http://www.cyberciti.biz/tips/openssh-deny-or-restrict-access-to-users-and-groups.html) ) .... So even if you somehow knew the password of a "sysadm" user on a particular system.... It does not help you. Without having an LDAP account and without being in the proper group that has permission to use "su" you can't get anywhere near "root" superpowers.

Maybe it's a bit paranoid but then again I sleep tip top at night ;)

---

### Post by Alkaif on 2008-08-20
hey guys, first up, really nice tutorial. I've been asked with an assignment at university to create a domain with a few clients in any server software of our liking. However most of the class had clearly gone in the microsoft direction because it supported AD/Domain Controller structure. After some careful googling (lol, not really that careful) I came to this thread. Excellent. I've been following through the instructions and it's great so far. Bang on precisely.

However I'm now headed into a road block. For some reason, after editing my smb.conf file and restarting the samba daemon:
```

root@siberia:/etc/samba# /etc/init.d/samba force-reload
 * Stopping Samba daemons                                                [ OK ]
   start-stop-daemon: warning: failed to kill 6344: No such process
 * Starting Samba daemons                                                [ OK ]

```

Samba seems to not reload. So I thought that maybe it is my config file, so i copied and pasted the smb.conf file that was provided in the "installing samba" step. and then re-issued the above command and it gives this error. However if i load the default smb.conf file that comes with samba and restart samba, it works perfectly.

a ps - A | grep smb output shows nothing either.

copy of my smb.conf can be found at: 
[http://paste2.org/p/63324](http://paste2.org/p/63324)

I thought maybe because ubuntu uses UTF-8, that would be the cause of the problem, so i created the file again under ubuntu and it still gives the above error.

a testparm output is as follows:
```

Load smb config files from /etc/samba/smb.conf
Processing section "[printers]"
Processing section "[print$]"
Loaded services file OK.
Server role: ROLE_DOMAIN_PDC
Press enter to see a dump of your service definitions

```
followed by dump of service definitions

any help will be greatly appreciated.

PS: We are using Ubuntu 8.04 :)

Final Edit:
Alright after reading page four, it was apparent that samba was not installed properly. I don't know why this occured, but it did so here's what I did.
sudo dpkg -P samba 
and then installed samba again
sudo apt-get install samba

and then copied and pasted the smb.conf file from the viewing pleasure. Edited necessary values
restarted samba using /etc/init.d/samba restart and voila it works :).

So save yourself the time and read this. I went through the hassle of reinstalling ubuntu only to figure this out later. But i suppose it is how we learn :)

### Post by thecha on 2008-08-23
I've been trying to find a free solution as a Windows 2003 SBS Replacement for the past few months on and off.  I don't have a lot of experience with command line linux, however I was able to follow your guide with no problems.. (well a few problems but I ironed them out)

I've completed the guide even the part about creating the SBS scripts and home folders... And I successfully added my Virtual XP machine to my Virtural Ubuntu Domain Server.  However I have one strange glitch I'm not sure how to fix it..

When I log onto the XP machine I ALWAYS get 2 errors with regards to roaming profiles... The XP machine then creates a new temporary profile each time the user logs in.  Which is slow... :confused: See the two attached pictures for the errors... 

I've attached 2 pictures with the problem I'm having... 

Another problem I had but solved was when Mapping the Home H: Drive... 

Your Allusers.bat file looks like this...
```
@echo off
REM    # SYNC THE TIME WITH THE SERVER
net time \\dc01-ubuntu.example.local /set /y
REM    # DELETE ALL MAPPED DRIVES
net use h: /delete
REM    # MAP ALL NECESSARY DRIVES
net use h: "\\dc01-ubuntu.example.local\ldaphome\%username%"
```

I had to remove the ".example.local" part from mine to get it to work???
```
@echo off
 off
REM    # SYNC THE TIME WITH THE SERVER
net time \\dc01-ubuntu /set /y
REM    # DELETE ALL MAPPED DRIVES
net use h: /delete
REM    # MAP ALL NECESSARY DRIVES
net use h: "\\dc01-ubuntu\ldaphome\%username%"

```

I'm not sure why?? Yours seems more specific?

Also I just noticed the script doesn't seem to auto execute on login??

Thanks!

### Post by thecha on 2008-08-24
> When I log onto the XP machine I ALWAYS get 2 errors with regards to roaming profiles... The XP machine then creates a new temporary profile each time the user logs in.  Which is slow... :confused: 

Error Message 1
> Windows cannot locate the server copy of your roaming profile and is attempting to log you on with your local profile.  Changes to the profile will not be copied to the server when you logoff.  Possible causes of this error include network problems or insufficient security rights.  If this problem persists, contact your network administrator.

DETAIL - **The network name cannot be found**.

The part about the network name cannot be found is concerning... So I pinged dc01-ubuntu and it worked however when I ping dc01-ubuntu.example.local it doesn't work...

Error Message 2
> Windows cannot find the local profile and is logging you on with a temporary profile.  Changes you make to this profile will be lost when you log off.

The allusers.bat script seems to run however I had to change any instances of *\\dc01-ubuntu.example.local* to *\\dc01-ubuntu*.

### Post by thecha on 2008-08-25
I've solved the problem!  In the samba.conf file i had

"log_i_n path =" instead of "log_o_n path ="

No more errors while logging into windows!  Now I'm going to figure out how to write a login script to do exactly what I need!

Thanks again Ricky for an amazing tutorial!

### Post by rahmath on 2008-08-28
Hello All,

As i mentioned in my first post, i will shed some light whenever i strike to do so... 

So here is the first point.

Ricky mentioned about the /etc/hosts file... as;

```
127.0.0.1       localhost
127.0.1.1       dc01-ubuntu dc01-ubuntu.example.local
```


This is not the right format to go with. For this environment, this might not showed up any errors... But with this entry there is a big chance of having errors to many network oriented services. Most of those kind of problems will give you strange functionality/behavior, which will be hard to troubleshoot even for an experienced professional.

So for any network related configurations, please make sure that, you had populated your /etc/hosts files with the correct format as the following;

```
127.0.0.1       localhost
127.0.1.1       dc01-ubuntu.example.local dc01-ubuntu
#IP              FQDN                      ALIAS  
```

Once if you populated the /etc/hosts file, you can verify this settings by the following commands;

hostname  --> should give your host name, here it is dc01-ubuntu
hostname -d --> should give your domain name, here it is example.local
hostname -f --> should give your fqdn, here it is dc01-ubuntu.example.local

```
root@dc01-ubuntu:~# cat /etc/hosts
127.0.0.1       localhost
127.0.1.1       dc01-ubuntu.example.com dc01-ubuntu

# The following lines are desirable for IPv6 capable hosts
::1     ip6-localhost ip6-loopback
fe00::0 ip6-localnet
ff00::0 ip6-mcastprefix
ff02::1 ip6-allnodes
ff02::2 ip6-allrouters
ff02::3 ip6-allhosts
root@dc01-ubuntu:~# hostname 
dc01-ubuntu
root@dc01-ubuntu:~# hostname -d
example.com
root@dc01-ubuntu:~# hostname -f
dc01-ubuntu.example.com
root@dc01-ubuntu:~# 

```

 
Hope this will be helpful for all...


NOTE: i guess(i dont have much exp with debian flavors), it is not advisable to put your fqdn in /etc/hostname. There you should only mention your actual hostname of the system. Here in this example, like "dc01-ubuntu". otherwise, most probably the above mentioned steps will not give you the exact results and which will ends up failure for many of the services. So my suggestion is, as the file name indicates, just keep the "hostname" in your /etc/hostname file.

I had noticed, in this discussions, few of the users is having strange results after doing configuring this domain controller. some times, system boot, sometimes hangs, service fails like that. So try to change the /etc/hosts and /etc/hostname file as mentioned above. and then see the result.

I ran into this same problem. I couldn't find a solution that would allow me to use /ldap_data as my ldap data store. If you find a solution that allows me to do this please message me. I did find a work around.

> **bscharff said:**
> 

I checked line 65 and it says:

```

directory       "/ldap_data"

```



Changing the line /ldap_data to the default data store location worked for me. That location is /var/lib/ldap.

```

directory       "/var/lib/ldap"

```

> **bscharff said:**
> 

I did
#chown -R openldap:openldap /ldap_data
and
#chmod 0777 /ldap_data

just to see if it was an easy issue to fix...
Same log error!

Any ideas?

FYI:
I'm not necessarily an experienced linux user, but I do know the basics.
I'm running all of the commands through putty on my WinXP Home desktop, if it matters.

Thanks in advance for your help!

I tried changing the user and group ownership of /ldap_data as well as the permissions but it didn't work for me either. It shouldn't matter if you are using putty. That is a solution, maybe someone else will be able to give their two cents as to why /ldap_data doesn't work.

### Post by shizakapayou on 2008-08-31
Changing the location never worked for me either.  Knowing where it is before installing is easiest - simply mount /var/lib/ldap to its own partition and be done with it.

Danking is correct that using putty makes no difference - it's just an SSH connection.  Do note that your XP Home machine cannot join the domain.

### Post by thecha on 2008-09-02
> **peter_rs said:**
> Hi,

I have the PDC working (winxp machines join the domain and users can log in) but there is one small issuse when a user logs in the comment field (-c) is not working.  eg in winxp when you click start instead of "Peter Hickey" it has just "peter".

I tried to fix it using the command below but still no luck.

smbldap-usermod -c "Peter Hickey" peter


I had the same problem... I've created my own script file to deal with this problem.
```
#!/bin/bash

echo $1
smbldap-useradd -a -c "$1" "$1"
cd /ldaphome/
mkdir "$1"
chown "$1" "$1"
chgrp "Domain Users" "$1"
chmod 700 "$1"
smbldap-usermod -d "/ldaphome/$1" "$1"
smbldap-groupmod -m "$1" "$2"

exit 0

```

To create your new users just type ./script.sh "User Name" "Group Name"
```
chmod 700 script.sh
./script.sh "Peter Hickey" "admin"
```

Hope this helps... I also one that reads multiple users from a file...  It takes 2 args at the command line The txt file, and the group... All users in the text file are on a new line...

```
#!/bin/bash

cwd=`pwd`
a=0
while read line
do
echo Creating user...   $line
smbldap-useradd -a -c "$line" "$line"
cd /ldaphome/
mkdir "$line"
chown "$line" "$line"
chgrp "Domain Users" "$line"
chmod 700 "$line"
smbldap-usermod -d "/ldaphome/$line" "$line"
smbldap-groupmod -m "$line" "$2"
#smbldap-usermod -J "$line"
smbpasswd -n -a "$line"
done < "$cwd/$1"


exit 0

```

What I would like this code to do, but I havn't been able to get it yet. Is to make it so that each users is activated and their userpassword is blank... I work in a school so I'm lucky if the majority of grade 1 kids can type their full name.  Never mind a password...

Hope it helps!
-Matt

### Post by jessecgross on 2008-09-10
Hi everyone. I have been following this how-to for testing purposes on a home network and have run into an issue. Granted I used Debian as my server and Ubuntu as my client, but hopefully because they are so similar someone can still assist me.


I have installed everything and configured everything without fault so far upon until the step of mounting a drive on my client machine. I have come to find that the client machine can not resolve the server by it's FQDN, but can with it's flat name. I further discovered that when pinging or using nslookup the flat name will resolve the FQDN and the correct ip, but still can not ping the full name. In addition I did determine that the client will mount the drive using the IP of the server (so I know that is working).

So I believe it is fairly obvious that it is a DNS issue, but because this is my first time with Bind I am fairly lost. Any help would be great. I have included the connectivity testing below. Let me know if you would like to see any conf file settings.

```
jesse@scottsummers:~$ sudo mount charlesxavier.cerebros.local:/ldaphome /ldaphom                                                                             e
[sudo] password for jesse:
mount.nfs: can't get address for charlesxavier.cerebros.local

jesse@scottsummers:~$ ping charlesxavier
PING charlesxavier.cerebros.local (192.168.1.40) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 192.168.1.40: icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.121 ms
64 bytes from 192.168.1.40: icmp_seq=2 ttl=64 time=0.118 ms

--- charlesxavier.cerebros.local ping statistics ---
2 packets transmitted, 2 received, 0% packet loss, time 5043ms
rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 0.118/0.119/0.121/0.011 ms
jesse@scottsummers:~$ ping charlesxavier.cerebros.local
ping: unknown host charlesxavier.cerebros.local
jesse@scottsummers:~$ ping cerebros.local
ping: unknown host cerebros.local
jesse@scottsummers:~$ nslookup charlesxavier
Server:         192.168.1.40
Address:        192.168.1.40#53

Name:   charlesxavier.cerebros.local
Address: 192.168.1.40

```

My experience is with Debian and I'm not very familiar with avahi-daemon, but check your /etc/resolv.conf file.

there should be;
search domain.local
nameserver X.X.X.X
nameserver X.X.X.X


with that I'm able to resolv names without having to add the full FQDN to the query.

EDIT:  Of course, you are supposed to replace domain.local with your domain.  Thats supposed to be self evident though.

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-09-17
Something I just noticed on an Ubuntu 7.10 installation which is configured as LDAP client vs. a Novell SLES 10 server:

It might happen that a user's group memberships are not correctly mapped. E.g. when you issue the "id" command on the server itself you see something like: ```
server:/home/johnny> id johnny
uid=5000(johnny) gid=5000(johnny) groups=5000(johnny),3000(users),7500(zimbra),7501(webauthors),7502(webapps)

```

But on this client (here: Ubuntu 7.10) I'd only see the user's main group, not the other memberships: ```
client:~> id johnny
uid=5000(johnny) gid=5000(johnny) 
``` ... the other group memberships were not visible to the client machine.

The key here is to make sure that the client has these lines in /etc/ldap.conf (in addition to what this excellent guide here already tells you -- so this file here is only an excerpt and by no means complete):```
...

## This one may not always be required but it is in our case:
pam_lookup_policy yes

## The server encrypts the passwords for us, so we just 
## need the MD5 hashes
pam_password md5

## Tell nss_ldap that we need to be a member of each group 
## of which our account has an "uniqueMember" entry
## => that's how the LDAP memberships get mapped to system groups
nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member

## I found this stuff at the URL indicated below:
## http://www.linuxlaboratory.org/?q=node/17
#
# This stuff helps to map a few other things from the LDAP server
# onto the local UNIX-style accounts
#
# After applying these settings the mappings should work tip top

pam_filter objectclass=posixAccount
pam_groupdn cn=YourMainGroupNameHere,ou=groups,dc=YourCompany,dc=YourTLD
pam_member_attribute memberuid
nss_base_passwd ou=people,dc=YourCompany,dc=YourTLD?one
nss_base_shadow ou=people,dc=YourCompany,dc=YourTLD?one
nss_base_group ou=groups,dc=YourCompany,dc=YourTLD?one

... 
``` After doing above slight changes and a reboot both LDAP server and LDAP client will now produce the same results when I issue an "id" command. Problem solved :)

---

### Post by kevinp on 2008-10-05
I have a client running Kubuntu 7.10 that was authenticating perfectly with an Ubuntu OpenLDAP server.

After I upgraded the client to Kubuntu 8.04, this authentication mechanism has partially broken.

A login attempt to KDM using an LDAP-only user results in the following error message:

```
Call to lnusertemp failed (temporary directories full?).  Check your installation.
```

So I login using a local user and try the following in a console:

su ldap-only-user

This is actually successful, but:
```
id: cannot find name for group ID 10001
I have no name!@ipp01:/etc/pam.d$   
```    

So the shell does not know its own username and group!

getent passwd
(this returns only the local users).

sudo getent passwd
(This returns the LDAP users as well).  So the root account successfully contacts LDAP, while non-root accounts don't.

Any ideas?  I can post config files upon request.  I don't want to clutter up the thread with files that may not be relevant.  I have compared all the ldap.conf and pam.d config files and they seem to match what is recommended.

I must say that OpenLDAP is always a moving target.  I have had to tweak the config files going from Ubuntu 6 to 7 to 8.  It would be nice to have some stability here.  If I want to upgrade my Ubuntu 7 LDAP server, I will have to budget a whole day of potential downtime to fix LDAP errors. :-(

---

### Post by click170 on 2008-10-05
> **kevinp said:**
> I have a client running Kubuntu 7.10 that was authenticating perfectly with an Ubuntu OpenLDAP server.

After I upgraded the client to Kubuntu 8.04, this authentication mechanism has partially broken.

A login attempt to KDM using an LDAP-only user results in the following error message:

```
Call to lnusertemp failed (temporary directories full?).  Check your installation.
```

So I login using a local user and try the following in a console:

su ldap-only-user

This is actually successful, but:
```
id: cannot find name for group ID 10001
I have no name!@ipp01:/etc/pam.d$   
```    

So the shell does not know its own username and group!

getent passwd
(this returns only the local users).

sudo getent passwd
(This returns the LDAP users as well).  So the root account successfully contacts LDAP, while non-root accounts don't.

Any ideas?  I can post config files upon request.  I don't want to clutter up the thread with files that may not be relevant.  I have compared all the ldap.conf and pam.d config files and they seem to match what is recommended.

I must say that OpenLDAP is always a moving target.  I have had to tweak the config files going from Ubuntu 6 to 7 to 8.  It would be nice to have some stability here.  If I want to upgrade my Ubuntu 7 LDAP server, I will have to budget a whole day of potential downtime to fix LDAP errors. :-(


At first I would have suspected your nsswitch.conf file was reverted by the upgrade, but because root is still able to successfully query, my next guess is that perhaps a configuration file required to complete an LDAP query has had its permissions changed to root-only?

I would go through the HowTo again and check each of the files in the HowTo with the files on your HD, if there are any discrepancies in permissions or configurations, thats likely your problem.

If you get to the end of the HowTo and all of your files are the same as its, then... I guess you'll just have to start posting config files :)

---

I admin a small network (of my company) and I had succefully setup an LDAP server on debian and a client machines running ubuntu.

When a user logins I have to run a script in order to refresh the local home folder with the home folder on the server (an alternative to the implementation of windows' offline folder).

What about autofs?
Autofs can be used to automatically mount directories and unmount them when not needed.  I use it myself.  It can use LDAP as its backend.

See

Whole section on replication.

### Post by walter13 on 2008-11-01
> **walter13 said:**
> Hi,
Thank you for this great documentation. It works very well for 8.04. Unfortunately, I've an issue with 8.10.
I don't know how to include schemas with the new configuration system of slapd available in Intrepid Ibex.
Indeed slapd no more use slapd.conf file for configuration, so I don't know how to include /etc/ldap/schema/samba.schema and /etc/ldap/schema/misc.schema usually done with adding the following two lines in /etc/ldap/slapd.conf:

include         /etc/ldap/schema/samba.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/misc.schema

Any help is welcome.

I found how to fix it.
Just edit /etc/default/slapd and change the first parameter to /etc/ldap/slapd.conf as explained in the file. You need to have already a slapd.conf file or it will create a folder with the new configuration system.

Hey Guys,

I work for a small non-profit college and we thought this would be a super way to administrate our little network on the cheap. I'm having a boat load of problems getting this howto to work on 8.1 though. I think most of it stems from the /etc/ldap/slapd.conf file. I specified it in /etc/default/slapd as the chap above suggested, but when I restart the service I get a permission denied on the slapd.conf file. When I try and run it as root, I get this:

/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 24: loglevel: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 27: modulepath: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 28: moduleload: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 31: sizelimit: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 35: tool-threads: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 41: backend: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 42: checkpoint: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 54: database: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 57: suffix: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 65: directory: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 69: dbconfig: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 76: dbconfig: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 78: dbconfig: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 80: dbconfig: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 83: index: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 86: lastmod: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 96: access: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 97: by: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 98: by: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 99: by: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 100: by: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 111: access: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 115: access: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 116: by: not found
/etc/ldap/slapd.conf: 117: by: not found

```

Now my config is almost identical to how the OP wrote it, I merely changed the domain name. Anyone have any idea what's going on here? I am following these instructions to the letter with the sole exception of changing the name of the domain.

```

# This is the main slapd configuration file. See slapd.conf(5) for more
# info on the configuration options.

#######################################################################
# Global Directives:
# Features to permit
#allow bind_v2

# Schema and objectClass definitions
include         /etc/ldap/schema/core.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/cosine.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/nis.schema
include         /etc/ldap/schema/inetorgperson.schema

# Where the pid file is put. The init.d script
# will not stop the server if you change this.
pidfile         /var/run/slapd/slapd.pid

# List of arguments that were passed to the server
argsfile        /var/run/slapd/slapd.args

# Read slapd.conf(5) for possible values
loglevel        0

# Where the dynamically loaded modules are stored
modulepath      /usr/lib/ldap
moduleload      back_bdb

# The maximum number of entries that is returned for a search operation
sizelimit 500

# The tool-threads parameter sets the actual amount of cpu's that is used
# for indexing.
tool-threads 1

#######################################################################
# Specific Backend Directives for bdb:
# Backend specific directives apply to this backend until another
# 'backend' directive occurs
backend         bdb
checkpoint 512 30

#######################################################################
# Specific Backend Directives for 'other':
# Backend specific directives apply to this backend until another
# 'backend' directive occurs
#backend                <other>

#######################################################################
# Specific Directives for database #1, of type bdb:
# Database specific directives apply to this databasse until another# 'database' directive occurs
database        bdb

# The base of your directory in database #1
suffix          "dc=baumancollege"

# rootdn directive for specifying a superuser on the database. This is needed
# for syncrepl.
# rootdn          "cn=admin,dc=baumancollege"

# Where the database file are physically stored for database #1
directory       "/var/lib/ldap"
#directory    "/ldap_data"

# For the Debian package we use 2MB as default but be sure to update this
# value if you have plenty of RAM
dbconfig set_cachesize 0 2097152 0

# Sven Hartge reported that he had to set this value incredibly high
# to get slapd running at all. See http://bugs.debian.org/303057
# for more information.

# Number of objects that can be locked at the same time.
dbconfig set_lk_max_objects 1500
# Number of locks (both requested and granted)
dbconfig set_lk_max_locks 1500

# Indexing options for database #1
index           objectClass eq

# Save the time that the entry gets modified, for database #1
lastmod         on

# Where to store the replica logs for database #1
# replogfile    /var/lib/ldap/replog

# The userPassword by default can be changed
# by the entry owning it if they are authenticated.
# Others should not be able to see it, except the
# admin entry below
# These access lines apply to database #1 only
access to attrs=userPassword,shadowLastChange
        by dn="cn=admin,dc=baumancollege" write
        by anonymous auth
        by self write
        by * none

# Ensure read access to the base for things like
# supportedSASLMechanisms.  Without this you may
# have problems with SASL not knowing what
# mechanisms are available and the like.
# Note that this is covered by the 'access to *'
# ACL below too but if you change that as people
# are wont to do you'll still need this if you
# want SASL (and possible other things) to work
# happily.
access to dn.base="" by * read

# The admin dn has full write access, everyone else
# can read everything.
access to *
        by dn="cn=admin,dc=baumancollege" write
        by * read

# For Netscape Roaming support, each user gets a roaming
# profile for which they have write access to
#access to dn=".*,ou=Roaming,o=morsnet"
#        by dn="cn=admin,dc=baumancollege" write
#        by dnattr=owner write

#######################################################################
# Specific Directives for database #2, of type 'other' (can be bdb too):
# Database specific directives apply to this databasse until another
# 'database' directive occurs
#database        <other>

```

Sorry if the answer to this is super easy, this is my first time really dealing with LDAP outside of Novell. Just to be clear, I haven't done anything past step6, I didn't want to continue on if LDAP wasn't going to run properly.

### Post by c.monty on 2008-11-15
hi!
I intend to set up SAMBA PDC + LDAP with roaming profiles.

following official SAMBA documentation the modified smb.conf is this:
```

#======================= Global Settings =======================

[global]

#### Browsing/Identification #####

wins support = yes
netbios name = vm0-ubuntu.domain.local
server string = %h Server (Samba, Ubuntu 8.04 LTS)

os level = 255

;local master = yes
;preferred master = yes


#### PDC ####
workgroup = DOMAIN
domain master = yes
domain logons = yes
;   passdb backend = tdbsam
passdb backend = ldapsam:ldap://localhost/
;   obey pam restrictions = yes
obey pam restrictions = no


#### Networking ####


#### Debugging/Accounting ####
log level = 2
log file = /var/log/samba/log.%m
max log size = 1000

# If you want Samba to only log through syslog then set the following
# parameter to 'yes'.
;   syslog only = no
syslog = 0
panic action = /usr/share/samba/panic-action %d


#### Authentication ####
security = user
encrypt passwords = true


#### LDAP ####
#	Begin: Custom LDAP Entries
#
ldap admin dn = cn=admin,dc=domain,dc=local
ldap suffix = dc=domain, dc=local
ldap group suffix = ou=Groups
ldap user suffix = ou=Users
ldap machine suffix = ou=Computers
ldap idmap suffix = ou=Users
; Do ldap passwd sync
ldap passwd sync = Yes
passwd program = /usr/sbin/smbldap-passwd %u
passwd chat = *New*password* %n\n *Retype*new*password* %n\n *all*authentication*tokens*updated*
add user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -m "%u"
ldap delete dn = yes
delete user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-userdel "%u"
add machine script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -w "%u"
add group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupadd -p "%g"
delete group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupdel "%g"
add user to group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -m "%u" "%g"
delete user from group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -x "%u" "%g"
set primary group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-usermod -g "%g" "%u"
#
#	End: Custom LDAP Entries

;   guest account = nobody
;   invalid users = root

unix password sync = yes

passwd program = /usr/bin/passwd %u
passwd chat = *Enter\snew\s*\spassword:* %n\n *Retype\snew\s*\spassword:* %n\n *password\supdated\ssuccessfully* .

pam password change = yes

map to guest = bad user

########## Domains ###########

domain logons = yes

logon path = \\%N\profiles\%u\%m
;logon drive = D:
;logon home = \\%L\%u\.win_profile\%m
logon script = logon.bat

;domain admin group = root administrator

# This allows Unix users to be created on the domain controller via the SAMR
# RPC pipe.  The example command creates a user account with a disabled Unix
# password; please adapt to your needs
; add user script = /usr/sbin/adduser --quiet --disabled-password --gecos "" %u


########## Printing ##########

# If you want to automatically load your printer list rather
# than setting them up individually then you'll need this
;   load printers = yes

# lpr(ng) printing. You may wish to override the location of the
# printcap file
;   printing = bsd
;   printcap name = /etc/printcap

# CUPS printing.  See also the cupsaddsmb(8) manpage in the
# cupsys-client package.
;   printing = cups
;   printcap name = cups

############ Misc ############

# Using the following line enables you to customise your configuration
# on a per machine basis. The %m gets replaced with the netbios name
# of the machine that is connecting
;   include = /home/samba/etc/smb.conf.%m

# Most people will find that this option gives better performance.
# See smb.conf(5) and /usr/share/doc/samba-doc/htmldocs/Samba3-HOWTO/speed.html
# for details
# You may want to add the following on a Linux system:
#         SO_RCVBUF=8192 SO_SNDBUF=8192
socket options = TCP_NODELAY

# The following parameter is useful only if you have the linpopup package
# installed. The samba maintainer and the linpopup maintainer are
# working to ease installation and configuration of linpopup and samba.
;   message command = /bin/sh -c '/usr/bin/linpopup "%f" "%m" %s; rm %s' &

# Domain Master specifies Samba to be the Domain Master Browser. If this
# machine will be configured as a BDC (a secondary logon server), you
# must set this to 'no'; otherwise, the default behavior is recommended.
;   domain master = auto

# Some defaults for winbind (make sure you're not using the ranges
# for something else.)
;   idmap uid = 10000-20000
;   idmap gid = 10000-20000
;   template shell = /bin/bash

# The following was the default behaviour in sarge,
# but samba upstream reverted the default because it might induce
# performance issues in large organizations.
# See Debian bug #368251 for some of the consequences of *not*
# having this setting and smb.conf(5) for details.
;   winbind enum groups = yes
;   winbind enum users = yes

# Setup usershare options to enable non-root users to share folders
# with the net usershare command.

# Maximum number of usershare. 0 (default) means that usershare is disabled.
;   usershare max shares = 100

# Allow users who've been granted usershare privileges to create
# public shares, not just authenticated ones
usershare allow guests = yes

# Samba functionality Time Server
time server = Yes
socket options = SO_KEEPALIVE IPTOS_LOWDELAY SO_SNDBUF=8192 SO_RCVBUF=8192

# Server Administration - all functions will be executed with system user root
admin users = root, Administrator

#======================= Share Definitions =======================

# Un-comment the following (and tweak the other settings below to suit)

### Post by santintin on 2008-11-17
finally I find a solution, I changed on /etc/default/slapd the line```
SLAPD_CONF=
``` for ```
SLAPD_CONF=/etc/ldap/slapd.conf
``` and now the openldap server works with the file /etc/ldap/slapd.conf and the populate command works fine.

### Post by 3StrikesDesign on 2008-11-18
> **shizakapayou said:**
> Anyone else having trouble with Intrepid as a client?

```

administrator@pathfinder:/etc/pam.d$ sudo auth-client-config -a -p open_ldap
Error in updating the file: 'nss_netgroup' not found
--
Errors found.  Aborting (no changes made)

```

Far as I can tell, I've fulfilled every dependency to that point on the client.  This is against a known-good server (this client was just reformatted from Hardy to Intrepid).  I'm running through it again, but wanted to ask anyway.


I am also getting this error, my clients running Hardy and below work great. However my 3 new servers running intrepid I am trying to connect are getting the nss_netgroup not found error. 

A little poking around offered up two solutions of which I tried the first one and works great, have not tired the second one.

**solution one:**
add the following line below "nss_shadow=shadow: compat ldap" in /etc/auth-client-config/profile.d/open_ldap
> nss_netgroup=netgroup: compat ldap

**solution two:**
add the following line below "nss_shadow=shadow: compat ldap" in /etc/auth-client-config/profile.d/open_ldap
> nss_netgroup=netgroup: nis

---

### Post by bLaCkMeTaLL on 2008-11-27
I quote this message since I experienced a similar problem.
Some time during my experiments, I wasn't able to create new users, receiving the following error:

Can't call method "get_value" on an undefined value at /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd line 204.

I think the problem is related to the ldap db, because when doing "getent passwd" I wasnt able to see the ldap groups, etc.
I deleted one file inside /var/lib/ldap/ and relaunched the daemon; after figuring the daemon was working, I added an user and it simply worked.

This probably happened because I added some extra indexes inside slapd.conf _after_ loading the db (to solve the issue, I commented them out too).

I also wish to express my gratitude to rickyjones for sharing with us this magnificient tutorial: you rock! :guitar:

> **sopsaare said:**
> Hey all!

I got rid of all those problems. It seems to me that somehow the smbldap-populate -g 30000 didnt work. It just used GID's from 500+. So it ****** things up, but I modified the values by hand and got rid of that problem. Then I saw that somehow dpkg hadn't make the "ldap.secret" file. But I made it by hand and got rid of those problems too. 

Then I faced some problems with "Network path not found" while I tried to join the domain. I fooled around with the windows and linux network configurations and got rid of it. Then I was able to join the domain. So first step closed.

Now I got into another problem:

root@lin:~# smbldap-useradd -a sampo
Can't call method "get_value" on an undefined value at /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd line 204.
root@lin:~#

The "-a" is only switch that doesn't work, anyone have any idea what could cause this??

### Post by uKrease on 2008-12-03
OK so I installed 8.04 and I`ve got a whole bunch further than I previously did, with one problem :

When I run smbldap-populate -u 30000 -g 30000 I get

failed to add entry : no global superior knowledge at /usr/sbin/smbldap-populate line 499

According to the openldap.org documentation this means :
"C.1.16. ldap_add/delete/modify/rename: no global superior knowledge

If the target entry name places is not within any of the databases the server is configured to hold and the server has no knowledge of a global superior, the server will indicate it is unwilling to perform the operation and provide the text "no global superior knowledge" as additional text.

Likely the entry name is incorrect, or the server is not properly configured to hold the named entry, or, in distributed directory environments, a default referral was not configured."

WTF does that mean ?

Any ideas on how to fix this ?

Thanks for your help

```

root@test-ubuntu64:/etc/ldap# cat ldap.conf
###DEBCONF###
##
## Configuration of this file will be managed by debconf as long as the
## first line of the file says '###DEBCONF###'
##
## You should use dpkg-reconfigure to configure this file via debconf
##

#
# @(#)$Id: ldap.conf,v 1.38 2006/05/15 08:13:31 lukeh Exp $
#
# This is the configuration file for the LDAP nameservice
# switch library and the LDAP PAM module.
#
# PADL Software
# http://www.padl.com
#

# Your LDAP server. Must be resolvable without using LDAP.
# Multiple hosts may be specified, each separated by a 
# space. How long nss_ldap takes to failover depends on
# whether your LDAP client library supports configurable
# network or connect timeouts (see bind_timelimit).
#host 127.0.0.1
# MY Server's internal IP is:
host 192.168.1.1

# The distinguished name of the search base.
base dc=example,dc=local

# Another way to specify your LDAP server is to provide an
# Unix Domain Sockets to connect to a local LDAP Server.
#uri ldap://127.0.0.1/
#uri ldaps://127.0.0.1/   
#uri ldapi://%2fvar%2frun%2fldapi_sock/
# Note: %2f encodes the '/' used as directory separator
uri ldap://srv1.example.local/

# The LDAP version to use (defaults to 3
# if supported by client library)
ldap_version 3

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with.
# Optional: default is to bind anonymously.
#binddn cn=proxyuser,dc=padl,dc=com

# The credentials to bind with. 
# Optional: default is no credential.
#bindpw secret

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with
# if the effective user ID is root. Password is
# stored in /etc/ldap.secret (mode 600)
rootbinddn cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local

# The port.
# Optional: default is 389.
#port 389

# The search scope.
#scope sub
#scope one
#scope base

# Search timelimit
#timelimit 30

# Bind/connect timelimit
#bind_timelimit 30

# Reconnect policy: hard (default) will retry connecting to
# the software with exponential backoff, soft will fail
# immediately.
#bind_policy hard
bind_policy soft

[...]

pam_password md5

[...]

```

-rw-------  1 root root      13 2008-12-04 22:55 ldap.secret

```

root@test-ubuntu64:/etc/auth-client-config/profile.d# cat open_ldap 
[open_ldap]
nss_passwd=passwd: compat ldap
nss_group=group: compat ldap

nss_netgroup=netgroup: compat ldap

root@test-ubuntu64:/etc# cat pam.d/common-account
#
# /etc/pam.d/common-account - authorization settings common to all services

# pre_auth-client-config # account	[success=1 default=ignore]	pam_ldap.so 
# pre_auth-client-config # # here's the fallback if no module succeeds
# pre_auth-client-config # account	requisite			pam_deny.so
# prime the stack with a positive return value if there isn't one already;
# this avoids us returning an error just because nothing sets a success code
# since the modules above will each just jump around

# pre_auth-client-config # # and here are more per-package modules (the "Additional" block)
# pre_auth-client-config # # end of pam-auth-update config

account    sufficient  pam_ldap.so
account    required    pam_deny.so

#
# /etc/pam.d/common-password - password-related modules common to all services
#
# This file is included from other service-specific PAM config files,
# and should contain a list of modules that define the services to be
# used to change user passwords.  The default is pam_unix.

# Explanation of pam_unix options:
#
# The "sha512" option enables salted SHA512 passwords.  Without this option,
# the default is Unix crypt.  Prior releases used the option "md5".
#
# The "obscure" option replaces the old `OBSCURE_CHECKS_ENAB' option in
# login.defs.
#
# See the pam_unix manpage for other options.

password  required    pam_deny.so

# pre_auth-client-config # # and here are more per-package modules (the "Additional" block)
# pre_auth-client-config # session	required	pam_unix.so 
# pre_auth-client-config # session	optional			pam_ldap.so 
# pre_auth-client-config # session	optional			pam_ck_connector.so nox11
# pre_auth-client-config # # end of pam-auth-update config
session    required    pam_limits.so
session    required    pam_mkhomedir.so skel=/etc/skel/ umask=0077
session    required    pam_unix.so
session    optional    pam_ldap.so

```

Note that I slightly modified the various /etc/pam.d/common-* in respect to the original /etc/auth-client-config/profile.d/open_ldap

Thanks in advance for anybody able/willing to help!

---

> hello rick...thaks for this wonderful tutorial... im afraid im missing something and it's not working for me...

i have followed your guide, except for that i already have a dns server with a primary zone for my domain "my.javier.com"

so i did not *install the BIND DNS Server*, nor did i *configure BIND9 and the Primary DNS Zone* and definitely  did not *configure the Server to use Itself for DNS*...

i think that my dns server is working fine since i made a nslookup pdc.my.javier.com from my dns server i get:

```

$nslookup pdc.my.javier.com
Server:         192.168.4.20
Address:        192.168.4.20#53

Name:   pdc.my.javier.com
Address: 192.168.4.23

```

this is correct, my dns server ip's is 192.168.4.20 and my pdc ip's is 192.168.4.23

however i cant make a windows xp client to join the Domain, i just get an error message on the client saying that the parameters are wrong.... i'm pretty sure i put my domain, user and password right...

can you help me please?

Windows domains usually require the domain controller to be a DNS server.  When I was demoing Server 2008, I forgot to set it up as DNS - the clients didn't react very well.  There may be a way to get around it, but you're probably best off setting up bind.

### Post by skillllllz on 2008-12-17
> **shizakapayou said:**

Very nice. For you, has this replaced Active Directory completely? What are some caveats, if any, for others looking to do the same?

I have tested this in the lab, and although everything appears to work perfectly, I am hesitant to deploy this in a production environment; I'd like to know what challenges others have faced.

### Post by shizakapayou on 2008-12-18
> **skillllllz said:**
> Very nice. For you, has this replaced Active Directory completely? What are some caveats, if any, for others looking to do the same?

I have tested this in the lab, and although everything appears to work perfectly, I am hesitant to deploy this in a production environment; I'd like to know what challenges others have faced.

When I started this job, there was basically no IT infrastructure.  They had around 35 employees at this main office, 50 computers or so, and everyone just logged in to their machines locally (many with no password).  The "file server" was an old Dimension running XP Home.

My first objective, obviously, was getting a domain in place.  Unfortunately, Windows Server was going to cost around $4,000 for software and licensing, not to mention a server.  I'd been using Ubuntu at home for a few years (though hardly an expert) so I decided to pursue that route instead.

So, yes, this is in place of Active Directory, though that was never used here.  My server:

PowerEdge 2950 III
2 x Quad-core Xeon 2.0GHz
8 GB RAM
6 x 500GB HDD in RAID 5 (/home is a 2.0TB partition with several network shares)

I'm looking into adding a direct-attach-storage device and a Quantum 16-cartridge LTO-4 tape autoloader to the server next year.  I need a ton more storage and some way to back it up.

As for issues, my previous job was a Windows environment with around 250 computers.  Management of those desktops (again, all Windows as I have now) was definitely easier since I could employ Group Policy on a wide scale.  The ability to use WSUS to ensure updates to the clients is fantastic, as is locking the machines down with GPO.  That said, my environment is much smaller now, so it's not as big an issue.  I still use group policy, just on the local machine instead of domain-wide.  You can do it remotely with this command from the Run box in Windows:

```
gpedit.msc /gpcomputer: Computername
```

I've used that on several machines to disable reboots after updates, etc.  It's a bit tedious, but it works.

My only problem has actually been users can't change their passwords on their own.  Not a big deal here, and apparently it works for most other folks in this thread.

The moral of all this....if you have a smaller company of Windows boxes, and don't want to pay the licensing, this is a great way to go.  A large company with the money to spend would probably appreciate the management aspects Windows Server would allow you.  In my case, this works out pretty well.

---

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-12-18
> **shizakapayou said:**
>  So, yes, this is in place of Active Directory .... I use SUSE as Windows domain + LDAP server, but Ubuntu as client OS ("apt" and the many repos with so much software to choose from is exactly what I want on the clients!). The advantage I see with SUSE on the server is that all the things from this very cool guide here can be accomplished easily in a couple of minutes (!!) --SUSE's setup program "yast" takes care of everything!-- where as doing the same with Ubuntu is a rather complicated and tedious task and obviously (guessing from the many messages where people are having problems) prone to error.

I really really wish Canonical would do something about this and make creating and running a directory server on top of Ubuntu way way way easier than it is now.

I don't have the time to spend 2-3 days with making OpenLDAP + SAMBA work and act as servers on Ubuntu. My bosses would kill me. 

Yes, this guide is very cool. But if "ease of use" is becoming an issue I would suggest to look at other distributions too, e.g. SUSE, CentOS, eBox ... Running OpenLDAP + Samba on Ubuntu is neither the easiest way and neither is it the only way. Just to mention this .... ;)

### Post by scorp123 on 2008-12-18
> **skillllllz said:**
> Do you know if SUSE, CentOS, or any other distros are easy to setup and use as a directory server from the CLI? SUSE's "yast" also works tip top in text mode, it then uses a so called "ncurses" text-interface instead of a graphical one. You still get to navigate menus etc. and it's still very very easy. This is precisely how I use it.

CentOS and RHEL use a similar way, e.g. most of their commands (usually "system-....." something) also have a "ncurses" variant; so they too can work in a text environment and yet remain friendly and easy to use.

Nobody please get me wrong here ... I am a fan of Ubuntu. I am a fan of the command shell. I love to use the shell .... But managing a directory server can be complex, and those chains of LDAP commands are not exactly easy and it's very easy to screw up. So a little bit "ease of use" isn't wrong at all, especially if you're a (lazy) admin and have tons of other important things to take care of ...

IMHO when evaluating something you should look at three things:

1. How powerful is it? e.g. can we really do some serious work with this?
2. How usable is it? e.g. can this be used "on the frontline"?
3. How manageable is it? e.g. can you still manage this while your boss is screaming at you over the phone because something isn't quite working right ... ?

Let's look at this here:

1. How powerful is it? Stupid question. This stuff here is very powerful and yes you can do some serious stuff with it. This also answers question 2.): yes, as demonstrated you can use this stuff for real in a productive environment .... BUT: Point 3.) is where all this falls flat onto its nose, unfortunately. If things go wrong then whatever I do has to happen fast or else I will have my users and ultimately my boss jumping at my throat ...  And this is where I personally find Ubuntu and the spartanic tools it provides (as far as using it as a directory server is concerned) quite a bit lacking, unfortunately. So for me it had to be something different, something I feel comfortable with, even when there is an emergency and lots of stress ... 

So if you really plan on using this for production: ask yourself those questions above. If you're fine with all three points above ... perfect. But if not you better start evaluating something else. ;)
.
.
.
.
.

### Post by heckheck on 2008-12-23
I have recently been experimenting with setting up LDAP on my Linux server to accommodate NFS authentication for Vista, which doesn't have built in UID/GID mapping, unlike the previous Services For Linux 3.5 (SFU) that MS provided for XP.  I have followed the steps outlined in the first post by rickyjones, I have gotten a working openLDAP server running.  I was able to get Windows Vista Ultimate 64 bit to join the domain I created (home.local), and it seems to be authenticating the LDAP user I created (heck).

I have run into problems getting an NFS exported mount point to mount as a network drive, however.  Under Vista, the drive mounts, but I have been unable to get it to mount with any credentials (it mounts as uid -2, gid -2).  I have installed Services for NFS and Subsystem for Unix-based Applications.  I edited the Services for NFS Properties dialog to enable 'Active Directory domain name' as 'home' as the Identity mapping source.  Is this the correct way to way to configure this property?  When I try to mount the drive, I am logged into Vista on the 'home domain' as HOME\heck.

I have attached a Wireshark packet trace of an attempt to establish the mapping of the network drive.  Here are the details of my network configuration.

Linux Server running Ubuntu 8.04 is named 'heckmedia' and has IP address 10.100.200.60
Vista Ultimate 64 is named 'heckquad' and has IP address 10.100.200.144
LDAP user logged into Vista is 'heck'
LDAP domain is home.local
Vista machine 'heckquad' is using 10.100.200.60 for DNS, and the Linux box is running bind9 as part of the LDAP setup
The exported NFS directory I am trying to mount is 'datatest', and has UID/GID credentials matching the LDAP entry for 'heck' (these ended up being UID 30000, GID 513, which I could see using PHP to query LDAP under Linux)

From the trace, the early part is the booting of Vista, and the logon of HOME\heck.  The mount operation starts around timestamp 73.89 sec, packet 512.  It seems Vista first tries to use SMB (Samba?) to find the mountpoint, and then some sort of HTTP mounting.  When those fail, it tries LDAP, and runs into a problem around packet 559 (73.986471 time) with the bindRequest response, where LDAP in is complaining of invalid credentials (realm changed to 'home').  I see in packet 557 that the realm in the response to the first bindRequest (556) contains a string saying the realm was 'heckmedia', which is the name of the Linux server.
I'm sure there is just something off in the drive mapping process, where the network name of the linux server is being substituted for the realm.  I just don't know enough to figure out how to fix it.

Anyway, I thought someone here could shed light on what may be going wrong.  I'm at my wits end trying to get this to work, and am about ready to throw in the towel on Vista and go back to XP, where NFS just worked in my network.

Thanks for any help you can provide,

-Jim Heck

### Post by NaughtyusMaximus on 2009-01-19
I've searched through here, but haven't found anything yet - so apologies if this has already been mentioned and I missed it.

I currently have a Windows 2003 AD server (primary and secondary).  I'd like to replace these servers with Ubuntu.  I've been able to get Ubuntu to work as a PDC/BDC for a *new* domain, but what I'd really like to do is have all of my current info (stored in the Windows AD servers) migrated over to Ubuntu.. Is this possible?

---

### Post by scorp123 on 2009-01-20
> **NaughtyusMaximus said:**
>  ... but what I'd really like to do is have all of my current info (stored in the Windows AD servers) migrated over to Ubuntu.. Is this possible? Short answer: No.

Long answer: Samba3 does not yet offer the same functions that Microsoft has put into their "Active Directory" (AD is far far more than just "Samba + LDAP + Kerberos" ...!) Samba4 is supposed to have all the functionality ... but it's not ready yet. See here:

[http://www.techworld.com.au/article/273515/active_directory_comes_linux_samba_4](http://www.techworld.com.au/article/273515/active_directory_comes_linux_samba_4)

---

### Post by NaughtyusMaximus on 2009-01-20
All that I use AD for is user logins, and authentication to smb shares (on another server).  I know these components should work - so the only show stopper for me right now is figuring out how to migrate data off of the current Windows servers.

---

### Post by whoop on 2009-01-20
Looks like I was not the only one having trouble with the /ldap_data directory.
Various people have reported "permission denied" problems (in this thread) when trying to configure slapd to use this directory.
I figured out that this error occurs due to apparmor (slapd was added to apparmor in ubuntu 8.04), and this newly created directory is not known to apparmor.
I have bypassed this via manual editing of usr.bin.slapd because I couldn't figure out how to use aa-logprof.
slapd starts up now (with the /ldap_data directory) and I can see OpenLDAP running on port 389.

here's my /etc/apparmor.d/usr.sbin.slapd
```

# vim:syntax=apparmor

#include <tunables/global>

/usr/sbin/slapd {
  #include <abstractions/base>
  #include <abstractions/nameservice>

  #include <abstractions/ssl_certs>
  /etc/ssl/private/ r,
  /etc/ssl/private/* r,

  /etc/sasldb2 r,

  capability dac_override,
  capability net_bind_service,
  capability setgid,
  capability setuid,

  /etc/gai.conf r,
  /etc/hosts.allow r,
  /etc/hosts.deny r,
  /etc/ldap/ldap.conf r,
  /etc/ldap/schema/* r,
  /etc/ldap/slapd.conf r,

  # the databases and logs
  /var/lib/ldap/ r,
  /var/lib/ldap/* rw,
  /ldap_data/ r,
  /ldap_data/* rw,

  # lock file
  /var/lib/ldap/alock kw,
  /ldap_data/alock kw,

  # pid files and sockets
  /var/run/slapd/* w,

  /usr/lib/ldap/ r,
  /usr/lib/ldap/* mr,

  /usr/sbin/slapd mr,
}


```

Basically all I did replicate every line with /var/lib/ldap/ and changed it to /ldap_data/
Feel free to criticize this modification as I didn't fool around with apparmor profiles before. There could be a much cleaner way to do this.

Hope this helps you guys getting this wonderful tutorial working on newer ubuntu versions.

---

### Post by mspisars on 2009-01-21
Ricky, first I want to thank you for the great How-To.

I have been able to use this how-to to setup Samba + OpenLDAP on Ubuntu Server 8.10 (note not 7.10) Intrepid Ibex.

Here are one major addition that you might want to consider adding to the how-to for future version to be compatible with the current releases.

With Ubuntu 8.10, OpenLDAP no longer uses slapd.conf but rather the slapd.d directory with the ldif files.

The following instructions are a way to work around that and still use a slapd.conf file for applications that are not updated to the latest OpenLDAP standards and require getting data from the slapd.conf file to function:

Modify the slapd.conf file according to your needs. [Mainly based on the How-To + your configuration]

Next we will update the configuration of OpenLDAP.

```
/etc/init.d/slapd stop
```

Backup the current slapd.d directory.

Change to the ldap directory: 

```
cd /etc/ldap
```

Move the old slapd.d to a backup location:

```
mv slapd.d slapd.d.bck
```

Now we will create the slapd.d directory and load the config from the slapd.conf file.
```

mkdir slapd.d
slaptest -f slapd.conf -F slapd.d
```

You should see:  config file testing succeeded as result.

Next we set the ownership of the slapd.d directory to openldap.

```
chown -R openldap:openldap slapd.d
```

And now we can restart OpenLDAP.

```
/etc/init.d/slapd start
```

If you don't see any errors, OpenLDAP is up and running with the new configuration.

You can repeat these steps each time that you change the slapd.conf file in order to load the new OpenLDAP configuration.

It is in any case a good idea to have a slapd.conf file since some scripts and applications look at that file to check the OpenLDAP configuration regardless of the fact that OpenLDAP is now configured using the slapd.d directory. 

Hope this helps for you in the near future...

Michael

---

### Post by whoop on 2009-01-21
Still trying to get this running under 8.04.1 (64 bit). As I previously stated you need to change the apparmor profile to get the /ldap_data/ directory working.

There are also people getting into trouble when running the following commands:
```

net getlocalsid

```
and
```

smbldap-populate -u 30000 -g 30000

```

People are reporting various authentication related problems. I have resolved the problem via the following routine (apply these steps before running the commands described above):
```

slappasswd

```
this command will ask for a password (twice) enter "12345" it will return an ssha hash in the following format:
```

{SSHA}XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX

```
open /etc/ldap/slapd.conf
and add/edit the following:
```

rootdn cn=admin,dc=example,dc=local
rootpw {SSHA}XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX

```

Now reboot or restart and the commands should run fine.
I'm off finding other problems now :-) It would be nice to get/keep this tutorial updated; at least for every LTS version until there is a better way to do this (maybe samba 4).

### Post by mspisars on 2009-01-21
> **whoop said:**
> Thanks for keeping this how-to updated for the later ubuntu versions, mspisars.
I am currently trying to get this to run under the current LTS version; posting changes here as I go allong.

It's not that I don't think that the new directory structure is bad, but to go from one file to a bunch of separate files is more cumbersome and I believe would add more then 10 pages to this almost 60 page How-To.

For instance to add just the Includes you would have to create a temp file then load then file into the directory with the proper command. I tried to install it using the new directory structure for Samba it is not worth the headache. Maybe is worth it for newer utilities and services. Maybe Samba 4? but then again they will have a built in LDAP directory in Samba 4.

Also, my comments about getting this to work on 8.10 Ibex, I did not create the /ldap_data directory as I did not care for a RAID 1 setup when my whole server is configured to with RAID 5.

Michael

### Post by whoop on 2009-01-21
I'm having trouble with the client(s) at the moment. I am using Ubuntu Intrepid Ibex 64 bit as a client. I have seen this problem in previous posts in this thread but I didn't see a solution for it (yet).

When I run this command I get the following error:
```

mount dc01-ubuntu.example.local:/ldaphome /ldaphome 
mount.nfs: can't get address for dc01-ubuntu.example.local

```
When I ping dc01-ubuntu.example.local it does not resolve, but when I ping dc01-ubuntu it does, 192.168.0.60 also resolves. I can even mount dc01-ubuntu:/ldaphome /ldaphome. 
When I ping dc01-ubuntu.example.local from the server it does resolve.

search example.local
nameserver 192.168.0.60

### Post by mspisars on 2009-01-22
Whoop,

For me I used only the first part of the domain (meaning the machine name) not the FQDN x.y.com i just used x. Otherwise you would have to copy your hosts file from one machine to the other, and make sure that your client is pointing to the bind9 DNS server that you set up as well.

Regards,

Michael

---

### Post by whoop on 2009-01-22
> **mspisars said:**
> Whoop,

For me I used only the first part of the domain (meaning the machine name) not the FQDN x.y.com i just used x. Otherwise you would have to copy your hosts file from one machine to the other, and make sure that your client is pointing to the bind9 DNS server that you set up as well.

Regards,

Michael

Thanks Michael, I know this works as I am able to ping dc01-ubuntu. But my point was that it should be possible to ping dc01-ubuntu.example.local as well, but we can't seem to be able to do that. If that's not supposed to be possible what the hell are local FQDN supposed to be for.
I mean, is this a bug, or are we miss configuring or something?

---

### Post by whoop on 2009-01-22
> **whoop said:**
> Thanks Michael, I know this works as I am able to ping dc01-ubuntu. But my point was that it should be possible to ping dc01-ubuntu.example.local as well, but we can't seem to be able to do that. If that's not supposed to be possible what the hell are local FQDN supposed to be for.
I mean, is this a bug, or are we miss configuring or something?

OK, I figured it out.

Short answer: do not use .local as avahi does not allow it.

If you must use .local (in any way) disable avahi or edit /etc/nsswitch.conf (unrecommended) this line:
```

hosts: files mdns4_minimal [NOTFOUND=return] dns mdns4

```
to this:
```

hosts: files dns mdns4

```
or this(unofficial):
```

hosts: files dns mdns4_minimal [NOTFOUND=return] mdns4

```

---
