### Post by flesh99 on 2009-02-05
With a few minor changes I combined [https://help.ubuntu.com/8.10/serverguide/C/samba-ldap.html](https://help.ubuntu.com/8.10/serverguide/C/samba-ldap.html) and this how-to for an 8.10 install. Everything seems to work locally however I cannot join the domain with an XP box. The error displayed on the screen is "The user name could not be found".

Here is the output of log level 2 (and believe me I wish I understood more of this):


```

[2009/02/05 17:38:59,  0] lib/util_sock.c:write_data(1059)
[2009/02/05 17:38:59,  0] lib/util_sock.c:get_peer_addr_internal(1596)
  getpeername failed. Error was Transport endpoint is not connected
  write_data: write failure in writing to client 0.0.0.0. Error Connection reset by peer
[2009/02/05 17:38:59,  0] smbd/process.c:srv_send_smb(74)
  Error writing 4 bytes to client. -1. (Transport endpoint is not connected)
[2009/02/05 17:38:59,  2] lib/smbldap.c:smbldap_open_connection(796)
  smbldap_open_connection: connection opened
[2009/02/05 17:38:59,  2] passdb/pdb_ldap.c:init_sam_from_ldap(571)
  init_sam_from_ldap: Entry found for user: root
[2009/02/05 17:38:59,  2] auth/auth.c:check_ntlm_password(308)
  check_ntlm_password:  authentication for user [root] -> [root] -> [root] succeeded
[2009/02/05 17:39:09,  2] lib/smbldap.c:smbldap_open_connection(796)
  smbldap_open_connection: connection opened
[2009/02/05 17:39:09,  2] passdb/pdb_ldap.c:init_sam_from_ldap(571)
  init_sam_from_ldap: Entry found for user: root
[2009/02/05 17:39:09,  2] auth/auth.c:check_ntlm_password(308)
  check_ntlm_password:  authentication for user [root] -> [root] -> [root] succeeded
[2009/02/05 17:39:09,  2] rpc_server/srv_samr_nt.c:_samr_LookupDomain(3490)
  Returning domain sid for domain SNOWCRASH -> S-1-5-21-535846975-1327043847-2130526193

```

I did notice the log didn't mention not finding a user and that root connected and authenticated so I am not getting the user not found issue. I will keep googling but after last night and most of today I decided to stop being stubborn and post here. I am willing to admit that after 80+ pages I may have missed a fix but I did find some and none of them worked.

Here are my configs and hopefully someone can spot a typo or a stupid mistake. Just to note that I had the same results with the roaming profiles and wins disabled. Since they are going to be used in my final config I figured I'd leave them in.

**smb.conf**
```

[global]
    workgroup = SNOWCRASH
    server string = %h server (Samba, Ubuntu)
    passdb backend = ldapsam:ldap://localhost/
    passwd program = /usr/bin/passwd %u
    passwd chat = *Enter\snew\sUNIX\spassword:* %n\n *Retype\snew\sUNIX\spassword:* %n\n *passwd:*password\supdated\ssuccessfully* .
    log level = 2
    syslog = 0
    log file = /var/log/samba/log.%m
    max log size = 10000
    name resolve order = lmhosts host wins bcast
    add user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -m %u
    delete user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-userdel %u
    add group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupadd -p %g
    delete group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupdel %g
    add user to group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -m %u %g
    delete user from group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -x %u" %g
    set primary group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-usermod -g %g" %u
    add machine script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -w %u
    logon script = allusers.bat
    logon path = \\%N\profiles
    logon drive = H:
    domain logons = Yes
    wins support = Yes
    ldap admin dn = cn=admin,dc=myth-cubed,dc=potter,dc=local
    ldap delete dn = Yes
    ldap group suffix = ou=Groups
    ldap idmap suffix = dc=myth-cubed,dc=potter,dc=local
    ldap machine suffix = ou=Computers
    ldap passwd sync = Yes
    ldap suffix = dc=myth-cubed,dc=potter,dc=local
    ldap user suffix = ou=Users
    panic action = /usr/share/samba/panic-action %d
    idmap uid = 10000-20000
    idmap gid = 10000-20000
    template shell = /bin/bash

[homes]
    comment = Home Directories
    read only = No
    create mask = 0700
    directory mask = 0700
    browseable = No

[netlogon]
    comment = Network Logon Service
    path = /home/netlogon
    guest ok = Yes
    share modes = No

[profiles]
    comment = Users profiles
    path = /home/profiles
    create mask = 0600
    directory mask = 0700
    browseable = No

```

**ldap.conf**
```

###DEBCONF###
##
## Configuration of this file will be managed by debconf as long as the
## first line of the file says '###DEBCONF###'
##
## You should use dpkg-reconfigure to configure this file via debconf
##

#
# @(#)$Id: ldap.conf,v 1.38 2006/05/15 08:13:31 lukeh Exp $
#
# This is the configuration file for the LDAP nameservice
# switch library and the LDAP PAM module.
#
# PADL Software
# http://www.padl.com
#

# Your LDAP server. Must be resolvable without using LDAP.
# Multiple hosts may be specified, each separated by a 
# space. How long nss_ldap takes to failover depends on
# whether your LDAP client library supports configurable
# network or connect timeouts (see bind_timelimit).
#host 127.0.0.1

# The distinguished name of the search base.
base dc=mythcubed,dc=potter,dc=local

# Another way to specify your LDAP server is to provide an
uri ldap://127.0.0.1:389/
# Unix Domain Sockets to connect to a local LDAP Server.
#uri ldap://127.0.0.1/
#uri ldaps://127.0.0.1/   
#uri ldapi://%2fvar%2frun%2fldapi_sock/
# Note: %2f encodes the '/' used as directory separator

# The LDAP version to use (defaults to 3
# if supported by client library)
ldap_version 3

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with.
# Optional: default is to bind anonymously.
#binddn cn=proxyuser,dc=padl,dc=com

# The credentials to bind with. 
# Optional: default is no credential.
#bindpw secret

# The distinguished name to bind to the server with
# if the effective user ID is root. Password is
# stored in /etc/ldap.secret (mode 600)
rootbinddn cn=manager,cn=myth-cubed,cn=potter,cn=local

# The port.
# Optional: default is 389.
#port 389

# The search scope.
#scope sub
#scope one
#scope base

# Search timelimit
#timelimit 30

# Bind/connect timelimit
#bind_timelimit 30

# Reconnect policy: hard (default) will retry connecting to
# the software with exponential backoff, soft will fail
# immediately.
bind_policy soft

# Idle timelimit; client will close connections
# (nss_ldap only) if the server has not been contacted
# for the number of seconds specified below.
#idle_timelimit 3600

# Filter to AND with uid=%s
#pam_filter objectclass=account

# The user ID attribute (defaults to uid)
#pam_login_attribute uid

# Search the root DSE for the password policy (works
# with Netscape Directory Server)
#pam_lookup_policy yes

# Check the 'host' attribute for access control
# Default is no; if set to yes, and user has no
# value for the host attribute, and pam_ldap is
# configured for account management (authorization)
# then the user will not be allowed to login.
#pam_check_host_attr yes

# Check the 'authorizedService' attribute for access
# control
# Default is no; if set to yes, and the user has no
# value for the authorizedService attribute, and
# pam_ldap is configured for account management
# (authorization) then the user will not be allowed
# to login.
#pam_check_service_attr yes

# Group to enforce membership of
#pam_groupdn cn=PAM,ou=Groups,dc=padl,dc=com

# Group member attribute
#pam_member_attribute uniquemember

# Specify a minium or maximum UID number allowed
#pam_min_uid 0
#pam_max_uid 0

# Template login attribute, default template user
# (can be overriden by value of former attribute
# in user's entry)
#pam_login_attribute userPrincipalName
#pam_template_login_attribute uid
#pam_template_login nobody

# HEADS UP: the pam_crypt, pam_nds_passwd,
# and pam_ad_passwd options are no
# longer supported.
#
# Do not hash the password at all; presume
# the directory server will do it, if
# necessary. This is the default.
pam_password md5

# Hash password locally; required for University of
# Michigan LDAP server, and works with Netscape
# Directory Server if you're using the UNIX-Crypt
# hash mechanism and not using the NT Synchronization
# service. 
#pam_password crypt

# Remove old password first, then update in
# cleartext. Necessary for use with Novell
# Directory Services (NDS)
#pam_password clear_remove_old
#pam_password nds

# RACF is an alias for the above. For use with
# IBM RACF
#pam_password racf

# Update Active Directory password, by
# creating Unicode password and updating
# unicodePwd attribute.
#pam_password ad

# Use the OpenLDAP password change
# extended operation to update the password.
#pam_password exop

# Redirect users to a URL or somesuch on password
# changes.
#pam_password_prohibit_message Please visit http://internal to change your password.

# RFC2307bis naming contexts
# Syntax:
# nss_base_XXX        base?scope?filter
# where scope is {base,one,sub}
# and filter is a filter to be &'d with the
# default filter.
# You can omit the suffix eg:
# nss_base_passwd    ou=People,
# to append the default base DN but this
# may incur a small performance impact.
#nss_base_passwd    ou=People,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_shadow    ou=People,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_group        ou=Group,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_hosts        ou=Hosts,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_services    ou=Services,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_networks    ou=Networks,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_protocols    ou=Protocols,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_rpc        ou=Rpc,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_ethers    ou=Ethers,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_netmasks    ou=Networks,dc=padl,dc=com?ne
#nss_base_bootparams    ou=Ethers,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_aliases    ou=Aliases,dc=padl,dc=com?one
#nss_base_netgroup    ou=Netgroup,dc=padl,dc=com?one

# attribute/objectclass mapping
# Syntax:
#nss_map_attribute    rfc2307attribute    mapped_attribute
#nss_map_objectclass    rfc2307objectclass    mapped_objectclass

# configure --enable-nds is no longer supported.
# NDS mappings
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member

# Services for UNIX 3.5 mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount User
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount User
#nss_map_attribute uid msSFU30Name
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember msSFU30PosixMember
#nss_map_attribute userPassword msSFU30Password
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFU30HomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFUHomeDirectory
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup Group
#pam_login_attribute msSFU30Name
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# configure --enable-mssfu-schema is no longer supported.
# Services for UNIX 2.0 mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount User
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount user
#nss_map_attribute uid msSFUName
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember posixMember
#nss_map_attribute userPassword msSFUPassword
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory msSFUHomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute shadowLastChange pwdLastSet
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup Group
#nss_map_attribute cn msSFUName
#pam_login_attribute msSFUName
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# RFC 2307 (AD) mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount user
#nss_map_objectclass shadowAccount user
#nss_map_attribute uid sAMAccountName
#nss_map_attribute homeDirectory unixHomeDirectory
#nss_map_attribute shadowLastChange pwdLastSet
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup group
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member
#pam_login_attribute sAMAccountName
#pam_filter objectclass=User
#pam_password ad

# configure --enable-authpassword is no longer supported
# AuthPassword mappings
#nss_map_attribute userPassword authPassword

# AIX SecureWay mappings
#nss_map_objectclass posixAccount aixAccount
#nss_base_passwd ou=aixaccount,?one
#nss_map_attribute uid userName
#nss_map_attribute gidNumber gid
#nss_map_attribute uidNumber uid
#nss_map_attribute userPassword passwordChar
#nss_map_objectclass posixGroup aixAccessGroup
#nss_base_group ou=aixgroup,?one
#nss_map_attribute cn groupName
#nss_map_attribute uniqueMember member
#pam_login_attribute userName
#pam_filter objectclass=aixAccount
#pam_password clear

# Netscape SDK LDAPS
#ssl on

# Netscape SDK SSL options
#sslpath /etc/ssl/certs

# OpenLDAP SSL mechanism
# start_tls mechanism uses the normal LDAP port, LDAPS typically 636
#ssl start_tls
#ssl on

# OpenLDAP SSL options
# Require and verify server certificate (yes/no)
# Default is to use libldap's default behavior, which can be configured in
# /etc/openldap/ldap.conf using the TLS_REQCERT setting.  The default for
# OpenLDAP 2.0 and earlier is "no", for 2.1 and later is "yes".
#tls_checkpeer yes

# CA certificates for server certificate verification
# At least one of these are required if tls_checkpeer is "yes"
#tls_cacertfile /etc/ssl/ca.cert
#tls_cacertdir /etc/ssl/certs

# Seed the PRNG if /dev/urandom is not provided
#tls_randfile /var/run/egd-pool

# SSL cipher suite
# See man ciphers for syntax
#tls_ciphers TLSv1

# Client certificate and key
# Use these, if your server requires client authentication.
#tls_cert
#tls_key

# Disable SASL security layers. This is needed for AD.
#sasl_secprops maxssf=0

# Override the default Kerberos ticket cache location.
#krb5_ccname FILE:/etc/.ldapcache

# SASL mechanism for PAM authentication - use is experimental
# at present and does not support password policy control
#pam_sasl_mech DIGEST-MD5
nss_initgroups_ignoreusers avahi,avahi-autoipd,backup,bin,bind,daemon,games,gdm,gnats,haldaemon,irc,klog,libuuid,list,lp,mail,man,messagebus,mysql,mythtv,news,ntp,openldap,polkituser,proxy,root,sshd,statd,sync,sys,syslog,ubuntu,uucp,www-data

```

**nsswitch.conf**
```

# /etc/nsswitch.conf
#
# Example configuration of GNU Name Service Switch functionality.
# If you have the `glibc-doc-reference' and `info' packages installed, try:
# `info libc "Name Service Switch"' for information about this file.

passwd: compat ldap
group: compat ldap
shadow: compat ldap

hosts:          files wins dns mdns4
networks:       files

protocols:      db files
services:       db files
ethers:         db files
rpc:            db files

netgroup: compat ldap

```

I don't have a slapd.conf as I set that up using ldifs and not the exported conf file as shown earlier. I can verify that all the necessary pieces are added if need be. I am just hoping someone can take a quick glance and tell where I have screwed it up...before I dig my eyes out with that spoon I mentioned. Thanks in advance...

steps:[I]Type in your domain name - in our example the domain name to enter is simply "example"

### Post by thecha on 2009-02-10
Hello, so I'm going through the process of setting up Ubuntu 7.10 clients for my network.  I have the windows machines working very well!  

I have followed both of the following sections from the tutorial to the letter. And I am able to authenticate and login on the client computers from a terminal.  My home directory is working well etc.
[LIST=1]
[*]Configure Ubuntu Server 7.10 (client) to Mount NFS Shares
[*]Configure Ubuntu Server 7.10 (client) for LDAP Authentication
[/LIST]

Now the problem is that I would like to have Ubuntu Desktop so I installed it... 
```
sudo apt-get install ubuntu-desktop
```

When I rebooted I was able to Login with one of the user accounts from the server.  However I get a series of GNOME 
errors... They are as follows.

> The application "evolution-alarm-notify" attempted to change an aspect of your configuration that your system administrator or operating system vendor does not allow you to change. Some of the settings you have selected may not take effect, or may not be restored next time you use the application.
```
*lock. Perhaps the problem is that you attempted to use GConf from two machines at once, and ORBit still has its default configuration that prevents remote CORBA connections - put "ORBIIOPIPv4=1" in /etc/orbitrc. As always, check the user.* syslog for details on problems gconfd encountered. There can only be one gconfd per home directory, and it must own a lockfile in ~/.gconfd and also lockfiles in individual storage locations such as ~/.gconf
No database available to save your configuration: Unable to store a value at key '/apps/evolution/calendar/sources', as the configuration server has no writable databases. There are some common causes of this problem: 1) your configuration path file /etc/gconf/2/path doesn't contain any databases or wasn't found 2) somehow we mistakenly created two gconfd processes 3) your operating system is misconfigured so NFS file locking doesn't work in your home directory or 4) your NFS client machine crashed and didn't properly notify the server on reboot that file locks should be dropped. If you have two gconfd processes (or had two at the time the second was launched), logging out, killing all copies of gconfd, and logging back in may help. If you have stale locks, remove ~/.gconf*/*lock. Perhaps the problem is that you attempted to use GConf from two machines at once, and ORBit still has its default configuration that prevents remote CORBA connections - put "ORBIIOPIPv4=1" in /etc/orbitrc. As always, check the user.* syslog for details on problems gconfd encountered. There can only be one gconfd per home directory, and it must own a lockfile in ~/.gconfd and also lockfiles in individual storage locations such as ~/.gconf
```
> The application "gnome-panel" attempted to change an aspect of your configuration that your system administrator or operating system vendor does not allow you to change. Some of the settings you have selected may not take effect, or may not be restored next time you use the application.
```
No database available to save your configuration: Unable to store a value at key '/apps/panel/toplevels/top_panel_screen0/size', as the configuration server has no writable databases. There are some common causes of this problem: 1) your configuration path file /etc/gconf/2/path doesn't contain any databases or wasn't found 2) somehow we mistakenly created two gconfd processes 3) your operating system is misconfigured so NFS file locking doesn't work in your home directory or 4) your NFS client machine crashed and didn't properly notify the server on reboot that file locks should be dropped. If you have two gconfd processes (or had two at the time the second was launched), logging out, killing all copies of gconfd, and logging back in may help. If you have stale locks, remove ~/.gconf*/*lock. Perhaps the problem is that you attempted to use GConf from two machines at once, and ORBit still has its default configuration that prevents remote CORBA connections - put "ORBIIOPIPv4=1" in /etc/orbitrc. As always, check the user.* syslog for details on problems gconfd encountered. There can only be one gconfd per home directory, and it must own a lockfile in ~/.gconfd and also lockfiles in individual storage locations such as ~/.gconf
No database available to save your configuration: Unable to store a value at key '/apps/panel/general/toplevel_id_list', as the configuration server has no writable databases. There are some common causes of this problem: 1) your configuration path file /etc/gconf/2/path doesn't contain any databases or wasn't found 2) somehow we mistakenly created two gconfd processes 3) your operating system is misconfigured so NFS file locking doesn't work in your home directory or 4) your NFS client machine crashed and didn't properly notify the server on reboot that file locks should be dropped. If you have two gconfd processes (or had two at the time the second was launched), logging out, killing all copies of gconfd, and logging back in may help. If you have stale locks, remove ~/.gconf*/*lock. Perhaps the problem is that you attempted to use GConf from two machines at once, and ORBit still has its default configuration that prevents remote CORBA connections - put "ORBIIOPIPv4=1" in /etc/orbitrc. As always, check the user.* syslog for details on problems gconfd encountered. There can only be one gconfd per home directory, and it must own a lockfile in ~/.gconfd and also lockfiles in individual storage locations such as ~/.gconf

There are some common causes of this problem: 1) your configuration path file /etc/gconf/2/path doesn't contain any databases or wasn't found 2) somehow we mistakenly created two gconfd processes 3) your operating system is misconfigured so NFS file locking doesn't work in your home directory or 4) your NFS client machine crashed and didn't properly notify the server on reboot that file locks should be dropped. If you have two gconfd processes (or had two at the time the second was launched), logging out, killing all copies of gconfd, and logging back in may help. If you have stale locks, remove ~/.gconf*/*lock. Perhaps the problem is that you attempted to use GConf from two machines at once, and ORBit still has its default configuration that prevents remote CORBA connections - put "ORBIIOPIPv4=1" in /etc/orbitrc. As always, check the user.* syslog for details on problems gconfd encountered. There can only be one gconfd per home directory, and it must own a lockfile in ~/.gconfd and also lockfiles in individual storage locations such as ~/.gconf

If you have two gconfd processes (or had two at the time the second was launched), logging out, killing all copies of gconfd, and logging back in may help. If you have stale locks, remove ~/.gconf*/*lock. Perhaps the problem is that you attempted to use GConf from two machines at once, and ORBit still has its default configuration that prevents remote CORBA connections - put "ORBIIOPIPv4=1" in /etc/orbitrc. As always, check the user.* syslog for details on problems gconfd encountered. There can only be one gconfd per home directory, and it must own a lockfile in ~/.gconfd and also lockfiles in individual storage locations such as ~/.gconf

If you have two gconfd processes (or had two at the time the second was launched), logging out, killing all copies of gconfd, and logging back in may help. If you have stale locks, remove ~/.gconf*/*lock. Perhaps the problem is that you attempted to use GConf from two machines at once, and ORBit still has its default configuration that prevents remote CORBA connections - put "ORBIIOPIPv4=1" in /etc/orbitrc. As always, check the user.* syslog for details on problems gconfd encountered. There can only be one gconfd per home directory, and it must own a lockfile in ~/.gconfd and also lockfiles in individual storage locations such as ~/.gconf
No database available to save your configuration: Unable to store a value at key '/apps/panel/applets/deskbar_screen0/locked', as the configuration server has no writable databases. There are some common causes of this problem: 1) your configuration path file /etc/gconf/2/path doesn't contain any databases or wasn't found 2) somehow we mistakenly created two gconfd processes 3) your operating system is misconfigured so NFS file locking doesn't work in your home directory or 4) your NFS client machine crashed and didn't properly notify the server on reboot that file locks should be dropped. ### Post by eti.que on 2009-02-16
Hi guys,

The install worked flawlessly on my Ubuntu 8.10 brand new install until I had to log onto my Vista station (which got correctly added to the domain).

I figured out that smbldap-populate didn't add my main user (the one you enter during the Ubuntu installation) to the directory.

I thought I could just create it using smbldap-useradd and it worked, I managed to login on my Vista station... but I figured out it gave this user the uid 30001 while my users uid on the system is 1000.

So I installed phpldapadmin and changed the uid there (using smbldap-usermod didn't work, told me that the uid 1000 was already taken).

Now I wonder what to do.

I've to admit that I'm a little bit confused. Could someone explain me what I am missing here ?
- why isn't the first ubuntu user not populated by smbldap-populate ?
- aren't the ldap users and the unix users supposed to have the same uid?

Thanks a lot in advance !

### Post by maver1ck4000 on 2009-02-18
Hey all, I had a really hard time getting this set up, I have read about all the pages on this thread and have not seen much about a solution to what I was seeing.  I think it has a lot to do with my lack of understanding about how ubuntu services get set on start up, this is my first Ubuntu server because we are mostly a RHEL company, and I am use to using chkconfig to edit my services and all of that good stuff.  I followed this guide on page 1 to a "T", I did a fresh install of 7.10 and I was off, everything was extremely smooth until reboot and after that I was not able to log in.  I did a lot of looking and double and triple checking my work and couldn't find anything wrong, the login prompt would show up, I would type my user name and password and nothing, it would just hang, the computer would still be functioning, I could venture to other screens via ctrl+alt+F# and try to log in and it would do the same thing every time.  I was able to hit ctrl+alt+del to restart everytime.  I researched the services for ubuntu and I tried to add them with like update.rc or something along those lines and it would tell me that it's already a start up process.  I broke down and installed GDM aka ubuntu-desktop package.  Now to do all of this I had to boot in to recovery mode, and once GDM was installed I simply did a /etc/init.d/gdm start and was taken to a GUI login screen.  Once logged in I was able to go to System>Administration>Services and check the boxes to enable LDAP, Samba and even SSH as well as a few others that I wanted.  

I was able to reboot and log in and everything started, I completed the steps and was able to put some windows machines on the domain right away.  This is on a physical server, and its running very nicely now, I was unable to get this close with 8.10, mostly because I don't have as good of an understanding for how it works but 7.10 is pretty easy to get going once you are able to get the services to start on reboot.  I'm pretty sure PAM was trying to find something about ldap on login and the service just was either hung or just wasnt started so it caused the system to kind of stay in limbo looking for an LDAP server.

### Post by davethelorax on 2009-02-20
I followed the examples posted earlier in this thread and successfully got my system to use LDAP to authenticate both UNIX and SAMBA logons.  This is great.  However, when I started changing user passwords (logged on as the normal users), I got the following error from smbldap-passwd:

[B]Failed to modify SMB password: Insufficient access at /usr/sbin/smbldap-passwd line 238, <STDIN> line 3.
[/B]

The UNIX password does get updated, but the SAMBA password does not.  This leaves the account in an odd state with a new password for SSH sessions and the old password for connecting from a Windows client.

This seems like a permissions problem, or perhaps some sort of an ACL issue.  I have tinkered with the access ACLs in the slapd.conf file, but that didn't help.  This exact problem was referenced in this thread about 14 months ago but I haven't seen any solutions (I may have missed it).  Has anyone else solved this problem.  I'd like to be able to let users change their own passwords.

Thanks,

--- Dave

### Post by yelpspoon on 2009-02-22
I had this same issue with Server 8.04 running by-the-book with the alternate directory for the openldap directories.

So from this:
[http://www.openldap.org/lists/openldap-software/200808/msg00203.html](http://www.openldap.org/lists/openldap-software/200808/msg00203.html)

I found:
"Starting from Ubuntu 8.04, slapd is protected by an apparmor profile. Since you're using a non-standard directory for you database, you'll get a permission error. You should see audit messages in /var/log/kern.log related to slapd."

THE SOLUTION from here:
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/DebuggingApparmor](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/DebuggingApparmor)

Edit
/etc/apparmor.d/usr.sbin.slap

With (my diff output)
37,38c37,38
<   /var/lib/ldap/ r,
<   /var/lib/ldap/* rw,
---
>   /ldap_data/ r,
>   /ldap_data/* rw,
41c41
<   /var/lib/ldap/alock kw,
---
>   /ldap_data/alock kw,

Then issue the follow...or variation of:
# cat /etc/apparmor.d/usr.sbin.slap | apparmor_parser -r
# /etc/init.d/apparmor reload

I rebooted as it is easier than restarting all the dependent services.  nmapping and ps-ing showed all was well.

HTH,
David

> **danking12 said:**
> I am using Ubuntu Server 8.04 and having an issue with the tutorial, during the openldap installation and configuration I have gotten to the second reconfigure stage and attempt to start openldap using:

```
/etc/init.d/slapd start
```

I then receive the following error stating that openldap has failed to start:

```
root@vervet:~# /etc/init.d/slapd start
Starting OpenLDAP: slapd - failed.

The operation failed but no output was produced. For hints on what went wrong please refer to the system's logfiles (e.g. /var/log/syslog) or try running the daemon in Debug mode like via "slapd -d 16383" (warning this will create copious output).

Below, you can find the command line options used by this script to run slapd. Do not forget to specify those options if you want to look to debugging output:
slapd -g openldap -u openldap -f /etc/ldap/slapd.conf

```

In the syslog the following error was produced:

```

Jul 30 16:53:43 vervet slapd[4255]: @(#) $OpenLDAP: slapd 2.4.9 (Jun 16 2008 08:09:52) $ ^Ibuildd@palmer:/build/buildd/openldap2.3-2.4.9/debian/build/servers/slapd

Jul 30 16:41:58 vervet slapd[4255]: /etc/ldap/slapd.conf: line 63: invalid path: Permission denied

Jul 30 16:41:58 vervet slapd[4255]: slapd stopped.

Jul 30 16:41:58 vervet slapd[4255]: connections_destroy: nothing to destroy.

```

Line 63 refers to the directory parameter that above in the instructions told me to set to /ldap_data, which I did and does exist in my directory structure. The ldap_data directory has the following permissions which were also set earlier in the instructions:

```

drwxr-xr-x  2 openldap openldap ... ldap_data

```

### Post by Yoshimit on 2009-03-26
> **mummuluri said:**
> Hello 

I tried your brief documentation in company. every thing is working fine but users unable to change their passwords in windows systems.

thanks

Kiran

To allow users to change their own passwords, change this option in smb.conf:

from:
'unix password sync = yes'

to:
'unix password sync = no'

worked for me, on linux (smbpasswd command) and windows (ctrl+alt+del dialog)

hope this could help you.

Yoshimit

### Post by albinootje on 2009-04-02
> **niko123 said:**
>  How come i don't see the desktop while trying out this tutorial...all i get is a black screen, where i can enter all my commands? How can i change it to see the desktop?


You installed the Ubuntu server edition ? That doesn't come with a GUI.
You can type in the following to get the Gnome desktop :
```

sudo apt-get install ubuntu-desktop

```
Or the somewhat lighter Xfce4 desktop :
```

sudo apt-get install xubuntu-desktop

```

---

### Post by GabrielSyme on 2009-04-06
Thank you so much, Ricky, for posting this guide. This has helped the non-profit organization I work at save a whole lot of money! 

I ran into a problem and after much beating of head against keyboard I have found a solution. The problem: windows 98. In this current set up, Windows 98 will not join the domain. In a previous setup of a samba PDC I had, windows 98 could. 

The reason win 98 can't join is that win 98 does not support Windows NT password hashing and samba by default only allows NT password hashing. Or rather, it by default does not allow lanman password hashing, which is what Win 98 needs. I at first thought this would be impossible to make work, but I found that in the LDAP entry for each user there is a field for "sambaLMPassword." This is the password hash that Windows 98 needs and the smbldap-tools keep updated (I think). So all we need to do is tell samba to allow lanman password authentication. 

just add this to the global section of smb.conf:
```
lanman auth = yes
client lanman auth = yes
```
I got this code from [here.]("http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/linux-general-1/samba-cannot-access-share-files-from-win98-but-winxp-ok-520705/page2.html")
I am not sure if the second line is needed, but everything is working fine with it...

And Win 98 should log in just fine! Hope this helps some body. 
The best solution would be to just can win 98 I think, but this wasn't practical in my situation, and the fix was pretty simple (once I found it).

```
The following error occurred when DNS was queried for the service
location (SRV) resource record used to locate a domain controller for
domain jajo:

The error was: "DNS name does not exist."
(error code 0x0000232B RCODE_NAME_ERROR)

The query was for the SRV record for _ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs.jajo

Common causes of this error include the following:

- The DNS SRV record is not registered in DNS.

- One or more of the following zones do not include delegation to its
child zone:

jajo
. (the root zone)
```

So I researched and I guess since I'm not using windows dns server which is built to work with AD, things like _ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs.jajo aren't there. ### Post by linuxPDC on 2009-05-16
Hello

I have followed this excellent howto. Everything works fine on the Linux PCs. However I encountered a problem getting Windows XP professional clients to join the Domain. I did the following steps:

[LIST=1]
[*]Log into the computer as an Administrative user (most likely **Administrator**)
[*]Right click "My Computer" and select "Properties"
[*]Select the "Computer Name" tab at the top
[*]Click the "Change" button near the bottom
[*]In this new window select the "Domain:" radio button in the "Member of" section
[*]Type in your domain name - in our example the domain name to enter is simply "**example**"
[*]Click the "OK" button
[*]A window should pop up asking you for a username and password. Use "**root**" and your root password which should still be "**12345**" unless you changed it.
[/LIST]
After this step I get a pop up telling me the following:
The following error occurred attempting to join the domain "**example":**
The user name could not be found.

### Post by AreaLeao on 2009-06-09
Sorry I couldn't find winbind.conf but I've found smbldap.conf and smbldap_bind.conf.

smbldap.conf
```
# $Source: /opt/cvs/samba/smbldap-tools/smbldap.conf,v $
# $Id: smbldap.conf,v 1.18 2005/05/27 14:28:47 jtournier Exp $
#
# smbldap-tools.conf : Q & D configuration file for smbldap-tools

#  This code was developped by IDEALX (http://IDEALX.org/) and
#  contributors (their names can be found in the CONTRIBUTORS file).
#
#                 Copyright (C) 2001-2002 IDEALX
#
#  This program is free software; you can redistribute it and/or
#  modify it under the terms of the GNU General Public License
#  as published by the Free Software Foundation; either version 2
#  of the License, or (at your option) any later version.
#
#  This program is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
#  but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
#  MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
#  GNU General Public License for more details.
#
#  You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
#  along with this program; if not, write to the Free Software
#  Foundation, Inc., 59 Temple Place - Suite 330, Boston, MA 02111-1307,
#  USA.

#  Purpose :
#       . be the configuration file for all smbldap-tools scripts

##############################################################################
#
# General Configuration
#
##############################################################################

# Put your own SID. To obtain this number do: "net getlocalsid".
# If not defined, parameter is taking from "net getlocalsid" return
SID="S-1-5-21-4205727931-4131263253-1851132061"

# Domain name the Samba server is in charged.
# If not defined, parameter is taking from smb.conf configuration file
# Ex: sambaDomain="IDEALX-NT"
sambaDomain="IDEALX-NT"

##############################################################################
#
# LDAP Configuration
#
##############################################################################

# Notes: to use to dual ldap servers backend for Samba, you must patch
# Samba with the dual-head patch from IDEALX. If not using this patch
# just use the same server for slaveLDAP and masterLDAP.
# Those two servers declarations can also be used when you have 
# . one master LDAP server where all writing operations must be done
# . one slave LDAP server where all reading operations must be done
#   (typically a replication directory)

# Slave LDAP server
# Ex: slaveLDAP=127.0.0.1
# If not defined, parameter is set to "127.0.0.1"
slaveLDAP="127.0.0.1"

# Slave LDAP port
# If not defined, parameter is set to "389"
slavePort="389"

# Master LDAP server: needed for write operations
# Ex: masterLDAP=127.0.0.1
# If not defined, parameter is set to "127.0.0.1"
masterLDAP="127.0.0.1"

# Master LDAP port
# If not defined, parameter is set to "389"
masterPort="389"

# Use TLS for LDAP
# If set to 1, this option will use start_tls for connection
# (you should also used the port 389)
# If not defined, parameter is set to "1"
ldapTLS="1"

# How to verify the server's certificate (none, optional or require)
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
verify="require"

# CA certificate
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
cafile="/etc/opt/IDEALX/smbldap-tools/ca.pem"

# certificate to use to connect to the ldap server
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
clientcert="/etc/opt/IDEALX/smbldap-tools/smbldap-tools.pem"

# key certificate to use to connect to the ldap server
# see "man Net::LDAP" in start_tls section for more details
clientkey="/etc/opt/IDEALX/smbldap-tools/smbldap-tools.key"

# LDAP Suffix
# Ex: suffix=dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG
suffix="dc=idealx,dc=org"

# Where are stored Users
# Ex: usersdn="ou=Users,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for usersdn
usersdn="ou=Users,${suffix}"

# Where are stored Computers
# Ex: computersdn="ou=Computers,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for computersdn
computersdn="ou=Computers,${suffix}"

# Where are stored Groups
# Ex: groupsdn="ou=Groups,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for groupsdn
groupsdn="ou=Groups,${suffix}"

# Where are stored Idmap entries (used if samba is a domain member server)
# Ex: groupsdn="ou=Idmap,dc=IDEALX,dc=ORG"
# Warning: if 'suffix' is not set here, you must set the full dn for idmapdn
idmapdn="ou=Idmap,${suffix}"

# Where to store next uidNumber and gidNumber available for new users and groups
# If not defined, entries are stored in sambaDomainName object.
# Ex: sambaUnixIdPooldn="sambaDomainName=${sambaDomain},${suffix}"
# Ex: sambaUnixIdPooldn="cn=NextFreeUnixId,${suffix}"
sambaUnixIdPooldn="sambaDomainName=IDEALX-NT,${suffix}"

# Default scope Used
scope="sub"

# Unix password encryption (CRYPT, MD5, SMD5, SSHA, SHA, CLEARTEXT)
hash_encrypt="SSHA"

# if hash_encrypt is set to CRYPT, you may set a salt format.
# default is "%s", but many systems will generate MD5 hashed
# passwords if you use "$1$%.8s". This parameter is optional!
crypt_salt_format="%s"

##############################################################################
# 
# Unix Accounts Configuration
# 
##############################################################################

# Login defs
# Default Login Shell
# Ex: userLoginShell="/bin/bash"
userLoginShell="/bin/bash"

# Home directory
# Ex: userHome="/home/%U"
userHome="/home/%U"

# Default mode used for user homeDirectory
userHomeDirectoryMode="700"

# Gecos
userGecos="System User"

# Default User (POSIX and Samba) GID
defaultUserGid="513"

# Default Computer (Samba) GID
defaultComputerGid="515"

# Skel dir
skeletonDir="/etc/skel"

# Default password validation time (time in days) Comment the next line if
# you don't want password to be enable for defaultMaxPasswordAge days (be
# careful to the sambaPwdMustChange attribute's value)
defaultMaxPasswordAge="45"

##############################################################################
#
# SAMBA Configuration
#
##############################################################################

# The UNC path to home drives location (%U username substitution)
# Just set it to a null string if you want to use the smb.conf 'logon home'
# directive and/or disable roaming profiles
# Ex: userSmbHome="\\PDC-SMB3\%U"
userSmbHome="\\PDC-SRV\%U"

# The UNC path to profiles locations (%U username substitution)
# Just set it to a null string if you want to use the smb.conf 'logon path'
# directive and/or disable roaming profiles
# Ex: userProfile="\\PDC-SMB3\profiles\%U"
userProfile="\\PDC-SRV\profiles\%U"

# The default Home Drive Letter mapping
# (will be automatically mapped at logon time if home directory exist)
# Ex: userHomeDrive="H:"
userHomeDrive="H:"

# The default user netlogon script name (%U username substitution)
# if not used, will be automatically username.cmd
# make sure script file is edited under dos
# Ex: userScript="startup.cmd" # make sure script file is edited under dos
userScript="logon.bat"

# Domain appended to the users "mail"-attribute
# when smbldap-useradd -M is used
# Ex: mailDomain="idealx.com"
mailDomain="idealx.com"

##############################################################################
#
# SMBLDAP-TOOLS Configuration (default are ok for a RedHat)
#
##############################################################################

# Allows not to use smbpasswd (if with_smbpasswd == 0 in smbldap_conf.pm) but
# prefer Crypt::SmbHash library
with_smbpasswd="0"
smbpasswd="/usr/bin/smbpasswd"

# Allows not to use slappasswd (if with_slappasswd == 0 in smbldap_conf.pm)
# but prefer Crypt:: libraries
with_slappasswd="0"
slappasswd="/usr/sbin/slappasswd"

# comment out the following line to get rid of the default banner
# no_banner="1"

```

And here is smbldap_bind.conf:

```

############################
# Credential Configuration #
############################
# Notes: you can specify two differents configuration if you use a
# master ldap for writing access and a slave ldap server for reading access
# By default, we will use the same DN (so it will work for standard Samba
# release)
slaveDN="cn=Manager,dc=idealx,dc=org"
slavePw="secret"
masterDN="cn=Manager,dc=idealx,dc=org"
masterPw="secret"
```


After reading these conf files, I'm beggining to understand the problem. :D
But I'm still accepting tips.

> **ingcabral said:**
> Seems to be something messing around in your config.
Please post your winbind.conf, smbldap_bind.conf and smbldap.conf

I'm thinking that the rootdn entry says something like

cn=admin,dc=local on those files. Remove the 'dc=local' and try again
Regards!

---

### Post by ingcabral on 2009-06-09
We can start trying replacing this entries in smbldap_bind.conf:

slaveDN="cn=Manager,dc=idealx,dc=org"
slavePw="secret"
masterDN="cn=Manager,dc=idealx,dc=org"
masterPw="secret"

with

slaveDN="cn=admin,dc=idealx,dc=org"
slavePw="your password"
masterDN="cn=admin,dc=idealx,dc=org"
masterPw="your password"

Remember that the entry from 'your passwords' is set when you run 'smbpasswd -W'

Regards,

Ariel

### Post by linuxPDC on 2009-06-11
> **linuxPDC said:**
> Hello

I have followed this excellent howto. Everything works fine on the Linux PCs. However I encountered a problem getting Windows XP professional clients to join the Domain. I did the following steps:

[LIST=1]
[*]Log into the computer as an Administrative user (most likely **Administrator**)
[*]Right click "My Computer" and select "Properties"
[*]Select the "Computer Name" tab at the top
[*]Click the "Change" button near the bottom
[*]In this new window select the "Domain:" radio button in the "Member of" section
[*]Type in your domain name - in our example the domain name to enter is simply "**example**"
[*]Click the "OK" button
[*]A window should pop up asking you for a username and password. Use "**root**" and your root password which should still be "**12345**" unless you changed it.
[/LIST]
After this step I get a pop up telling me the following:
The following error occurred attempting to join the domain "**example":**
The user name could not be found.

I checked the ldap data and found that an entry for the XP pc "winxp001" has been added to ou=Computers as "winxp001$". In the log folder, I found a log file for the xp pc which says:
passdb/pdb_interface.c:pdb_default_create_user(336)
_samr_create_user: Running the command `/usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -w "winxp001$"' gave 9

Does any body know how to resolve this issue?

Resolved the problem and got my Debian samba + ldap PDC up and running finally. I got the solution idea from the following post:

[http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/linux-networking-3/username-could-not-be-found-samba-163486/](http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/linux-networking-3/username-could-not-be-found-samba-163486/)

If you face the same problem, you need to create a user account for the WIN XP machine after ading the dollar sign to the end such as "xxx" should be "xxx$" usind the following command:

```
/usr/sbin/useradd -s /bin/false -d /home/nobody %u
```

Then try joining the domain.

I hope in next version of samba-ldap package takes care of creating the linux user issue then create it in samba afterwords.

Thanks every body for no help.

### Post by tbg58 on 2009-08-22
There is an important change in the code from Ubuntu Server 7.10 to 8.04 which affects these instructions - starting with 8.04 the server software includes AppArmor which among other things protects the specified directories of systems software.
The LDAP installation in these instructions calls for the creation of alternate directories to those specified in the default application.  AppArmor prevents LDAP from working in the alternate location if you follow these instructions on Ubuntu Server 8.04.  This leaves you with two alternatives:
1.  Use the default directory for LDAP and skip the instructions for the alternate directory structure.
2.  Use the alternate directory structure and edit the LDAP configuration files and the AppArmor profile for LDAP.

Here's a link to the information about AppArmor
[http://www.ubuntu.com/products/whatisubuntu/serveredition/features/apparmor](http://www.ubuntu.com/products/whatisubuntu/serveredition/features/apparmor)

You're not the only one having trouble getting LDAP configured.  I'm running into the same problem as you and have not completed my installation, but I did get far enough to find out it was AppArmor that was hosing the LDAP installation in the alternative directory.
As soon as I make some progress I'll report my results here - sorry I can't post a complete solution right away, but I have some deadlines pending on some projects.  Getting this server working is on my list, and I will keep this thread posted.
Hope this at least helps to point some folks in the right direction - perhaps someone with a few free cycles can correct the documentation and update for 8.04 LTS.
Rock on...

---

### Post by dragonslayr on 2009-08-28
I spent much time getting this type of solution to work a coupla of years ago. I finally put it into production use and it worked great. Then something really really strange happened. I lost both mirrored hard drives at once "raptors".

Restores were giving me a problem because they first relied on installing a base operating system "Feisty server eddition". The install kept hanging..  The clock is ticking and the domain controller is down.. 1/2 of day one was getting users "functional"
I also made the bad decision to use "redirects" to speed up roaming profiles..  

In a nutshell, I was in bad shape, and under the gun. This time around, I'm choosing a "canned" solution. Ebox. It's come a long way and the main operating system will run in a vm with the files on a separate hard drive. In case of ultimate disaster, the vm can be brought up quickly on another machine..  

Just thought I'd share since this just happened last week and was quite the horror story..  At the least I should have had a ghost image of the original operating system. When a PDC is down, it's very bad news.. Make triple methods of recovery plans and be careful of how you "marry" it.. Just a heads up for you guys..

---

### Post by dragonslayr on 2009-09-08
> **senor_smile said:**
> Did you actually get the PDC feature of ebox working?  I tried and couldn't get a single xp box to connect to the domain.  The instructions are very limited on their site.  If so, would you be able to post some information on how you got it up and running?

I'd imagine you ran into a samba problem after you changed your ip. That happened to me and I could not join. These commands will do the trick.

/etc/init.d/samba stop
rm /var/cache/samba/browse.dat
rm /var/lib/samba/wins.dat
sudo /etc/init.d/samba start

When Client PCs (Windows XP P2) log on to domain, it takes a long time to load (~15 minutes). It also happens when shutting down the PC (Waiting for saving the user settings). 

When it happens, I just reset the user account (by deleting the old account and create a new one with the same username - his profile is saved) to fix it.

Any idea about this?

This is my smb.conf file:

> 
[FONT=monospace][global]
        # Domain name ..
        workgroup = ABCDEF
        # Server name - as seen by Windows PCs ..
        netbios name = SERVER

        # Be a PDC ..
        domain logons = Yes
        domain master = Yes
        # Be a WINS server ..
        wins support = true

        obey pam restrictions = Yes
        dns proxy = No
        os level = 35
        log file = /var/log/samba/log.%m
        max log size = 1000
        syslog = 0
        panic action = /usr/share/samba/panic-action %d
        pam password change = Yes

        # Allows users on WinXP PCs to change their password when they press Ctrl-Alt-Del
        unix password sync = no
        ldap passwd sync = yes

        # Printing from PCs will go via CUPS ..
        load printers = yes
        printing = cups
        printcap name = cups

        # Use LDAP for Samba user accounts and groups ..
        passdb backend = ldapsam:ldap://localhost

        # This must match init.ldif ..
        ldap suffix = dc=abcdef
        # The password for cn=admin MUST be stored in /etc/samba/secrets.tdb
        # This is done by running 'sudo smbpasswd -w'.
        ldap admin dn = cn=admin,dc=abcdef

        # 4 OUs that Samba uses when creating user accounts, computer accounts, etc.
        # (Because we are using smbldap-tools, call them 'Users', 'Computers', etc.)
        ldap machine suffix = ou=Computers
        ldap user suffix = ou=Users
        ldap group suffix = ou=Groups
        ldap idmap suffix = ou=Idmap
        # Samba and LDAP server are on the same server in this example.
        ldap ssl = no

        # Scripts for Samba to use if it creates users, groups, etc.
        add user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -m '%u'
        delete user script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-userdel %u
        add group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupadd -p '%g'
        delete group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupdel '%g'
        add user to group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -m '%u' '%g'
        delete user from group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-groupmod -x '%u' '%g'
        set primary group script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-usermod -g '%g' '%u'

        # Script that Samba users when a PC joins the domain ..
        # (when changing 'Computer Properties' on the PC)
        add machine script = /usr/sbin/smbldap-useradd -w '%u'

        # Values used when a new user is created ..
        # (Note: '%L' does not work properly with smbldap-tools 0.9.4-1)
       logon path = \\%N\%U\profile
      logon drive = H:
         logon home = \\%N\%U

        logon script =


        # This is required for Windows XP client ..
        server signing = auto
        server schannel = Auto

[homes]
        comment = Home Directories
        valid users = %S
        read only = No
        browseable = No

[netlogon]
        comment = Network Logon Service
        path = /var/lib/samba/netlogon
        admin users = root
        guest ok = Yes
        browseable = No

[Profiles]
        comment = Roaming Profile Share
        # would probably change this to elsewhere in a production system ..
        path = /var/lib/samba/profiles
        read only = No
        profile acls = Yes
        browsable = No

[printers]
        comment = All Printers
        path = /var/spool/samba
        use client driver = Yes
        create mask = 0600
        guest ok = Yes
        printable = Yes
        browseable = No
        public = yes
        writable = yes
        admin users = root
        write list = root

[print$]
        comment = Printer Drivers Share
        path = /var/lib/samba/printers
        write list = root
        create mask = 0664
        directory mask = 0775
        admin users = root

[/FONT]

Nice, you got it working on an LTS!!! Are there many things you had to do differently than in this tutorial? 
Also I believe that some configuration stuff was changed in LDAP. Although the old way could still work it's probably deprecated. Did you just try to get it to work (which is cool enough as it is :D ) or did you really take into account the differences between 7.10 and 8.04?

The reason I am asking this is because I have the opinion that this topic in general is very "under"-documented (all is vague, incomplete, outdated). This tutorial is the only valuable piece out there.
This is so strange as this is a very important and fundamental topic.
I think the community needs at least a complete and fully functioning guide which works with the latest LTS. 
Same goes for NFS, NIS, Kerberos etc.

So please **vote** to change this:
[http://brainstorm.ubuntu.com/idea/22151/](http://brainstorm.ubuntu.com/idea/22151/)

### Post by spliner on 2009-11-02
This guide is designed for 7.10 (now obsolete).  It also works just fine on 8.04 LTS if you're careful about the ldap_data directory (App Armor will cause you problems).  To solve that, simply use the standard directory rather than a custom one and it should work just fine.  I've been able to set up a working PDC using 8.04 several times.  Anything above that I'd caution you to test first with this guide (and I'd test any version first on a VM if possible no matter what you intend to do for a production server).  I currently have a PDC running on 7.10 and one on 8.04.  I have not attempted the upgrade on the 7.10 but since its updates are no longer maintained I will have to do this soon.

My other suggestion is read the entire thread (yes it's very long) and take notes as you go for those that have done upgrades to the newer versions of Ubuntu.

I would also suggest using an LTS release for any production server as it'll give you more time to use the thing before an upgrade is necessary (which can possibly break things).

Have fun!  And please post your results.  =)

---

This guide worked perfectly up until I started implementing it. I work at the corporate office for a retail chain and I want to use this with each of our locations. The way out network is setup, we are using 192.168.1.XX at the corporate office, then 192.168.2.xx, 3.xx, etc, for our stores. 

I am able to join the domain from within our network, but when attempting  to connect from outside our LAN from say a workstation with the ip of 192.168.6.100, I get this error:
> 
A domain controller for the domain example could not be contacted. Details:
> Note: This information is intended for a network administrator.  If you are not your network's administrator, notify the administrator that you received this information, which has been recorded in the file C:\WINDOWS\debug\dcdiag.txt.

The domain name example might be a NetBIOS domain name.  If this is the case, verify that the domain name is properly registered with WINS.

If you are certain that the name is not a NetBIOS domain name, then the following information can help you troubleshoot your DNS configuration.

The following error occurred when DNS was queried for the service location (SRV) resource record used to locate a domain controller for domain example:

The error was: "DNS name does not exist."
(error code 0x0000232B RCODE_NAME_ERROR)

The query was for the SRV record for _ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs.example

Common causes of this error include the following:

- The DNS SRV record is not registered in DNS.

- One or more of the following zones do not include delegation to its child zone:

example
. (the root zone)

For information about correcting this problem, click Help.Is this something I can change in the configuration?

### Post by spliner on 2009-11-20
FYI for anyone installing a server using this guide on 8.04:

There is a newer version of Webadmin out, so when you use wget to download the package use the following address:

wget [http://downloads.sourceforge.net/project/webadmin/webmin/1.490/webmin_1.490_all.deb](http://downloads.sourceforge.net/project/webadmin/webmin/1.490/webmin_1.490_all.deb)

Substitute the new filename for the rest of the instructions for installing webadmin (IE: webmin_1.490_all.deb instead of webmin_1.400_all.deb like in the original guide).

Also, for using this guide with 8.04 instead of 7.10, I suggest using the default directory for ldap_data instead of creating a custom one.

This was probably covered earlier in this discussion but thought I'd mention it for those who are having to sift through this huge thread.

Spliner

### Post by senor_smile on 2009-11-21
> **juengerman said:**
> This guide worked perfectly up until I started implementing it. I work at the corporate office for a retail chain and I want to use this with each of our locations. The way out network is setup, we are using 192.168.1.XX at the corporate office, then 192.168.2.xx, 3.xx, etc, for our stores. 

I am able to join the domain from within our network, but when attempting  to connect from outside our LAN from say a workstation with the ip of 192.168.6.100, I get this error:
Details:
Is this something I can change in the configuration?

Are you able to ping the IP address of the samba server from the machine that you're trying to connect to the domain?  I suspect that their netmasks are 24 bit (i.e. 255.255.255.0) and can therefore only talk to other i.p. addresses in their own subnet.  If you changed all netmasks to 255.255.0.0 (which would make the network 192.168.0.0/16 ) you might be able to have some luck.

If you changed all netmasks to 255.255.0.0 (which would make the network 192.168.0.0/16 ) you might be able to have some luck.

### Post by leijijiang on 2010-01-02
> **rickyjones said:**
> I've identified one major issue with this so far - apparently users cannot change their passwords.

```

ricky@dc01-ubuntu:~$ passwd
Enter login(LDAP) password: 12345
passwd: Authentication information cannot be recovered
passwd: password unchanged
ricky@dc01-ubuntu:~$ smbldap-passwd
Identity validation...
enter your UNIX password: 12345
Changing UNIX and samba passwords for ricky
New password: 54321
Retype new password: 54321
failed to modify entry: Insufficient access at /usr/sbin/smbldap-passwd line 227, <STDIN> line 3.
ricky@dc01-ubuntu:~$

```Looks like it is time to do some more research.

EDIT:

Apparently "smbpasswd" will successfully change your password as an FYI. Source: [https://listman.redhat.com/archives/k12osn/2007-October/msg00102.html](https://listman.redhat.com/archives/k12osn/2007-October/msg00102.html)

I just tested this and it works.

-Richard

To fix the issue "failed to modify entry: Insufficient access", you can modify the slapd.conf like this:

# The userPassword by default can be changed
# by the entry owning it if they are authenticated.
# Others should not be able to see it, except the
# admin entry below
# These access lines apply to database #1 only
access to attrs=userPassword,shadowLastChange,shadowMax,sambaNTPassword,sambaLMPassword,sambaPwdLastSet,sambaPwdMustChange,sambaAcctFlags
        by dn="cn=admin,dc=ultraelectron,dc=com" write
        by anonymous auth
        by self write
        by * none

---

I'd like to be able to let users change their own passwords.

To fix this issue, you can modify the slapd.conf like this:

# The userPassword by default can be changed
# by the entry owning it if they are authenticated.
# Others should not be able to see it, except the
# admin entry below
# These access lines apply to database #1 only
access to attrs=userPassword,shadowLastChange,shadowMax,samb aNTPassword,sambaLMPassword,sambaPwdLastSet,sambaP wdMustChange,sambaAcctFlags
        by dn="cn=admin,dc=ultraelectron,dc=com" write
        by anonymous auth
        by self write
        by * none

### Post by spliner on 2010-01-05
> **leijijiang said:**
> To fix this issue, you can modify the slapd.conf like this:

# The userPassword by default can be changed
# by the entry owning it if they are authenticated.
# Others should not be able to see it, except the
# admin entry below
# These access lines apply to database #1 only
access to attrs=userPassword,shadowLastChange,shadowMax,samb aNTPassword,sambaLMPassword,sambaPwdLastSet,sambaP wdMustChange,sambaAcctFlags
        by dn="cn=admin,dc=ultraelectron,dc=com" write
        by anonymous auth
        by self write
        by * none

I believe you can also comment out the following line in the smb.conf file to accomplish the same thing.  Your solution might be the better one?  I'll have to give it a try, thanks!

unix password sync = yes

The problem with commenting out that line is that Samba won't try and sync passwords with the unix accounts.  This could become a problem if you allow your users to log into the Samba PDC as well as their workstations.  But for Windows it seems to fix the problem for me.

Spliner

### Post by spliner on 2010-01-07
**Getting things to work with Windows 7:**

I've had some success getting Windows 7 Pro and Ultimate clients to authenticate on my test server using this guide.  I used Ubuntu 8.04LTS, set up the guide as normal, but added an extra step.

Complete the guide down to step 7, following the suggestions from others in this thread about changes that need to be done for 8.04 (very few).

Call it step 7.5...  Follow this guide to upgrade the default Samba install to 3.4.3:  [http://www.jeremycole.com/blog/2009/12/01/upgrade-samba-3-0-28a-to-3-4-3-on-ubuntu-8-04-lts/](http://www.jeremycole.com/blog/2009/12/01/upgrade-samba-3-0-28a-to-3-4-3-on-ubuntu-8-04-lts/)

**_[COLOR="Red"]Updated:[/COLOR]_**  Samba 3.4.4 stable is out and fixes some trust issues with Windows 7.  The guide above is not updated to reflect Samba 3.4.4 but works all the same if you substitute the correct filenames/directories for 3.4.4 instead of 3.4.3.  I used the guide above to upgrade from 3.4.3 to 3.4.4 without any issues.

Continue on with Step 8.

You might also want to substitute Webmin 1.500 (newest version) when installing it, but other than that everything seems to work except changing passwords on Windows 7 clients.  To accomplish that, simply follow one of the more recent suggestions posted here.

**Getting a Windows 7 client to join the domain:**

Windows 7 needs some registry modification done to it in order for it to use a SAMBA PDC.  The steps are simple and should not cause any undue harm to your PC/OS.  You will need to use the Windows Registry Editor to make the changes (or find a patch somewhere that has been created and apply it).  I prefer to edit the registry myself, so that is what I&#8217;ll post here.

This information is taken directly from the Samba Windows 7 Wiki at [http://wiki.samba.org/index.php/Windows7](http://wiki.samba.org/index.php/Windows7).

The key(s) you want to modify are:

HKLM\System\CCS\Services\LanmanWorkstation\Parameters
            DWORD  DomainCompatibilityMode = 1
            DWORD  DNSNameResolutionRequired = 0

Do not modify any other keys as stated in some other guides on the net.  The Wiki above gives you the correct values if you have made this mistake.

When adding a Windows 7 client to the domain, follow this procedure:

1) Make sure you have set up your static IP, and make sure you have the DNS set to the DNS IP of your server for this to work at all.
2) Click Start, then RIGHT CLICK &#8220;Computer&#8221; and LEFT CLICK &#8220;Properties&#8221;.
3) Where it says &#8220;Computer name, domain, and workgroup settings&#8221; click &#8220;Change Settings&#8221;.
4) Simply click &#8220;Change&#8221; and enter the domain name under &#8220;Member of&#8221; with the radial button for &#8220;Domain&#8221; selected.  Click OK (possibly several times). and enter &#8220;root&#8221; as the username, and your password for the sysadmin account. 
5) You will receive a DNS error, simply click out of this error and ignore it.  It is currently assumed this will be fixed in upcoming versions of Samba but it does not affect functionality of a Windows 7 client (to my knowledge).

**_Other interesting things to change on Windows 7:_**

**Disabling Cached Logons**

Modify the following registry key:

HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\MICROSOFT\WINDOWS NT\CURRENT VERSION\WINLOGON

Change the Value for &#8220;CachedLogonsCount&#8221; to 0 instead of the default 10.  This turns off cached logins.

By changing &#8220;CachedLogonsCount&#8221; to 0, you tell Windows not to allow logins unless the username and password can be verified by your PDC.  You can still log into the PC with a local Administrator account if you choose to do so.  But logging into the domain is halted if the PDC is unavailable.

**Using Windows XP to connect to a Windows 7 Remote Desktop Session**
(This has nothing really to do with Ubuntu/Samba)

Since I use Windows XP at the moment for my workstation, I had some issues connecting with the Windows 7 VM I created using Remote Desktop (Which is faster than VMWare&#8217;s console).  For testing reasons you may want to use the instructions located at the link below to enable Network Level Authentication on an XP SP3 PC so that you can connect to the Windows 7 PC using Remote Desktop Connection.  It really has nothing to do with the Samba setup, but may make things easier for testing so I&#8217;ve included instructions here:

[http://support.microsoft.com/kb/951608](http://support.microsoft.com/kb/951608)

Basically you need to modify two registry keys, and reboot the PC for this to work:

HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa
-Right Click &#8220;Security Packages&#8221; and click modify, then add &#8220;tspkg&#8221; to the list.
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders
-RIGHT CLICK &#8220;SecurityProviders&#8221; and click modify, then add credssp.dll to the list.

Close the registry editor, and reboot the PC.  You should now be able to connect to any Windows 7 PC using Remote Desktop Connection.

**Free 90-day trial of Windows 7 Enterprise now available**

Also, there is a free 90-day trial available of Windows 7 Enterprise for anyone who wants to test with this guide.  It can be found here:  [http://technet.microsoft.com/en-us/evalcenter/cc442495.aspx](http://technet.microsoft.com/en-us/evalcenter/cc442495.aspx)

Enjoy.  I'll be trying to duplicate this again next week.  If I run into any issues that I forgot to mention I'll post here.  If anyone else runs into problems feel free to ask and I'll try and find an answer.  I believe the above guide for installing Samba 3.4.3 can be used with an already functioning server set up using this guide.  I started from scratch, added that extra step and all worked well.  If anyone does an upgrade on a system that has been in use and it works well please post here.

I would like to preserve the ldap schema (usernames + passwords as well as home drives...)

### Post by rabbit83 on 2010-08-06
> **judyg8or said:**
> How can I set the permissions so that they are stagnant and do not change based on the last user that edited the document?

I had the same problems with my server. The best solution I found was to share the directory via samba, so I can set the permissions via fstab and smb.conf. Works fine now with both WinXP and Ubuntu clients.

I also havent mounted the share in /ldaphome but in /mnt.

### Post by mitanc on 2012-07-24
Hi All,

I been running this config for a while and yesterday I stupidly did a sudo apt-get upgrade and now my windows 7 client machine cannot log in to the domain.  I get the trust relationship has failed error.  

Any ideas how to proceed?

Please help!  Thanks!

---

### Post by skillllllz on 2012-07-24
> **mitanc said:**
> Hi All,

I been running this config for a while and yesterday I stupidly did a sudo apt-get upgrade and now my windows 7 client machine cannot log in to the domain.  I get the trust relationship has failed error.  

Any ideas how to proceed?

Please help!  Thanks!

This can and does happen on normal functioning Windows networks too. The solution is simple:

Disjoin the Windows PC from the domain, then join again and reboot. As always, be sure to backup the PC before you do anything. Also, before you begin, make sure you have access to a local admin account on the Windows PC; this will make life easier if you run into trouble and cannot join the domain again.

---

Also, before you begin, make sure you have access to a local admin account on the Windows PC; this will make life easier if you run into trouble and cannot join the domain again.

Hmm, i tried that.  Disconnected from DOMAIN no problem.  Re-connect to domain, again no issue.

Final reboot, try to log on as a user of that domain onto the domain trust relationship issue.
